title: Persistence-based topological optimization: a survey
summary: Computational topology provides a tool, persistent homology, to extract quantitative descriptors from structured objects (images, graphs, point clouds, etc). These descriptors can then be involved in optimization problems, typically as a way to incorporate topological priors or to regularize machine learning models. This is usually achieved by minimizing adequate, topologically-informed losses based on these descriptors, which, in turn, naturally raises theoretical and practical questions about the possibility of optimizing such loss functions using gradient-based algorithms. This has been an active research field in the topological data analysis community over the last decade, and various techniques have been developed to enable optimization of persistence-based loss functions with gradient descent schemes. This survey presents the current state of this field, covering its theoretical foundations, the algorithmic aspects, and showcasing practical uses in several applications. It includes a detailed introduction to persistence theory and, as such, aims at being accessible to mathematicians and data scientists newcomers to the field. It is accompanied by an open-source library which implements the different approaches covered in this survey, providing a convenient playground for researchers to get familiar with the field.

AI.tldr: Survey of gradient-based optimization techniques for persistence-based losses in persistent homology, covering theory, algorithms, and applications; accompanied by an open-source software library.
AI.motivation: Enable integration of topological descriptors into machine learning and optimization workflows by enabling differentiable, trainable, persistence-based losses.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review and synthesis, with a taxonomy of optimization approaches (differentiable surrogates, subgradient methods, backpropagation through diagrams, and kernel/feature-based representations), theoretical foundations, algorithms, and practical guidance; includes an open-source library implementing the approaches.
AI.result: Consolidated framework and taxonomy of gradient-based persistence optimization, practical guidelines, and a software toolkit to experiment with the methods across applications.
AI.conclusion: The field has matured to support robust gradient-based optimization of persistence-based losses; the survey and accompanying software provide a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners to adopt and extend these techniques.

title: Shortest Paths in Geodesic Unit-Disk Graphs
comment: A preliminary version will appear in SoCG 2026. This version further improves the result in the preliminary version by proposing a new dynamic data structure for priority-queue updates (Section 4)
summary: Let $S$ be a set of $n$ points in a polygon $P$ with $m$ vertices. The geodesic unit-disk graph $G(S)$ induced by $S$ has vertex set $S$ and contains an edge between two vertices whenever their geodesic distance in $P$ is at most one. In the weighted version, each edge is assigned weight equal to the geodesic distance between its endpoints; in the unweighted version, every edge has weight $1$. Given a source point $s \in S$, we study the problem of computing shortest paths from $s$ to all vertices of $G(S)$. To the best of our knowledge, this problem has not been investigated previously. A naive approach constructs $G(S)$ explicitly and then applies a standard shortest path algorithm for general graphs, but this requires quadratic time in the worst case, since $G(S)$ may contain $Ω(n^2)$ edges. In this paper, we give the first subquadratic-time algorithms for this problem. For the weighted case, when $P$ is a simple polygon, we obtain an $O(m + n \log^{2} n \log^{2} m)$-time algorithm. For the unweighted case, we provide an $O(m + n \log n \log^{2} m)$-time algorithm for simple polygons, and an $O(\sqrt{n} (n+m)\log(n+m))$-time algorithm for polygons with holes. To achieve these results, we develop a data structure for deletion-only geodesic unit-disk range emptiness queries, as well as a data structure for constructing implicit additively weighted geodesic Voronoi diagrams in simple polygons. In addition, we propose a dynamic data structure that extends Bentley's logarithmic method from insertions to priority-queue updates, namely insertion and delete-min operations. These results may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: Subquadratic-time single-source shortest-path algorithms for geodesic unit-disk graphs in polygonal domains, for both weighted and unweighted variants, with new geometric data structures and a dynamic extension of Bentley's method.
AI.motivation: Motivate outperforming the naive quadratic approach caused by potentially dense G(S) (Ω(n^2) edges) and enable efficient shortest-path computations in geodesic metric spaces within polygons.
AI.method: Introduce new data structures: (i) deletion-only geodesic unit-disk range emptiness queries; (ii) implicit additively weighted geodesic Voronoi diagrams in simple polygons; and (iii) a dynamic extension of Bentley's logarithmic method supporting insertions and delete-min in priority queues. Use these to design subquadratic SSSP algorithms for both weighted and unweighted cases; time bounds depend on m (polygon vertices) and n (sites).
AI.result: Weighted simple-polygon case: O(m + n log^2 n log^2 m). Unweighted simple-polygon case: O(m + n log n log^2 m). Polygons with holes: O(√n (n+m) log(n+m)).
AI.conclusion: First subquadratic-time algorithms for single-source shortest paths in geodesic unit-disk graphs within polygonal domains are obtained; new geometric data structures and a dynamic priority-queue framework are of independent interest for related problems.

title: Approximating Pareto Sum via Bounded Monotone Min-Plus Convolution
summary:
The Pareto sum of two-dimensional point sets $P$ and $Q$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is defined as the skyline of the points in their Minkowski sum. The problem of efficiently computing the Pareto sum arises frequently in bi-criteria optimization algorithms. Prior work establishes that computing the Pareto sum of sets $P$ and $Q$ of size $n$ suffers from conditional lower bounds that rule out strongly subquadratic $O(n^{2-ε})$-time algorithms, even when the output size is $Θ(n)$. Naturally, we ask: How efficiently can we \emph{approximate} Pareto sums, both in theory and practice? Can we beat the near-quadratic-time state of the art for exact algorithms?
  On the theoretical side, we formulate a notion of additively approximate Pareto sets and show that computing an approximate Pareto set is \emph{fine-grained equivalent} to Bounded Monotone Min-Plus Convolution. Leveraging a remarkable $\tilde{O}(n^{1.5})$-time algorithm for the latter problem (Chi, Duan, Xie, Zhang; STOC '22), we thus obtain a strongly subquadratic (and conditionally optimal) approximation algorithm for computing Pareto sums.
  On the practical side, we engineer different algorithmic approaches for approximating Pareto sets on realistic instances. Our implementations enable a granular trade-off between approximation quality and running time/output size compared to the state of the art for exact algorithms established in (Funke, Hespe, Sanders, Storandt, Truschel; Algorithmica '25). Perhaps surprisingly, the (theoretical) connection to Bounded Monotone Min-Plus Convolution remains beneficial even for our implementations: in particular, we implement a simplified, yet still subquadratic version of an algorithm due to Chi, Duan, Xie and Zhang, which on some sufficiently large instances outperforms the competing quadratic-time approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper studies approximation of Pareto sums for 2D point sets (skyline of Minkowski sums). It shows additively approximate Pareto sets are fine-grained equivalent to Bounded Monotone Min-Plus Convolution, enabling a strongly subquadratic approximation algorithm using a recent n^{1.5}-time algorithm. It also reports practical algorithm engineering: a simplified subquadratic version of the same approach and experiments indicating competitive performance against exact, quadratic-time baselines on large instances.
AI.motivation: Bi-criteria optimization frequently uses Pareto sums (skylines of Minkowski sums). Exact computation has near-quadratic conditional lower bounds, motivating exploration of efficient approximations that may outperform exact algorithms in practice.
AI.method: Theory: define additively approximate Pareto sets; prove a fine-grained equivalence to Bounded Monotone Min-Plus Convolution; leverage the STOC'22 n^{1.5}-time algorithm for the convolution to obtain subquadratic approximation. Practice: implement and compare multiple approximation strategies; adapt a simplified, subquadratic variant of the Chi–Duan–Xie–Zhang approach; evaluate on realistic instances against state-of-the-art exact methods.
AI.result: Theoretical: obtains a strongly subquadratic approximation algorithm for Pareto sums, conditional on the convolution result; practically feasible thanks to the equivalence. Practical: implementations enable a tunable trade-off between approximation quality, running time, and output size; the simplified subquadratic variant sometimes outperforms quadratic-time baselines on large instances.
AI.conclusion: Approximating Pareto sums via the connection to Bounded Monotone Min-Plus Convolution is both theoretically sound and practically productive. The min-plus convolution perspective fuels subquadratic algorithms and beneficial implementations, suggesting promising directions for higher dimensions and richer objective structures.

title: Saranga: MilliWatt Ultrasound for Navigation in Visually Degraded Environments on Palm-Sized Aerial Robots
summary: Tiny palm-sized aerial robots possess exceptional agility and cost-effectiveness in navigating confined and cluttered environments. However, their limited payload capacity directly constrains the sensing suite on-board the robot, thereby limiting critical navigational tasks in Global Positioning System (GPS)-denied wild scenes. Common methods for obstacle avoidance use cameras and LIght Detection And Ranging (LIDAR), which become ineffective in visually degraded conditions such as low visibility, dust, fog or darkness. Other sensors, such as RAdio Detection And Ranging (RADAR), have high power consumption, making them unsuitable for tiny aerial robots. Inspired by bats, we propose Saranga, a low-power ultrasound-based perception stack that localizes obstacles using a dual sonar array. We present two key solutions to combat the low Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio of $-4.9$ decibels: physical noise reduction and a deep learning based denoising method. Firstly, we present a practical way to block propeller induced ultrasound noise on the weak echoes. The second solution is to train a neural network to utilize the \textcolor{black}{long horizon of ultrasound echoes} for finding signal patterns under high amounts of uncorrelated noise where classical methods were insufficient. We generalize to the real world by using a synthetic data generation pipeline and limited real noise data for training. We enable a palm-sized aerial robot to navigate in visually degraded conditions of dense fog, darkness, and snow in a cluttered environment with thin and transparent obstacles using only on-board sensing and computation. We provide extensive real world results to demonstrate the efficacy of our approach.

AI.tldr: Tiny palm-sized UAV uses dual ultrasound with noise mitigation and DL denoising to perform obstacle avoidance in GPS-denied, visually degraded environments using on-board sensing; synthetic data to train the model; demonstrated real-world viability.
AI.motivation: Accepted sensing payload constraints on tiny aerial robots and the failure of cameras/LiDAR in fog, dust, darkness. Ultrasound is low-power but noisy; aim to enable reliable onboard perception in GPS-denied cluttered scenes.
AI.method: Hardware: dual sonar array with procedures to block propeller-induced ultrasound noise. Software: deep learning denoising leveraging long-range ultrasound echoes; synthetic data generation pipeline with limited real noise data for training; on-board computation for inference.
AI.result: Achieves navigation in visually degraded conditions (fog, darkness, snow) with a low SNR of -4.9 dB; extensive real-world tests validate feasibility of the approach for cluttered environments using only onboard sensing.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of low-power ultrasound-based perception for tiny UAVs in challenging conditions; scalable with synthetic data; performance depends on noise characteristics and training data; future work could improve noise suppression and multi-modal integration.

title: ARC-AGI-3: A New Challenge for Frontier Agentic Intelligence
summary: We introduce ARC-AGI-3, an interactive benchmark for studying agentic intelligence through novel, abstract, turn-based environments in which agents must explore, infer goals, build internal models of environment dynamics, and plan effective action sequences without explicit instructions. Like its predecessors ARC-AGI-1 and 2, ARC-AGI-3 focuses entirely on evaluating fluid adaptive efficiency on novel tasks, while avoiding language and external knowledge. ARC-AGI-3 environments only leverage Core Knowledge priors and are difficulty-calibrated via extensive testing with human test-takers. Our testing shows humans can solve 100% of the environments, in contrast to frontier AI systems which, as of March 2026, score below 1%. In this paper, we present the benchmark design, its efficiency-based scoring framework grounded in human action baselines, and the methodology used to construct, validate, and calibrate the environments.

AI.tldr: ARC-AGI-3 presents an interactive, turn-based benchmark to test agentic intelligence via abstract tasks, emphasizing internal model-building and planning with Core Knowledge priors; humans solve all tasks while frontier AI lagging (<1% by 2026); methodology includes design, human-grounded efficiency scoring, and environment calibration.
AI.motivation: Fill the evaluation gap for agentic capabilities on novel tasks without relying on language or external knowledge, using human baselines to calibrate difficulty.
AI.method: Design of novel, abstract, turn-based environments; use Core Knowledge priors; extensive human testing to calibrate difficulty; efficiency-based scoring anchored to human performance; construction, validation, and calibration procedures described.
AI.result: Humans achieve 100% success across environments; frontier AI systems score below 1% as of March 2026; the paper provides the benchmark design, scoring framework, and validation methodology.
AI.conclusion: ARC-AGI-3 offers a rigorous, human-grounded benchmark for evaluating adaptive efficiency on novel tasks, potentially offering stricter evaluation than prior ARC variants.

title: DyMRL: Dynamic Multispace Representation Learning for Multimodal Event Forecasting in Knowledge Graph
comment: Accepted to The ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW '26). This version is published under a CC BY license
summary: Accurate representation of multimodal knowledge is crucial for event forecasting in real-world scenarios. However, existing studies have largely focused on static settings, overlooking the dynamic acquisition and fusion of multimodal knowledge. 1) At the knowledge acquisition level, how to learn time-sensitive information of different modalities, especially the dynamic structural modality. Existing dynamic learning methods are often limited to shallow structures across heterogeneous spaces or simple unispaces, making it difficult to capture deep relation-aware geometric features. 2) At the knowledge fusion level, how to learn evolving multimodal fusion features. Existing knowledge fusion methods based on static coattention struggle to capture the varying historical contributions of different modalities to future events. To this end, we propose DyMRL, a Dynamic Multispace Representation Learning approach to efficiently acquire and fuse multimodal temporal knowledge. 1) For the former issue, DyMRL integrates time-specific structural features from Euclidean, hyperbolic, and complex spaces into a relational message-passing framework to learn deep representations, reflecting human intelligences in associative thinking, high-order abstracting, and logical reasoning. Pretrained models endow DyMRL with time-sensitive visual and linguistic intelligences. 2) For the latter concern, DyMRL incorporates advanced dual fusion-evolution attention mechanisms that assign dynamic learning emphases equally to different modalities at different timestamps in a symmetric manner. To evaluate DyMRL's event forecasting performance through leveraging its learned multimodal temporal knowledge in history, we construct four multimodal temporal knowledge graph benchmarks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DyMRL outperforms state-of-the-art dynamic unimodal and static multimodal baseline methods.

AI.tldr: DyMRL provides a dynamic multispace representation learning framework for multimodal temporal knowledge graphs, integrating time-sensitive structural features from Euclidean, hyperbolic, and complex spaces with dual fusion-evolution attention to capture evolving modality contributions, validated on four benchmarks where it surpasses dynamic unimodal and static multimodal baselines.
AI.motivation: Accurate event forecasting requires learning time-sensitive, deep geometric representations across modalities; existing methods rely on static coattention or shallow dynamics and fail to capture deep relation-aware geometry in heterogeneous spaces.
AI.method: DyMRL combines (1) time-aware structural feature learning from Euclidean, hyperbolic, and complex spaces via relational message passing to obtain deep representations, aided by pretrained time-sensitive visual and linguistic models, and (2) dual fusion-evolution attention that symmetrically assigns dynamic emphasis to modalities at different timestamps. It further evaluates on four multimodal temporal knowledge graph benchmarks and compares with dynamic unimodal and static multimodal baselines.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show that DyMRL achieves superior event forecasting performance over state-of-the-art dynamic unimodal and static multimodal baselines across four benchmarks; extensive experiments and ablations corroborate the effectiveness of time-sensitive structural learning and dynamic fusion.
AI.conclusion: DyMRL effectively learns deep, time-sensitive multimodal representations and dynamic fusion features, enabling more accurate multimodal temporal reasoning and event forecasting.

title: MedOpenClaw: Auditable Medical Imaging Agents Reasoning over Uncurated Full Studies
summary: Currently, evaluating vision-language models (VLMs) in medical imaging tasks oversimplifies clinical reality by relying on pre-selected 2D images that demand significant manual labor to curate. This setup misses the core challenge of realworld diagnostics: a true clinical agent must actively navigate full 3D volumes across multiple sequences or modalities to gather evidence and ultimately support a final decision. To address this, we propose MEDOPENCLAW, an auditable runtime designed to let VLMs operate dynamically within standard medical tools or viewers (e.g., 3D Slicer). On top of this runtime, we introduce MEDFLOWBENCH, a full-study medical imaging benchmark covering multi-sequence brain MRI and lung CT/PET. It systematically evaluates medical agentic capabilities across viewer-only, tool-use, and open-method tracks. Initial results reveal a critical insight: while state-of-the-art LLMs/VLMs (e.g., Gemini 3.1 Pro and GPT-5.4) can successfully navigate the viewer to solve basic study-level tasks, their performance paradoxically degrades when given access to professional support tools due to a lack of precise spatial grounding. By bridging the gap between static-image perception and interactive clinical workflows, MEDOPENCLAW and MEDFLOWBENCH establish a reproducible foundation for developing auditable, full-study medical imaging agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces MEDOPENCLAW auditable runtime and MEDFLOWBENCH benchmark for evaluating vision-language models in full 3D medical imaging workflows; shows tool access can degrade performance due to spatial grounding gaps.
AI.motivation: Current VLM evaluation uses pre-selected 2D images; real clinical diagnostics require interactive 3D navigation across modalities; need auditable, full-study benchmarks and runtimes.
AI.method: Develop MEDOPENCLAW runtime integrated with standard medical viewers (e.g., 3D Slicer); build MEDFLOWBENCH multi-sequence brain MRI and lung CT/PET benchmark; evaluate across viewer-only, tool-use, and open-method tracks using state-of-the-art LLMs/VLMs (Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4).
AI.result: LLMs/VLMs can navigate viewers to solve basic study-level tasks but performance declines when tools are available due to imprecise spatial grounding, highlighting a gap between perception and interactive workflows.
AI.conclusion: MEDOPENCLAW and MEDFLOWBENCH provide a reproducible foundation for auditable, full-study medical imaging agents and bridge static perception with clinical workflows.

title: FODMP: Fast One-Step Diffusion of Movement Primitives Generation for Time-Dependent Robot Actions
summary: Diffusion models are increasingly used for robot learning, but current designs face a clear trade-off. Action-chunking diffusion policies like ManiCM are fast to run, yet they only predict short segments of motion. This makes them reactive, but unable to capture time-dependent motion primitives, such as following a spring-damper-like behavior with built-in dynamic profiles of acceleration and deceleration. Recently, Movement Primitive Diffusion (MPD) partially addresses this limitation by parameterizing full trajectories using Probabilistic Dynamic Movement Primitives (ProDMPs), thereby enabling the generation of temporally structured motions. Nevertheless, MPD integrates the motion decoder directly into a multi-step diffusion process, resulting in prohibitively high inference latency that limits its applicability in real-time control settings. We propose FODMP (Fast One-step Diffusion of Movement Primitives), a new framework that distills diffusion models into the ProDMPs trajectory parameter space and generates motion using a single-step decoder. FODMP retains the temporal structure of movement primitives while eliminating the inference bottleneck through single-step consistency distillation. This enables robots to execute time-dependent primitives at high inference speed, suitable for closed-loop vision-based control. On standard manipulation benchmarks (MetaWorld, ManiSkill), FODMP runs up to 10 times faster than MPD and 7 times faster than action-chunking diffusion policies, while matching or exceeding their success rates. Beyond speed, by generating fast acceleration-deceleration motion primitives, FODMP allows the robot to intercept and securely catch a fast-flying ball, whereas action-chunking diffusion policy and MPD respond too slowly for real-time interception.

AI.tldr: FODMP distills diffusion models into ProDMP trajectory parameters for single-step decoding, delivering fast, time-dependent movement primitives with comparable or better success rates than prior diffusion-based methods, enabling real-time control.
AI.motivation: Overcome the speed–temporal-structure trade-off in diffusion-based robot policies: action-chunking methods are fast but lack time-dependent dynamics; MPD retains temporal structure but is too slow for real-time control.
AI.method: Distill diffusion predictions into Probabilistic Dynamic Movement Primitives (ProDMPs) trajectory parameter space and use a single-step decoder via consistency distillation to preserve temporal structure while eliminating iterative inference.
AI.result: Significant speedups: FODMP up to 10× faster than MPD and 7× faster than action-chunking diffusion policies on MetaWorld/ManiSkill, with matching or superior success rates; enables fast acceleration profiles that allow interception of fast-moving objects.
AI.conclusion: FODMP resolves the latency bottleneck in diffusion-based movement generation by leveraging one-shot consistency distillation into movement primitives, enabling high-speed, temporally structured control suitable for closed-loop vision-based robotics.

title: When Is Collective Intelligence a Lottery? Multi-Agent Scaling Laws for Memetic Drift in LLMs
summary: Multi-agent systems powered by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in settings that shape consequential decisions, both directly and indirectly. Yet it remains unclear whether their outcomes reflect collective reasoning, systematic bias, or mere chance. Recent work has sharpened this question with naming games, showing that even when no individual agent favors any label a priori, populations rapidly break symmetry and reach consensus. Here, we reveal the mechanism by introducing a minimal model, Quantized Simplex Gossip (QSG), and trace the microscopic origin of this agreement to mutual in-context learning. In QSG, agents maintain internal belief states but learn from one another's sampled outputs, so one agent's arbitrary choice becomes the next agent's evidence and can compound toward agreement. By analogy with neutral evolution, we call this sampling-driven regime memetic drift. QSG predicts a crossover from a drift-dominated regime, where consensus is effectively a lottery, to a selection regime, where weak biases are amplified and shape the outcome. We derive scaling laws for drift-induced polarization as a function of population size, communication bandwidth, in-context adaptation rate, and agents' internal uncertainty, and we validate them in both QSG simulations and naming-game experiments with LLM populations. Together, these results provide a framework for studying the collective mechanisms of social representation formation in multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: A minimal model (Quantized Simplex Gossip) explains how consensus emerges in LLM-based multi-agent populations via memetic drift: agents learn from each other’s sampled outputs, causing arbitrary choices to propagate and lead to polarization. There is a drift-dominated regime (consensus like a lottery) and a selection regime (weak biases amplify). The authors derive scaling laws and validate them with simulations and naming-game experiments, offering a framework to study collective social representations in multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: To determine whether collective outcomes in LLM-driven multi-agent systems reflect genuine collective reasoning, systematic bias, or random chance. The paper targets the mechanism behind rapid symmetry breaking and consensus in agent populations, and aims to identify microscopic drivers and their dependence on system parameters.
AI.method: Introduce Quantized Simplex Gossip (QSG): agents hold internal belief states and update by learning from sampled outputs of others, so one agent’s arbitrary choice becomes evidence for the next. This sampling-driven process is termed memetic drift. The authors analyze drift vs selection regimes, derive scaling laws for polarization as a function of population size, communication bandwidth, in-context adaptation rate, and internal uncertainty, and validate predictions through QSG simulations and naming-game experiments with LLM populations.
AI.result: Prediction of a crossover from drift-dominated consensus (a lottery) to a selection-driven regime where biases are amplified. Established scaling laws linking polarization to population size, bandwidth, adaptation rate, and uncertainty. Validation of these laws in both simulated QSG models and real naming-game experiments with LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Provides a general framework for studying how social representations form in multi-agent systems, elucidating when consensus arises from stochastic drift versus bias amplification, and highlighting the role of mutual in-context learning as the microscopic mechanism behind collective behavior.

title: How unconstrained machine-learning models learn physical symmetries
summary: The requirement of generating predictions that exactly fulfill the fundamental symmetry of the corresponding physical quantities has profoundly shaped the development of machine-learning models for physical simulations. In many cases, models are built using constrained mathematical forms that ensure that symmetries are enforced exactly. However, unconstrained models that do not obey rotational symmetries are often found to have competitive performance, and to be able to \emph{learn} to a high level of accuracy an approximate equivariant behavior with a simple data augmentation strategy. In this paper, we introduce rigorous metrics to measure the symmetry content of the learned representations in such models, and assess the accuracy by which the outputs fulfill the equivariant condition. We apply these metrics to two unconstrained, transformer-based models operating on decorated point clouds (a graph neural network for atomistic simulations and a PointNet-style architecture for particle physics) to investigate how symmetry information is processed across architectural layers and is learned during training. Based on these insights, we establish a rigorous framework for diagnosing spectral failure modes in ML models. Enabled by this analysis, we demonstrate that one can achieve superior stability and accuracy by strategically injecting the minimum required inductive biases, preserving the high expressivity and scalability of unconstrained architectures while guaranteeing physical fidelity.

AI.tldr: This work develops metrics to quantify symmetry content in unconstrained ML models for physical simulations, applies them to transformer-based architectures on decorated point clouds, diagnoses spectral symmetry-related failure modes, and shows that introducing minimal inductive biases yields greater stability and fidelity without sacrificing expressivity.
AI.motivation: Constrained models enforce exact physical symmetries, but unconstrained models often perform well with data augmentation. There is a need to quantify how well such models learn and preserve symmetry and to diagnose spectral failure modes to ensure physical fidelity.
AI.method: Introduce rigorous metrics for symmetry content of learned representations and evaluation of equivariance accuracy. Apply these metrics to two unconstrained transformer-based models (a graph neural network for atomistic simulations and a PointNet-like model for particle physics) to track symmetry processing across layers and during training. Develop a framework to diagnose spectral failure modes and test inductive-bias interventions.
AI.result: Metrics reveal how symmetry is encoded across layers and during training. The spectral-diagnostic framework identifies failure modes where symmetry is inadequately captured. Strategically injecting the minimal necessary inductive biases improves stability and accuracy while preserving the expressive power of unconstrained architectures and ensuring physical fidelity.
AI.conclusion: A rigorous symmetry-analysis framework for unconstrained ML in physics enables precise diagnosis of spectral failures and demonstrates that minimal symmetry-enforcing biases can enhance stability and accuracy without compromising model expressivity.

title: From Weights to Concepts: Data-Free Interpretability of CLIP via Singular Vector Decomposition
summary: As vision-language models are deployed at scale, understanding their internal mechanisms becomes increasingly critical. Existing interpretability methods predominantly rely on activations, making them dataset-dependent, vulnerable to data bias, and often restricted to coarse head-level explanations. We introduce SITH (Semantic Inspection of Transformer Heads), a fully data-free, training-free framework that directly analyzes CLIP's vision transformer in weight space. For each attention head, we decompose its value-output matrix into singular vectors and interpret each one via COMP (Coherent Orthogonal Matching Pursuit), a new algorithm that explains them as sparse, semantically coherent combinations of human-interpretable concepts. We show that SITH yields coherent, faithful intra-head explanations, validated through reconstruction fidelity and interpretability experiments. This allows us to use SITH for precise, interpretable weight-space model edits that amplify or suppress specific concepts, improving downstream performance without retraining. Furthermore, we use SITH to study model adaptation, showing how fine-tuning primarily reweights a stable semantic basis rather than learning entirely new features.

AI.tldr: SITH provides data-free, training-free weight-space interpretability for transformer heads: decomposing attention value-output matrices into singular vectors and expressing them as sparse, semantically coherent concepts via COMP; enables precise head-level explanations and direct weight edits to adjust concepts, and reveals that fine-tuning mainly reweights existing semantic basis rather than creating new features.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of input-based interpretability methods (dataset dependence, bias, coarse explanations) by analyzing model internals in weight space; enable faithful, fine-grained explanations and reversible edits without retraining; understand model adaptation.
AI.method: For each attention head in CLIP's vision transformer, decompose its value-output matrix into singular vectors. Interpret each singular vector using COMP (Coherent Orthogonal Matching Pursuit), expressing it as a sparse, semantically coherent combination of human-interpretable concepts. Validate explanations via reconstruction fidelity and interpretability experiments. Use weight-space edits to amplify or suppress specific concepts. Analyze adaptation by comparing fine-tuning behavior to reweighting of a stable semantic basis.
AI.result: SITH yields coherent, faithful intra-head explanations with high reconstruction fidelity and interpretable mappings. Weight-space edits can improve downstream performance without retraining. Fine-tuning primarily reweights a stable semantic basis rather than learning entirely new features, suggesting a shift in how concepts are utilized rather than new concept creation.
AI.conclusion: SITH demonstrates that data-free, training-free, weight-space analysis can produce precise, interpretable explanations and effective concept-level edits for transformer heads, and reveals that model adaptation tends to reweight existing semantic components rather than introduce new ones.

title: A Nonvolatile Switchable-polarity EPM Valve
summary: Scalable control of pneumatic and fluidic networks remains fundamentally constrained by architectures that require continuous power input, dense external control hardware, and fixed routing topologies. Current valve arrays rely on such continuous actuation and mechanically fixed routing, imposing substantial thermal and architectural overhead. Here, we introduce the Switchable-polarity ElectroPermanent Magnet (S-EPM), a fundamentally new bistable magnetic architecture that deterministically reverses its external magnetic polarity through transient electrical excitation. By reconfiguring internal flux pathways within a composite magnet assembly, the S-EPM establishes two stable, opposing magnetic configurations without requiring sustained power. We integrate this architecture into a compact pinch-valve to robustly control pneumatic and liquid media. This state-encoded magnetic control enables logic-embedded fluidic networks, including decoders, hierarchical distribution modules, and a nonvolatile six-port routing array. These systems provide address-based routing and programmable compositional control, offering features like individual port isolation that are impossible with standard mechanically coupled rotary valves. By embedding functionality in persistent magnetic states rather than continuous power or static plumbing, this work establishes a scalable foundation for digital fluidics and autonomous laboratory platforms.

AI.tldr: Bistable switchable-polarity electropermanent magnet (S-EPM) reverses external magnetic polarity via transient electrical excitation, enabling nonvolatile, programmable control of fluidic networks and addressable routing without continuous power.
AI.motivation: Current pneumatic/fluidic control relies on continuously powered valve actuation and fixed routing, incurring high energy, thermal, and hardware overhead. A nonvolatile, reconfigurable magnetic architecture could enable scalable, autonomous fluidic systems.
AI.method: Develop S-EPM with reconfigurable internal flux paths to produce two stable magnetic states. Integrate into a compact pinch-valve to control pneumatic/liquid media. Build logic-embedded fluidic networks including decoders, hierarchical distribution modules, and a nonvolatile six-port routing array for address-based routing and individual port isolation.
AI.result: Achieves two stable magnetic configurations without sustained power, enables compact pinch-valve control of fluids, and realizes nonvolatile routing with programmable compositional control and isolation, supporting scalable digital fluidics.
AI.conclusion: Embedding persistent magnetic states rather than continuous actuation or fixed plumbing provides a scalable foundation for digital fluidics and autonomous laboratory platforms.

title: AutoSAM: an Agentic Framework for Automating Input File Generation for the SAM Code with Multi-Modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: In the design and safety analysis of advanced reactor systems, constructing input files for system-level thermal-hydraulics codes such as the System Analysis Module (SAM) remains a labor-intensive task. Analysts must extract and reconcile design data from heterogeneous engineering documents and manually translate it into solver-specific syntax. In this paper, we present AutoSAM, an agentic framework that automates SAM input file generation. The framework combines a large language model agent with retrieval-augmented generation over the solver's user guide and theory manual, together with specialized tools for analyzing PDFs, images, spreadsheets, and text files. AutoSAM ingests unstructured engineering documents, including system diagrams, design reports, and data tables, extracts simulation-relevant parameters into a human-auditable intermediate representation, and synthesizes validated, solver-compatible input decks. Its multimodal retrieval pipeline integrates scientific text extraction, vision-based figure interpretation, semantic embedding, and query answering. We evaluate AutoSAM on four case studies of increasing complexity: a single-pipe steady-state model, a solid-fuel channel with temperature reactivity feedback, the Advanced Burner Test Reactor core, and the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment primary loop. Across all cases, the agent produces runnable SAM models consistent with expected thermal-hydraulic behavior while explicitly identifying missing data and labeling assumed values. The framework achieves 100% utilization of structured inputs, about 88% extraction from PDF text, and 100% completeness in vision-based geometric extraction. These results demonstrate a practical path toward prompt-driven reactor modeling, in which analysts provide system descriptions and supporting documentation while the agent translates them into transparent, and executable, SAM simulations.

AI.tldr: AutoSAM automates System Analysis Module input generation by combining an LLM agent with retrieval-augmented generation and multimodal tools to extract and translate unstructured engineering documents into runnable, auditable SAM input decks.
AI.motivation: Manual construction of SAM input files from heterogeneous design documents is labor-intensive and error-prone; automating extraction, reconciliation, and solver-syntax translation can improve efficiency, traceability, and reliability.
AI.method: An agentic framework that integrates an LLM agent with retrieval-augmented generation over the solver’s user guide and theory manual, plus specialized tools for PDFs, images, spreadsheets, and text files. It ingests unstructured documents (diagrams, reports, data tables), extracts simulation-relevant parameters into a human-auditable intermediate representation, and synthesizes solver-compatible input decks. A multimodal retrieval pipeline combines text extraction, vision-based figure interpretation, semantic embedding, and query answering to support parameter extraction and validation.
AI.result: Evaluated on four case studies of increasing complexity (single-pipe steady-state model, solid-fuel channel with temperature reactivity feedback, Advanced Burner Test Reactor core, Molten Salt Reactor Experiment primary loop). The agent produced runnable SAM models aligned with expected thermal-hydraulic behavior, identified missing data, and labeled assumed values. Quantitatively, 100% utilization of structured inputs, about 88% extraction from PDF text, and 100% completeness in vision-based geometric extraction.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a practical path toward prompt-driven reactor modeling, where analysts provide system descriptions and supporting documentation and the agent translates them into transparent, executable SAM simulations.

title: Experiential Reflective Learning for Self-Improving LLM Agents
comment: Published as a conference paper at the ICLR 2026 MemAgents Workshop
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled the development of autonomous agents capable of complex reasoning and multi-step problem solving. However, these agents struggle to adapt to specialized environments and do not leverage past interactions, approaching each new task from scratch regardless of their accumulated experience. We introduce Experiential Reflective Learning (ERL), a simple self-improvement framework that enables rapid environment adaptation through experiential learning. ERL reflects on task trajectories and outcomes to generate heuristics, capturing actionable lessons that transfer across tasks. At test time, relevant heuristics are retrieved based on the current task and injected into the agent's context to guide execution. On the Gaia2 benchmark, ERL improves success rate by 7.8% over a ReAct baseline, with large gains in task completion reliability, and outperforms prior experiential learning methods. Through systematic ablations, we find that selective retrieval is essential and that heuristics provide more transferable abstractions than few-shot trajectory prompting. These results demonstrate that reflecting on single-attempt experiences to extract transferable heuristics enables effective agent self-improvement.

AI.tldr: ERL enables rapid adaptation by extracting transferable heuristics from single-task trajectories; at test time, heuristics are selectively retrieved and injected to guide the agent, yielding 7.8% Gaia2 improvement over ReAct and outperforming prior experiential methods.
AI.motivation: Agents struggle to generalize across specialized environments and forget past interactions; there is a need to transfer lessons learned from prior experiences to new tasks.
AI.method: Reflect on task trajectories to extract actionable heuristics; build a bank of heuristics; at test time, retrieve relevant heuristics based on the current task and inject them into the agent's context to guide execution.
AI.result: Gaia2 benchmark: +7.8% success rate over a ReAct baseline; improved task completion reliability; outperforms prior experiential learning methods; ablations show selective retrieval is essential and heuristics provide more transferable abstractions than few-shot trajectory prompting.
AI.conclusion: Reflecting on single experiences to derive transferable heuristics enables effective agent self-improvement and rapid environment adaptation.

title: ReDiPrune: Relevance-Diversity Pre-Projection Token Pruning for Efficient Multimodal LLMs
summary: Recent multimodal large language models are computationally expensive because Transformers must process a large number of visual tokens. We present \textbf{ReDiPrune}, a training-free token pruning method applied before the vision-language projector, where visual features remain rich and discriminative. Unlike post-projection pruning methods that operate on compressed representations, ReDiPrune selects informative tokens directly from vision encoder outputs, preserving fine-grained spatial and semantic cues. Each token is scored by a lightweight rule that jointly consider text-conditioned relevance and max-min diversity, ensuring the selected tokens are both query-relevant and non-redundant. ReDiPrune is fully plug-and-play, requiring no retraining or architectural modifications, and can be seamlessly inserted between the encoder and projector. Across four video and five image benchmarks, it consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off. For example, on EgoSchema with LLaVA-NeXT-Video-7B, retaining only 15\% of visual tokens yields a +2.0\% absolute accuracy gain while reducing computation by more than $6\times$ in TFLOPs. Code is available at https://github.com/UA-CVML/ReDiPrune.

AI.tldr: Training-free token pruning (ReDiPrune) selects informative visual tokens from vision encoder outputs before the vision-language projector, optimizing text-conditioned relevance and diversity; achieves better accuracy-efficiency without retraining; demonstrated on multiple video/image benchmarks with notable gains (e.g., +2.0% accuracy and >6x TFLOPs reduction on EgoSchema with LLaVA-NeXT-Video-7B).
AI.motivation: Reduce computation in multimodal LLMs by pruning tokens before the feature projection stage, preserving discriminative cues and enabling plug-and-play integration, addressing the inefficiency of processing dense visual token streams.
AI.method: Score each visual token with a lightweight rule that jointly considers text-conditioned relevance and max-min diversity; select a subset of tokens before the vision-language projector; no retraining or architectural changes required; plug-and-play insertion between encoder and projector; applicable to both image and video encoders.
AI.result: Across four video and five image benchmarks, ReDiPrune consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off; e.g., EgoSchema with LLaVA-NeXT-Video-7B: retaining 15% of tokens yields +2.0% absolute accuracy gain while reducing TFLOPs by >6x; code released.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, plug-and-play token pruning method that maintains fine-grained spatial/semantic information while cutting computation; shows robust gains across diverse benchmarks; suitable for deployment in real-world multimodal LLM systems.

title: Characterization of Constraints in Flexible Unknown Environments
summary: This paper presents an online path planning algorithm for safe autonomous manipulation of a flexibly constrained object in an unknown environment. Methods for real time identification and characterization of perceived flexible constraints and global stiffness are presented. Used in tandem, these methods allow a robot to simultaneously explore, characterize, and manipulate an elastic system safely. Navigation without a-priori knowledge of the system is achieved using constraint exploration based on local force and position information. The perceived constraint stiffness is considered at multiple poses along an object's (system) trajectory. Using stiffness eigenvector information, global stiffness behavior is characterized and identified using an atlas of simple mechanical constraints, such as hinges and planar constraints. Validation of these algorithms is carried out by simulation and experimentally. The ability to recognize several common simple mechanical constraints (such as a flexible hinge) in real time, and to subsequently identify relevant screw parameters is demonstrated. These results suggest the feasibility of simultaneous global constrain/stiffness exploration and safe manipulation of flexibly constrained objects. We believe that this approach will eventually enable safe cooperative manipulation in applications such as organ retraction and manipulation during surgery

AI.tldr: Online path planning for safe manipulation of flexibly constrained objects in unknown environments using real-time constraint exploration and stiffness analysis.
AI.motivation: Enable safe autonomous manipulation of elastic systems with unknown constraints, crucial for real-time exploration and manipulation in unstructured and potentially surgical environments.
AI.method: Real-time constraint exploration using local force/position data; multi-pose stiffness estimation; characterization via stiffness eigenvectors; atlas of simple constraints (hinges, planar) to identify global stiffness behavior; simultaneous exploration and manipulation; validation via simulation and experiments; extraction of screw parameters.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time recognition of common simple constraints (e.g., flexible hinges) and identification of relevant screw parameters; supports feasibility of simultaneous global constraint/stiffness exploration and safe manipulation; validated in both simulation and experiments.
AI.conclusion: The approach is feasible and promising for safe cooperative manipulation of flexibly constrained objects, with potential applications in surgery and organ retraction.

title: Trust as Monitoring: Evolutionary Dynamics of User Trust and AI Developer Behaviour
summary: AI safety is an increasingly urgent concern as the capabilities and adoption of AI systems grow. Existing evolutionary models of AI governance have primarily examined incentives for safe development and effective regulation, typically representing users' trust as a one-shot adoption choice rather than as a dynamic, evolving process shaped by repeated interactions. We instead model trust as reduced monitoring in a repeated, asymmetric interaction between users and AI developers, where checking AI behaviour is costly. Using evolutionary game theory, we study how user trust strategies and developer choices between safe (compliant) and unsafe (non-compliant) AI co-evolve under different levels of monitoring cost and institutional regimes. We complement the infinite-population replicator analysis with stochastic finite-population dynamics and reinforcement learning (Q-learning) simulations. Across these approaches, we find three robust long-run regimes: no adoption with unsafe development, unsafe but widely adopted systems, and safe systems that are widely adopted. Only the last is desirable, and it arises when penalties for unsafe behaviour exceed the extra cost of safety and users can still afford to monitor at least occasionally. Our results formally support governance proposals that emphasise transparency, low-cost monitoring, and meaningful sanctions, and they show that neither regulation alone nor blind user trust is sufficient to prevent evolutionary drift towards unsafe or low-adoption outcomes.

AI.tldr: Trust in AI governance is dynamic and shaped by repeated user–developer interactions, not a one-shot choice. With costly monitoring, three long-run regimes emerge: no adoption with unsafe AI, unsafe but widely adopted AI, and safe, widely adopted AI; only the latter is desirable and arises when penalties for unsafe behavior exceed extra safety costs and monitoring is affordable.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in evolutionary models of AI governance by treating trust as a dynamic process influenced by repeated monitoring and sanctions, rather than a one-shot adoption decision.
AI.method: Combine infinite-population replicator dynamics, stochastic finite-population dynamics, and Q-learning simulations to study co-evolution of user trust strategies and developer safety choices (safe vs. unsafe) under varying monitoring costs and institutional regimes.
AI.result: Across approaches, three robust long-run regimes emerge: (1) no adoption with unsafe development, (2) unsafe systems that are widely adopted, (3) safe systems that are widely adopted. The desirable regime is (3), arising when unsafe penalties exceed safety costs and monitoring remains affordable. The analysis supports governance strategies that enable transparency, low-cost monitoring, and meaningful sanctions.
AI.conclusion: Regulation or blinded trust alone cannot prevent drift toward unsafe or low-adoption outcomes; effective governance requires credible sanctions and affordable monitoring to sustain safe, widely adopted AI.

title: Learning Mesh-Free Discrete Differential Operators with Self-Supervised Graph Neural Networks
summary: Mesh-free numerical methods provide flexible discretisations for complex geometries; however, classical meshless discrete differential operators typically trade low computational cost for limited accuracy or high accuracy for substantial per-stencil computation. We introduce a parametrised framework for learning mesh-free discrete differential operators using a graph neural network trained via polynomial moment constraints derived from truncated Taylor expansions. The model maps local stencils relative positions directly to discrete operator weights. The current work demonstrates that neural networks can learn classical polynomial consistency while retaining robustness to irregular neighbourhood geometry. The learned operators depend only on local geometry, are resolution-agnostic, and can be reused across particle configurations and governing equations. We evaluate the framework using standard numerical analysis diagnostics, showing improved accuracy over Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics, and a favourable accuracy-cost trade-off relative to a representative high-order consistent mesh-free method in the moderate-accuracy regime. Applicability is demonstrated by solving the weakly compressible Navier-Stokes equations using the learned operators.

AI.tldr: Parametrised graph neural network learns mesh-free discrete differential operators from local geometry using polynomial moment constraints; achieves polynomial consistency and robustness to irregular neighborhoods; shows improved accuracy over SPH and favorable cost-accuracy relative to a high-order mesh-free method; demonstrated on weakly compressible Navier–Stokes.
AI.motivation: Mesh-free discretisations trade accuracy against computational cost; classical operators are either inexpensive with limited accuracy or highly accurate with heavy per-stencil computation. A learned operator framework aims to deliver polynomial consistency, locality, and reuse across problems while being robust to irregular particle configurations.
AI.method: Train a graph neural network to map local stencil relative positions to discrete operator weights. Enforce polynomial consistency via polynomial moment constraints derived from truncated Taylor expansions. Operators depend only on local geometry, are resolution-agnostic, and reusable across particle configurations and governing equations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations with standard numerical analysis diagnostics show improved accuracy relative to Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) and a favorable accuracy-cost trade-off compared with a representative high-order consistent mesh-free method in moderate-accuracy regimes. Applicability demonstrated by solving the weakly compressible Navier–Stokes equations using the learned operators.
AI.conclusion: Learned, geometry-driven mesh-free operators can achieve polynomial consistency and robustness while maintaining efficient costs, enabling flexible and reusable discretisations for complex geometries and a range of PDEs.

title: KitchenTwin: Semantically and Geometrically Grounded 3D Kitchen Digital Twins
summary: Embodied AI training and evaluation require object-centric digital twin environments with accurate metric geometry and semantic grounding. Recent transformer-based feedforward reconstruction methods can efficiently predict global point clouds from sparse monocular videos, yet these geometries suffer from inherent scale ambiguity and inconsistent coordinate conventions. This mismatch prevents the reliable fusion of these dimensionless point cloud predictions with locally reconstructed object meshes. We propose a novel scale-aware 3D fusion framework that registers visually grounded object meshes with transformer-predicted global point clouds to construct metrically consistent digital twins. Our method introduces a Vision-Language Model (VLM)-guided geometric anchor mechanism that resolves this fundamental coordinate mismatch by recovering an accurate real-world metric scale. To fuse these networks, we propose a geometry-aware registration pipeline that explicitly enforces physical plausibility through gravity-aligned vertical estimation, Manhattan-world structural constraints, and collision-free local refinement. Experiments on real indoor kitchen environments demonstrate improved cross-network object alignment and geometric consistency for downstream tasks, including multi-primitive fitting and metric measurement. We additionally introduce an open-source indoor digital twin dataset with metrically scaled scenes and semantically grounded and registered object-centric mesh annotations.

AI.tldr: Proposes a scale-aware 3D fusion framework that aligns transformer-predicted global point clouds with object-centric meshes to create metrically consistent digital twins, using a VLM-guided scale anchor and physics-based registration (gravity, Manhattan constraints, collision-free refinement).
AI.motivation: Overcomes scale ambiguity and inconsistent coordinate conventions between monocular transformer reconstructions (dimensionless, coordinate-mismatched) and locally reconstructed meshes to enable metrically accurate, object-centric digital twins in indoor environments.
AI.method: 1) VLM-guided geometric anchor to recover real-world metric scale and align predictions with ground truth metrics. 2) A geometry-aware registration pipeline enforcing physical plausibility via gravity-aligned vertical estimation, Manhattan-world structural constraints, and collision-free local refinement. 3) Fusion of visually grounded object meshes with transformer-predicted global point clouds to produce metrically consistent digital twins. 4) Open-source indoor digital twin dataset with metrically scaled scenes and semantically grounded, registered object-centric mesh annotations.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved cross-network object alignment and geometric consistency for downstream tasks such as multi-primitive fitting and metric measurement in real indoor kitchen environments.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables metrically consistent digital twins by bridging dimensionless transformer reconstructions with metrically scaled meshes through VLM-based scale anchoring and physics-guided registration; accompanied by a public dataset for benchmarking.

title: Integrated Multi-Drone Task Allocation, Sequencing, and Optimal Trajectory Generation in Obstacle-Rich 3D Environments
comment: Resubmission following accepted appeal (MOD-78958). Resubmitting to cs.RO with cross-lists cs.MA and cs.AI as advised by arXiv Support
summary: Coordinating teams of aerial robots in cluttered three-dimensional (3D) environments requires a principled integration of discrete mission planning-deciding which robot serves which goals and in what order -- with continuous-time trajectory synthesis that enforces collision avoidance and dynamic feasibility. This paper introduces IMD-TAPP (Integrated Multi-Drone Task Allocation and Path Planning), an end-to-end framework that jointly addresses multi-goal allocation, tour sequencing, and safe trajectory generation for quadrotor teams operating in obstacle-rich spaces. IMD--TAPP first discretizes the workspace into a 3D navigation graph and computes obstacle-aware robot-to-goal and goal-to-goal travel costs via graph-search-based pathfinding. These costs are then embedded within an Injected Particle Swarm Optimization (IPSO) scheme, guided by multiple linear assignment, to efficiently explore coupled assignment/ordering alternatives and to minimize mission makespan. Finally, the resulting waypoint tours are transformed into time-parameterized minimum-snap trajectories through a generation-and-optimization routine equipped with iterative validation of obstacle clearance and inter-robot separation, triggering re-planning when safety margins are violated. Extensive MATLAB simulations across cluttered 3D scenarios demonstrate that IMD--TAPP consistently produces dynamically feasible, collision-free trajectories while achieving competitive completion times. In a representative case study with two drones serving multiple goals, the proposed approach attains a minimum mission time of 136~s while maintaining the required safety constraints throughout execution.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end integrated framework (IMD-TAPP) for multi-drone task allocation, sequencing, and safe trajectory generation in cluttered 3D spaces, combining a 3D navigation graph, graph-search costs, IPSO with linear assignment, and time-parameterized minimum-snap trajectories with re-planning.
AI.motivation: To bridge discrete mission planning (which drone gets which goals and in what order) with continuous trajectory synthesis under collision avoidance and dynamic feasibility in obstacle-rich 3D environments.
AI.method: 1) Discretize workspace into a 3D navigation graph. 2) Compute obstacle-aware robot-to-goal and goal-to-goal costs using graph-search-based pathfinding. 3) Embed these costs in an Injected Particle Swarm Optimization scheme guided by multiple linear assignment to explore coupled allocation/ordering. 4) Convert resulting waypoint tours into time-parameterized minimum-snap trajectories via a generation-and-optimization routine with iterative obstacle clearance and inter-robot separation validation. 5) Trigger re-planning if safety margins are violated.
AI.result: Extensive MATLAB simulations show dynamically feasible, collision-free trajectories with competitive completion times across cluttered 3D scenarios. In a two-drone case, the method achieves a minimum mission time of 136 s while maintaining safety constraints.
AI.conclusion: IMD-TAPP demonstrates that tightly integrated discrete task allocation and continuous trajectory synthesis can yield safe, efficient multi-drone operations in obstacle-rich 3D environments, with re-planning to enforce safety margins enhancing robustness.

title: Formal Semantics for Agentic Tool Protocols: A Process Calculus Approach
summary: The emergence of large language model agents capable of invoking external tools has created urgent need for formal verification of agent protocols. Two paradigms dominate this space: Schema-Guided Dialogue (SGD), a research framework for zero-shot API generalization, and the Model Context Protocol (MCP), an industry standard for agent-tool integration. While both enable dynamic service discovery through schema descriptions, their formal relationship remains unexplored. Building on prior work establishing the conceptual convergence of these paradigms, we present the first process calculus formalization of SGD and MCP, proving they are structurally bisimilar under a well-defined mapping Phi. However, we demonstrate that the reverse mapping Phi^{-1} is partial and lossy, revealing critical gaps in MCP's expressivity. Through bidirectional analysis, we identify five principles -- semantic completeness, explicit action boundaries, failure mode documentation, progressive disclosure compatibility, and inter-tool relationship declaration -- as necessary and sufficient conditions for full behavioral equivalence. We formalize these principles as type-system extensions MCP+, proving MCP+ is isomorphic to SGD. Our work provides the first formal foundation for verified agent systems and establishes schema quality as a provable safety property.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes SGD and MCP, showing structural bisimilarity via a mapping Phi, but Phi^{-1} is partial (law). It identifies five necessary and sufficient conditions (semantic completeness, explicit action boundaries, failure mode documentation, progressive disclosure compatibility, inter-tool relationship declaration) for full behavioral equivalence, and introduces MCP+ with a type-system that achieves isomorphism to SGD, establishing a formal basis for verified agent systems and schema quality as a safety property.
AI.motivation: To bridge two dominant paradigms for agent-tool integration (SGD and MCP) by providing a formal, process-calculus-based correspondence, enabling verified, safe interoperability and highlighting expressivity gaps in MCP.
AI.method: Develop a process calculus formalization capturing SGD and MCP semantics, define a mapping Phi between the two formalisms, prove structural bisimilarity under Phi, analyze reverse mapping Phi^{-1} for completeness, derive five principles as necessary conditions for equivalence, and encode these as MCP+ type-system extensions to achieve isomorphism with SGD.
AI.result: Bidirectional analysis demonstrates that SGD and MCP are structurally bisimilar under Phi, while Phi^{-1} is partial and lossy, revealing expressivity gaps in MCP. Five principles are identified and formalized into MCP+ extensions that restore isomorphism with SGD, providing a formal safety foundation and schema-quality guarantee.
AI.conclusion: Formal verification of agent protocols is feasible via a calculus-based correspondence between SGD and MCP; MCP+ offers a complete, isomorphic target to SGD, enabling provable safety properties and setting a baseline for schema quality in agent systems.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network Digital Twin for Dynamic Tray-Wise Modeling of Distillation Columns under Transient Operating Conditions
summary: Digital twin technology, when combined with physics-informed machine learning with simulation results of Aspen, offers transformative capabilities for industrial process monitoring, control, and optimization. In this work, the proposed model presents a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) digital twin framework for the dynamic, tray-wise modeling of binary distillation columns operating under transient conditions. The architecture of the proposed model embeds fundamental thermodynamic constraints, including vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) described by modified Raoult's law, tray-level mass and energy balances, and the McCabe-Thiele graphical methodology directly into the neural network loss function via physics residual terms. The model is trained and evaluated on a high-fidelity synthetic dataset of 961 timestamped measurements spanning 8 hours of transient operation, generated in Aspen HYSYS for a binary HX/TX distillation system comprising 16 sensor streams. An adaptive loss-weighting scheme balances the data fidelity and physics consistency objectives during training. Compared to five data-driven baselines (LSTM, vanilla MLP, GRU, Transformer, DeepONet), the proposed PINN achieves an RMSE of 0.00143 for HX mole fraction prediction (R^2 = 0.9887), representing a 44.6% reduction over the best data-only baseline, while strictly satisfying thermodynamic constraints. Tray-wise temperature and composition profiles predicted under transient perturbations demonstrate that the digital twin accurately captures column dynamics including feed tray responses, reflux ratio variations, and pressure transients. These results establish the proposed PINN digital twin as a robust foundation for real-time soft sensing, model-predictive control, and anomaly detection in industrial distillation processes.

AI.tldr: PINN-based digital twin for binary distillation, integrating thermodynamic constraints (VLE with modified Raoult's law, tray-level balances, McCabe–Thiele) into loss; trained on Aspen-simulated data; outperforms data-only baselines with RMSE 0.00143 (R^2=0.9887); supports real-time soft sensing, MPC, and anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Develop accurate, physically consistent, real-time models for transient distillation processes to improve monitoring, control, and fault detection beyond purely data-driven approaches.
AI.method: Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) that enforces thermodynamic constraints via physics residuals in the loss: VLE via modified Raoult's law, tray-level mass and energy balances, and McCabe–Thiele framework embedded as differentiable terms. Adaptive loss weighting between data fidelity and physics consistency. Trained on a synthetic 961-timestamp dataset (8 hours) from Aspen HYSYS for a 16-sensor binary HX/TX distillation column. Compared against five baselines (LSTM, MLP, GRU, Transformer, DeepONet).
AI.result: Achieves RMSE of 0.00143 for HX mole fraction (R^2 = 0.9887), a 44.6% reduction over the best data-only baseline. Accurately captures tray-wise temperature and composition dynamics under transient perturbations (feed changes, reflux, pressure transients). Demonstrates potential for real-time soft sensing, model-predictive control, and anomaly detection in industrial distillation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PINN digital twin offers a thermodynamically consistent, highly accurate framework for transient distillation modeling, enabling robust real-time monitoring and control. While validated on synthetic Aspen data, the approach shows promise for real plants and can be extended to multi-component systems and uncertainty-aware control. Future work should address real-world data integration, scalability, and uncertainty quantification.

title: UniICL: Systematizing Unified Multimodal In-context Learning through a Capability-Oriented Taxonomy
summary: In-context Learning enables training-free adaptation via demonstrations but remains highly sensitive to example selection and formatting. In unified multimodal models spanning understanding and generation, this sensitivity is exacerbated by cross-modal interference and varying cognitive demands. Consequently, In-context Learning efficacy is often non-monotonic and highly task-dependent. To diagnose these behaviors, we introduce a six-level capability-oriented taxonomy that categorizes the functional role of demonstrations from basic perception to high-order discernment. Guided by this cognitive framework, we construct UniICL-760K, a large-scale corpus featuring curated 8-shot In-context Learning episodes across 15 subtasks, alongside UniICL-Bench for rigorous, controlled evaluation. As an architectural intervention to stabilize few-shot adaptation, we propose the Context-Adaptive Prototype Modulator, a lightweight, plug-and-play module. Evaluations on UniICL-Bench show that our approach yields highly competitive unified results, outperforming larger-parameter multimodal large language model baselines on most understanding In-context Learning tasks. Data and code will be available soon at https://github.com/xuyicheng-zju/UniICL.

AI.tldr: A taxonomy-driven framework for diagnosing and stabilizing in-context learning (ICL) in unified multimodal models, introducing UniICL-760K and UniICL-Bench, plus a Context-Adaptive Prototype Modulator to improve 8-shot ICL across 15 subtasks; demonstrates competitive results against larger LMM baselines; code/data forthcoming.
AI.motivation: ICL in unified multimodal models is highly sensitive to example selection/formatting and suffers from cross-modal interference and varying cognitive demands. A structured taxonomy and standardized benchmarks are needed to diagnose, stabilize, and reliably evaluate ICL across tasks.
AI.method: Develop a six-level capability-oriented taxonomy of demonstration roles; construct UniICL-760K (8-shot episodes across 15 subtasks); build UniICL-Bench for controlled evaluation; propose a Context-Adaptive Prototype Modulator as a lightweight plug-and-play architectural intervention; conduct evaluations comparing to larger LMM baselines.
AI.result: The Context-Adaptive Prototype Modulator yields competitive performance on UniICL-Bench, surpassing larger-parameter multimodal LLM baselines on most understanding ICL tasks; data and code to be released.
AI.conclusion: A cognitive-framework-inspired taxonomy and standardized benchmark enable diagnosis of non-monotonic ICL behavior in unified multimodal models, and the proposed modulator helps stabilize few-shot adaptation, enabling smaller models to achieve strong ICL performance.

title: CROSS: A Mixture-of-Experts Reinforcement Learning Framework for Generalizable Large-Scale Traffic Signal Control
summary: Recent advances in robotics, automation, and artificial intelligence have enabled urban traffic systems to operate with increasing autonomy towards future smart cities, powered in part by the development of adaptive traffic signal control (ATSC), which dynamically optimizes signal phases to mitigate congestion and optimize traffic. However, achieving effective and generalizable large-scale ATSC remains a significant challenge due to the diverse intersection topologies and highly dynamic, complex traffic demand patterns across the network. Existing RL-based methods typically use a single shared policy for all scenarios, whose limited representational capacity makes it difficult to capture diverse traffic dynamics and generalize to unseen environments. To address these challenges, we propose CROSS, a novel Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-based decentralized RL framework for generalizable ATSC. We first introduce a Predictive Contrastive Clustering (PCC) module that forecasts short-term state transitions to identify latent traffic patterns, followed by clustering and contrastive learning to enhance pattern-level representation. We further design a Scenario-Adaptive MoE module that augments a shared policy with multiple experts, thus enabling adaptive specialization and more flexible scenario-specific strategies. We conduct extensive experiments in the SUMO simulator on both synthetic and real-world traffic datasets. Compared with state-of-the-art baselines, CROSS achieves superior performance and generalization through improved representation of diverse traffic scenarios.

AI.tldr: CROSS uses a Mixture-of-Experts RL framework with Predictive Contrastive Clustering and scenario-adaptive experts to achieve generalizable, decentralized adaptive traffic signal control across diverse intersection topologies and demand patterns.
AI.motivation: Generalizable large-scale adaptive traffic signal control (ATSC) is hindered by diverse intersection topologies and highly dynamic traffic demand; single-policy RL models struggle to generalize to unseen environments.
AI.method: Introduce Predictive Contrastive Clustering (PCC) to forecast short-term state transitions, win latent traffic patterns with clustering and contrastive learning; implement a Scenario-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) module that augments a shared policy with multiple experts for scenario-specific strategies.
AI.result: Extensive SUMO-based experiments on synthetic and real-world data show CROSS achieving superior performance and better generalization over state-of-the-art baselines by improving pattern-level representations and adaptive specialization.
AI.conclusion: A Mixture-of-Experts decentralized RL framework with PCC and scenario-adaptive MoE provides improved generalization for ATSC in diverse networks, contributing a scalable approach for future smart-city traffic management.

title: Supervising Ralph Wiggum: Exploring a Metacognitive Co-Regulation Agentic AI Loop for Engineering Design
summary: The engineering design research community has studied agentic AI systems that use Large Language Model (LLM) agents to automate the engineering design process. However, these systems are prone to some of the same pathologies that plague humans. Just as human designers, LLM design agents can fixate on existing paradigms and fail to explore alternatives when solving design challenges, potentially leading to suboptimal solutions. In this work, we propose (1) a novel Self-Regulation Loop (SRL), in which the Design Agent self-regulates and explicitly monitors its own metacognition, and (2) a novel Co-Regulation Design Agentic Loop (CRDAL), in which a Metacognitive Co-Regulation Agent assists the Design Agent in metacognition to mitigate design fixation, thereby improving system performance for engineering design tasks. In the battery pack design problem examined here, we found that the novel CRDAL system generates designs with better performance, without significantly increasing the computational cost, compared to a plain Ralph Wiggum Loop (RWL) and the metacognitively self-assessing Self-Regulation Loop (SRL). Also, we found that the CRDAL system navigated through the latent design space more effectively than both SRL and RWL. However, the SRL did not generate designs with significantly better performance than RWL, even though it explored a different region of the design space. The proposed system architectures and findings of this work provide practical implications for future development of agentic AI systems for engineering design.

AI.tldr: CRDAL, a metacognition-assisted co-regulation loop, improves design performance and exploration for LLM-based engineering design agents over RWL and SRL, with modest cost.
AI.motivation: Mitigate design fixation and improve performance of agentic AI in engineering design by enabling explicit metacognitive regulation (SRL) and cooperative regulation (CRDAL).
AI.method: Introduce Self-Regulation Loop (SRL) where the Design Agent monitors its own metacognition; introduce Co-Regulation Design Agentic Loop (CRDAL) with a Metacognitive Co-Regulation Agent aiding the Design Agent; evaluate on battery pack design; compare against a plain Ralph Wiggum Loop (RWL) and SRL.
AI.result: CRDAL yields designs with better performance than RWL and SRL without significant cost increase; CRDAL navigates the latent design space more effectively; SRL does not achieve significantly higher performance than RWL, even though it explores a different region.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SRL/CRDAL architectures offer practical implications for future development of agentic AI in engineering design, with CRDAL showing potential to mitigate design fixation and enhance performance at modest additional cost.

title: Can LLMs Beat Classical Hyperparameter Optimization Algorithms? A Study on autoresearch
summary: The autoresearch repository enables an LLM agent to search for optimal hyperparameter configurations on an unconstrained search space by editing the training code directly. Given a fixed compute budget and constraints, we use \emph{autoresearch} as a testbed to compare classical hyperparameter optimization (HPO) algorithms against LLM-based methods on tuning the hyperparameters of a small language model. Within a fixed hyperparameter search space, classical HPO methods such as CMA-ES and TPE consistently outperform LLM-based agents. However, an LLM agent that directly edits training source code in an unconstrained search space narrows the gap to classical methods substantially despite using only a self-hosted open-weight 27B model. Methods that avoid out-of-memory failures outperform those with higher search diversity, suggesting that reliability matters more than exploration breadth. While small and mid-sized LLMs struggle to track optimization state across trials, classical methods lack domain knowledge.
