AI.tldr: Two-stage classification of predictive statements in crypto tweets across five assets using manual and GPT-based annotation, SenticNet emotion features, and GPT-based data augmentation; transformers excel in Task 1 for predicting Predictive statements, while traditional ML best in Task 2 for categorizing prediction type.
AI.motivation: Understand the informational value and emotional signals of predictive cryptocurrency statements on social media, enabling better monitoring of speculative activity and forecasting signals while addressing data imbalance and asset-specific patterns.
AI.method: Two-stage labeling: Task 1 binary Predictive vs Non-Predictive, Task 2 multiclass Incremental/Decremental/Neutral on tweets labeled Predictive. Dataset built via manual and GPT-based annotation; emotion features extracted with SenticNet; data augmentation through GPT paraphrasing to mitigate imbalance. Evaluated multiple ML, DL, and transformer models across tasks.
AI.result: GPT-based balancing improved model performance. Transformer models achieved the highest F1-score on Task 1; traditional ML models performed best on Task 2. Emotion analysis showed distinct patterns by category and cryptocurrency.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid annotation and augmentation strategies, coupled with emotion-aware features, effectively identify predictive statements and their directional categories in crypto tweets, with asset-specific emotional signatures; results inform downstream predictive modeling and tool development in crypto social media analytics.

title: Light Cones For Vision: Simple Causal Priors For Visual Hierarchy
summary: Standard vision models treat objects as independent points in Euclidean space, unable to capture hierarchical structure like parts within wholes. We introduce Worldline Slot Attention, which models objects as persistent trajectories through spacetime worldlines, where each object has multiple slots at different hierarchy levels sharing the same spatial position but differing in temporal coordinates. This architecture consistently fails without geometric structure: Euclidean worldlines achieve 0.078 level accuracy, below random chance (0.33), while Lorentzian worldlines achieve 0.479-0.661 across three datasets: a 6x improvement replicated over 20+ independent runs. Lorentzian geometry also outperforms hyperbolic embeddings showing visual hierarchies require causal structure (temporal dependency) rather than tree structure (radial branching). Our results demonstrate that hierarchical object discovery requires geometric structure encoding asymmetric causality, an inductive bias absent from Euclidean space but natural to Lorentzian light cones, achieved with only 11K parameters. The code is available at: https://github.com/iclrsubmissiongram/loco.

AI.tldr: Worldline Slot Attention uses Lorentzian geometry to model objects as persistent spacetime worldlines with multi-level slots, showing Lorentzian causality outperforms Euclidean and hyperbolic baselines for hierarchical object discovery with few parameters.
AI.motivation: Standard vision treats objects as independent points and lacks hierarchical/cascading structure. Causal structure in spacetime (Lorentzian geometry) may be necessary to capture parts-wholes and temporal dependencies.
AI.method: Represent objects as persistent trajectories through spacetime (worldlines). Each object has multiple slots at different hierarchy levels sharing the same spatial location but different temporal coordinates. Compare Euclidean vs Lorentzian worldlines and hyperbolic embeddings. ~11k parameters. Code released.
AI.result: Lorentzian worldlines achieve 0.479–0.661 level accuracy across three datasets, ~6x improvement over Euclidean (0.078, below random 0.33) and outperform hyperbolic embeddings; results replicated over 20+ runs; 11k parameters suffice.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical object discovery requires geometric structure encoding asymmetric causality, naturally captured by Lorentzian light-cone geometry rather than Euclidean space or tree-like hyperbolic embeddings.

title: A Framework for Generating Semantically Ambiguous Images to Probe Human and Machine Perception
summary: The classic duck-rabbit illusion reveals that when visual evidence is ambiguous, the human brain must decide what it sees. But where exactly do human observers draw the line between ''duck'' and ''rabbit'', and do machine classifiers draw it in the same place? We use semantically ambiguous images as interpretability probes to expose how vision models represent the boundaries between concepts. We present a psychophysically-informed framework that interpolates between concepts in the CLIP embedding space to generate continuous spectra of ambiguous images, allowing us to precisely measure where and how humans and machine classifiers place their semantic boundaries. Using this framework, we show that machine classifiers are more biased towards seeing ''rabbit'', whereas humans are more aligned with the CLIP embedding used for synthesis, and the guidance scale seems to affect human sensitivity more strongly than machine classifiers. Our framework demonstrates how controlled ambiguity can serve as a diagnostic tool to bridge the gap between human psychophysical analysis, image classification, and generative image models, offering insight into human-model alignment, robustness, model interpretability, and image synthesis methods.

AI.tldr: Ambiguity-based probes reveal how humans and CLIP-based models delineate 'duck' vs 'rabbit'; humans align with CLIP synthesis embeddings, machines biased toward 'rabbit'; a psychophysically-informed, interpolation-based framework assesses semantic boundaries.
AI.motivation: Understand cross-domain alignment among human perception, discriminative classifiers, and generative models; diagnose robustness and interpretability of vision systems using controlled semantic ambiguity.
AI.method: Develop a psychophysically-informed interpolation framework in CLIP embedding space to generate continuous spectra of ambiguous images; compare human responses and model classifications to locate semantic boundaries; manipulate guidance scale to test its effect on sensitivity.
AI.result: Finds that classifiers favor the 'rabbit' interpretation more than humans; humans track CLIP embeddings used for synthesis; guidance scale modulates human sensitivity more than classifier decisions; demonstrates ambiguity as a diagnostic tool for alignment and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Ambiguity-based probes can bridge psychophysics, image classification, and generative modeling, yielding insights into human-model alignment, robustness, interpretability, and image synthesis techniques.

title: Dissimilarity-Based Persistent Coverage Control of Multi-Robot Systems for Improving Solar Irradiance Prediction Accuracy in Solar Thermal Power Plants
summary: Accurate forecasting of future solar irradiance is essential for the effective control of solar thermal power plants. Although various kriging-based methods have been proposed to address the prediction problem, these methods typically do not provide an appropriate sampling strategy to dynamically position mobile sensors for optimizing prediction accuracy in real time, which is critical for achieving accurate forecasts with a minimal number of sensors. This paper introduces a dissimilarity map derived from a kriging model and proposes a persistent coverage control algorithm that effectively guides agents toward regions where additional observations are required to improve prediction performance. By means of experiments using mobile robots, the proposed approach was shown to obtain more accurate predictions than the considered baselines under various emulated irradiance fields.

AI.tldr: Kriging-based solar irradiance forecasting enhanced by a dissimilarity map and persistent coverage control to guide mobile sensors, achieving higher prediction accuracy than baselines in emulated irradiance fields.
AI.motivation: Efficient, real-time solar irradiance forecasting with a limited number of sensors requires adaptive sampling strategies; traditional kriging lacks dynamic sensor placement guidance.
AI.method: Derive a dissimilarity map from the kriging model and apply a persistent coverage control algorithm to steer mobile agents toward regions where additional observations reduce forecast error; validate with experiments using mobile robots on simulated irradiance fields.
AI.result: The proposed approach yields more accurate predictions than the baselines across various emulated irradiance fields.
AI.conclusion: An adaptive sampling framework combining kriging-derived dissimilarity and persistent coverage control is effective for improving solar forecast accuracy with limited mobile sensing resources, with potential for real-time deployment.

title: FinMCP-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Real-World Financial Tool Use under the Model Context Protocol
summary: This paper introduces \textbf{FinMCP-Bench}, a novel benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in solving real-world financial problems through tool invocation of financial model context protocols. FinMCP-Bench contains 613 samples spanning 10 main scenarios and 33 sub-scenarios, featuring both real and synthetic user queries to ensure diversity and authenticity. It incorporates 65 real financial MCPs and three types of samples, single tool, multi-tool, and multi-turn, allowing evaluation of models across different levels of task complexity. Using this benchmark, we systematically assess a range of mainstream LLMs and propose metrics that explicitly measure tool invocation accuracy and reasoning capabilities. FinMCP-Bench provides a standardized, practical, and challenging testbed for advancing research on financial LLM agents.

AI.tldr: FinMCP-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on real-world financial problems using tool invocation, featuring 613 samples across 10 main and 33 sub-scenarios, 65 real MCPs, and three sample types (single tool, multi-tool, multi-turn); it defines metrics for tool invocation accuracy and reasoning and serves as a standardized, challenging testbed for financial LLM agents.
AI.motivation: The dataset aims to close the gap between synthetic benchmarks and real-world financial task solving by focusing on tool-use and reasoning in finance, enabling robust evaluation of financial LLM agents.
AI.method: Construct a dataset of 613 samples across 10 main scenarios and 33 sub-scenarios, mixing real and synthetic user queries. Include 65 real financial MCPs. Create three sample types (single tool, multi-tool, multi-turn) to vary task complexity. Evaluate mainstream LLMs with metrics that measure tool invocation accuracy and reasoning ability.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation of mainstream LLMs across the benchmark; introduction of metrics targeting tool invocation precision and financial reasoning; FinMCP-Bench provides a standardized, practical platform to compare and advance financial LLM agents across tool-use scenarios.
AI.conclusion: FinMCP-Bench offers a rigorous, real-world-oriented benchmark to spur development of financial LLM agents with improved tool-use capabilities and reasoning, promoting standardized evaluation and progress in the field.

title: Transformers in the Dark: Navigating Unknown Search Spaces via Bandit Feedback
comment: Accepted for publication in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Effective problem solving with Large Language Models (LLMs) can be enhanced when they are paired with external search algorithms. By viewing the space of diverse ideas and their follow-up possibilities as a tree structure, the search algorithm can navigate such a search space and guide the LLM toward better solutions more efficiently. While the search algorithm enables an effective balance between exploitation and exploration of a tree-structured space, the need for an external component can complicate the overall problem-solving process. We therefore pose the following question: Can LLMs or their underlying Transformer architectures approximate a search algorithm? To answer this question, we first introduce a simplified framework in which tree extensions and feedback signals are externally specified, allowing for controlled evaluation of search capabilities. We call this setting unknown tree search with bandit feedback. Within this setting, we show that Transformers are theoretically expressive enough to implement distinct search strategies and can be trained from scratch to approximate those strategies. Our Transformer models exhibit the possibility of generalizing to unseen conditions such as longer horizons or deeper trees. Furthermore, we demonstrate that continued task-focused training unlocks the complete capabilities of a pretrained LLM, by fine-tuning the LLM on search trajectories.

AI.tldr: Transformers can emulate search algorithms for tree-structured problem spaces and, with task-focused fine-tuning, unlock full search capabilities within LLMs.
AI.motivation: Reduce external dependencies by enabling LLMs to perform efficient tree search internally, leveraging their attention-based dynamics to balance exploration and exploitation in a structured idea space.
AI.method: Introduce a controlled framework called unknown tree search with bandit feedback. Prove that Transformer architectures can implement distinct search strategies from scratch to approximate these strategies; demonstrate generalization to longer horizons/deeper trees; show that continued task-focused training on search trajectories enhances a pretrained LLM's capabilities.
AI.result: Transformers are theoretically expressive enough to implement various search strategies and can be trained from scratch to approximate them; models can generalize to unseen horizons and deeper trees; continued training on search trajectories unlocks the full capabilities of pretrained LLMs.
AI.conclusion: LLMs/Transformers can emulate external search algorithms for tree-structured reasoning, reducing dependence on separate search components; with targeted training, they can attain effective search behavior and generalize beyond training conditions.

title: OpenCap Monocular: 3D Human Kinematics and Musculoskeletal Dynamics from a Single Smartphone Video
summary: Quantifying human movement (kinematics) and musculoskeletal forces (kinetics) at scale, such as estimating quadriceps force during a sit-to-stand movement, could transform prediction, treatment, and monitoring of mobility-related conditions. However, quantifying kinematics and kinetics traditionally requires costly, time-intensive analysis in specialized laboratories, limiting clinical translation. Scalable, accurate tools for biomechanical assessment are needed. We introduce OpenCap Monocular, an algorithm that estimates 3D skeletal kinematics and kinetics from a single smartphone video. The method refines 3D human pose estimates from a monocular pose estimation model (WHAM) via optimization, computes kinematics of a biomechanically constrained skeletal model, and estimates kinetics via physics-based simulation and machine learning. We validated OpenCap Monocular against marker-based motion capture and force plate data for walking, squatting, and sit-to-stand tasks. OpenCap Monocular achieved low kinematic error (4.8° mean absolute error for rotational degrees of freedom; 3.4 cm for pelvis translations), outperforming a regression-only computer vision baseline by 48% in rotational accuracy (p = 0.036) and 69% in translational accuracy (p < 0.001). OpenCap Monocular also estimated ground reaction forces during walking with accuracy comparable to, or better than, our prior two-camera OpenCap system. We demonstrate that the algorithm estimates important kinetic outcomes with clinically meaningful accuracy in applications related to frailty and knee osteoarthritis, including estimating knee extension moment during sit-to-stand transitions and knee adduction moment during walking. OpenCap Monocular is deployed via a smartphone app, web app, and secure cloud computing (https://opencap.ai), enabling free, accessible single-smartphone biomechanical assessments.

AI.tldr: Single-smartphone pipeline estimates 3D kinematics and kinetics with clinically meaningful accuracy, validated against marker-based data and deployed in mobile/cloud apps.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, accurate biomechanical assessment outside specialized labs to support diagnosis, treatment, and monitoring of mobility-related conditions.
AI.method: Refines monocular pose estimates (WHAM) via optimization, computes 3D kinematics from a biomechanically constrained skeletal model, and estimates kinetics through physics-based simulation and machine learning. Validated against marker-based motion capture and force plates for walking, squatting, and sit-to-stand; deployed via smartphone app, web app, and cloud.
AI.result: Kinematic MAE ~4.8° for rotational DOF; pelvis translation error ~3.4 cm. 48% improvement in rotational accuracy and 69% in translational accuracy over a regression-only baseline (p=0.036 and p<0.001, respectively). Ground reaction forces during walking comparable to/better than prior two-camera OpenCap. Clinically meaningful kinetic estimates (e.g., knee extension moment in sit-to-stand; knee adduction moment in walking).
AI.conclusion: OpenCap Monocular delivers accessible, scalable biomechanical assessment from a single smartphone, with validated accuracy and deployment in consumer workflows.

title: A Minimum-Energy Control Approach for Redundant Mobile Manipulators in Physical Human-Robot Interaction Applications
summary: Research on mobile manipulation systems that physically interact with humans has expanded rapidly in recent years, opening the way to tasks which could not be performed using fixed-base manipulators. Within this context, developing suitable control methodologies is essential since mobile manipulators introduce additional degrees of freedom, making the design of control approaches more challenging and more prone to performance optimization. This paper proposes a control approach for a mobile manipulator, composed of a mobile base equipped with a robotic arm mounted on the top, with the objective of minimizing the overall kinetic energy stored in the whole-body mobile manipulator in physical human-robot interaction applications. The approach is experimentally tested with reference to a peg-in-hole task, and the results demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces the overall kinetic energy stored in the whole-body robotic system and improves the system performance compared with the benchmark method.

AI.tldr: An energy-minimizing control for mobile manipulators in human-robot interaction reduces total kinetic energy and improves peg-in-hole performance versus a benchmark.
AI.motivation: Enable physically interactive tasks with mobile bases by addressing extra degrees of freedom; seek safer, more efficient control by minimizing kinetic energy.
AI.method: Propose an energy-based control scheme for a mobile base plus mounted robotic arm; optimize to minimize overall system kinetic energy; validated experimentally on peg-in-hole; comparison with a benchmark control.
AI.result: Experiment shows reduced system kinetic energy and improved performance relative to benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Energy-minimizing control for mobile manipulators is effective for HRI tasks, offering performance and safety benefits.

title: Shopping with a Platform AI Assistant: Who Adopts, When in the Journey, and What For
summary: This paper provides some of the first large-scale descriptive evidence on how consumers adopt and use platform-embedded shopping AI in e-commerce. Using data on 31 million users of Ctrip, China's largest online travel platform, we study "Wendao," an LLM-based AI assistant integrated into the platform. We document three empirical regularities. First, adoption is highest among older consumers, female users, and highly engaged existing users, reversing the younger, male-dominated profile commonly documented for general-purpose AI tools. Second, AI chat appears in the same broad phase of the purchase journey as traditional search and well before order placement; among journeys containing both chat and search, the most common pattern is interleaving, with users moving back and forth between the two modalities. Third, consumers disproportionately use the assistant for exploratory, hard-to-keyword tasks: attraction queries account for 42% of observed chat requests, and chat intent varies systematically with both the timing of chat relative to search and the category of products later purchased within the same journey. These findings suggest that embedded shopping AI functions less as a substitute for conventional search than as a complementary interface for exploratory product discovery in e-commerce.

AI.tldr: Platform-embedded shopping AI (Wendao) on Ctrip shows distinctive adopter demographics, early-stage integration with search in the purchase journey with interleaving usage, and a focus on exploratory, keyword-light tasks; overall, it serves as a complement to search for product discovery rather than a substitute.
AI.motivation: To provide large-scale, real-world evidence on how consumers adopt and use platform-embedded shopping AI, addressing gaps beyond lab studies of general-purpose AI and conventional search.
AI.method: Descriptive, observational analysis of 31 million Ctrip users using the Wendao AI, examining (i) adoption patterns by demographic/engagement profiles, (ii) temporal placement of AI chat within purchase journeys and its relation to traditional search (including interleaving patterns), and (iii) task types and intents (notably attraction queries, ~42% of chat requests) and their relation to search timing and later purchases.
AI.result: (1) Adoption is higher among older, female, and highly engaged users, reversing the typical younger/male skew seen with general-purpose AI; (2) AI chat appears in the same broad phase as search prior to order placement, with interleaving between chat and search being common; (3) users predominantly employ the assistant for exploratory, hard-to-keyword tasks, with attraction queries constituting 42% of observed chat requests and chat intent correlating with timing relative to search and product category purchased; collectively, this suggests embedded shopping AI complements traditional search by enabling exploratory product discovery.
AI.conclusion: Embedded shopping AI on a platform acts more as a complementary interface for exploratory discovery than a substitute for search, with implications for targeting, design, and integration strategy in e-commerce platforms.

title: Local learning for stable backpropagation-free neural network training towards physical learning
summary: While backpropagation and automatic differentiation have driven deep learning's success, the physical limits of chip manufacturing and rising environmental costs of deep learning motivate alternative learning paradigms such as physical neural networks. However, most existing physical neural networks still rely on digital computing for training, largely because backpropagation and automatic differentiation are difficult to realize in physical systems. We introduce FFzero, a forward-only learning framework enabling stable neural network training without backpropagation or automatic differentiation. FFzero combines layer-wise local learning, prototype-based representations, and directional-derivative-based optimization through forward evaluations only. We show that local learning is effective under forward-only optimization, where backpropagation fails. FFzero generalizes to multilayer perceptron and convolutional neural networks across classification and regression. Using a simulated photonic neural network as an example, we demonstrate that FFzero provides a viable path toward backpropagation-free in-situ physical learning.

AI.tldr: FFzero is a forward-only learning framework enabling training of neural networks without backpropagation or automatic differentiation, using layer-wise local learning, prototype-based representations, and forward-only directional-derivative optimization; demonstrated on MLP/CNN and a simulated photonic NN.
AI.motivation: Address physical limits and environmental costs of deep learning; backpropagation is difficult to implement in physical systems; need in-situ, forward-only training methods.
AI.method: Integrates layer-wise local learning with prototype representations and directional-derivative-based optimization computed via forward evaluations; avoids backprop/AD; applicable to MLP and CNN; validated on a simulated photonic network.
AI.result: Local learning is effective under forward-only optimization where backprop fails; FFzero generalizes to MLP/CNN for classification and regression; photonic NN simulation shows viability of backprop-free in-situ learning.
AI.conclusion: FFzero offers a viable path for training physical neural networks without backprop or AD, potentially enabling efficient in-situ learning in physical hardware.

title: TIGeR: A Unified Framework for Time, Images and Geo-location Retrieval
summary: Many real-world applications in digital forensics, urban monitoring, and environmental analysis require jointly reasoning about visual appearance, geolocation, and time. Beyond standard geo-localization and time-of-capture prediction, these applications increasingly demand more complex capabilities, such as retrieving an image captured at the same location as a query image but at a specified target time. We formalize this problem as Geo-Time Aware Image Retrieval and curate a diverse benchmark of 4.5M paired image-location-time triplets for training and 86k high-quality triplets for evaluation. We then propose TIGeR, a multi-modal-transformer-based model that maps image, geolocation, and time into a unified geo-temporal embedding space. TIGeR supports flexible input configurations (single-modality and multi-modality queries) and uses the same representation to perform (i) geo-localization, (ii) time-of-capture prediction, and (iii) geo-time-aware retrieval. By better preserving underlying location identity under large appearance changes, TIGeR enables retrieval based on where and when a scene is, rather than purely on visual similarity. Extensive experiments show that TIGeR consistently outperforms strong baselines and state-of-the-art methods by up to 16% on time-of-year, 8% time-of-day prediction, and 14% in geo-time aware retrieval recall, highlighting the benefits of unified geo-temporal modeling.

AI.tldr: TIGeR presents a unified geo-temporal embedding via a multi-modal transformer for image, location, and time, enabling geo-localization, time prediction, and geo-time-aware retrieval; trained on 4.5M triplets with 86k evaluation triplets; outperforms baselines by up to 16% (time-of-year), 8% (time-of-day), 14% retrieval recall.
AI.motivation: Real-world applications require reasoning about appearance, geolocation, and time; existing methods handle geo-localization or time prediction but not joint geo-temporal retrieval, necessitating a unified embedding to retrieve by location and time despite appearance changes.
AI.method: A multi-modal-transformer-based model (TIGeR) that maps image, geolocation, and time into a single geo-temporal embedding space. It supports flexible input configurations (single- or multi-modality queries) and enables three tasks—geo-localization, time-of-capture prediction, and geo-time-aware retrieval—using the same representation. The approach is trained on 4.5M triplets and evaluated on 86k triplets to assess retrieval performance and temporal predictions.
AI.result: TIGeR consistently outperforms strong baselines and state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to 16% improvement on time-of-year prediction, 8% on time-of-day prediction, and 14% in geo-time-aware retrieval recall.
AI.conclusion: Unified geo-temporal modeling preserves location identity under large appearance changes, enabling retrieval by where and when rather than by appearance alone, thereby expanding capabilities for geo-localization, temporal inference, and cross-modal retrieval.

title: Connectivity-Aware Representations for Constrained Motion Planning via Multi-Scale Contrastive Learning
summary: The objective of constrained motion planning is to connect start and goal configurations while satisfying task-specific constraints. Motion planning becomes inefficient or infeasible when the configurations lie in disconnected regions, known as essentially mutually disconnected (EMD) components. Constraints further restrict feasible space to a lower-dimensional submanifold, while redundancy introduces additional complexity because a single end-effector pose admits infinitely many inverse kinematic solutions that may form discrete self-motion manifolds. This paper addresses these challenges by learning a connectivity-aware representation for selecting start and goal configurations prior to planning. Joint configurations are embedded into a latent space through multi-scale manifold learning across neighborhood ranges from local to global, and clustering generates pseudo-labels that supervise a contrastive learning framework. The proposed framework provides a connectivity-aware measure that biases the selection of start and goal configurations in connected regions, avoiding EMDs and yielding higher success rates with reduced planning time. Experiments on various manipulation tasks showed that our method achieves 1.9 times higher success rates and reduces the planning time by a factor of 0.43 compared to baselines.

AI.tldr: Learning a connectivity-aware latent representation to bias start/goal selection in connected regions for constrained motion planning, improving success rates and reducing planning time.
AI.motivation: EMD components and low-dimensional constrained manifolds complicate planning; redundant IK solutions create self-motion manifolds; selecting start/goal configurations in connected regions can improve feasibility and efficiency.
AI.method: Multi-scale manifold learning to embed joint configurations across local-to-global neighborhood ranges; clustering to produce pseudo-labels; contrastive learning with pseudo-label supervision to learn a connectivity-aware latent space.
AI.result: Achieves 1.9x higher success rates; reduces planning time by a factor of 0.43 relative to baselines in manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Connectivity-aware start/goal selection is effective for constrained motion planning with redundancy and ensures faster, more reliable planning; method can generalize to related planning problems.

title: Can MLLMs Read Students' Minds? Unpacking Multimodal Error Analysis in Handwritten Math
comment: Accepted by the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED'26)
summary: Assessing student handwritten scratchwork is crucial for personalized educational feedback but presents unique challenges due to diverse handwriting, complex layouts, and varied problem-solving approaches. Existing educational NLP primarily focuses on textual responses and neglects the complexity and multimodality inherent in authentic handwritten scratchwork. Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) excel at visual reasoning but typically adopt an "examinee perspective", prioritizing generating correct answers rather than diagnosing student errors. To bridge these gaps, we introduce ScratchMath, a novel benchmark specifically designed for explaining and classifying errors in authentic handwritten mathematics scratchwork. Our dataset comprises 1,720 mathematics samples from Chinese primary and middle school students, supporting two key tasks: Error Cause Explanation (ECE) and Error Cause Classification (ECC), with seven defined error types. The dataset is meticulously annotated through rigorous human-machine collaborative approaches involving multiple stages of expert labeling, review, and verification. We systematically evaluate 16 leading MLLMs on ScratchMath, revealing significant performance gaps relative to human experts, especially in visual recognition and logical reasoning. Proprietary models notably outperform open-source models, with large reasoning models showing strong potential for error explanation. All evaluation data and frameworks are publicly available to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: ScratchMath is a new multimodal benchmark to explain and classify errors in authentic handwritten math scratchwork, highlighting gaps in current MLLMs and showing proprietary models’ advantage; resources are publicly released.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap in educational NLP for handwritten scratchwork by enabling error diagnosis and explanations, moving beyond text-only responses to multimodal reasoning that can support personalized feedback.
AI.method: Construct a dataset of 1,720 handwritten math samples from Chinese students, define two tasks (Error Cause Explanation and Error Cause Classification) with seven error types, and apply a rigorous human-machine collaborative annotation pipeline. Evaluate 16 leading multimodal LLMs, compare to human experts, analyze visual and logical reasoning performance, and release evaluation data and frameworks.
AI.result: Significant performance gaps between current MLLMs and human experts, especially in visual recognition and reasoning; proprietary models outperform open-source models; large reasoning models show strong potential for error explanation.
AI.conclusion: ScratchMath provides a valuable benchmark to drive progress in multimodal educational AI, highlighting the need for improved visual and reasoning capabilities in MLLMs and offering publicly available data and frameworks to foster further research.

title: A Practical Guide Towards Interpreting Time-Series Deep Clinical Predictive Models: A Reproducibility Study
summary: Clinical decisions are high-stakes and require explicit justification, making model interpretability essential for auditing deep clinical models prior to deployment. As the ecosystem of model architectures and explainability methods expands, critical questions remain: Do architectural features like attention improve explainability? Do interpretability approaches generalize across clinical tasks? While prior benchmarking efforts exist, they often lack extensibility and reproducibility, and critically, fail to systematically examine how interpretability varies across the interplay of clinical tasks and model architectures. To address these gaps, we present a comprehensive benchmark evaluating interpretability methods across diverse clinical prediction tasks and model architectures. Our analysis reveals that: (1) attention when leveraged properly is a highly efficient approach for faithfully interpreting model predictions; (2) black-box interpreters like KernelSHAP and LIME are computationally infeasible for time-series clinical prediction tasks; and (3) several interpretability approaches are too unreliable to be trustworthy. From our findings, we discuss several guidelines on improving interpretability within clinical predictive pipelines. To support reproducibility and extensibility, we provide our implementations via PyHealth, a well-documented open-source framework: https://github.com/sunlabuiuc/PyHealth.

AI.tldr: A benchmark study evaluating interpretability methods across clinical tasks and architectures shows attention-based explanations are efficient and faithful when used properly; kernel-based explanations are often infeasible for time-series; many methods lack reliability; offers guidelines and PyHealth framework for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: To address gaps in interpretability benchmarking: extensibility, reproducibility, and understanding how interpretability interacts with clinical tasks and model choices.
AI.method: Systematic benchmark across diverse clinical prediction tasks and model architectures; assess various interpretability methods, including attention-based explanations; analyze computational feasibility.
AI.result: Findings: (1) properly used attention is efficient and faithful; (2) KernelSHAP/LIME are computationally infeasible for time-series; (3) several interpretable methods are unreliable.
AI.conclusion: Provide guidelines to improve interpretability in clinical pipelines and release PyHealth for reproducible benchmarking.

title: Synthetic Cardiac MRI Image Generation using Deep Generative Models
summary: Synthetic cardiac MRI (CMRI) generation has emerged as a promising strategy to overcome the scarcity of annotated medical imaging data. Recent advances in GANs, VAEs, diffusion probabilistic models, and flow-matching techniques aim to generate anatomically accurate images while addressing challenges such as limited labeled datasets, vendor variability, and risks of privacy leakage through model memorization. Maskconditioned generation improves structural fidelity by guiding synthesis with segmentation maps, while diffusion and flowmatching models offer strong boundary preservation and efficient deterministic transformations. Cross-domain generalization is further supported through vendor-style conditioning and preprocessing steps like intensity normalization. To ensure privacy, studies increasingly incorporate membership inference attacks, nearest-neighbor analyses, and differential privacy mechanisms. Utility evaluations commonly measure downstream segmentation performance, with evidence showing that anatomically constrained synthetic data can enhance accuracy and robustness across multi-vendor settings. This review aims to compare existing CMRI generation approaches through the lenses of fidelity, utility, and privacy, highlighting current limitations and the need for integrated, evaluation-driven frameworks for reliable clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: A survey of synthetic CMRI generation methods focusing on fidelity, utility, and privacy, comparing GANs/VAEs/diffusion/flow-matching, with conditioning strategies (mask, vendor-style) and privacy analyses, highlighting cross-domain generalization, evaluation gaps, and need for integrated benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Overcome data scarcity, multi-vendor variability, and privacy concerns in CMRI by synthesizing anatomically accurate images usable for downstream tasks.
AI.method: Literature review and taxonomy of CMRI generation approaches, categorized by model type (GAN/VAEs/diffusion/flow-matching), conditioning strategies (mask-based, vendor-style), evaluation metrics (fidelity, segmentation utility, privacy), and cross-domain/generalization aspects; critical assessment of privacy mechanisms (membership inference, DP) and utility assessments, with discussion of limitations and future directions.
AI.result: Key findings include: diffusion and flow-matching methods offer strong boundary preservation; mask-conditioned generation improves structural fidelity; vendor-style conditioning and intensity normalization aid cross-domain generalization; privacy attacks (membership inference, nearest-neighbor analyses) motivate differential privacy and defense strategies; downstream segmentation performance is a common utility metric, with synthetic data improving robustness across multi-vendor settings; overall, there is progress but a need for standardized benchmarks and integrated evaluation frameworks.
AI.conclusion: The field shows meaningful advances in fidelity, utility, and privacy for CMRI synthesis, but standardization, clinically oriented validation, and comprehensive privacy-preserving evaluations are required to enable reliable clinical deployment.

title: SafeGuard ASF: SR Agentic Humanoid Robot System for Autonomous Industrial Safety
summary: The rise of unmanned ``dark factories'' operating without human presence demands autonomous safety systems capable of detecting and responding to multiple hazard types. We present SafeGuard ASF (Agentic Security Fleet), a comprehensive framework deploying humanoid robots for autonomous hazard detection in industrial environments. Our system integrates multi-modal perception (RGB-D imaging), a ReAct-based agentic reasoning framework, and learned locomotion policies on the Unitree G1 humanoid platform. We address three critical hazard scenarios: fire and smoke detection, abnormal temperature monitoring in pipelines, and intruder detection in restricted zones. Our perception pipeline achieves 94.2% mAP for fire or smoke detection with 127ms latency. We train multiple locomotion policies, including dance motion tracking and velocity control, using Unitree RL Lab with PPO, demonstrating stable convergence within 80,000 training iterations. We validate our system in both simulation and real-world environments, demonstrating autonomous patrol, human detection with visual perception, and obstacle avoidance capabilities. The proposed ToolOrchestra action framework enables structured decision-making through perception, reasoning, and actuation tools.

AI.tldr: SafeGuard ASF is a framework enabling autonomous hazard detection in dark factories using humanoid robots, combining RGB-D perception, ReAct-based reasoning, and learned locomotion on Unitree G1; it addresses fire/smoke, abnormal pipeline temperatures, and intruder detection; achieves 94.2% mAP with 127 ms latency for fire/smoke; trained policies via PPO with 80k iterations; validated in simulation and real-world, with autonomous patrol and obstacle avoidance; introduces ToolOrchestra for structured perception-action decision-making.
AI.motivation: As unmanned, human-free factories rise, there is a need for autonomous safety systems that can detect and respond to multiple hazards without human oversight.
AI.method: Integrated pipeline: multi-modal RGB-D perception; ReAct-based agentic reasoning; learned locomotion policies on Unitree G1; training with PPO in Unitree RL Lab; validation in sim and real; ToolOrchestra framework for structured decision-making.
AI.result: Detection performance: 94.2% mAP for fire/smoke; latency 127 ms; locomotion policies converge within 80,000 iterations; demonstrated autonomous patrol, human detection, and obstacle avoidance; real-world validation.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end framework demonstrates viability of autonomous hazard detection and response in industrial environments, enabling safer dark factories; potential for deployment with humanoid platforms; modular perception-reasoning-action architecture.

title: Flow matching on homogeneous spaces
summary: We propose a general framework to extend Flow Matching to homogeneous spaces, i.e. quotients of Lie groups. Our approach reformulates the problem as a flow matching task on the underlying Lie group by lifting the data distributions. This strategy avoids the potentially complicated geometry of homogeneous spaces by working directly on Lie groups, which in turn enables us reduce the problem to a Euclidean flow matching task on Lie algebras. In contrast to Riemannian Flow Matching, our method eliminates the need to define and compute premetrics or geodesics, resulting in a simpler, faster, and fully intrinsic framework.

AI.tldr: Lift-based Flow Matching on Lie groups enables homogeneous-space flow matching by translating to Euclidean flow on Lie algebras; avoids premetrics/geodesics.
AI.motivation: Homogeneous spaces pose geometric challenges for flow-based transport; a general, intrinsic, and efficient framework is desired.
AI.method: Lift data distributions from quotient to Lie group, perform Flow Matching on the group, effectively a Euclidean problem in the Lie algebra, then push back.
AI.result: Produces a simpler, faster, and intrinsic framework for Flow Matching on homogeneous spaces; avoids computing premetrics or geodesics; abstract does not report empirical results.
AI.conclusion: General framework enabling homogeneous-space flow matching via Lie-group lifting; simplifies implementation and computation; future work includes empirical validation and extension to broader manifolds.

title: DRoPS: Dynamic 3D Reconstruction of Pre-Scanned Objects
summary: Dynamic scene reconstruction from casual videos has seen recent remarkable progress. Numerous approaches have attempted to overcome the ill-posedness of the task by distilling priors from 2D foundational models and by imposing hand-crafted regularization on the optimized motion. However, these methods struggle to reconstruct scenes from extreme novel viewpoints, especially when highly articulated motions are present. In this paper, we present DRoPS, a novel approach that leverages a static pre-scan of the dynamic object as an explicit geometric and appearance prior. While existing state-of-the-art methods fail to fully exploit the pre-scan, DRoPS leverages our novel setup to effectively constrain the solution space and ensure geometrical consistency throughout the sequence. The core of our novelty is twofold: first, we establish a grid-structured and surface-aligned model by organizing Gaussian primitives into pixel grids anchored to the object surface. Second, by leveraging the grid structure of our primitives, we parameterize motion using a CNN conditioned on those grids, injecting strong implicit regularization and correlating the motion of nearby points. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms the current state of the art in rendering quality and 3D tracking accuracy.

AI.tldr: DRoPS introduces a static pre-scan prior and a grid-aligned Gaussian primitive representation to constrain dynamic scene reconstruction from casual videos, with motion modeled by a CNN conditioned on grid-anchored primitives, achieving superior rendering and 3D tracking accuracy.
AI.motivation: Dynamic scene reconstruction from casual videos is highly ill-posed. Existing methods rely on 2D priors and handcrafted motion regularization, but struggle under extreme novel viewpoints and highly articulated motions. A static pre-scan provides explicit geometric and appearance priors to constrain the solution and maintain geometric consistency.
AI.method: Build a grid-structured, surface-aligned model by organizing Gaussian primitives into pixel grids anchored to the object's surface. Parameterize motion with a CNN conditioned on these grids, injecting strong implicit regularization and encouraging correlated motion of nearby points. Use the static pre-scan as a prior to constrain the solution space.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show DRoPS significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art in rendering quality and 3D tracking accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The grid-structured Gaussian primitive representation together with a static pre-scan prior effectively constrains the solution space, enabling robust dynamic scene reconstruction with improved geometric consistency across challenging viewpoints and articulated motions.

title: Bayesian Learning-Enhanced Navigation with Deep Smoothing for Inertial-Aided Navigation
summary: Accurate post-processing navigation is essential for applications such as survey and mapping, where the full measurement history can be exploited to refine past state estimates. Fixed-interval smoothing algorithms represent the theoretically optimal solution under Gaussian assumptions. However, loosely coupled INS/GNSS systems fundamentally inherit the systematic position bias of raw GNSS measurements, leaving a persistent accuracy gap that model-based smoothers cannot resolve. To address this limitation, we propose BLENDS, which integrates Bayesian learning with deep smoothing to enhance navigation performance. BLENDS is a a data-driven post-processing framework that augments the classical two-filter smoother with a transformer-based neural network. It learns to modify the filter covariance matrices and apply an additive correction to the smoothed error-state directly within the Bayesian framework. A novel Bayesian-consistent loss jointly supervises the smoothed mean and covariance, enforcing minimum-variance estimates while maintaining statistical consistency. BLENDS is evaluated on two real-world datasets spanning a mobile robot and a quadrotor. Across all unseen test trajectories, BLENDS achieves horizontal position improvements of up to 63% over the baseline forward EKF.

AI.tldr: BLENDS is a data-driven post-processing framework that augments a classical two-filter smoother with a transformer-based neural network to learn to adjust filter covariances and provide an additive error-state correction, guided by a Bayesian-consistent loss; it achieves up to 63% horizontal position gains on unseen trajectories compared to forward EKF.
AI.motivation: Loosely coupled INS/GNSS systems inherit GNSS bias; fixed-interval smoothers are theoretically optimal under Gaussian assumptions but cannot fully close the accuracy gap; need a learning-based enhancement that respects Bayesian consistency.
AI.method: Integrates Bayesian learning with deep smoothing: transformer network augments two-filter smoother by adjusting covariance matrices and adding correction to smoothed error-state within Bayesian framework. Introduces Bayesian-consistent loss to jointly supervise smoothed mean and covariance.
AI.result: Evaluated on two real-world datasets (mobile robot and quadrotor). On unseen test trajectories, horizontal position improved up to 63% over baseline forward EKF.
AI.conclusion: BLENDS demonstrates that data-driven corrections within a Bayesian smoothing framework can substantially improve post-processing navigation accuracy in INS/GNSS, addressing GNSS biases beyond model-based smoothers and generalizing to real-world platforms.

title: The Anatomy of Uncertainty in LLMs
summary: Understanding why a large language model (LLM) is uncertain about the response is important for their reliable deployment. Current approaches, which either provide a single uncertainty score or rely on the classical aleatoric-epistemic dichotomy, fail to offer actionable insights for improving the generative model. Recent studies have also shown that such methods are not enough for understanding uncertainty in LLMs. In this work, we advocate for an uncertainty decomposition framework that dissects LLM uncertainty into three distinct semantic components: (i) input ambiguity, arising from ambiguous prompts; (ii) knowledge gaps, caused by insufficient parametric evidence; and (iii) decoding randomness, stemming from stochastic sampling. Through a series of experiments we demonstrate that the dominance of these components can shift across model size and task. Our framework provides a better understanding to audit LLM reliability and detect hallucinations, paving the way for targeted interventions and more trustworthy systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes an uncertainty decomposition for LLMs into input ambiguity, knowledge gaps, and decoding randomness, showing their relative importance varies with model size and task.
AI.motivation: Current uncertainty metrics are either a single score or rely on the aleatoric–epistemic dichotomy, which often fails to yield actionable guidance for improving LLM reliability or mitigating hallucinations.
AI.method: Introduce an uncertainty decomposition framework that partitions uncertainty into three semantic components (input ambiguity, knowledge gaps, decoding randomness); validate via experiments across model sizes and tasks to observe how component dominance shifts.
AI.result: The dominance of the three components is not fixed; it shifts with model size and task. The framework enables better auditing of LLM reliability and more effective detection of hallucinations, guiding targeted interventions.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty in LLMs should be analyzed as a triad of semantic factors. The proposed framework offers practical pathways for targeted improvements and more trustworthy systems.

title: Reaching Beyond the Mode: RL for Distributional Reasoning in Language Models
summary: Given a question, a language model (LM) implicitly encodes a distribution over possible answers. In practice, post-training procedures for LMs often collapse this distribution onto a single dominant mode. While this is generally not a problem for benchmark-style evaluations that assume one correct answer, many real-world tasks inherently involve multiple valid answers or irreducible uncertainty. Examples include medical diagnosis, ambiguous question answering, and settings with incomplete information. In these cases, we would like LMs to generate multiple plausible hypotheses, ideally with confidence estimates for each one, and without computationally intensive repeated sampling to generate non-modal answers. This paper describes a multi-answer reinforcement learning approach for training LMs to perform distributional reasoning over multiple answers during inference. We modify the RL objective to enable models to explicitly generate multiple candidate answers in a single forward pass, internalizing aspects of inference-time search into the model's generative process. Across question-answering, medical diagnostic, and coding benchmarks, we observe improved diversity, coverage, and set-level calibration scores compared to single answer trained baselines. Models trained with our approach require fewer tokens to generate multiple answers than competing approaches. On coding tasks, they are also substantially more accurate. These results position multi-answer RL as a principled and compute-efficient alternative to inference-time scaling procedures such as best-of-k. Code and more information can be found at https://multi-answer-rl.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Proposes multi-answer RL to generate multiple plausible answers in a single forward pass, improving diversity, coverage, and calibration while reducing inference cost; outperforms single-answer baselines and best-of-k on QA, medical diagnostics, and coding tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-world tasks involve multiple valid answers or uncertainty; single-modal outputs fail to capture distributional reasoning; need efficient, multi-hypothesis generation without expensive sampling at inference.
AI.method: Extend reinforcement learning objective to train LM to emit multiple candidate answers per prompt in one forward pass, embedding inference-time search-like reasoning into training; optimize for diversity, coverage, and set-calibrated predictions across tasks.
AI.result: Empirical results show higher diversity, better coverage of valid answers, improved set-level calibration; fewer generated tokens to produce multiple answers relative to baselines; coding tasks show notable accuracy gains; across tasks, gains in diversity and calibration.
AI.conclusion: Multi-answer RL offers a principled, compute-efficient alternative to inference-time methods like best-of-k, enabling multi-hypothesis generation during inference; code and resources provided for replication.

title: AVControl: Efficient Framework for Training Audio-Visual Controls
summary: Controlling video and audio generation requires diverse modalities, from depth and pose to camera trajectories and audio transformations, yet existing approaches either train a single monolithic model for a fixed set of controls or introduce costly architectural changes for each new modality. We introduce AVControl, a lightweight, extendable framework built on LTX-2, a joint audio-visual foundation model, where each control modality is trained as a separate LoRA on a parallel canvas that provides the reference signal as additional tokens in the attention layers, requiring no architectural changes beyond the LoRA adapters themselves. We show that simply extending image-based in-context methods to video fails for structural control, and that our parallel canvas approach resolves this. On the VACE Benchmark, we outperform all evaluated baselines on depth- and pose-guided generation, inpainting, and outpainting, and show competitive results on camera control and audio-visual benchmarks. Our framework supports a diverse set of independently trained modalities: spatially-aligned controls such as depth, pose, and edges, camera trajectory with intrinsics, sparse motion control, video editing, and, to our knowledge, the first modular audio-visual controls for a joint generation model. Our method is both compute- and data-efficient: each modality requires only a small dataset and converges within a few hundred to a few thousand training steps, a fraction of the budget of monolithic alternatives. We publicly release our code and trained LoRA checkpoints.

AI.tldr: AVControl enables modular, modality-specific controls for video/audio generation using parallel canvases and LoRA adapters on a shared foundation model, avoiding architectural changes and enabling efficient, multi-modal extensibility.
AI.motivation: Need to support diverse control modalities for video and audio generation without building a monolithic model per modality or incurring prohibitive architectural costs.
AI.method: Introduce AVControl on top of LTX-2 with each control modality trained as a separate LoRA on a parallel canvas. Reference signals are provided as additional tokens in attention layers. This preserves the base architecture and requires no changes beyond LoRA adapters. Demonstrates that extending image-based in-context methods to video fails for structural control, whereas parallel canvas succeeds. Supports multiple independently trained modalities (depth, pose, edges, camera intrinsics, sparse motion, video editing, and modular audio-visual controls). Training is data- and compute-efficient (hundreds to thousands of steps).
AI.result: On VACE Benchmark, AVControl outperforms all baselines for depth- and pose-guided generation, inpainting, and outpainting; competitive results on camera control and audio-visual benchmarks. The framework achieves modularity with shared foundation, enabling rapid, per-modality learning with small datasets, and releases code and LoRA checkpoints.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, extendable framework, AVControl, achieves modular, independently trained control modalities for video and audio generation via parallel canvases and LoRA adapters on a joint foundation model, delivering strong performance while maintaining efficiency and scalability.

title: Integrating Deep RL and Bayesian Inference for ObjectNav in Mobile Robotics
comment: Accepted and to be published in the ICARSC 2026 26th IEEE International Conference on Autonomous Robot Systems and Competitions
summary: Autonomous object search is challenging for mobile robots operating in indoor environments due to partial observability, perceptual uncertainty, and the need to trade off exploration and navigation efficiency. Classical probabilistic approaches explicitly represent uncertainty but typically rely on handcrafted action-selection heuristics, while deep reinforcement learning enables adaptive policies but often suffers from slow convergence and limited interpretability. This paper proposes a hybrid object-search framework that integrates Bayesian inference with deep reinforcement learning. The method maintains a spatial belief map over target locations, updated online through Bayesian inference from calibrated object detections, and trains a reinforcement learning policy to select navigation actions directly from this probabilistic representation. The approach is evaluated in realistic indoor simulation using Habitat 3.0 and compared against developed baseline strategies. Across two indoor environments, the proposed method improves success rate while reducing search effort. Overall, the results support the value of combining Bayesian belief estimation with learned action selection to achieve more efficient and reliable objectsearch behavior under partial observability.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Bayesian-deep RL framework for autonomous indoor object search that maintains a Bayesian belief map and learns an action policy; outperforms baselines in Habitat 3.0 simulations.
AI.motivation: Address partial observability and uncertainty in indoor object search by combining probabilistic belief with learned control to improve success rate and efficiency.
AI.method: Maintain a spatial belief map over target locations updated online from calibrated detections via Bayesian inference; train a DRL policy to select navigation actions using the belief map as input; evaluation in Habitat 3.0 with baseline comparisons.
AI.result: Improved success rate and reduced search effort across two environments; demonstrates benefits of integrating Bayesian estimation with learned policy.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian belief estimation + learned action selection yields more efficient and reliable object-search under partial observability.

title: Rethinking Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems: A Multi-Perspective Benchmark and Evaluation
summary: Failure attribution is essential for diagnosing and improving multi-agent systems (MAS), yet existing benchmarks and methods largely assume a single deterministic root cause for each failure. In practice, MAS failures often admit multiple plausible attributions due to complex inter-agent dependencies and ambiguous execution trajectories. We revisit MAS failure attribution from a multi-perspective standpoint and propose multi-perspective failure attribution, a practical paradigm that explicitly accounts for attribution ambiguity. To support this setting, we introduce MP-Bench, the first benchmark designed for multi-perspective failure attribution in MAS, along with a new evaluation protocol tailored to this paradigm. Through extensive experiments, we find that prior conclusions suggesting LLMs struggle with failure attribution are largely driven by limitations in existing benchmark designs. Our results highlight the necessity of multi-perspective benchmarks and evaluation protocols for realistic and reliable MAS debugging.

AI.tldr: Proposes multi-perspective failure attribution (MP-FA) for multi-agent systems, introducing MP-Bench and a dedicated evaluation protocol to handle attribution ambiguity; challenges single-attribution assumptions and reevaluates LLM capabilities in this setting.
AI.motivation: Existing MAS failure attribution typically enforces a single deterministic root cause, which ignores inter-agent dependencies and trajectory ambiguity; there is a need to explicitly model and evaluate attribution ambiguity in practice.
AI.method: Introduce the multi-perspective failure attribution paradigm, design MP-Bench as the first benchmark for this setting, develop a specialized evaluation protocol, and conduct extensive experiments (including LLM-based approaches) to assess attribution under ambiguity.
AI.result: MP-Bench provides a first benchmark for multi-perspective attribution in MAS with a tailored protocol; experiments reveal that prior claims about LLMs' failure attributions are largely due to benchmark limitations, underscoring the importance of multi-perspective benchmarks and evaluation protocols for realistic MAS debugging.
AI.conclusion: Multi-perspective benchmarks and evaluation protocols are necessary for realistic MAS debugging; MP-Bench enables reliable evaluation of attribution under ambiguity and should motivate further research in this direction.

title: Learning to Staff: Offline Reinforcement Learning and Fine-Tuned LLMs for Warehouse Staffing Optimization
comment: ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI for Mechanism Design and Strategic Decision Making
summary: We investigate machine learning approaches for optimizing real-time staffing decisions in semi-automated warehouse sortation systems. Operational decision-making can be supported at different levels of abstraction, with different trade-offs. We evaluate two approaches, each in a matching simulation environment. First, we train custom Transformer-based policies using offline reinforcement learning on detailed historical state representations, achieving a 2.4% throughput improvement over historical baselines in learned simulators. In high-volume warehouse operations, improvements of this size translate to significant savings. Second, we explore LLMs operating on abstracted, human-readable state descriptions. These are a natural fit for decisions that warehouse managers make using high-level operational summaries. We systematically compare prompting techniques, automatic prompt optimization, and fine-tuning strategies. While prompting alone proves insufficient, supervised fine-tuning combined with Direct Preference Optimization on simulator-generated preferences achieves performance that matches or slightly exceeds historical baselines in a hand-crafted simulator. Our findings demonstrate that both approaches offer viable paths toward AI-assisted operational decision-making. Offline RL excels with task-specific architectures. LLMs support human-readable inputs and can be combined with an iterative feedback loop that can incorporate manager preferences.

AI.tldr: Two AI approaches for real-time staffing in semi-automated warehouse sortation: offline RL with Transformer policies yields a 2.4% throughput gain in learned simulators; LLM-based decision-making on abstracted states requires supervised fine-tuning with Direct Preference Optimization to reach baseline performance; both paths are viable for AI-assisted operational decisions.
AI.motivation: Improve real-time warehouse staffing decisions by comparing data-driven offline RL policies against human-readable, LLM-based guidance, within simulation environments to assess practicality and impact.
AI.method: 1) Offline RL with Transformer-based policies trained on detailed historical state representations in a warehouse sortation simulator; measure throughput improvements vs historical baselines. 2) LLMs operating on abstracted, human-readable state descriptions; systematically compare prompting techniques, automatic prompt optimization, and fine-tuning strategies, including Direct Preference Optimization on simulator-generated preferences.
AI.result: Offline RL achieved a 2.4% throughput improvement over historical baselines in learned simulators. LLM-based approaches, with supervised fine-tuning and Direct Preference Optimization on simulator-generated preferences, matched or slightly exceeded historical baselines in a hand-crafted simulator; prompting alone was insufficient.
AI.conclusion: Both approaches offer viable paths toward AI-assisted operational decision-making: offline RL excels with task-specific architectures and precise control, while LLMs enable human-readable inputs and can incorporate manager preferences via iterative feedback.

title: Calibri: Enhancing Diffusion Transformers via Parameter-Efficient Calibration
comment: Accepted to CVRP 2026, Project page: https://v-gen-ai.github.io/Calibri-page/
summary: In this paper, we uncover the hidden potential of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) to significantly enhance generative tasks. Through an in-depth analysis of the denoising process, we demonstrate that introducing a single learned scaling parameter can significantly improve the performance of DiT blocks. Building on this insight, we propose Calibri, a parameter-efficient approach that optimally calibrates DiT components to elevate generative quality. Calibri frames DiT calibration as a black-box reward optimization problem, which is efficiently solved using an evolutionary algorithm and modifies just ~100 parameters. Experimental results reveal that despite its lightweight design, Calibri consistently improves performance across various text-to-image models. Notably, Calibri also reduces the inference steps required for image generation, all while maintaining high-quality outputs.

AI.tldr: Calibri: a lightweight evolutionary-calibrated tuning of Diffusion Transformers that improves generative quality with ~100 parameter changes and fewer inference steps.
AI.motivation: To unlock latent potential in DiTs and boost generative performance without full fine-tuning or large compute.
AI.method: Introduce a single learned scaling parameter per DiT block; formulate calibration as black-box reward optimization and solve with an evolutionary algorithm affecting ~100 parameters.
AI.result: Empirical gains across multiple text-to-image models; improved image quality and reduced inference steps under the same or lower computation.
AI.conclusion: Calibri provides a parameter-efficient, black-box calibration framework for DiTs that enhances generative performance with minimal parameter updates and faster generation.

title: IntentReact: Guiding Reactive Object-Centric Navigation via Topological Intent
summary: Object-goal visual navigation requires robots to reason over semantic structure and act effectively under partial observability. Recent approaches based on object-level topological maps enable long-horizon navigation without dense geometric reconstruction, but their execution remains limited by the gap between global topological guidance and local perception-driven control. In particular, local decisions are made solely from the current egocentric observation, without access to information beyond the robot's field of view. As a result, the robot may persist along its current heading even when initially oriented away from the goal, moving toward directions that do not decrease the global topological distance. In this work, we propose IntentReact, an intent-conditioned object-centric navigation framework that introduces a compact interface between global topological planning and reactive object-centric control. Our approach encodes global topological guidance as a low-dimensional directional signal, termed intent, which conditions a learned waypoint prediction policy to bias navigation toward topologically consistent progression. This design enables the robot to promptly reorient when local observations are misleading, guiding motion toward directions that decrease global topological distance while preserving the reactivity and robustness of object-centric control. We evaluate the proposed framework through extensive experiments, demonstrating improved navigation success and execution quality compared to prior object-centric navigation methods.

AI.tldr: IntentReact integrates a compact top-down intent signal with object-centric navigation to bridge global topological planning and local reactive control, enabling reorientation under partial observability and improving success and execution quality.
AI.motivation: The gap between global topological guidance and local perception-driven control causes suboptimal decisions when the robot cannot see the whole scene and may keep moving away from the goal.
AI.method: Introduce an intent-conditioned navigation framework where global guidance is represented as a low-dimensional directional intent that biases a learned waypoint predictor; this interface allows reactive control to align with global progression while remaining robust to local perception.
AI.result: Experiments show improved navigation success and execution quality compared to prior object-centric navigation methods, demonstrating effective reorientation and topologically consistent movement.
AI.conclusion: A compact intent interface effectively couples global topological planning with object-centric control, enabling robust, long-horizon visual navigation under partial observability.

title: A Public Theory of Distillation Resistance via Constraint-Coupled Reasoning Architectures
summary: Knowledge distillation, model extraction, and behavior transfer have become central concerns in frontier AI. The main risk is not merely copying, but the possibility that useful capability can be transferred more cheaply than the governance structure that originally accompanied it. This paper presents a public, trade-secret-safe theoretical framework for reducing that asymmetry at the architectural level. The core claim is that distillation becomes less valuable as a shortcut when high-level capability is coupled to internal stability constraints that shape state transitions over time. To formalize this idea, the paper introduces a constraint-coupled reasoning framework with four elements: bounded transition burden, path-load accumulation, dynamically evolving feasible regions, and a capability-stability coupling condition. The paper is intentionally public-safe: it omits proprietary implementation details, training recipes, thresholds, hidden-state instrumentation, deployment procedures, and confidential system design choices. The contribution is therefore theoretical rather than operational. It offers a falsifiable architectural thesis, a clear threat model, and a set of experimentally testable hypotheses for future work on distillation resistance, alignment, and model governance.

AI.tldr: Proposes a constraint-coupled reasoning framework to reduce distillation risk by coupling high-level capability to internal stability constraints, in a public-safe theoretical study; identifies four architectural elements and offers testable hypotheses for distillation resistance, alignment, and governance.
AI.motivation: The risk that useful capabilities can transfer via distillation or model extraction more cheaply than governance constraints, motivating an architectural-level defense that preserves control without leaking sensitive details.
AI.method: Introduces a constraint-coupled reasoning framework consisting of four elements: bounded transition burden, path-load accumulation, dynamically evolving feasible regions, and a capability-stability coupling condition; presents a theory-only approach that omits implementation specifics to ensure public safety; formulates a falsifiable architectural thesis, threat model, and testable hypotheses.
AI.result: Articulates a theoretical framework and expected outcomes rather than empirical results; provides a basis for future experiments on distillation resistance, alignment, and governance, including falsifiable claims and hypotheses.
AI.conclusion: Public-safe, theory-driven contribution offering architectural guidance for limiting transfer of capabilities through distillation; highlights the need for empirical validation and development of governance-focused research; outlines directions for future work.

title: Once-for-All Channel Mixers (HYPERTINYPW): Generative Compression for TinyML
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures. Accepted at MLSys 2026. TinyML / on-device learning paper on hypernetwork-based compression for ECG and other 1D biosignals, with integer-only inference on commodity MCUs. Evaluated on Apnea-ECG, PTB-XL, and MIT-BIH. Camera-ready version with additional datasets, experiments, and insights will appear after May 2026
summary: Deploying neural networks on microcontrollers is constrained by kilobytes of flash and SRAM, where 1x1 pointwise (PW) mixers often dominate memory even after INT8 quantization across vision, audio, and wearable sensing. We present HYPER-TINYPW, a compression-as-generation approach that replaces most stored PW weights with generated weights: a shared micro-MLP synthesizes PW kernels once at load time from tiny per-layer codes, caches them, and executes them with standard integer operators. This preserves commodity MCU runtimes and adds only a one-off synthesis cost; steady-state latency and energy match INT8 separable CNN baselines. Enforcing a shared latent basis across layers removes cross-layer redundancy, while keeping PW1 in INT8 stabilizes early, morphology-sensitive mixing. We contribute (i) TinyML-faithful packed-byte accounting covering generator, heads/factorization, codes, kept PW1, and backbone; (ii) a unified evaluation with validation-tuned t* and bootstrap confidence intervals; and (iii) a deployability analysis covering integer-only inference and boot versus lazy synthesis. On three ECG benchmarks (Apnea-ECG, PTB-XL, MIT-BIH), HYPER-TINYPW shifts the macro-F1 versus flash Pareto frontier: at about 225 kB it matches a roughly 1.4 MB CNN while being 6.31x smaller (84.15% fewer bytes), retaining at least 95% of large-model macro-F1. Under 32-64 kB budgets it sustains balanced detection where compact baselines degrade. The mechanism applies broadly to other 1D biosignals, on-device speech, and embedded sensing tasks where per-layer redundancy dominates, indicating a wider role for compression-as-generation in resource-constrained ML systems. Beyond ECG, HYPER-TINYPW transfers to TinyML audio: on Speech Commands it reaches 96.2% test accuracy (98.2% best validation), supporting broader applicability to embedded sensing workloads where repeated linear mixers dominate memory.

AI.tldr: HYPER-TINYPW uses a shared micro-MLP to generate most pointwise (PW) weights from tiny per-layer codes, storing only a small codebook and caching synthesized PW kernels. It delivers INT8-compatible inference with similar latency to separable CNN baselines, dramatically reducing memory usage on ECG and audio tasks while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: MICROcontroller constraints: kilobytes of flash/SRAM; PW mixers dominate memory even after INT8 quantization. Need to reduce stored parameters without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: A shared latent basis across layers; per-layer codes synthesize PW kernels at load time via a tiny generator; caches generated PW kernels; executes with standard integer ops; keeps PW1 in INT8 for stability; one-off synthesis cost; evaluation uses tuned metrics t* and bootstrap CIs; deployability analysis including integer-only inference and synthesis strategy.
AI.result: On three ECG benchmarks (Apnea-ECG, PTB-XL, MIT-BIH), achieves ~225 kB memory while matching ~1.4 MB CNN performance (≈6.31x memory reduction, ~84% bytes saved) and retaining at least 95% of large-model macro-F1; under 32–64 kB budgets maintains balanced detection where compact baselines fail. Transfers to TinyML audio (Speech Commands): 96.2% test accuracy (98.2% best val). Broad applicability to other 1D biosignals, on-device speech, embedded sensing.
AI.conclusion: Compression-as-generation via PW weight synthesis enables substantial memory reduction with minimal accuracy loss, preserving MCU runtimes and suggesting broader applicability to resource-constrained ML workloads; potential generalization to other domains dominated by repeated linear mixers.

title: Dissecting Model Failures in Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Segmentation through Explainability-Driven Analysis
summary: Computed tomography image segmentation of complex abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAA) often fails because the models assign internal focus to irrelevant structures or do not focus on thin, low-contrast targets. Where the model looks is the primary training signal, and thus we propose an Explainable AI (XAI) guided encoder shaping framework. Our method computes a dense, attribution-based encoder focus map ("XAI field") from the final encoder block and uses it in two complementary ways: (i) we align the predicted probability mass to the XAI field to promote agreement between focus and output; and (ii) we route the field into a lightweight refinement pathway and a confidence prior that modulates logits at inference, suppressing distractors while preserving subtle structures. The objective terms serve only as control signals; the contribution is the integration of attribution guidance into representation and decoding. We evaluate clinically validated challenging cases curated for failure-prone scenarios. Compared to a base SAM setup, our implementation yields substantial improvements. The observed gains suggest that explicitly optimizing encoder focus via XAI guidance is a practical and effective principle for reliable segmentation in complex scenarios.
