AI.tldr: XAI-guided encoder shaping improves CT AAA segmentation by aligning encoder attention with output through a dense attribution field, yielding substantial gains over a base SAM in failure-prone cases.
AI.motivation: Abdominal aortic aneurysm CT segmentation is hindered by irrelevant internal focus and thin low-contrast targets. An interpretable signal indicating where the model should look can guide representation and decoding to improve reliability.
AI.method: Compute a dense attribution-based encoder focus map (XAI field) from the final encoder block. Use it to (i) align predicted probability mass with the field; and (ii) feed a lightweight refinement pathway and a confidence prior that modulates logits during inference. The loss terms act as control signals; the main contribution is integrating attribution guidance into the representation and decoding pipeline.
AI.result: Evaluation on clinically validated, failure-prone cases shows substantial improvements over a base SAM setup, indicating that encoder focus optimization via XAI guidance enhances segmentation reliability for complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly optimizing encoder focus with XAI guidance is a practical, effective principle for reliable segmentation in challenging CT imaging tasks.

title: UMBRELLA: Uncertainty-aware Multi-robot Reactive Coordination under Dynamic Temporal Logic Tasks
summary: Multi-robot systems can be extremely efficient for accomplishing team-wise tasks by acting concurrently and collaboratively. However, most existing methods either assume static task features or simply replan when environmental changes occur. This paper addresses the challenging problem of coordinating multi-robot systems for collaborative tasks involving dynamic and moving targets. We explicitly model the uncertainty in target motion prediction via Conformal Prediction(CP), while respecting the spatial-temporal constraints specified by Linear Temporal Logic (LTL). The proposed framework (UMBRELLA) combines the Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) over partial plans with uncertainty-aware rollouts, and introduces a CP-based metric to guide and accelerate the search. The objective is to minimize the Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) of the average makespan. For tasks released online, a receding-horizon planning scheme dynamically adjusts the assignments based on updated task specifications and motion predictions. Spatial and temporal constraints among the tasks are always ensured, and only partial synchronization is required for the collaborative tasks during online execution. Extensive large-scale simulations and hardware experiments demonstrate substantial reductions in both the average makespan and its variance by 23% and 71%, compared with static baselines.

AI.tldr: UMBRELLA uses CP-informed MCTS for online multi-robot coordination with dynamic targets under LTL constraints, optimizing CVaR of makespan with substantial gains over static baselines.
AI.motivation: Efficiently coordinating multi-robot teams to handle moving targets under uncertainty while strictly satisfying spatial-temporal constraints.
AI.method: Integrates Conformal Prediction for target-motion uncertainty, Monte Carlo Tree Search over partial plans with CP-guided rollouts, and a receding-horizon planning scheme. Objective minimizes CVaR of the average makespan; constraints from Linear Temporal Logic are maintained with only partial synchronization during online execution.
AI.result: Large-scale simulations and hardware experiments show a 23% reduction in average makespan and a 71% reduction in its variance compared with static baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware search augmented by CP metrics accelerates planning and improves robustness for online, constraint-satisfying multi-robot collaboration involving dynamic targets.

title: System-Anchored Knee Estimation for Low-Cost Context Window Selection in PDE Forecasting
summary: Autoregressive neural PDE simulators predict the evolution of physical fields one step at a time from a finite history, but low-cost context-window selection for such simulators remains an unformalized problem. Existing approaches to context-window selection in time-series forecasting include exhaustive validation, direct low-cost search, and system-theoretic memory estimation, but they are either expensive, brittle, or not directly aligned with downstream rollout performance. We formalize explicit context-window selection for fixed-window autoregressive neural PDE simulators as an independent low-cost algorithmic problem, and propose \textbf{System-Anchored Knee Estimation (SAKE)}, a two-stage method that first identifies a small structured candidate set from physically interpretable system anchors and then performs knee-aware downstream selection within it. Across all eight PDEBench families evaluated under the shared \(L\in\{1,\dots,16\}\) protocol, SAKE is the strongest overall matched-budget low-cost selector among the evaluated methods, achieving 67.8\% Exact, 91.7\% Within-1, 6.1\% mean regret@knee, and a cost ratio of 0.051 (94.9\% normalized search-cost savings).

AI.tldr: SAKE is a two-stage, low-cost context-window selector for fixed-window autoregressive neural PDE simulators, yielding strong rollout performance with substantially reduced search cost across eight PDEBench families.
AI.motivation: Formalize and solve the problem of selecting context windows for autoregressive neural PDE simulators, addressing shortcomings of exhaustive validation, low-cost search, and memory-estimation approaches.
AI.method: Two-stage SAKE: (1) build a small, interpretable candidate set using system anchors; (2) perform knee-aware downstream selection within that set to pick the window.
AI.result: Strong performance across PDEBench: 67.8% Exact, 91.7% Within-1, 6.1% mean regret@knee; cost ratio 0.051; 94.9% normalized search-cost savings, evaluated on eight PDEBench families with shared L in {1,...,16}.
AI.conclusion: SAKE offers the strongest, low-cost, matched-budget context-window selector among evaluated methods, achieving near-optimal accuracy with substantial search-cost savings.

title: GraphER: An Efficient Graph-Based Enrichment and Reranking Method for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Semantic search in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems is often insufficient for complex information needs, particularly when relevant evidence is scattered across multiple sources. Prior approaches to this problem include agentic retrieval strategies, which expand the semantic search space by generating additional queries. However, these methods do not fully leverage the organizational structure of the data and instead rely on iterative exploration, which can lead to inefficient retrieval. Another class of approaches employs knowledge graphs to model non-semantic relationships through graph edges. Although effective in capturing richer proximities, such methods incur significant maintenance costs and are often incompatible with the vector stores used in most production systems. To address these limitations, we propose GraphER, a graph-based enrichment and reranking method that captures multiple forms of proximity beyond semantic similarity. GraphER independently enriches data objects during offline indexing and performs graph-based reranking over candidate objects at query time. This design does not require a knowledge graph, allowing GraphER to integrate seamlessly with standard vector stores. In addition, GraphER is retriever-agnostic and introduces negligible latency overhead. Experiments on multiple retrieval benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: GraphER is a graph-based enrichment and reranking framework for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) that adds offline graph-based enrichment and online graph-based reranking, compatible with standard vector stores, retriever-agnostic, with low latency, improving retrieval when evidence spans multiple sources.
AI.motivation: Semantic search in RAG often fails for complex needs where evidence is scattered across sources. Agentic retrieval expands query space but is inefficient, while knowledge-graph approaches capture richer relations yet incur high maintenance costs and clash with common vector stores. A need exists for a practical method that captures multiple forms of proximity without heavy infrastructure.
AI.method: GraphER performs offline graph-based enrichment of data objects during indexing and online graph-based reranking of candidate objects at query time. It does not require an external knowledge graph, integrates with standard vector stores, and is retriever-agnostic with negligible latency overhead.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple retrieval benchmarks demonstrates the effectiveness of GraphER in improving retrieval performance across diverse data sets.
AI.conclusion: GraphER provides a practical, scalable solution that captures multiple proximity types beyond semantic similarity, supports seamless integration with existing vector stores, and incurs minimal latency while enhancing retrieval in RAG systems.

title: GoldiCLIP: The Goldilocks Approach for Balancing Explicit Supervision for Language-Image Pretraining
summary: Until recently, the success of large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) has primarily relied on billion-sample datasets, posing a significant barrier to progress. Latest works have begun to close this gap by improving supervision quality, but each addresses only a subset of the weaknesses in contrastive pretraining. We present GoldiCLIP, a framework built on a Goldilocks principle of finding the right balance of supervision signals. Our multifaceted training framework synergistically combines three key innovations: (1) a text-conditioned self-distillation method to align both text-agnostic and text-conditioned features; (2) an encoder integrated decoder with Visual Question Answering (VQA) objective that enables the encoder to generalize beyond the caption-like queries; and (3) an uncertainty-based weighting mechanism that automatically balances all heterogeneous losses. Trained on just 30 million images, 300x less data than leading methods, GoldiCLIP achieves state-of-the-art among data-efficient approaches, improving over the best comparable baseline by 2.2 points on MSCOCO retrieval, 2.0 on fine-grained retrieval, and 5.9 on question-based retrieval, while remaining competitive with billion-scale models. Project page: https://petsi.uk/goldiclip.

AI.tldr: GoldiCLIP is a data-efficient vision–language model framework that combines three synergistic innovations—text-conditioned self-distillation, an encoder-integrated decoder with a VQA objective, and an uncertainty-based loss weighting scheme—to achieve state-of-the-art results among data-efficient methods using only 30M images, outperforming strong baselines on MSCOCO retrieval, fine-grained retrieval, and question-based retrieval while remaining competitive with billion-scale models.
AI.motivation: Large-scale VLMs traditionally rely on billion-sample datasets, which hinders progress in data efficiency. This work targets the broader weaknesses of contrastive pretraining by delivering a holistic, data-efficient training framework that improves multiple loss signals simultaneously.
AI.method: (1) Text-conditioned self-distillation to align text-agnostic and text-conditioned features; (2) An encoder-integrated decoder with a VQA objective to extend the encoder’s capability beyond caption-like queries; (3) An uncertainty-based weighting mechanism to automatically balance heterogeneous losses. Training data: ~30M images.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among data-efficient approaches, with +2.2 points on MSCOCO retrieval, +2.0 on fine-grained retrieval, and +5.9 on question-based retrieval, while remaining competitive with billion-scale models.
AI.conclusion: A Goldilocks principle–driven, multifaceted training framework can substantially close the gap between data-efficient and billion-scale VLMs by balanced supervision signals, enabling strong performance with significantly less data.

title: System Design for Maintaining Internal State Consistency in Long-Horizon Robotic Tabletop Games
summary: Long-horizon tabletop games pose a distinct systems challenge for robotics: small perceptual or execution errors can invalidate accumulated task state, propagate across decision-making modules, and ultimately derail interaction. This paper studies how to maintain internal state consistency in turn-based, multi-human robotic tabletop games through deliberate system design rather than isolated component improvement. Using Mahjong as a representative long-horizon setting, we present an integrated architecture that explicitly maintains perceptual, execution, and interaction state, partitions high-level semantic reasoning from time-critical perception and control, and incorporates verified action primitives with tactile-triggered recovery to prevent premature state corruption. We further introduce interaction-level monitoring mechanisms to detect turn violations and hidden-information breaches that threaten execution assumptions. Beyond demonstrating complete-game operation, we provide an empirical characterization of failure modes, recovery effectiveness, cross-module error propagation, and hardware-algorithm trade-offs observed during deployment. Our results show that explicit partitioning, monitored state transitions, and recovery mechanisms are critical for sustaining executable consistency over extended play, whereas monolithic or unverified pipelines lead to measurable degradation in end-to-end reliability. The proposed system serves as an empirical platform for studying system-level design principles in long-horizon, turn-based interaction.

AI.tldr: An integrated robotics system for long-horizon, turn-based tabletop games that maintains explicit state and uses verification and recovery to prevent error propagation, demonstrated on Mahjong.
AI.motivation: To address error accumulation and state drift in long-horizon robotic interactions by focusing on system-level design rather than component-level improvements.
AI.method: Developed an architecture that explicitly maintains perceptual, execution, and interaction state; separates high-level semantic reasoning from time-critical perception/control; uses verified action primitives with tactile-triggered recovery; adds interaction-level monitoring to detect turn violations and hidden-information leaks; empirical deployment in Mahjong; analyze failure modes and cross-module propagation; evaluate hardware-algorithm trade-offs.
AI.result: Achieved complete-game operation; demonstrated sustained executable consistency; observed that explicit partitioning and recovery improve reliability; monolithic pipelines degrade end-to-end reliability.
AI.conclusion: System-level design principles—explicit state partitioning, monitored state transitions, and recovery mechanisms—are essential for long-horizon turn-based interactions; the architecture provides an empirical platform for studying these principles.

title: From Stateless to Situated: Building a Psychological World for LLM-Based Emotional Support
summary: In psychological support and emotional companionship scenarios, the core limitation of large language models (LLMs) lies not merely in response quality, but in their reliance on local next-token prediction, which prevents them from maintaining the temporal continuity, stage awareness, and user consent boundaries required for multi-turn intervention. This stateless characteristic makes systems prone to premature advancement, stage misalignment, and boundary violations in continuous dialogue. To address this problem, we argue that the key challenge in process-oriented emotional support is not simply generating natural language, but constructing a sustainably updatable external situational structure for the model. We therefore propose LEKIA 2.0, a situated LLM architecture that separates the cognitive layer from the executive layer, thereby decoupling situational modeling from intervention execution. This design enables the system to maintain stable representations of the user's situation and consent boundaries throughout ongoing interaction. To evaluate this process-control capability, we further introduce a Static-to-Dynamic online evaluation protocol for multi-turn interaction. LEKIA achieved an average absolute improvement of approximately 31% over prompt-only baselines in deep intervention loop completion. The results suggest that an external situational structure is a key enabling condition for building stable, controllable, and situated emotional support systems.

AI.tldr: LEKIA 2.0 presents a situated LLM architecture that decouples cognitive modeling from intervention execution via an external situational structure, improving multi-turn emotional support by ~31% over prompt-only baselines; introduces a Static-to-Dynamic online evaluation protocol for process control.
AI.motivation: LLMs rely on local next-token prediction and lack persistent state, stage awareness, and consent boundary handling in multi-turn psychological support, causing continuity and boundary violations.
AI.method: Propose LEKIA 2.0 architecture that separates cognitive and executive layers, maintaining a stable external situational model for user state and consent, and introduce a Static-to-Dynamic online evaluation protocol for multi-turn interaction.
AI.result: LEKIA achieved an average absolute improvement of ~31% over prompt-only baselines in deep intervention loop completion, indicating the external situational structure effectively enables stable, controllable, and situated emotional support.
AI.conclusion: An external situational structure is a key enabling condition for building stable, controllable, and situated emotional support systems, by decoupling situational modeling from intervention execution.

title: CVA: Context-aware Video-text Alignment for Video Temporal Grounding
summary: We propose Context-aware Video-text Alignment (CVA), a novel framework to address a significant challenge in video temporal grounding: achieving temporally sensitive video-text alignment that remains robust to irrelevant background context. Our framework is built on three key components. First, we propose Query-aware Context Diversification (QCD), a new data augmentation strategy that ensures only semantically unrelated content is mixed in. It builds a video-text similarity-based pool of replacement clips to simulate diverse contexts while preventing the ``false negative" caused by query-agnostic mixing. Second, we introduce the Context-invariant Boundary Discrimination (CBD) loss, a contrastive loss that enforces semantic consistency at challenging temporal boundaries, making their representations robust to contextual shifts and hard negatives. Third, we introduce the Context-enhanced Transformer Encoder (CTE), a hierarchical architecture that combines windowed self-attention and bidirectional cross-attention with learnable queries to capture multi-scale temporal context. Through the synergy of these data-centric and architectural enhancements, CVA achieves state-of-the-art performance on major VTG benchmarks, including QVHighlights and Charades-STA. Notably, our method achieves a significant improvement of approximately 5 points in Recall@1 (R1) scores over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its effectiveness in mitigating false negatives.

AI.tldr: Context-aware Video-text Alignment (CVA) introduces three components—Query-aware Context Diversification (QCD), Context-invariant Boundary Discrimination (CBD) loss, and Context-enhanced Transformer Encoder (CTE)—to achieve temporally sensitive yet robust video-text alignment for video temporal grounding (VTG), reporting state-of-the-art on QVHighlights and Charades-STA with ~5 point R1 gains.
AI.motivation: The VTG problem requires precise temporal alignment between video segments and natural language queries while remaining robust to irrelevant background content. Existing methods struggle with false negatives and context shifts; there is a need for data-centric augmentation and architectural mechanisms that preserve semantic alignment under contextual variation.
AI.method: 1) QCD: a data augmentation strategy that builds a video-text similarity-based pool of replacement clips, enabling mixing of only semantically unrelated content to simulate diverse contexts while avoiding false negatives from query-agnostic mixing. 2) CBD loss: a contrastive objective enforcing semantic consistency at hard temporal boundaries, making representations robust to contextual shifts and hard negatives. 3) CTE: a hierarchical transformer encoder with windowed self-attention and bidirectional cross-attention, augmented with learnable queries to capture multi-scale temporal context.
AI.result: CVA achieves state-of-the-art performance on VTG benchmarks, notably QVHighlights and Charades-STA, with a significant improvement of roughly 5 points in Recall@1 (R1) over prior methods, indicating stronger temporal-text alignment and reduced false negatives.
AI.conclusion: The combination of data-centric augmentation (QCD) and architectural innovations (CBD loss and CTE) yields robust, temporally sensitive video-text grounding, elevating VTG performance and suggesting good generalization to related tasks; future work could further explore augmentation strategies and cross-dataset robustness.

title: Attention-based Pin Site Image Classification in Orthopaedic Patients with External Fixators
summary: Pin sites represent the interface where a metal pin or wire from the external environment passes through the skin into the internal environment of the limb. These pins or wires connect an external fixator to the bone to stabilize the bone segments in a patient with trauma or deformity. Because these pin sites represent an opportunity for external skin flora to enter the internal environment of the limb, infections of the pin site are common. These pin site infections are painful, annoying, and cause increased morbidity to the patients. Improving the identification and management of pin site infections would greatly enhance the patient experience when external fixators are used. For this, this paper collects and produces a dataset on pin sites wound infections and proposes a deep learning (DL) method to classify pin sites images based on their appearance: Group A displayed signs of inflammation or infection, while Group B showed no evident complications. Unlike studies that primarily focus on open wounds, our research includes potential interventions at the metal pin/skin interface. Our attention-based deep learning model addresses this complexity by emphasizing relevant regions and minimizing distractions from the pins. Moreover, we introduce an Efficient Redundant Reconstruction Convolution (ERRC) method to enhance the richness of feature maps while reducing the number of parameters. Our model outperforms baseline methods with an AUC of 0.975 and an F1-score of 0.927, requiring only 5.77 M parameters. These results highlight the potential of DL in differentiating pin sites only based on visual signs of infection, aligning with healthcare professional assessments, while further validation with more data remains essential.

AI.tldr: A DL-based method with attention and ERRC for pin-site infection classification; achieves high AUC (0.975) and F1 (0.927) with 5.77M params on a new dataset distinguishing inflamed/infected vs normal pin-site images; potential clinical utility but needs more data.
AI.motivation: Pin site infections at external fixator interfaces are common and increase patient morbidity; enabling accurate, image-based identification could improve management and outcomes.
AI.method: Propose an attention-based deep learning model that focuses on relevant regions around the pin–skin interface and introduces Efficient Redundant Reconstruction Convolution (ERRC) to enrich feature maps while keeping parameter count low. Dataset of pin-site images labeled Group A (infection/inflammation) and Group B (no complications).
AI.result: Model outperforms baseline methods with AUC 0.975 and F1 0.927, using only 5.77M parameters, highlighting effective differentiation of pin-site infections from images inspired by clinical assessment; provides a route for image-based screening.
AI.conclusion: DL approach shows promise for noninvasive pin-site infection classification from images, consistent with clinician judgment, but requires validation with larger datasets and diverse populations.

title: MMaDA-VLA: Large Diffusion Vision-Language-Action Model with Unified Multi-Modal Instruction and Generation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models aim to control robots for manipulation from visual observations and natural-language instructions. However, existing hierarchical and autoregressive paradigms often introduce architectural overhead, suffer from temporal inconsistency and long-horizon error accumulation, and lack a mechanism to capture environment dynamics without extra modules. To this end, we present MMaDA-VLA, a fully native pre-trained large diffusion VLA model that unifies multi-modal understanding and generation in a single framework. Our key idea is a native discrete diffusion formulation that embeds language, images, and continuous robot controls into one discrete token space and trains a single backbone with masked token denoising to jointly generate a future goal observation and an action chunk in parallel. Iterative denoising enables global, order-free refinement, improving long-horizon consistency while grounding actions in predicted future visual outcomes without auxiliary world models. Experiments across simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks show state-of-the-art performance, achieving 98.0% average success on LIBERO and 4.78 average length on CALVIN.

AI.tldr: A unified native discrete diffusion VLA model (MMaDA-VLA) that merges language, images, and robot controls in a single discrete space to generate future observations and actions in parallel, improving long-horizon consistency without extra world models.
AI.motivation: Address architectural overhead of hierarchical/autoregressive VLA models, temporal inconsistency, error accumulation over long horizons, and lack of a dynamics-grounded mechanism without extra modules.
AI.method: Proposes native discrete diffusion formulation embedding language, vision, and controls into one token space; trains a single backbone with masked token denoising to jointly generate a future goal observation and an action chunk; performs iterative denoising for global, order-free refinement; no auxiliary world models.
AI.result: Empirical results on simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks show state-of-the-art performance, achieving 98.0% average success on LIBERO and 4.78 average length on CALVIN.
AI.conclusion: A unified VLA framework that improves long-horizon consistency and grounding of actions in predicted future visuals by leveraging a single diffusion backbone in discrete space, achieving strong performance without additional modules.

title: Mechanistically Interpreting Compression in Vision-Language Models
summary: Compressed vision-language models (VLMs) are widely used to reduce memory and compute costs, making them a suitable choice for real-world deployment. However, compressing these models raises concerns about whether internal computations and safety behaviors are preserved. In this work, we use causal circuit analysis and crosscoder-based feature comparisons to examine how pruning and quantization fundamentally change the internals across representative VLMs. We observe that pruning generally keeps circuit structure intact but rotates and attenuates internal features, while quantization modifies the circuits at a higher level yet leaves the surviving features better aligned. Leveraging this insight, we also introduce VLMSafe-420, a novel benchmark that pairs harmful inputs with matched benign counterfactuals across various safety categories. Our findings show that pruning causes a sharp drop in genuine refusal behavior, suggesting that the choice of compression has safety implications.

AI.tldr: Pruning vs quantization affect VLM internals differently; pruning harms safety refusals, while quantization preserves feature alignment; introduces VLMSafe-420 benchmark for safety evaluation in compressed VLMs.
AI.motivation: Compressing vision-language models reduces memory and compute but may alter internal computations and safety behaviors. Understanding how pruning and quantization reshape network circuitry is essential for safe deployment.
AI.method: Apply causal circuit analysis and crosscoder-based feature comparisons to assess internal changes from pruning vs quantization across representative VLMs; introduce VLMSafe-420 benchmark pairing harmful inputs with matched benign counterfactuals across safety categories.
AI.result: Pruning tends to preserve circuit structure but rotates/attenuates internal features; quantization modifies circuits at a higher level yet leaves surviving features better aligned; pruning causes a sharp decline in genuine refusal behavior, indicating safety implications of compression.
AI.conclusion: Compression strategy (pruning vs quantization) materially affects safety behavior of VLMs. The proposed analysis framework and VLMSafe-420 benchmark provide tools to evaluate and mitigate safety risks in compressed models.

title: A Systematic Empirical Study of Grokking: Depth, Architecture, Activation, and Regularization
summary: Grokking the delayed transition from memorization to generalization in neural networks remains poorly understood, in part because prior empirical studies confound the roles of architecture, optimization, and regularization. We present a controlled study that systematically disentangles these factors on modular addition (mod 97), with matched and carefully tuned training regimes across models. Our central finding is that grokking dynamics are not primarily determined by architecture, but by interactions between optimization stability and regularization. Specifically, we show: (1) \textbf{depth has a non-monotonic effect}, with depth-4 MLPs consistently failing to grok while depth-8 residual networks recover generalization, demonstrating that depth requires architectural stabilization; (2) \textbf{the apparent gap between Transformers and MLPs largely disappears} (1.11$\times$ delay) under matched hyperparameters, indicating that previously reported differences are largely due to optimizer and regularization confounds; (3) \textbf{activation function effects are regime-dependent}, with GELU up to 4.3$\times$ faster than ReLU only when regularization permits memorization; and (4) \textbf{weight decay is the dominant control parameter}, exhibiting a narrow ``Goldilocks'' regime in which grokking occurs, while too little or too much prevents generalization. Across 3--5 seeds per configuration, these results provide a unified empirical account of grokking as an interaction-driven phenomenon. Our findings challenge architecture-centric interpretations and clarify how optimization and regularization jointly govern delayed generalization.

AI.tldr: Grokking in neural networks is driven by the interaction of optimization stability and regularization, not architecture alone; depth has non-monotonic effects, Transformer-MLP gaps shrink under matched hyperparameters, activation regimes depend on regularization, and weight decay controls generalization delay within a narrow window.
AI.motivation: To disentangle architecture, optimization, and regularization in grokking and determine what governs delayed generalization.
AI.method: Controlled, matched-training regime experiments across architectures (e.g., depth-4 and depth-8 MLPs, residual networks, Transformers) on modular addition (mod 97), with multiple seeds to assess variability.
AI.result: Depth shows non-monotonic effects (depth-4 MLPs fail to grok; depth-8 residuals grok). The Transformer-MLP performance gap largely disappears under matched hyperparameters. Activation effects are regime-dependent (GELU faster than ReLU only when memorization is allowed). Weight decay emerges as the dominant control parameter, with a narrow Goldilocks window enabling grokking.
AI.conclusion: Grokking is an interaction-driven phenomenon between optimization and regularization. Architecture alone does not explain delayed generalization; previous architecture-centric interpretations are challenged and parameter choices crucially shape outcomes.

title: Generative Adversarial Perturbations with Cross-paradigm Transferability on Localized Crowd Counting
summary: State-of-the-art crowd counting and localization are primarily modeled using two paradigms: density maps and point regression. Given the field's security ramifications, there is active interest in model robustness against adversarial attacks. Recent studies have demonstrated transferability across density-map-based approaches via adversarial patches, but cross-paradigm attacks (i.e., across both density map-based models and point regression-based models) remain unexplored. We introduce a novel adversarial framework that compromises both density map and point regression architectural paradigms through a comprehensive multi-task loss optimization. For point-regression models, we employ scene-density-specific high-confidence logit suppression; for density-map approaches, we use peak-targeted density map suppression. Both are combined with model-agnostic perceptual constraints to ensure that perturbations are effective and imperceptible to the human eye. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our attack, achieving on average a 7X increase in Mean Absolute Error compared to clean images while maintaining competitive visual quality, and successfully transferring across seven state-of-the-art crowd models with transfer ratios ranging from 0.55 to 1.69. Our approach strikes a balance between attack effectiveness and imperceptibility compared to state-of-the-art transferable attack strategies. The source code is available at https://github.com/simurgh7/CrowdGen

AI.tldr: Cross-paradigm adversarial attack on crowd counting models that compromises both density-map and point-regression architectures via a multi-task loss, achieving 7x MAE increase and transferable across seven models.
AI.motivation: Address robustness gaps in crowd counting against adversarial perturbations and the lack of cross-paradigm attack studies.
AI.method: Joint multi-task optimization: for point-regression models apply scene-density-specific high-confidence logit suppression; for density-map models apply peak-targeted density map suppression; incorporate model-agnostic perceptual constraints; enable cross-model transfer.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness: average 7x MAE increase over clean images; transfer across seven state-of-the-art crowd models with transfer ratios 0.55–1.69; comparable visual perturbations; code released.
AI.conclusion: Highlights vulnerability of crowd counting systems to cross-paradigm adversarial attacks and motivates development of robust defenses; achieves a balance between attack strength and imperceptibility.

title: Visualizing Impedance Control in Augmented Reality for Teleoperation: Design and User Evaluation
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, submitted to IEEE RO-MAN 2026
summary: Teleoperation for contact-rich manipulation remains challenging, especially when using low-cost, motion-only interfaces that provide no haptic feedback. Virtual reality controllers enable intuitive motion control but do not allow operators to directly perceive or regulate contact forces, limiting task performance. To address this, we propose an augmented reality (AR) visualization of the impedance controller's target pose and its displacement from each robot end effector. This visualization conveys the forces generated by the controller, providing operators with intuitive, real-time feedback without expensive haptic hardware. We evaluate the design in a dual-arm manipulation study with 17 participants who repeatedly reposition a box with and without the AR visualization. Results show that AR visualization reduces completion time by 24% for force-critical lifting tasks, with no significant effect on sliding tasks where precise force control is less critical. These findings indicate that making the impedance target visible through AR is a viable approach to improve human-robot interaction for contact-rich teleoperation.

AI.tldr: AR visualization of the impedance controller's target pose improves performance in contact-rich teleoperation: 24% faster completion for force-critical lifting tasks; no significant effect on sliding tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of haptic feedback in low-cost, motion-only teleoperation interfaces. By making impedance targets visible via AR, operators can perceive and regulate contact forces in real time without expensive haptic hardware.
AI.method: Introduce AR overlay that visualizes the impedance controller's target pose and the displacement to each robot end effector. Evaluate in a dual-arm manipulation study (n=17) where participants repeatedly reposition a box with and without AR visualization, comparing performance on force-critical lifting vs sliding tasks.
AI.result: AR visualization reduced task completion time by 24% for force-critical lifting tasks; no significant effect on sliding tasks where precise force control is less critical.
AI.conclusion: Visible impedance targets via AR is a viable approach to enhance human–robot interaction in contact-rich teleoperation without relying on tactile feedback hardware.

title: MP-MoE: Matrix Profile-Guided Mixture of Experts for Precipitation Forecasting
summary: Precipitation forecasting remains a persistent challenge in tropical regions like Vietnam, where complex topography and convective instability often limit the accuracy of Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) models. While data-driven post-processing is widely used to mitigate these biases, most existing frameworks rely on point-wise objective functions, which suffer from the ``double penalty'' effect under minor temporal misalignments. In this work, we propose the Matrix Profile-guided Mixture of Experts (MP-MoE), a framework that integrates conventional intensity loss with a structural-aware Matrix Profile objective. By leveraging subsequence-level similarity rather than point-wise errors, the proposed loss facilitates more reliable expert selection and mitigates excessive penalization caused by phase shifts. We evaluate MP-MoE on rainfall datasets from two major river basins in Vietnam across multiple horizons, including 1-hour intensity and accumulated rainfall over 12, 24, and 48 hours. Experimental results demonstrate that MP-MoE outperforms raw NWP and baseline learning methods in terms of Mean Critical Success Index (CSI-M) for heavy rainfall events, while significantly reducing Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) values. These findings highlight the framework's efficacy in capturing peak rainfall intensities and preserving the morphological integrity of storm events.

AI.tldr: Matrix Profile-guided Mixture of Experts (MP-MoE) uses a structural loss based on subsequence similarity to mitigate phase shift penalties in post-processing NWP for tropical rainfall, improving heavy rainfall detection (CSI-M) and reducing temporal misalignment (DTW) across 1h to 48h horizons in Vietnam basins.
AI.motivation: Address the double penalty of point-wise losses in data-driven post-processing of NWP under convective tropical rainfall, where misalignment between predicted and observed rainfall peaks degrades forecast quality and event morphology.
AI.method: Integrate conventional intensity loss with a Matrix Profile-guided objective within a Mixture of Experts framework. Use subsequence-level similarity to guide expert selection, reducing penalties from phase shifts. Evaluate on 1h intensity and 12/24/48h accumulations across two Vietnamese basins.
AI.result: MP-MoE outperforms raw NWP and baseline methods on heavy rainfall CSI-M and significantly lowers DTW, indicating better detection of heavy events and preservation of storm morphology.
AI.conclusion: Structural-aware, subsequence-based loss within an MoE framework effectively mitigates temporal misalignment and enhances rainfall post-processing in tropical regions, showing promise for improving extreme rainfall predictions.

title: Optimal High-Probability Regret for Online Convex Optimization with Two-Point Bandit Feedback
summary:
We consider the problem of Online Convex Optimization (OCO) with two-point bandit feedback in an adversarial environment.
  In this setting, a player attempts to minimize a sequence of adversarially generated convex loss functions, while only observing the value of each function at two points.
  While it is well-known that two-point feedback allows for gradient estimation, achieving tight high-probability regret bounds for strongly convex functions still remained open as highlighted by \citet{agarwal2010optimal}. The primary challenge lies in the heavy-tailed nature of bandit gradient estimators, which makes standard concentration analysis difficult.
  In this paper, we resolve this open challenge by providing the first high-probability regret bound of $O(d(\log T + \log(1/δ))/μ)$ for $μ$-strongly convex losses. Our result is minimax optimal with respect to both the time horizon $T$ and the dimension $d$.

AI.tldr: First high-probability regret bound for two-point bandit OCO with μ-strongly convex losses; bound scales as O(d(log T + log(1/δ))/μ), minimax optimal.
AI.motivation: Extend two-point bandit feedback in adversarial OCO to strongly convex case with tight high-probability regret, addressing the open challenge due to heavy-tailed gradient estimators per Agarwal 2010.
AI.method: Developed a gradient-estimation scheme from two-point feedback and a non-asymptotic analysis that handles heavy-tailed estimators; derives high-probability regret bound with explicit dependence on d, T, δ, μ.
AI.result: Achieves a high-probability regret bound of O(d(log T + log(1/δ))/μ) for μ-strongly convex losses; bound is minimax optimal in T and d.
AI.conclusion: Resolves the open question by providing tight high-probability guarantees for two-point bandit OCO under strong convexity; establishes minimax optimality.

title: DCARL: A Divide-and-Conquer Framework for Autoregressive Long-Trajectory Video Generation
summary: Long-trajectory video generation is a crucial yet challenging task for world modeling primarily due to the limited scalability of existing video diffusion models (VDMs). Autoregressive models, while offering infinite rollout, suffer from visual drift and poor controllability. To address these issues, we propose DCARL, a novel divide-and-conquer, autoregressive framework that effectively combines the structural stability of the divide-and-conquer scheme with the high-fidelity generation of VDMs. Our approach first employs a dedicated Keyframe Generator trained without temporal compression to establish long-range, globally consistent structural anchors. Subsequently, an Interpolation Generator synthesizes the dense frames in an autoregressive manner with overlapping segments, utilizing the keyframes for global context and a single clean preceding frame for local coherence. Trained on a large-scale internet long trajectory video dataset, our method achieves superior performance in both visual quality (lower FID and FVD) and camera adherence (lower ATE and ARE) compared to state-of-the-art autoregressive and divide-and-conquer baselines, demonstrating stable and high-fidelity generation for long trajectory videos up to 32 seconds in length.

AI.tldr: DCARL divides long video generation into a two-stage autoregressive framework: a keyframe generator establishes global structure without temporal compression, followed by an interpolation generator that densely fills frames in autoregressive, overlapping segments using keyframes and a single preceding frame for local coherence.
AI.motivation: Long-trajectory video generation is hindered by the limited scalability of video diffusion models and the drift/controllability issues of autoregressive approaches. A method that preserves global structural stability while maintaining high-fidelity frame synthesis is needed.
AI.method: DCARL proposes a divide-and-conquer autoregressive framework with two components. (1) Keyframe Generator (KFG) trained without temporal compression to produce long-range, globally consistent structural anchors. (2) Interpolation Generator (IG) synthesizes dense frames in autoregressive, overlapping segments, leveraging keyframes for global context and a single clean preceding frame for local coherence. The model is trained on a large-scale internet long trajectory video dataset.
AI.result: DCARL achieves superior visual quality and camera adherence compared with state-of-the-art autoregressive and divide-and-conquer baselines, as evidenced by lower FID and FVD (visual quality) and lower ATE and ARE (camera adherence). It demonstrates stable, high-fidelity generation for long trajectory videos up to 32 seconds.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid divide-and-conquer autoregressive framework can combine the stability of structural anchors with the fidelity of diffusion-based generation, enabling scalable, high-quality long-trajectory video generation up to 32 seconds.

title: Temporally Decoupled Diffusion Planning for Autonomous Driving
summary: Motion planning in dynamic urban environments requires balancing immediate safety with long-term goals. While diffusion models effectively capture multi-modal decision-making, existing approaches treat trajectories as monolithic entities, overlooking heterogeneous temporal dependencies where near-term plans are constrained by instantaneous dynamics and far-term plans by navigational goals. To address this, we propose Temporally Decoupled Diffusion Model (TDDM), which reformulates trajectory generation via a noise-as-mask paradigm. By partitioning trajectories into segments with independent noise levels, we implicitly treat high noise as information voids and weak noise as contextual cues. This compels the model to reconstruct corrupted near-term states by leveraging internal correlations with better-preserved temporal contexts. Architecturally, we introduce a Temporally Decoupled Adaptive Layer Normalization (TD-AdaLN) to inject segment-specific timesteps. During inference, our Asymmetric Temporal Classifier-Free Guidance utilizes weakly noised far-term priors to guide immediate path generation. Evaluations on the nuPlan benchmark show TDDM approaches or exceeds state-of-the-art baselines, particularly excelling in the challenging Test14-hard subset.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based trajectory planner called TDDM partitions trajectories into temporally independent segments with noise as a mask, enabling near-term state reconstruction via preserved context; it uses TD-AdaLN for segment-aware timesteps and asymmetric guidance for inference, achieving competitive results on nuPlan, especially in hard cases.
AI.motivation: Dynamic urban environments require balancing immediate safety with long-term goals. Existing diffusion-based planners treat trajectories monolithically and fail to exploit heterogeneous temporal dependencies. A temporal decoupling with noise-based masking can encourage the model to recover near-term states by leveraging internal temporal correlations.
AI.method: Introduce Temporally Decoupled Diffusion Model (TDDM) that partitions trajectories into segments with independent noise levels. High noise corresponds to information voids; low noise provides contextual cues. Train to reconstruct corrupted near-term states using internal temporal correlations. Add Temporally Decoupled Adaptive Layer Normalization (TD-AdaLN) to inject segment-specific timesteps. During inference, apply Asymmetric Temporal Classifier-Free Guidance, using weakly noised far-term priors to guide immediate path generation.
AI.result: On the nuPlan benchmark, TDDM approaches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, with pronounced gains on the challenging Test14-hard subset.
AI.conclusion: Temporal decoupling via noise-as-mask and segment-aware normalization improves diffusion-based trajectory generation in dynamic environments. The combination of TD-AdaLN and asymmetric guidance enhances near-term reconstruction while preserving long-horizon goals, showing strong empirical performance and potential applicability to other sequential planning problems.

title: Sparse Visual Thought Circuits in Vision-Language Models
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) improve interpretability in multimodal models, but it remains unclear whether SAE features form modular, composable units for reasoning-an assumption underlying many intervention-based steering methods. We test this modularity hypothesis and find it often fails: intervening on a task-selective feature set can modestly improve reasoning accuracy, while intervening on the union of two such sets reliably induces output drift (large unintended changes in predictions) and degrades accuracy, even under norm-matched perturbations. This non modular circuit interference is consistent with shared internal pathways where feature unions amplify activation shifts. We develop a reproducible causal pipeline to localize and test these sparse visual thought circuits in Qwen3-VL-8B. On a controlled synthetic benchmark with seven task types and three difficulty levels, linear probes identify a mid decoder locus for task type information. We train SAEs at this layer, construct task-selective sets via an explicit rule, and perform inference time scaling and ablation while quantifying accuracy and drift. Our findings-validated with bootstrapped subsamples and permutation controls, and replicated across multiple VLM families and five diverse datasets clarify the boundaries of SAE feature composability and provide a rigorous diagnostic framework for more reliable VLM control.

AI.tldr: SAEs in multimodal models are not reliably modular for reasoning: intervening on single task-selective SAE features can help, but combining them causes large unintended drift and degrades performance. The authors propose a reproducible causal pipeline to localize sparse visual thought circuits and diagnose feature composability across VLMs and datasets.
AI.motivation: Intervention-based steering assumes SAE-derived features form modular, composable units for task-specific reasoning in VLMs. This modularity is uncertain and its limits are unknown, potentially undermining safety and reliability of interventions.
AI.method: 1) Identify task-type information at a mid-decoder layer via linear probes in Qwen3-VL-8B under a synthetic benchmark with seven tasks and three difficulty levels. 2) Train sparse autoencoders at this layer to create task-selective sets. 3) Build explicit rules to select sets, then perform inference-time scaling and ablation. 4) Quantify accuracy and output drift, with bootstrapping and permutation controls. 5) Replicate experiments across multiple VLM families and five diverse datasets.
AI.result: Intervening on a single task-selective SAE set yields modest accuracy gains; intervening on the union of two such sets causes substantial output drift and degraded accuracy, even with norm-matched perturbations. The drift aligns with shared internal pathways and amplification of activation shifts when combining feature sets.
AI.conclusion: SAE features in VLMs are not reliably modular or composable for scalable control. The work provides a rigorous causal diagnostic framework and a reproducible pipeline to localize and test sparse visual thought circuits, clarifying the boundaries of SAE composability and enabling more reliable VLM control.

title: Epistemic Compression: The Case for Deliberate Ignorance in High-Stakes AI
summary: Foundation models excel in stable environments, yet often fail where reliability matters most: medicine, finance, and policy. This Fidelity Paradox is not just a data problem; it is structural. In domains where rules change over time, extra model capacity amplifies noise rather than capturing signal. We introduce Epistemic Compression: the principle that robustness emerges from matching model complexity to the shelf life of the data, not from scaling parameters. Unlike classical regularization, which penalizes weights post hoc, Epistemic Compression enforces parsimony through architecture: the model structure itself is designed to reduce overfitting by making it architecturally costly to represent variance that exceeds the evidence in the data. We operationalize this with a Regime Index that separates Shifting Regime (unstable, data-poor; simplicity wins) from Stable Regime (invariant, data-rich; complexity viable). In an exploratory synthesis of 15 high-stakes domains, this index was concordant with the empirically superior modeling strategy in 86.7% of cases (13/15). High-stakes AI demands a shift from scaling for its own sake to principled parsimony.

AI.tldr: Proposes Epistemic Compression: tailor model complexity to data shelf life via architecture-based parsimony; a Regime Index maps Shifting vs Stable regimes; preliminary cross-domain evidence suggests better models in most high-stakes domains; implies scaling alone is insufficient.
AI.motivation: Address the Fidelity Paradox: foundation models underperform in domains where reliability and non-stationarity matter; formalize a principle to achieve robustness by matching complexity to data availability, rather than increasing parameters indiscriminately.
AI.method: Introduce Regime Index that classifies data regimes; define architectural mechanisms to enforce parsimony; perform exploratory synthesis across 15 high-stakes domains to compare concordance with empirically superior modeling strategies.
AI.result: In 13 of 15 domains (86.7%), the Regime Index-aligned strategy concorded with the empirically superior modeling approach.
AI.conclusion: High-stakes AI should prioritize principled parsimony over scaling; architecture-level constraints can improve robustness to regime shifts; warrants further validation and domain-specific design.

title: WAFT-Stereo: Warping-Alone Field Transforms for Stereo Matching
summary: We introduce WAFT-Stereo, a simple and effective warping-based method for stereo matching. WAFT-Stereo demonstrates that cost volumes, a common design used in many leading methods, are not necessary for strong performance and can be replaced by warping with improved efficiency. WAFT-Stereo ranks first on ETH3D, KITTI and Middlebury public benchmarks, reducing the zero-shot error by 81% on ETH3D benchmark, while being 1.8-6.7x faster than competitive methods. Code and model weights are available at https://github.com/princeton-vl/WAFT-Stereo.

AI.tldr: WAFT-Stereo is a warp-based stereo method that replaces cost volumes with warping, achieving state-of-the-art performance with higher efficiency.
AI.motivation: Cost volumes are computationally and memory-intensive; it is unclear if they are essential for strong stereo performance. A simpler, more efficient approach may yield comparable or superior results.
AI.method: A simple warping-based architecture that obviates explicit cost volumes, using differentiable warping across disparities to compare features and aggregate evidence for disparity estimation, resulting in a leaner, faster pipeline.
AI.result: The approach ranks first on ETH3D, KITTI, and Middlebury public benchmarks. It reduces the zero-shot error by 81% on ETH3D and is 1.8–6.7× faster than competitive methods.
AI.conclusion: Warping-based stereo can match or exceed the performance of cost-volume methods while offering substantial efficiency gains; the work advocates rethinking the necessity of cost volumes in stereo.

title: LILAC: Language-Conditioned Object-Centric Optical Flow for Open-Loop Trajectory Generation
summary: We address language-conditioned robotic manipulation using flow-based trajectory generation, which enables training on human and web videos of object manipulation and requires only minimal embodiment-specific data. This task is challenging, as object trajectory generation from pre-manipulation images and natural language instructions requires appropriate instruction-flow alignment. To tackle this challenge, we propose the flow-based Language Instruction-guided open-Loop ACtion generator (LILAC). This flow-based Vision-Language-Action model (VLA) generates object-centric 2D optical flow from an RGB image and a natural language instruction, and converts the flow into a 6-DoF manipulator trajectory. LILAC incorporates two key components: Semantic Alignment Loss, which strengthens language conditioning to generate instruction-aligned optical flow, and Prompt-Conditioned Cross-Modal Adapter, which aligns learned visual prompts with image and text features to provide rich cues for flow generation. Experimentally, our method outperformed existing approaches in generated flow quality across multiple benchmarks. Furthermore, in physical object manipulation experiments using free-form instructions, LILAC demonstrated a superior task success rate compared to existing methods. The project page is available at https://lilac-75srg.kinsta.page/.

AI.tldr: A flow-based language-conditioned manipulation framework (LILAC) that generates 2D optical flow from an image and instruction and converts it to a 6-DoF trajectory, using Semantic Alignment Loss and a Prompt-Conditioned Cross-Modal Adapter; achieves superior flow quality and higher task success in physical experiments compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Language-conditioned robotic manipulation is challenging due to the need to align instruction flow with object trajectories, and the availability of diverse training data (human/web videos) with minimal embodiment-specific data.
AI.method: Proposes LILAC, a flow-based Vision-Language-Action model that outputs object-centric 2D optical flow from RGB image + language instruction, then maps flow to a 6-DoF manipulator trajectory. Introduces Semantic Alignment Loss to strengthen language-conditioned flow, and a Prompt-Conditioned Cross-Modal Adapter to align visual prompts with image and text features.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in generated flow quality across multiple benchmarks. In physical object manipulation with free-form instructions, LILAC achieves higher task success rates than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective flow-based language-conditioned manipulation by coupling instruction-conditioned flow generation with cross-modal prompting, enabling robust generalization to diverse instructions and settings; a project page is provided.

title: ElephantBroker: A Knowledge-Grounded Cognitive Runtime for Trustworthy AI Agents
summary: Large Language Model based agents increasingly operate in high stakes, multi turn settings where factual grounding is critical, yet their memory systems typically rely on flat key value stores or plain vector retrieval with no mechanism to track the provenance or trustworthiness of stored knowledge. We present ElephantBroker, an open source cognitive runtime that unifies a Neo4j knowledge graph with a Qdrant vector store through the Cognee SDK to provide durable, verifiable agent memory. The system implements a complete cognitive loop (store, retrieve, score, compose, protect, learn) comprising a hybrid five source retrieval pipeline, an eleven dimension competitive scoring engine for budget constrained context assembly, a four state evidence verification model, a five stage context lifecycle with goal aware assembly and continuous compaction, a six layer cheap first guard pipeline for safety enforcement, an AI firewall providing enforceable tool call interception and multi tier safety scanning, a nine stage consolidation engine that strengthens useful patterns while decaying noise, and a numeric authority model governing multi organization identity with hierarchical access control. Architectural validation through a comprehensive test suite of over 2,200 tests spanning unit, integration, and end to end levels confirms subsystem correctness. The modular design supports three deployment tiers, five profile presets with inheritance, multi gateway isolation, and a management dashboard for human oversight, enabling configurations from lightweight memory only agents to full cognitive runtimes with enterprise grade safety and auditability.

AI.tldr: ElephantBroker offers a durable, verifiable memory for LLM agents by unifying a Neo4j knowledge graph with a Qdrant vector store via Cognee SDK, enabling a complete cognitive loop with multi-layer safety and auditability, validated by a 2,200-test suite and adaptable deployment tiers.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of provenance tracking and trustworthiness in current memory systems for high-stakes, multi-turn LLM agents, ensuring factual grounding and governance.
AI.method: Open-source cognitive runtime ElephantBroker unifies Neo4j knowledge graph with a Qdrant vector store via the Cognee SDK and implements a complete cognitive loop (store, retrieve, score, compose, protect, learn). It features a hybrid five-source retrieval pipeline, an eleven-dimension competitive scoring engine for budget-constrained context assembly, a four-state evidence verification model, a five-stage context lifecycle with goal-aware assembly and continuous compaction, a six-layer cheap-first guard pipeline, an AI firewall with enforceable tool call interception and multi-tier safety scanning, a nine-stage consolidation engine, and a numeric authority model for multi-organization identity with hierarchical access control. Architectural validation is achieved through a test suite of over 2,200 tests across unit, integration, and end-to-end levels. The design supports three deployment tiers, five profile presets with inheritance, multi-gateway isolation, and a management dashboard for human oversight, enabling configurations from memory-only agents to enterprise-grade safety and auditability.
AI.result: Demonstrates durable, verifiable agent memory; architectural validation via >2,200 tests confirms subsystem correctness; supports configurations from memory-only agents to enterprise-grade safety and auditability.
AI.conclusion: ElephantBroker delivers a comprehensive cognitive runtime with robust safety, provenance, and memory management, enabling scalable, auditable LLM agents across diverse deployment scenarios.

title: Intern-S1-Pro: Scientific Multimodal Foundation Model at Trillion Scale
summary: We introduce Intern-S1-Pro, the first one-trillion-parameter scientific multimodal foundation model. Scaling to this unprecedented size, the model delivers a comprehensive enhancement across both general and scientific domains. Beyond stronger reasoning and image-text understanding capabilities, its intelligence is augmented with advanced agent capabilities. Simultaneously, its scientific expertise has been vastly expanded to master over 100 specialized tasks across critical science fields, including chemistry, materials, life sciences, and earth sciences. Achieving this massive scale is made possible by the robust infrastructure support of XTuner and LMDeploy, which facilitates highly efficient Reinforcement Learning (RL) training at the 1-trillion parameter level while ensuring strict precision consistency between training and inference. By seamlessly integrating these advancements, Intern-S1-Pro further fortifies the fusion of general and specialized intelligence, working as a Specializable Generalist, demonstrating its position in the top tier of open-source models for general capabilities, while outperforming proprietary models in the depth of specialized scientific tasks.

AI.tldr: Intern-S1-Pro is a 1e12-parameter scientific multimodal foundation model, combining general and specialized science capabilities with advanced agent features. Built with XTuner/LMDeploy, it enables efficient RL training, achieving precision-consistent training and inference. It aims to be a top-tier open-source generalist that excels in >100 scientific tasks across chemistry, materials, life science, and earth science, outperforming proprietary models in scientific depth.
AI.motivation: Create a unified, scalable AI system capable of strong general reasoning and broad, deep scientific expertise across multiple domains, enabling high-performance tasks in both everyday and domain-specific contexts.
AI.method: Scale a multimodal transformer to one trillion parameters, equip with agent capabilities, and train using RL with infrastructure support from XTuner and LMDeploy to ensure efficiency and precision consistency between training and inference; evaluate across general and >100 specialized scientific tasks.
AI.result: Achieves strong general reasoning and image-text understanding, enhanced agent capabilities, and mastery of >100 specialized scientific tasks across chemistry, materials, life sciences, and earth sciences. Positioned as a top open-source generalist while outperforming proprietary models in scientific depth.
AI.conclusion: Intern-S1-Pro exemplifies a Specializable Generalist that fuses general and specialized intelligence, pushing open-source models toward state-of-the-art performance in both broad capabilities and deep scientific expertise.

title: NeuroVLM-Bench: Evaluation of Vision-Enabled Large Language Models for Clinical Reasoning in Neurological Disorders
comment: 53 pages, 12 figures. Manuscript submitted to the BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making journal
summary: Recent advances in multimodal large language models enable new possibilities for image-based decision support. However, their reliability and operational trade-offs in neuroimaging remain insufficiently understood. We present a comprehensive benchmarking study of vision-enabled large language models for 2D neuroimaging using curated MRI and CT datasets covering multiple sclerosis, stroke, brain tumors, other abnormalities, and normal controls. Models are required to generate multiple outputs simultaneously, including diagnosis, diagnosis subtype, imaging modality, specialized sequence, and anatomical plane. Performance is evaluated across four directions: discriminative classification with abstention, calibration, structured-output validity, and computational efficiency. A multi-phase framework ensures fair comparison while controlling for selection bias. Across twenty frontier multimodal models, the results show that technical imaging attributes such as modality and plane are nearly solved, whereas diagnostic reasoning, especially subtype prediction, remains challenging. Tumor classification emerges as the most reliable task, stroke is moderately solvable, while multiple sclerosis and rare abnormalities remain difficult. Few-shot prompting improves performance for several models but increases token usage, latency, and cost. Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5-Chat achieve the strongest overall diagnostic performance, while Gemini-2.5-Flash offers the best efficiency-performance trade-off. Among open-weight architectures, MedGemma-1.5-4B demonstrates the most promising results, as under few-shot prompting, it approaches the zero-shot performance of several proprietary models, while maintaining perfect structured output. These findings provide practical insights into performance, reliability, and efficiency trade-offs, supporting standardized evaluation of multimodal LLMs in neuroimaging.

AI.tldr: A benchmarking study evaluating vision-enabled LLMs on 2D neuroimaging tasks across MRI/CT, highlighting strong handling of imaging attributes but limited diagnostic reasoning, with tumor classification most reliable and MS/rare abnormalities hardest; few-shot prompting helps but raises cost; among models, Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5-Chat excel in overall diagnostics, Gemini-2.5-Flash offers best efficiency, and MedGemma-1.5-4B shows strong open-weight performance.
AI.motivation: Assess reliability and trade-offs of multimodal LLMs in neuroimaging, addressing gaps in cross-model, multi-output evaluation and establishing a standardized benchmarking framework for clinically relevant tasks.
AI.method: Multi-phase fair-comparison framework evaluating 20 frontier vision-enabled LLMs on curated 2D MRI/CT datasets (MS, stroke, tumors, other abnormalities, controls). Models must output multiple structured labels (diagnosis, subtype, modality, sequence, plane). Evaluations cover discriminative classification with abstention, calibration, structured-output validity, and computational efficiency, with bias-control measures.
AI.result: Imaging attributes (modality, plane) nearly solved across models; diagnostic reasoning, especially subtype prediction, remains challenging. Tumor classification is most reliable; stroke moderate; MS and rare abnormalities hard. Few-shot prompting boosts performance but increases token usage, latency, and cost. Top performers: Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5-Chat (overall diagnostics); Gemini-2.5-Flash (best efficiency). Among open-weight models, MedGemma-1.5-4B is most promising, approaching zero-shot of some proprietary models under few-shot prompting while yielding perfect structured outputs.
AI.conclusion: The work provides actionable insights into performance, reliability, and efficiency trade-offs for multimodal LLMs in neuroimaging and advocates standardized evaluation; points to avenues for improving diagnostic reasoning (esp. subtypes, rare abnormalities) and optimizing prompting strategies and model efficiency.

title: When Sensing Varies with Contexts: Context-as-Transform for Tactile Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) can be particularly susceptible to acquisition contexts with only a few labeled samples. A typical scenario is tactile sensing, where the acquisition context ({\it e.g.}, diverse devices, contact state, and interaction settings) degrades performance due to a lack of standardization. In this paper, we propose Context-as-Transform FSCIL (CaT-FSCIL) to tackle the above problem. We decompose the acquisition context into a structured low-dimensional component and a high-dimensional residual component. The former can be easily affected by tactile interaction features, which are modeled as an approximately invertible Context-as-Transform family and handled via inverse-transform canonicalization optimized with a pseudo-context consistency loss. The latter mainly arises from platform and device differences, which can be mitigated with an Uncertainty-Conditioned Prototype Calibration (UCPC) that calibrates biased prototypes and decision boundaries based on context uncertainty. Comprehensive experiments on the standard benchmarks HapTex and LMT108 have demonstrated the superiority of the proposed CaT-FSCIL.

AI.tldr: CaT-FSCIL introduces Context-as-Transform FSCIL, decomposing acquisition context into a structured low-dimensional part (modeled as an invertible transform with inverse-transform canonicalization via pseudo-context consistency loss) and a high-dimensional residual part (mitigated by Uncertainty-Conditioned Prototype Calibration). It reports superior results on tactile benchmarks HapTex and LMT108.
AI.motivation: FSCIL performance is highly sensitive to acquisition context, especially in tactile sensing where device diversity and interaction states cause distribution shifts and label scarcity.
AI.method: Decompose context into a structured low-dimensional component and a high-dimensional residual. The low-dimensional part is handled via an approximately invertible Context-as-Transform family with inverse-transform canonicalization optimized with a pseudo-context consistency loss. The high-dimensional residual, stemming from device/platform differences, is addressed with Uncertainty-Conditioned Prototype Calibration (UCPC) to adjust biased prototypes and decision boundaries based on context uncertainty.
AI.result: Experimental results on HapTex and LMT108 demonstrate the proposed method’s superiority over baselines, indicating robustness to context variations in FSCIL.
AI.conclusion: CaT-FSCIL effectively mitigates context-induced degradation in FSCIL for tactile sensing by separating and normalizing context components and calibrating prototypes under uncertainty, yielding improved performance on standard benchmarks.

title: The Order Is The Message
summary: In a controlled experiment on modular arithmetic ($p = 9973$), varying only example ordering while holding all else constant, two fixed-ordering strategies achieve 99.5\% test accuracy by epochs 487 and 659 respectively from a training set comprising 0.3\% of the input space, well below established sample complexity lower bounds for this task under IID ordering. The IID baseline achieves 0.30\% after 5{,}000 epochs from identical data. An adversarially structured ordering suppresses learning entirely. The generalizing model reliably constructs a Fourier representation whose fundamental frequency is the Fourier dual of the ordering structure, encoding information present in no individual training example, with the same fundamental emerging across all seeds tested regardless of initialization or training set composition. We discuss implications for training efficiency, the reinterpretation of grokking, and the safety risks of a channel that evades all content-level auditing.

AI.tldr: Two fixed-ordering strategies rapidly achieve near-perfect test accuracy (99.5%) in a modular arithmetic task with p=9973 from a tiny 0.3% data subset, far faster than IID learning; adversarial ordering blocks learning. The generalization uses a Fourier representation whose fundamental frequency matches the Fourier dual of the ordering, a property consistent across seeds. Implications include training efficiency, reinterpretation of grokking, and potential safety risks from covert channels evading content auditing.
AI.motivation: Examine how example ordering affects learning dynamics, sample efficiency, and generalization in a structured mathematical task, and explore the broader implications for grokking and model safety.
AI.method: Controlled experiments on a modular arithmetic task with p=9973. Compare two fixed-ordering strategies against an IID baseline while keeping data content constant. Training uses only 0.3% of the input space; measure epochs to reach 99.5% test accuracy. Introduce an adversarially structured ordering to assess learning suppression. Analyze the learned representation and report whether the model constructs a Fourier representation with a fundamental frequency tied to the ordering (Fourier dual of ordering). Test across multiple seeds to assess robustness.
AI.result: Two fixed-ordering strategies reach 99.5% test accuracy by epochs 487 and 659, using 0.3% of the input data, significantly faster than the IID baseline which attains 0.30% after 5,000 epochs. An adversarial ordering suppresses learning entirely. The generalizing model appears to construct a Fourier representation whose fundamental frequency is the Fourier dual of the ordering structure, consistent across seeds regardless of initialization or training data composition.
AI.conclusion: The findings have potential implications for training efficiency and reinterpreting grokking; they also raise safety concerns about channels that could bypass content-level auditing by encoding information in the learning dynamics and representations.

title: CORA: A Pathology Synthesis Driven Foundation Model for Coronary CT Angiography Analysis and MACE Risk Assessment
summary: Coronary artery disease, the leading cause of cardiovascular mortality worldwide, can be assessed non-invasively by coronary computed tomography angiography (CCTA). Despite progress in automated CCTA analysis using deep learning, clinical translation is constrained by the scarcity of expert-annotated datasets. Furthermore, widely adopted label-free pretraining strategies, such as masked image modeling, are intrinsically biased toward global anatomical statistics, frequently failing to capture the spatially localized pathological features of coronary plaques. Here, we introduce CORA, a 3D vision foundation model for comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment. CORA learns directly from volumetric CCTA via a pathology-centric, synthesis-driven self-supervised framework. By utilizing an anatomy-guided lesion synthesis engine, the model is explicitly trained to detect simulated vascular abnormalities, biasing representation learning toward clinically relevant disease features rather than dominant background anatomy. We trained CORA on a large-scale cohort of 12,801 unlabeled CCTA volumes and comprehensively evaluated the model across multi-center datasets from nine independent hospitals. Across diagnostic and anatomical tasks, including plaque characterization, stenosis detection, and coronary artery segmentation, CORA consistently outperformed the state-of-the-art 3D vision foundation models, achieving up to a 29\% performance gain. Crucially, by coupling the imaging encoder with a large language model, we extended CORA into a multimodal framework that significantly improved 30-day major adverse cardiac event (MACE) risk stratification. Our results establish CORA as a scalable and extensible foundation for unified anatomical assessment and cardiovascular risk prediction.

AI.tldr: CORA is introduced as a 3D vision foundation model for cardiovascular risk assessment from unlabeled CCTA, using a pathology-centric synthesis-driven self-supervised framework. It leverages an anatomy-guided lesion synthesis engine, trained on 12,801 volumes, and evaluated across nine hospitals, outperforming prior 3D foundation models by up to 29% and improving 30-day MACE risk stratification when coupled with an LLM.
AI.motivation: Clinical translation of automated CCTA analysis is limited by limited expert-annotated datasets and bias in label-free pretraining toward global anatomy, which misses localized plaque features crucial for disease assessment.
AI.method: A pathology-centric, synthesis-driven self-supervised framework with an anatomy-guided lesion synthesis engine that biases representation learning toward disease-relevant features. Trained on 12,801 unlabeled CCTA volumes. Evaluation on multi-center datasets from nine hospitals across plaque characterization, stenosis detection, and coronary artery segmentation. A multimodal extension couples the imaging encoder with a large language model to improve 30-day MACE risk stratification.
AI.result: CORA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art 3D vision foundation models across diagnostic and anatomical tasks, with up to a 29% performance gain. The multimodal CORA-LLM framework significantly improves 30-day MACE risk stratification.
AI.conclusion: CORA is a scalable, extensible foundation for unified anatomical assessment and cardiovascular risk prediction in CCTA, enabling robust, label-efficient learning and potential clinical translation.

title: Towards Generalizable Robotic Data Flywheel: High-Dimensional Factorization and Composition
summary: The lack of sufficiently diverse data, coupled with limited data efficiency, remains a major bottleneck for generalist robotic models, yet systematic strategies for collecting and curating such data are not fully explored.
