Task diversity arises from implicit factors that are sparsely distributed across multiple dimensions and are difficult to define explicitly. To address this challenge, we propose F-ACIL, a heuristic factor-aware compositional iterative learning framework that enables structured data factorization and promotes compositional generalization. F-ACIL decomposes the data distribution into structured factor spaces such as object, action, and environment. Based on the factorized formulation, we develop a factor-wise data collection and an iterative training paradigm that promotes compositional generalization over the high-dimensional factor space, leading to more effective utilization of real-world robotic demonstrations. With extensive real-world experiments, we show that F-ACIL can achieve more than 45% performance gains with 5-10$\times$ fewer demonstrations comparing to that of which without the strategy. The results suggest that structured factorization offers a practical pathway toward efficient compositional generalization in real-world robotic learning. We believe F-ACIL can inspire more systematic research on building generalizable robotic data flywheel strategies. More demonstrations can be found at: https://f-acil.github.io/

AI.tldr: F-ACIL introduces a factor-aware, compositional, iterative learning framework for robotics that factorizes data into structured spaces (object, action, environment) and uses factor-wise data collection with iterative training to enhance compositional generalization, achieving >45% gains with 5–10x fewer demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Diversity-limited datasets and low data efficiency hinder generalist robotic models. Task diversity arises from implicit, multi-dimensional factors that are hard to specify directly.
AI.method: Decompose the data distribution into structured factor spaces (e.g., object, action, environment). Develop factor-wise data collection and an iterative training loop that encourages compositional generalization across the high-dimensional factor space.
AI.result: Extensive real-world experiments show more than 45% performance gains with 5-10× fewer demonstrations compared to baselines without the strategy.
AI.conclusion: Structured factorization offers a practical path to efficient compositional generalization in real-world robotic learning; F-ACIL may motivate broader data-centric strategies for generalizable robotic data flywheels; demonstration data available at the project site.

title: RubricEval: A Rubric-Level Meta-Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Judges in Instruction Following
summary: Rubric-based evaluation has become a prevailing paradigm for evaluating instruction following in large language models (LLMs). Despite its widespread use, the reliability of these rubric-level evaluations remains unclear, calling for meta-evaluation. However, prior meta-evaluation efforts largely focus on the response level, failing to assess the fine-grained judgment accuracy that rubric-based evaluation relies on. To bridge this gap, we introduce RubricEval. Our benchmark features: (1) the first rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark for instruction following, (2) diverse instructions and responses spanning multiple categories and model sources, and (3) a substantial set of 3,486 quality-controlled instances, along with Easy/Hard subsets that better differentiates judge performance. Our experiments reveal that rubric-level judging remains far from solved: even GPT-4o, a widely adopted judge in instruction-following benchmarks, achieves only 55.97% on Hard subset. Considering evaluation paradigm, rubric-level evaluation outperforms checklist-level, explicit reasoning improves accuracy, and both together reduce inter-judge variance. Through our established rubric taxonomy, we further identify common failure modes and offer actionable insights for reliable instruction-following evaluation.

AI.tldr: RubricEval introduces a rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark for instruction following, with 3,486 quality-controlled instances (Easy/Hard) across diverse instructions and model sources; findings show rubric-level judging is far from solved (GPT-4o at 55.97% on Hard); rubric-level evaluation generally outperforms checklist-level; explicit reasoning improves accuracy and reduces inter-judge variance; a rubric taxonomy identifies common failure modes and guides reliable evaluation.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of fine-grained, rubric-level meta-evaluation in instruction-following; previous work focused on response-level evaluation and checklist approaches.
AI.method: Construct RubricEval benchmark with diverse instructions/responses; quality-control to 3,486 instances; Easy/Hard subsets; compare rubric-level vs checklist-level judging; test explicit reasoning; analyze inter-judge variance; develop rubric taxonomy; provide actionable insights.
AI.result: GPT-4o achieves 55.97% on Hard subset; rubric-level judging improves reliability; explicit reasoning helps; combination reduces inter-judge variance; rubric taxonomy reveals common failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-level evaluation is still imperfect; provide guidance for reliable evaluation and points for future work, including broader judge baselines and improved rubric design.

title: SIGMA: Structure-Invariant Generative Molecular Alignment for Chemical Language Models via Autoregressive Contrastive Learning
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to ICML 2026. Primary category: cs.LG (Machine Learning); Secondary: cs.AI, q-bio.QM
summary: Linearized string representations serve as the foundation of scalable autoregressive molecular generation; however, they introduce a fundamental modality mismatch where a single molecular graph maps to multiple distinct sequences. This ambiguity leads to \textit{trajectory divergence}, where the latent representations of structurally equivalent partial graphs drift apart due to differences in linearization history. To resolve this without abandoning the efficient string formulation, we propose Structure-Invariant Generative Molecular Alignment (SIGMA). Rather than altering the linear representation, SIGMA enables the model to strictly recognize geometric symmetries via a token-level contrastive objective, which explicitly aligns the latent states of prefixes that share identical suffixes. Furthermore, we introduce Isomorphic Beam Search (IsoBeam) to eliminate isomorphic redundancy during inference by dynamically pruning equivalent paths. Empirical evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate that SIGMA bridges the gap between sequence scalability and graph fidelity, yielding superior sample efficiency and structural diversity in multi-parameter optimization compared to strong baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes SIGMA, a structure-invariant alignment for linearized string representations in autoregressive molecular generation to address trajectory divergence, paired with Isomorphic Beam Search to remove isomorphic redundancy; yields improved sample efficiency and molecular diversity in multi-parameter optimization experiments.
AI.motivation: Address modality mismatch between linearized string encodings and underlying molecular graphs, which causes trajectory divergence where latent prefixes diverge despite identical suffixes, harming fidelity and sample efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce a token-level contrastive objective that enforces structure-invariant representations by aligning latent states of prefixes sharing identical suffixes; add Isomorphic Beam Search (IsoBeam) to prune isomorphic, redundant inference paths.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on standard benchmarks show improved sample efficiency and greater structural diversity during multi-parameter optimization compared to strong baselines, indicating better balance between sequence scalability and graph fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Structure-Invariant Generative Molecular Alignment (SIGMA) plus IsoBeam offer a practical approach to reconcile linear sequence representations with graph-level fidelity, enabling more efficient and diverse molecular generation.

title: Towards automatic smoke detector inspection: Recognition of the smoke detectors in industrial facilities and preparation for future drone integration
summary: Fire safety consists of a complex pipeline, and it is a very important topic of concern. One of its frontal parts are the smoke detectors, which are supposed to provide an alarm prior to a massive fire appears. As they are often difficult to reach due to high ceilings or problematic locations, an automatic inspection system would be very beneficial as it could allow faster revisions, prevent workers from dangerous work in heights, and make the whole process cheaper. In this study, we present the smoke detector recognition part of the automatic inspection system, which could easily be integrated to the drone system. As part of our research, we compare two popular convolutional-based object detectors YOLOv11 and SSD widely used on embedded devices together with the state-of-the-art transformer-based RT-DETRv2 with the backbones of different sizes. Due to a complicated way of collecting a sufficient amount of data for training in the real-world environment, we also compare several training strategies using the real and semi-synthetic data together with various augmentation methods. To achieve a robust testing, all models were evaluated on two test datasets with an expected and difficult appearance of the smoke detectors including motion blur, small resolution, or not complete objects. The best performing detector is the YOLOv11n, which reaches the average mAP@0.5 score of 0.884. Our code, pretrained models and dataset are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Compared YOLOv11, SSD, and RT-DETRv2 (with various backbones) for smoke detector detection in drone-based inspection; used real and semi-synthetic data with augmentations; evaluated on two datasets; YOLOv11n achieved mAP@0.5 = 0.884; code/dataset publicly available.
AI.motivation: Enable automated, safe, and cost-effective inspection of hard-to-reach smoke detectors (e.g., on high ceilings) using drones; reduce human risk and improve maintenance speed.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple detectors (YOLOv11, SSD, RT-DETRv2) with backbones of different sizes; train with real and semi-synthetic data plus augmentations; test on two challenging datasets containing motion blur, small objects, and incomplete detectors; integrate results within a drone-assisted inspection system.
AI.result: Best-performing detector is YOLOv11n with average mAP@0.5 of 0.884; other performance details not specified; code, pretrained models, and dataset are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: YOLOv11n is the most effective choice for smoke detector detection in drone inspections among the tested models; the study demonstrates the viability of real/semi-synthetic training strategies and provides resources for reproducibility.

title: Accurate Surface and Reflectance Modelling from 3D Radar Data with Neural Radiance Fields
summary: Robust scene representation is essential for autonomous systems to safely operate in challenging low-visibility environments. Radar has a clear advantage over cameras and lidars in these conditions due to its resilience to environmental factors such as fog, smoke, or dust. However, radar data is inherently sparse and noisy, making reliable 3D surface reconstruction challenging. To address these challenges, we propose a neural implicit approach for 3D mapping from radar point clouds, which jointly models scene geometry and view-dependent radar intensities. Our method leverages a memory-efficient hybrid feature encoding to learn a continuous Signed Distance Field (SDF) for surface reconstruction, while also capturing radar-specific reflective properties. We show that our approach produces smoother, more accurate 3D surface reconstructions compared to existing lidar-based reconstruction methods applied to radar data, and can reconstruct view-dependent radar intensities. We also show that in general, as input point clouds get sparser, neural implicit representations render more faithful surfaces, compared to traditional explicit SDFs and meshing techniques.

AI.tldr: A neural implicit 3D reconstruction framework for radar point clouds that jointly models geometry and view-dependent radar intensities using a memory-efficient hybrid encoding to learn a continuous SDF, outperforming lidar-based methods on radar data and maintaining accuracy as data sparsity increases.
AI.motivation: Robust 3D scene understanding in low-visibility conditions where radar is resilient to fog, smoke, and dust; traditional radar reconstructions struggle due to sparse/noisy data, necessitating methods that can learn continuous surfaces and capture radar-specific reflectivity.
AI.method: A neural implicit representation that learns a continuous Signed Distance Field (SDF) for surfaces from radar point clouds, coupled with a memory-efficient hybrid feature encoding to also model view-dependent radar intensities and reflectivity; integrates geometry and radar-specific properties to improve reconstruction quality.
AI.result: Produces smoother, more accurate 3D surfaces than lidar-based reconstructions when applied to radar data; capable of reconstructing view-dependent radar intensities; exhibits better fidelity than explicit SDFs/meshing as input sparsity increases.
AI.conclusion: Neural implicit representations are particularly advantageous for sparse radar data, enabling faithful surface reconstruction and view-dependent reflectivity modeling, surpassing traditional explicit methods and suggesting robustness of the approach in challenging sensing conditions.

title: UniAI-GraphRAG: Synergizing Ontology-Guided Extraction, Multi-Dimensional Clustering, and Dual-Channel Fusion for Robust Multi-Hop Reasoning
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems face significant challenges in complex reasoning, multi-hop queries, and domain-specific QA. While existing GraphRAG frameworks have made progress in structural knowledge organization, they still have limitations in cross-industry adaptability, community report integrity, and retrieval performance. This paper proposes UniAI-GraphRAG, an enhanced framework built upon open-source GraphRAG. The framework introduces three core innovations: (1) Ontology-Guided Knowledge Extraction that uses predefined Schema to guide LLMs in accurately identifying domain-specific entities and relations; (2) Multi-Dimensional Community Clustering Strategy that improves community completeness through alignment completion, attribute-based clustering, and multi-hop relationship clustering; (3) Dual-Channel Graph Retrieval Fusion that balances QA accuracy and performance through hybrid graph and community retrieval. Evaluation results on MultiHopRAG benchmark show that UniAI-GraphRAG outperforms mainstream open source solutions (e.g.LightRAG) in comprehensive F1 scores, particularly in inference and temporal queries. The code is available at https://github.com/UnicomAI/wanwu/tree/main/rag/rag_open_source/rag_core/graph.

AI.tldr: UniAI-GraphRAG extends GraphRAG with three innovations—ontology-guided knowledge extraction, multi-dimensional community clustering, and dual-channel retrieval fusion—achieving higher F1 on MultiHopRAG, especially for inference and temporal queries, compared to LightRAG.
AI.motivation: RAG systems struggle with complex reasoning, multi-hop queries, and domain-specific QA; GraphRAG improves knowledge organization but lacks cross-industry adaptability, integrity in community reports, and retrieval performance. This work aims to address these gaps with an ontology-guided, multi-dimensional clustering, and hybrid retrieval framework.
AI.method: 1) Ontology-Guided Knowledge Extraction using predefined schema to guide LLMs in identifying domain entities/relations. 2) Multi-Dimensional Community Clustering: alignment completion, attribute-based clustering, multi-hop relationship clustering to improve community completeness. 3) Dual-Channel Graph Retrieval Fusion: hybrid graph and community retrieval to balance QA accuracy and efficiency.
AI.result: On the MultiHopRAG benchmark, UniAI-GraphRAG outperforms mainstream open-source solutions (e.g., LightRAG) in comprehensive F1, with notable gains in inference and temporal queries.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enhances retrieval-augmented generation by combining guided extraction, richer community structure, and hybrid retrieval, leading to better cross-domain performance; code is publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.

title: OptiSAR-Net++: A Large-Scale Benchmark and Transformer-Free Framework for Cross-Domain Remote Sensing Visual Grounding
summary: Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to localize specific targets in remote sensing images using natural language expressions. However, existing methods are restricted to single-sensor domains, i.e., either optical or synthetic aperture radar (SAR), limiting their real-world applicability. In this paper, we introduce the Cross-Domain RSVG (CD-RSVG) task and construct OptSAR-RSVG, the first large-scale benchmark dataset for this setting. To tackle the challenges of cross-domain feature modeling, computational inefficiency, and fine-grained semantic discrimination, we propose OptiSAR-Net++. Our framework features a patch-level Low-Rank Adaptation Mixture of Experts (PL-MoE) for efficient cross-domain feature decoupling. To mitigate the substantial computational overhead of Transformer decoding frameworks, we adopt a CLIP-based contrastive paradigm and further incorporate dynamic adversarial negative sampling, thereby transforming generative regression into an efficient cross-modal matching process. Additionally, a text-guided dual-gate fusion module (TGDF-SSA) and a region-aware auxiliary head are introduced to enhance semantic-visual alignment and spatial modeling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OptiSAR-Net++ achieves SOTA performance on both OptSAR-RSVG and DIOR-RSVG benchmarks, offering significant advantages in localization accuracy and efficiency. Our code and dataset will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: A cross-domain remote sensing visual grounding framework (OptiSAR-Net++) with a new CD-RSVG task and OptSAR-RSVG benchmark, achieving state-of-the-art results across optical and SAR imagery while improving efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current RS visual grounding methods are limited to single-sensor domains (optical or SAR), hindering real-world applicability. There is a need for cross-domain feature modeling, improved efficiency, and fine-grained semantic discrimination between modalities.
AI.method: Proposes OptiSAR-Net++ with: (1) patch-level Low-Rank Adaptation Mixture of Experts (PL-MoE) for cross-domain feature decoupling; (2) CLIP-based contrastive learning with dynamic adversarial negative sampling to replace generative regression with cross-modal matching; (3) a text-guided dual-gate fusion module (TGDF-SSA) and a region-aware auxiliary head to enhance semantic-visual alignment and spatial modeling.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on OptSAR-RSVG and DIOR-RSVG benchmarks, with notable gains in localization accuracy and computational efficiency. The authors also release code and a large-scale dataset.
AI.conclusion: The paper defines the CD-RSVG task and delivers OptSAR-RSVG as a scalable benchmark. OptiSAR-Net++ provides effective cross-domain RS-VSL grounding, balancing semantic alignment and efficiency, and sets a new standard for cross-domain RS grounding research.

title: A Mentalistic Interface for Probing Folk-Psychological Attribution to Non-Humanoid Robots
comment: Preprint submitted to IEEE. 8 pages, 21 figures
summary: This paper presents an experimental platform for studying intentional-state attribution toward a non-humanoid robot. The system combines a simulated robot, realistic task environments, and large language model-based explanatory layers that can express the same behavior in mentalistic, teleological, or mechanistic terms. By holding behavior constant while varying the explanatory frame, the platform provides a controlled way to investigate how language and framing shape the adoption of the intentional stance in robotics.

AI.tldr: A controlled experimental platform combining simulation, task environments, and LLM-based explanations to study how framing (mentalistic, teleological, mechanistic) affects intentional-state attribution to a non-humanoid robot, keeping behavior constant.
AI.motivation: To understand how language and framing influence the adoption of the intentional stance in robotics, addressing how people attribute mental states to machines in human-robot interaction and the explainability problem.
AI.method: Integrates a simulated robot, realistic task environments, and an LLM-driven explanatory layer capable of rendering the same behavior in mentalistic, teleological, or mechanistic frames; experiments vary explanatory frame while keeping observed behavior fixed to isolate framing effects, enabling measurement of intentional-state attribution.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility and provides a workflow for comparing explanatory frames; outlines instruments and metrics to assess attribution of intentional states; serves as a proof-of-concept platform rather than reporting full empirical results.
AI.conclusion: The platform offers a modular, controllable method to study how framing shapes adoption of the intentional stance in robotics, with implications for robot explainability and user perception. Future work includes formal experiments, broader generalization across robot types, quantifying framing effects, and exploring ethical considerations.

title: Trace2Skill: Distill Trajectory-Local Lessons into Transferable Agent Skills
summary: Equipping Large Language Model (LLM) agents with domain-specific skills is critical for tackling complex tasks. Yet, manual authoring creates a severe scalability bottleneck. Conversely, automated skill generation often yields fragile or fragmented results because it either relies on shallow parametric knowledge or sequentially overfits to non-generalizable trajectory-local lessons. To overcome this, we introduce Trace2Skill, a framework that mirrors how human experts author skills: by holistically analyzing broad execution experience before distilling it into a single, comprehensive guide. Instead of reacting sequentially to individual trajectories, Trace2Skill dispatches a parallel fleet of sub-agents to analyze a diverse pool of executions. It extracts trajectory-specific lessons and hierarchically consolidates them into a unified, conflict-free skill directory via inductive reasoning. Trace2Skill supports both deepening existing human-written skills and creating new ones from scratch. Experiments in challenging domains, such as spreadsheet, VisionQA and math reasoning, show that Trace2Skill significantly improves upon strong baselines, including Anthropic's official xlsx skills. Crucially, this trajectory-grounded evolution does not merely memorize task instances or model-specific quirks: evolved skills transfer across LLM scales and generalize to OOD settings. For example, skills evolved by Qwen3.5-35B on its own trajectories improved a Qwen3.5-122B agent by up to 57.65 absolute percentage points on WikiTableQuestions. Ultimately, our results demonstrate that complex agent experience can be packaged into highly transferable, declarative skills -- requiring no parameter updates, no external retrieval modules, and utilizing open-source models as small as 35B parameters.

AI.tldr: Trace2Skill is a parallel, trajectory-grounded framework that builds a unified, transferable skill directory for LLM agents by analyzing diverse executions and inductively consolidating lessons; scalable, domain-agnostic, and requires no retraining or external retrieval modules.
AI.motivation: Manual authoring of domain skills is a scalability bottleneck; automated skills often rely on trajectory-local data and fail to generalize across tasks and models. There is a need for holistic, cross-trajectory skill extraction that yields robust, transferable capabilities.
AI.method: Deploy a parallel fleet of sub-agents to analyze a broad pool of executions; extract trajectory-specific insights; inductively consolidate these insights into a single, conflict-free skill directory; support both deepening existing skills and creating new ones; achieve this without parameter updates or external retrieval modules, and demonstrate with open-source models as small as 35B.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines including Anthropic’s xlsx skills; skills transfer across model scales and generalize to out-of-distribution settings; e.g., skills evolved by Qwen3.5-35B on its trajectories improved a Qwen3.5-122B agent on WikiTableQuestions by up to 57.65 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Complex agent experience can be packaged into transferable, declarative skills that do not require retraining or retrieval modules; effective with relatively small open-source models and applicable across domains (e.g., spreadsheet, VisionQA, math reasoning).

title: Process-Aware AI for Rainfall-Runoff Modeling: A Mass-Conserving Neural Framework with Hydrological Process Constraints
summary: Machine learning models can achieve high predictive accuracy in hydrological applications but often lack physical interpretability. The Mass-Conserving Perceptron (MCP) provides a physics-aware artificial intelligence (AI) framework that enforces conservation principles while allowing hydrological process relationships to be learned from data. In this study, we investigate how progressively embedding physically meaningful representations of hydrological processes within a single MCP storage unit improves predictive skill and interpretability in rainfall-runoff modeling. Starting from a minimal MCP formulation, we sequentially introduce bounded soil storage, state-dependent conductivity, variable porosity, infiltration capacity, surface ponding, vertical drainage, and nonlinear water-table dynamics. The resulting hierarchy of process-aware MCP models is evaluated across 15 catchments spanning five hydroclimatic regions of the continental United States using daily streamflow prediction as the target. Results show that progressively augmenting the internal physical structure of the MCP unit generally improves predictive performance. The influence of these process representations is strongly hydroclimate dependent: vertical drainage substantially improves model skill in arid and snow-dominated basins but reduces performance in rainfall-dominated regions, while surface ponding has comparatively small effects. The best-performing MCP configurations approach the predictive skill of a Long Short-Term Memory benchmark while maintaining explicit physical interpretability. These results demonstrate that embedding hydrological process constraints within AI architectures provides a promising pathway toward interpretable and process-aware rainfall-runoff modeling.

AI.tldr: Progressively embedding physics in a mass-conserving perceptron (MCP) enhances rainfall-runoff predictions and interpretability; performance gains depend on hydroclimate, with vertical drainage beneficial in arid/snow basins but not in rainfall-dominated ones; best MCPs rival LSTM while remaining interpretable.
AI.motivation: ML models in hydrology achieve high accuracy but often lack physical interpretability and mass-conservation. A physics-aware AI (MCP) aims to enforce conservation while learning hydrological relationships from data.
AI.method: Develop a hierarchy of process-aware MCP units by incrementally adding hydrological representations (bounded soil storage, state-dependent conductivity, variable porosity, infiltration capacity, surface ponding, vertical drainage, nonlinear water-table dynamics). Evaluate across 15 catchments in five US hydroclimatic regions using daily streamflow as the target; compare against an LSTM benchmark.
AI.result: Progressive augmentation of the MCP's internal physical structure generally improves predictive skill. Effects are hydroclimate dependent: vertical drainage improves arid and snow-dominated basins but reduces performance in rainfall-dominated regions; surface ponding has smaller effects. The best MCP configurations approach the predictive skill of an LSTM while maintaining explicit physical interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Embedding hydrological process constraints within AI architectures is a promising path toward interpretable, process-aware rainfall-runoff modeling.

title: SurgPhase: Time efficient pituitary tumor surgery phase recognition via an interactive web platform
summary:
Accurate surgical phase recognition is essential for analyzing procedural workflows, supporting intraoperative decision-making, and enabling data-driven improvements in surgical education and performance evaluation. In this work, we present a comprehensive framework for phase recognition in pituitary tumor surgery (PTS) videos, combining self-supervised representation learning, robust temporal modeling, and scalable data annotation strategies. Our method achieves 90\% accuracy on a held-out test set, outperforming current state-of-the-art approaches and demonstrating strong generalization across variable surgical cases.
  A central contribution of this work is the integration of a collaborative online platform designed for surgeons to upload surgical videos, receive automated phase analysis, and contribute to a growing dataset. This platform not only facilitates large-scale data collection but also fosters knowledge sharing and continuous model improvement. To address the challenge of limited labeled data, we pretrain a ResNet-50 model using the self-supervised framework on 251 unlabeled PTS videos, enabling the extraction of high-quality feature representations. Fine-tuning is performed on a labeled dataset of 81 procedures using a modified training regime that incorporates focal loss, gradual layer unfreezing, and dynamic sampling to address class imbalance and procedural variability.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised, temporally-aware surgical phase recognition for pituitary tumor surgery with a collaborative data platform; achieves 90% test accuracy, outperforming SOTA and generalizing across cases.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and labeling bottlenecks in surgical phase recognition; enable scalable, collaborative data collection and sharing to improve models and education.
AI.method: Pretrain a ResNet-50 with a self-supervised framework on 251 unlabeled pituitary tumor surgery videos; fine-tune on 81 labeled procedures using a modified regime with focal loss, gradual layer unfreezing, and dynamic sampling; incorporate robust temporal modeling; deploy a collaborative online platform for surgeons to upload videos, obtain automated phase analysis, and contribute to a growing dataset.
AI.result: Achieves 90% accuracy on a held-out test set, outperforming current state-of-the-art methods; demonstrates strong generalization across variable surgical cases.
AI.conclusion: Integrating a collaborative data platform with self-supervised pretraining and targeted fine-tuning yields strong phase recognition performance and scalable model improvement, with potential to enhance surgical education and intraoperative decision-support.

title: Fast-dVLA: Accelerating Discrete Diffusion VLA to Real-Time Performance
summary: This paper proposes a novel approach to address the challenge that pretrained VLA models often fail to effectively improve performance and reduce adaptation costs during standard supervised finetuning (SFT). Some advanced finetuning methods with auxiliary training objectives can improve performance and reduce the number of convergence steps. However, they typically incur significant computational overhead due to the additional losses from auxiliary tasks. To simultaneously achieve the enhanced capabilities of auxiliary training with the simplicity of standard SFT, we decouple the two objectives of auxiliary task training within the parameter space, namely, enhancing general capabilities and fitting task-specific action distributions. To deliver this goal, we only need to train the model to converge on a small-scale task set using two distinct training strategies. The difference between the resulting model parameters can then be interpreted as capability vectors provided by auxiliary tasks. These vectors are then merged with pretrained parameters to form a capability-enhanced meta model. Moreover, when standard SFT is augmented with a lightweight orthogonal regularization loss, the merged model attains performance comparable to auxiliary finetuned baselines with reduced computational overhead. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach is highly effective across diverse robot tasks. Project page: https://chris1220313648.github.io/Fast-dVLA/

AI.tldr: A framework that decouples auxiliary-task training from standard supervised finetuning by learning capability vectors via parameter differences on a small task set, merging them with pretrained weights, optionally using an orthogonal regularization during SFT to match auxiliary-finetuned baselines with lower cost.
AI.motivation: Address the overhead and inefficiency of auxiliary training in fine-tuning pretrained visual-language-action (VLA) models while preserving gains in general capabilities and task-specific performance.
AI.method: Train on a small-scale task set with two distinct strategies to produce two parameter configurations; interpret their difference as a capability vector from auxiliary tasks; merge this vector with the pretrained parameters to form a capability-enhanced meta model; during standard SFT, apply lightweight orthogonal regularization to align with the merged model and reduce computational overhead.
AI.result: Empirical results across diverse robot tasks show that the approach yields strong performance with reduced overhead, and the merged model with orthogonal regularization matches auxiliary-finetuned baselines while being more efficient; a project page is provided for details.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling auxiliary objectives in parameter space and using a simple orthogonal regularization during SFT effectively enhances model capabilities without the heavy cost of auxiliary losses, showing promise for efficient transfer learning in robotic tasks.

title: The Competence Shadow: Theory and Bounds of AI Assistance in Safety Engineering
summary:
As AI assistants become integrated into safety engineering workflows for Physical AI systems, a critical question emerges: does AI assistance improve safety analysis quality, or introduce systematic blind spots that surface only through post-deployment incidents? This paper develops a formal framework for AI assistance in safety analysis. We first establish why safety engineering resists benchmark-driven evaluation: safety competence is irreducibly multidimensional, constrained by context-dependent correctness, inherent incompleteness, and legitimate expert disagreement. We formalize this through a five-dimensional competence framework capturing domain knowledge, standards expertise, operational experience, contextual understanding, and judgment.
  We introduce the competence shadow: the systematic narrowing of human reasoning induced by AI-generated safety analysis. The shadow is not what the AI presents, but what it prevents from being considered. We formalize four canonical human-AI collaboration structures and derive closed-form performance bounds, demonstrating that the competence shadow compounds multiplicatively to produce degradation far exceeding naive additive estimates.
  The central finding is that AI assistance in safety engineering is a collaboration design problem, not a software procurement decision. The same tool degrades or improves analysis quality depending entirely on how it is used. We derive non-degradation conditions for shadow-resistant workflows and call for a shift from tool qualification toward workflow qualification for trustworthy Physical AI.

AI.tldr: AI assistance in safety analysis is a collaboration-design problem; the 'competence shadow' can degrade analysis quality; four collaboration structures; effects are multiplicative; workflow qualification is essential.
AI.motivation: Determine whether AI assistance improves or harms safety analysis in Physical AI, and formalize human-AI collaboration within safety workflows.
AI.method: Propose a five-dimensional competence framework (domain knowledge, standards expertise, operational experience, contextual understanding, judgment); define the competence shadow; formalize four canonical human-AI collaboration structures; derive closed-form performance bounds; identify non-degradation conditions; advocate workflow qualification.
AI.result: Shows that competence shadow can cause multiplicative degradation in safety analysis quality; outcomes depend critically on collaboration design and usage; provides conditions for shadow-resistant workflows.
AI.conclusion: Shifts focus from tool procurement to workflow design for trustworthy Physical AI; proposes design guidelines and non-degradation conditions to mitigate shadow.

title: Layer-Specific Lipschitz Modulation for Fault-Tolerant Multimodal Representation Learning
summary: Modern multimodal systems deployed in industrial and safety-critical environments must remain reliable under partial sensor failures, signal degradation, or cross-modal inconsistencies. This work introduces a mathematically grounded framework for fault-tolerant multimodal representation learning that unifies self-supervised anomaly detection and error correction within a single architecture. Building upon a theoretical analysis of perturbation propagation, we derive Lipschitz- and Jacobian-based criteria that determine whether a neural operator amplifies or attenuates localized faults. Guided by this theory, we propose a two-stage self-supervised training scheme: pre-training a multimodal convolutional autoencoder on clean data to preserve localized anomaly signals in the latent space, and expanding it with a learnable compute block composed of dense layers for correction and contrastive objectives for anomaly identification. Furthermore, we introduce layer-specific Lipschitz modulation and gradient clipping as principled mechanisms to control sensitivity across detection and correction modules. Experimental results on multimodal fault datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach improves both anomaly detection accuracy and reconstruction under sensor corruption. Overall, this framework bridges the gap between analytical robustness guarantees and practical fault-tolerant multimodal learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces a fault-tolerant multimodal learning framework with theoretical guarantees, unifying self-supervised anomaly detection and error correction via perturbation analysis; uses Lipschitz/Jacobian criteria and a two-stage training pipeline with a corrective dense block and contrastive objectives; includes layer-wise Lipschitz modulation and gradient clipping to control sensitivity; shows improved detection and reconstruction under sensor corruption.
AI.motivation: Industrial and safety-critical multimodal systems must remain reliable under partial sensor failures, degraded signals, or cross-modal inconsistencies; current approaches lack a principled, unified treatment of detection and correction with robustness guarantees.
AI.method: Performs a perturbation-propagation analysis to derive Lipschitz- and Jacobian-based criteria that predict whether a neural operator will amplify or attenuate faults. Proposes a two-stage training scheme: (1) pre-train a multimodal convolutional autoencoder on clean data to preserve localized anomaly signals in latent space; (2) expand with a learnable dense compute block for correction and add contrastive objectives for anomaly identification. Introduces layer-specific Lipschitz modulation and gradient clipping to control sensitivity across detection and correction modules.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multimodal fault datasets shows improved anomaly detection accuracy and better reconstruction under sensor corruption, demonstrating that the framework provides both enhanced detection and robustness for fault-tolerant multimodal learning.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges analytical robustness guarantees with practical fault-tolerant multimodal learning by delivering a unified architecture that balances detection and correction under partial sensor failures.

title: Self-Supervised Learning for Knee Osteoarthritis: Diagnostic Limitations and Prognostic Value of Uncurated Hospital Data
summary: This study assesses whether self-supervised learning (SSL) improves knee osteoarthritis (OA) modeling for diagnosis and prognosis relative to ImageNet-pretrained initialization. We compared (i) image-only SSL pretrained on knee radiographs from the OAI, MOST, and NYU cohorts, and (ii) multimodal image-text SSL pretrained on uncurated hospital knee radiographs paired with radiologist impressions. For diagnostic Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) grade prediction, SSL offered mixed results. While image-only SSL improved accuracy during linear probing (frozen encoder), it did not outperform ImageNet pretraining during full fine-tuning. Similarly, multimodal SSL failed to improve grading performance. We attribute this to severe bias in the uncurated hospital pretraining corpus (93% estimated KL grade 3), which limited alignment with the balanced diagnostic task. In contrast, this same multimodal initialization significantly improved prognostic modeling. It outperformed ImageNet baselines in predicting 4-year structural incidence and progression, including on external validation (MOST AUROC: 0.701 vs. 0.599 at 10% labeled data). Overall, while uncurated hospital image-text data may be ineffective for learning diagnosis due to severity bias, it provides a strong signal for prognostic modeling when the downstream task aligns with pretraining data distribution

AI.tldr: SSL pretraining on knee radiographs yields mixed diagnostic gains but improves prognosis when pretraining data distribution aligns; uncurated hospital data biases hinder diagnosis but aid prognosis.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether SSL improves knee OA diagnosis/prognosis relative to ImageNet initialization; compare image-only SSL vs multimodal image-text SSL across datasets.
AI.method: Two SSL paradigms: (i) image-only SSL pretrained on knee radiographs from OAI, MOST, and NYU; (ii) multimodal image-text SSL pretrained on uncurated hospital knee radiographs paired with radiologist impressions. Evaluate on diagnostic Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) grade and 4-year structural prognosis, including external validation.
AI.result: Diagnosis: image-only SSL improved linear probing accuracy but did not surpass ImageNet during full fine-tuning; multimodal SSL did not improve grading due to bias from a highly skewed pretraining corpus (about 93% KL grade 3). Prognosis: multimodal SSL outperformed ImageNet baselines, including external validation (MOST AUROC 0.701 vs. 0.599 at 10% labeled data). Overall, uncurated hospital image-text data can be ineffective for learning diagnosis due to severity bias, but provides strong signal for prognosis when the downstream task aligns with the pretraining distribution.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised pretraining with curated image data may help diagnostic performance under linear evaluation, whereas uncurated image-text SSL can substantially boost prognosis when the downstream task distribution matches the pretraining data.

title: Can Users Specify Driving Speed? Bench2Drive-Speed: Benchmark and Baselines for Desired-Speed Conditioned Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving (E2E-AD) has achieved remarkable progress. However, one practical and useful function has been long overlooked: users may wish to customize the desired speed of the policy or specify whether to allow the autonomous vehicle to overtake. To bridge this gap, we present Bench2Drive-Speed, a benchmark with metrics, dataset, and baselines for desired-speed conditioned autonomous driving. We introduce explicit inputs of users' desired target-speed and overtake/follow instructions to driving policy models. We design quantitative metrics, including Speed-Adherence Score and Overtake Score, to measure how faithfully policies follow user specifications, while remaining compatible with standard autonomous driving metrics. To enable training of speed-conditioned policies, one approach is to collect expert demonstrations that strictly follow speed requirements, an expensive and unscalable process in the real world. An alternative is to adapt existing regular driving data by treating the speed observed in future frames as the target speed for training. To investigate this, we construct CustomizedSpeedDataset, composed of 2,100 clips annotated with experts demonstrations, enabling systematic investigation of supervision strategies. Our experiments show that, under proper re-annotation, models trained on regular driving data perform comparably to on expert demonstrations, suggesting that speed supervision can be introduced without additional complex real-world data collection. Furthermore, we find that while target-speed following can be achieved without degrading regular driving performance, executing overtaking commands remains challenging due to the inherent difficulty of interactive behaviors. All code, datasets and baselines are available at https://github.com/Thinklab-SJTU/Bench2Drive-Speed

AI.tldr: Bench2Drive-Speed introduces a speed-conditioned end-to-end driving benchmark with explicit user inputs (target speed and overtaking/follow commands), new metrics (Speed-Adherence Score, Overtake Score), and the CustomizedSpeedDataset; shows speed supervision can be learned from re-annotated regular driving data, with overtaking remaining challenging; provides code, datasets, baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between user preferences and autonomous driving policies by enabling explicit control over speed and overtaking, and by providing a standardized benchmark to quantify fidelity to user directives.
AI.method: Introduce explicit input modalities for target speed and overtake/follow instructions; define Speed-Adherence and Overtake metrics; build CustomizedSpeedDataset by re-annotating future-frame speeds as targets; train and evaluate speed-conditioned policies using regular driving data vs expert demonstrations; release code/datasets.
AI.result: Demonstrates that target-speed following can be learned from re-annotated regular data and match expert demonstrations with proper supervision; speed control does not degrade regular driving performance, but overtaking commands remain difficult due to the interactive nature; provides benchmark, baselines, and open-source resources.
AI.conclusion: Bench2Drive-Speed provides a practical framework to study speed-conditioned autonomous driving, showing feasibility of speed supervision from existing data and highlighting the need for improved handling of interactive maneuvers like overtaking; resources are released for community use.

title: Probabilistic Abstract Interpretation on Neural Networks via Grids Approximation
summary: Probabilistic abstract interpretation is a theory used to extract particular properties of a computer program when it is infeasible to test every single inputs. In this paper we apply the theory on neural networks for the same purpose: to analyse density distribution flow of all possible inputs of a neural network when a network has uncountably many or countable but infinitely many inputs. We show how this theoretical framework works in neural networks and then discuss different abstract domains and corresponding Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverses together with abstract transformers used in the framework. We also present experimental examples to show how this framework helps to analyse real world problems.

AI.tldr: Applies probabilistic abstract interpretation to neural networks to analyze the density flow of all possible inputs (uncountable or infinite countable), using various abstract domains and Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverses with abstract transformers; supported by experimental demonstrations.
AI.motivation: To reason about neural networks over the full input space where exhaustive testing is infeasible, by deriving probabilistic/abstract properties that hold across inputs.
AI.method: Embed neural network computations into a probabilistic abstract interpretation framework; select and compare abstract domains; leverage Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverses to handle linear transformations; design abstract transformers; validate with experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrates applicability of the framework to neural networks; experiments illustrate how the density flow of inputs can be analyzed and how the framework provides insights for real-world problems.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic abstract interpretation is viable for neural network analysis; the combination of abstract domains and Moore–Penrose-based transformers enables tractable, broad-input analysis; future work should address domain choices, precision, and scalability.

title: SEVerA: Verified Synthesis of Self-Evolving Agents
summary: Recent advances have shown the effectiveness of self-evolving LLM agents on tasks such as program repair and scientific discovery. In this paradigm, a planner LLM synthesizes an agent program that invokes parametric models, including LLMs, which are then tuned per task to improve performance. However, existing self-evolving agent frameworks provide no formal guarantees of safety or correctness. Because such programs are often executed autonomously on unseen inputs, this lack of guarantees raises reliability and security concerns. We formulate agentic code generation as a constrained learning problem, combining hard formal specifications with soft objectives capturing task utility. We introduce Formally Guarded Generative Models (FGGM), which allow the planner LLM to specify a formal output contract for each generative model call using first-order logic. Each FGGM call wraps the underlying model in a rejection sampler with a verified fallback, ensuring every returned output satisfies the contract for any input and parameter setting. Building on FGGM, we present SEVerA (Self-Evolving Verified Agents), a three-stage framework: Search synthesizes candidate parametric programs containing FGGM calls; Verification proves correctness with respect to hard constraints for all parameter values, reducing the problem to unconstrained learning; and Learning applies scalable gradient-based optimization, including GRPO-style fine-tuning, to improve the soft objective while preserving correctness. We evaluate SEVerA on Dafny program verification, symbolic math synthesis, and policy-compliant agentic tool use ($τ^2$-bench). Across tasks, SEVerA achieves zero constraint violations while improving performance over unconstrained and SOTA baselines, showing that formal behavioral constraints not only guarantee correctness but also steer synthesis toward higher-quality agents.

AI.tldr: Formally Guarded Generative Models (FGGM) wrap model calls with formal output contracts using first-order logic, enabling safe, verifiable agentic code generation. The SEVerA framework (Search–Verify–Learn) synthesizes, verifies, and optimizes self-evolving agents with zero constraint violations across tasks, outperforming unconstrained and SOTA baselines.
AI.motivation: To address safety, correctness, and reliability concerns in self-evolving LLM agents that autonomously generate and execute code with parametric models, by enforcing formal guarantees via contracts and verified fallbacks.
AI.method: Introduce FGGM to enforce formal output contracts for each model call, implemented as a rejection sampler with a verified fallback. Build SEVerA with three stages: (1) Search for candidate parametric programs containing FGGM calls, (2) Verification to prove correctness for all parameter values, reducing to an unconstrained learning problem, (3) Learning to optimize the soft objective while preserving correctness, including GRPO-style fine-tuning.
AI.result: SEVerA achieves zero constraint violations across tasks (Dafny program verification, symbolic math synthesis, and policy-compliant tool use on τ^2-bench). It also improves performance over unconstrained and state-of-the-art baselines, indicating formal constraints guide synthesis to higher-quality, safer agents.
AI.conclusion: Formally constrained agentic generation can guarantee safety and correctness while enhancing performance; FGGM and SEVerA provide a practical framework for building reliable, self-evolving AI agents.

title: ICTPolarReal: A Polarized Reflection and Material Dataset of Real World Objects
summary: Accurately modeling how real-world materials reflect light remains a core challenge in inverse rendering, largely due to the scarcity of real measured reflectance data. Existing approaches rely heavily on synthetic datasets with simplified illumination and limited material realism, preventing models from generalizing to real-world images. We introduce a large-scale polarized reflection and material dataset of real-world objects, captured with an 8-camera, 346-light Light Stage equipped with cross/parallel polarization. Our dataset spans 218 everyday objects across five acquisition dimensions-multiview, multi-illumination, polarization, reflectance separation, and material attributes-yielding over 1.2M high-resolution images with diffuse-specular separation and analytically derived diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and surface normals. Using this dataset, we train and evaluate state-of-the-art inverse and forward rendering models on intrinsic decomposition, relighting, and sparse-view 3D reconstruction, demonstrating significant improvements in material separation, illumination fidelity, and geometric consistency. We hope that our work can establish a new foundation for physically grounded material understanding and enable real-world generalization beyond synthetic training regimes. Project page: https://jingyangcarl.github.io/ICTPolarReal/

AI.tldr: A large-scale real-world polarized reflection and material dataset (ICTPolarReal) with 1.2M high-resolution images from 218 objects, captured via an 8-camera, 346-light Light Stage with cross/parallel polarization, providing multiview, multi-illumination, polarization, reflectance separation, and material attributes, plus analytically derived diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and surface normals. Trains/evaluates state-of-the-art inverse and forward rendering models on intrinsic decomposition, relighting, and sparse-view 3D reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Real-world reflectance data are scarce; synthetic datasets with simplified illumination fail to generalize to real images. A large, realistic, polarized, and physically grounded dataset is needed to improve material understanding and generalization.
AI.method: Data capture using cross/parallel polarization across five acquisition dimensions (multiview, multi-illumination, polarization, reflectance separation, material attributes) with an 8-camera, 346-light Light Stage. Analytically derive diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and surface normals. Train and evaluate state-of-the-art inverse and forward rendering models on intrinsic decomposition, relighting, and sparse-view 3D reconstruction.
AI.result: Significant improvements in material separation, illumination fidelity, and geometric consistency when trained/evaluated on this dataset, demonstrating improved generalization to real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A foundation for physically grounded material understanding with real-world generalization beyond synthetic training regimes; project page provided for more details.

title: Persistent Robot World Models: Stabilizing Multi-Step Rollouts via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Action-conditioned robot world models generate future video frames of the manipulated scene given a robot action sequence, offering a promising alternative for simulating tasks that are difficult to model with traditional physics engines. However, these models are optimized for short-term prediction and break down when deployed autoregressively: each predicted clip feeds back as context for the next, causing errors to compound and visual quality to rapidly degrade. We address this through the following contributions. First, we introduce a reinforcement learning (RL) post-training scheme that trains the world model on its own autoregressive rollouts rather than on ground-truth histories. We achieve this by adapting a recent contrastive RL objective for diffusion models to our setting and show that its convergence guarantees carry over exactly. Second, we design a training protocol that generates and compares multiple candidate variable-length futures from the same rollout state, reinforcing higher-fidelity predictions over lower-fidelity ones. Third, we develop efficient, multi-view visual fidelity rewards that combine complementary perceptual metrics across camera views and are aggregated at the clip level for dense, low-variance training signal. Fourth, we show that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art for rollout fidelity on the DROID dataset, outperforming the strongest baseline on all metrics (e.g., LPIPS reduced by 14% on external cameras, SSIM improved by 9.1% on the wrist camera), winning 98% of paired comparisons, and achieving an 80% preference rate in a blind human study.

AI.tldr: Introduces reinforcement learning post-training for action-conditioned world models to improve long-horizon video rollouts, with multi-future training and multi-view fidelity rewards, achieving state-of-the-art rollout fidelity on DROID.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive video predictions in robot world models degrade quickly due to compounding errors; need stable long-horizon fidelity for planning and simulation.
AI.method: Three-part approach: (1) RL post-training that trains the world model on autoregressive rollouts using a contrastive RL objective adapted from diffusion models; (2) training protocol generating and comparing multiple candidate variable-length futures from the same rollout state to reinforce higher-fidelity predictions; (3) efficient, multi-view visual fidelity rewards combining perceptual metrics across camera views and aggregated at the clip level.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art rollout fidelity on the DROID dataset, surpassing baselines across metrics (e.g., LPIPS −14% on external cameras, SSIM +9.1% on wrist camera); 98% of paired comparisons favor the proposed method; 80% human-study preference in blind test.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RL post-training, multi-future evaluation, and multi-view fidelity rewards effectively mitigate compounding errors in action-conditioned world models, enabling more reliable long-horizon video predictions and improved realism for robotic simulation and planning.

title: Distribution and Clusters Approximations as Abstract Domains in Probabilistic Abstract Interpretation to Neural Network Analysis
summary: The probabilistic abstract interpretation framework of neural network analysis analyzes a neural network by analyzing its density distribution flow of all possible inputs. The grids approximation is one of abstract domains the framework uses which abstracts concrete space into grids. In this paper, we introduce two novel approximation methods: distribution approximation and clusters approximation. We show how these two methods work in theory with corresponding abstract transformers with help of illustrations of some simple examples.

AI.tldr: Two grid-domain approximations for probabilistic abstract interpretation of neural nets are introduced: distribution approximation and clusters approximation, each with theoretical abstract transformers and simple illustrative examples.
AI.motivation: To analyze the flow of input density through neural networks within a probabilistic abstract interpretation framework, addressing the limitations of coarse grid abstractions by introducing two scalable approximation methods.
AI.method: Introduce two grid-domain approximation schemes within probabilistic abstract interpretation: (1) distribution approximation, which models input densities within grid cells; (2) clusters approximation, which aggregates inputs into clusters to reduce complexity. Derive corresponding abstract transformers and illustrate their operation with simple examples.
AI.result: Formulates the theoretical foundations of both approximation methods and their abstract transformers; demonstrates the methods via simple illustrative examples within the grid abstract domain.
AI.conclusion: Both distribution and clusters approximations extend grid-based probabilistic abstract interpretation for neural networks, offering different trade-offs between precision and efficiency; theoretical groundwork laid with illustrative demonstrations; potential directions include empirical evaluation and integration of both schemes.

title: Vision Hopfield Memory Networks
summary: Recent vision and multimodal foundation backbones, such as Transformer families and state-space models like Mamba, have achieved remarkable progress, enabling unified modeling across images, text, and beyond. Despite their empirical success, these architectures remain far from the computational principles of the human brain, often demanding enormous amounts of training data while offering limited interpretability. In this work, we propose the Vision Hopfield Memory Network (V-HMN), a brain-inspired foundation backbone that integrates hierarchical memory mechanisms with iterative refinement updates. Specifically, V-HMN incorporates local Hopfield modules that provide associative memory dynamics at the image patch level, global Hopfield modules that function as episodic memory for contextual modulation, and a predictive-coding-inspired refinement rule for iterative error correction. By organizing these memory-based modules hierarchically, V-HMN captures both local and global dynamics in a unified framework. Memory retrieval exposes the relationship between inputs and stored patterns, making decisions more interpretable, while the reuse of stored patterns improves data efficiency. This brain-inspired design therefore enhances interpretability and data efficiency beyond existing self-attention- or state-space-based approaches. We conducted extensive experiments on public computer vision benchmarks, and V-HMN achieved competitive results against widely adopted backbone architectures, while offering better interpretability, higher data efficiency, and stronger biological plausibility. These findings highlight the potential of V-HMN to serve as a next-generation vision foundation model, while also providing a generalizable blueprint for multimodal backbones in domains such as text and audio, thereby bridging brain-inspired computation with large-scale machine learning.

AI.tldr: Vision Hopfield Memory Network (V-HMN): a brain-inspired backbone that fuses hierarchical memory modules (local Hopfield memories at patch level and global Hopfield memories for episodic/contextual memory) with a predictive-coding-inspired refinement rule, enabling iterative error correction. It achieves competitive results on vision benchmarks while improving interpretability and data efficiency, and offers a blueprint for multimodal backbones that align with brain-like computation.
AI.motivation: Current vision and multimodal foundation backbones (e.g., Transformers, state-space models like Mamba) are powerful but data-hungry and often lack interpretability and explicit memory mechanisms. The work aims to bring brain-inspired memory dynamics into a unified backbone to improve data efficiency and interpretability.
AI.method: Incorporate local Hopfield modules to provide associative memory at the image patch level and global Hopfield modules as episodic memory for contextual modulation. Use a predictive-coding-inspired refinement rule for iterative error correction. Organize memory-based modules hierarchically to capture local and global dynamics in a single framework. Memory retrieval exposes input–pattern relationships, aiding interpretability; reuse of stored patterns improves data efficiency.
AI.result: Experiments on public computer vision benchmarks show V-HMN achieving competitive performance against widely adopted backbone architectures, with enhanced interpretability, higher data efficiency, and stronger biological plausibility.
AI.conclusion: V-HMN demonstrates the potential of brain-inspired memory-augmented backbones as a future vision foundation model and provides a generalizable blueprint for multimodal backbones in domains like text and audio, bridging brain-inspired computation with large-scale machine learning.

title: TIGFlow-GRPO: Trajectory Forecasting via Interaction-Aware Flow Matching and Reward-Driven Optimization
summary: Human trajectory forecasting is important for intelligent multimedia systems operating in visually complex environments, such as autonomous driving and crowd surveillance. Although Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) has shown strong ability in modeling trajectory distributions from spatio-temporal observations, existing approaches still focus primarily on supervised fitting, which may leave social norms and scene constraints insufficiently reflected in generated trajectories. To address this issue, we propose TIGFlow-GRPO, a two-stage generative framework that aligns flow-based trajectory generation with behavioral rules. In the first stage, we build a CFM-based predictor with a Trajectory-Interaction-Graph (TIG) module to model fine-grained visual-spatial interactions and strengthen context encoding. This stage captures both agent-agent and agent-scene relations more effectively, providing more informative conditional features for subsequent alignment. In the second stage, we perform Flow-GRPO post-training,where deterministic flow rollout is reformulated as stochastic ODE-to-SDE sampling to enable trajectory exploration, and a composite reward combines view-aware social compliance with map-aware physical feasibility. By evaluating trajectories explored through SDE rollout, GRPO progressively steers multimodal predictions toward behaviorally plausible futures. Experiments on the ETH/UCY and SDD datasets show that TIGFlow-GRPO improves forecasting accuracy and long-horizon stability while generating trajectories that are more socially compliant and physically feasible. These results suggest that the proposed framework provides an effective way to connect flow-based trajectory modeling with behavior-aware alignment in dynamic multimedia environments.

AI.tldr: A two-stage flow-based trajectory generator (TIGFlow-GRPO) aligns CFM with social/scene constraints via a Trajectory-Interaction-Graph module and Flow-GRPO post-training using ODE-to-SDE sampling and a composite reward, achieving better accuracy and social compliance on ETH/UCY and SDD.
AI.motivation: Current flow-based trajectory modeling often relies on supervised fitting, which can underrepresent social norms and scene constraints in generated trajectories. There is a need to incorporate behaviorally plausible constraints into the modeling process.
AI.method: Stage 1 uses a Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) predictor augmented with a Trajectory-Interaction-Graph (TIG) to model fine-grained visual-spatial interactions (agent-agent and agent-scene). Stage 2 performs Flow-GRPO post-training by reformulating deterministic flow rollout as stochastic ODE-to-SDE sampling for trajectory exploration, with a composite reward that combines view-aware social compliance and map-aware physical feasibility.
AI.result: On ETH/UCY and SDD, TIGFlow-GRPO improves forecasting accuracy and long-horizon stability, producing trajectories that are more socially compliant and physically feasible.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively couples flow-based trajectory generation with behavior-aware alignment, showing promise for dynamic multimedia environments and potentially improving safety-critical trajectory predictions.

title: Intelligent Navigation and Obstacle-Aware Fabrication for Mobile Additive Manufacturing Systems
summary: As the demand for mass customization increases, manufacturing systems must become more flexible and adaptable to produce personalized products efficiently. Additive manufacturing (AM) enhances production adaptability by enabling on-demand fabrication of customized components directly from digital models, but its flexibility remains constrained by fixed equipment layouts. Integrating mobile robots addresses this limitation by allowing manufacturing resources to move and adapt to changing production requirements. Mobile AM Robots (MAMbots) combine AM with mobile robotics to produce and transport components within dynamic manufacturing environments. However, the dynamic manufacturing environments introduce challenges for MAMbots. Disturbances such as obstacles and uneven terrain can disrupt navigation stability, which in turn affects printing accuracy and surface quality. This work proposes a universal mobile printing-and-delivery platform that couples navigation and material deposition, addressing the limitations of earlier frameworks that treated these processes separately. A real-time control framework is developed to plan and control the robot's navigation, ensuring safe motion, obstacle avoidance, and path stability while maintaining print quality. The closed-loop integration of sensing, mobility, and manufacturing provides real-time feedback for motion and process control, enabling MAMbots to make autonomous decisions in dynamic environments. The framework is validated through simulations and real-world experiments that test its adaptability to trajectory variations and external disturbances. Coupled navigation and printing together enable MAMbots to plan safe, adaptive trajectories, improving flexibility and adaptability in manufacturing.

AI.tldr: Universal mobile printing-and-delivery platform for mobile AM robots (MAMbots) integrating navigation and deposition with real-time closed-loop control to operate in dynamic environments; validated by simulations and experiments, enabling autonomous, adaptable manufacturing.
AI.motivation: Mass customization demands flexible production; AM offers customization but fixed layouts limit adaptability; mobile robots offer reconfigurable resources; need integrated navigation and printing to maintain print quality amid disturbances.
AI.method: Develop real-time control framework that couples navigation planning, obstacle avoidance, path stability, and deposition control; closed-loop sensing, mobility, and manufacturing feedback; real-time decision making for dynamic environments; tested in simulations and real-world experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrates safe, adaptive trajectories and maintained print quality under disturbances; improved flexibility/adaptability; platform enables autonomous decisions in dynamic settings.
AI.conclusion: Coupling navigation and printing is essential for capable MAMbots; the framework advances autonomous, robust AM in dynamic environments; potential for broader deployment in flexible manufacturing.

title: A Gait Foundation Model Predicts Multi-System Health Phenotypes from 3D Skeletal Motion
summary: Gait is increasingly recognized as a vital sign, yet current approaches treat it as a symptom of specific pathologies rather than a systemic biomarker. We developed a gait foundation model for 3D skeletal motion from 3,414 deeply phenotyped adults, recorded via a depth camera during five motor tasks. Learned embeddings outperformed engineered features, predicting age (Pearson r = 0.69), BMI (r = 0.90), and visceral adipose tissue area (r = 0.82). Embeddings significantly predicted 1,980 of 3,210 phenotypic targets; after adjustment for age, BMI, VAT, and height, gait provided independent gains in all 18 body systems in males and 17 of 18 in females, and improved prediction of clinical diagnoses and medication use. Anatomical ablation revealed that legs dominated metabolic and frailty predictions while torso encoded sleep and lifestyle phenotypes. These findings establish gait as an independent multi-system biosignal, motivating translation to consumer-grade video and its integration as a scalable, passive vital sign.

AI.tldr: 3D gait embeddings from a large adult cohort predict broad phenotypes and act as a multi-system biomarker, enabling passive vital-sign monitoring.
AI.motivation: Current gait analyses largely target disease-specific symptoms; a systemic, scalable gait biomarker could enable passive health surveillance using consumer video.
AI.method: Train a gait foundation model on 3,414 deeply phenotyped adults using 3D skeletal motion captured by a depth camera across five motor tasks; compare learned embeddings to engineered features for predicting age, BMI, and visceral adipose tissue (VAT); perform a phenome-wide association across 3,210 phenotypes; conduct anatomical ablations to attribute signals to body segments.
AI.result: Embeddings predict age (r=0.69), BMI (r=0.90), VAT area (r=0.82). They significantly predict 1,980 of 3,210 phenotypes; after adjusting for age, BMI, VAT, and height, gait provides independent gains in all 18 body systems in males and 17 of 18 in females; improves prediction of clinical diagnoses and medication use; anatomical ablation shows legs drive metabolic/frailty predictions, torso encodes sleep and lifestyle phenotypes.
AI.conclusion: Gait constitutes an independent, multi-system biosignal with potential translation to consumer-grade video and integration as a scalable, passive vital sign.

title: Train at Moving Edge: Online-Verified Prompt Selection for Efficient RL Training of Large Reasoning Model
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become essential for post-training large language models (LLMs) in reasoning tasks. While scaling rollouts can stabilize training and enhance performance, the computational overhead is a critical issue. In algorithms like GRPO, multiple rollouts per prompt incur prohibitive costs, as a large portion of prompts provide negligible gradients and are thus of low utility. To address this problem, we investigate how to select high-utility prompts before the rollout phase. Our experimental analysis reveals that sample utility is non-uniform and evolving: the strongest learning signals concentrate at the ``learning edge", the intersection of intermediate difficulty and high uncertainty, which shifts as training proceeds. Motivated by this, we propose HIVE (History-Informed and online-VErified prompt selection), a dual-stage framework for data-efficient RL. HIVE utilizes historical reward trajectories for coarse selection and employs prompt entropy as a real-time proxy to prune instances with stale utility. By evaluating HIVE across multiple math reasoning benchmarks and models, we show that HIVE yields significant rollout efficiency without compromising performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes HIVE, a history-informed, online-verified prompt selection framework for data-efficient RL in post-training LLMs, reducing rollout costs by selecting high-utility prompts and pruning low-utility ones using historical rewards and prompt entropy.
AI.motivation: RL-based post-training for LLMs is computationally expensive due to many rollouts; utility of prompts is non-uniform and shifts over training; need pre-rollout selectivity to improve efficiency.
AI.method: Two-stage: (1) coarse selection using historical reward trajectories; (2) real-time pruning using prompt entropy to discard stale-utility prompts. Evaluated on math reasoning benchmarks across models.
AI.result: Significant rollout efficiency gains without sacrificing performance; improved data efficiency; robust across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: History-informed and online-verified prompt selection (HIVE) enables more efficient RL for LLM reasoning tasks by focusing on high-utility prompts and dynamically pruning others; the approach is generalizable to similar settings.

title: Infinite Gaze Generation for Videos with Autoregressive Diffusion
summary: Predicting human gaze in video is fundamental to advancing scene understanding and multimodal interaction. While traditional saliency maps provide spatial probability distributions and scanpaths offer ordered fixations, both abstractions often collapse the fine-grained temporal dynamics of raw gaze. Furthermore, existing models are typically constrained to short-term windows ($\approx$ 3-5s), failing to capture the long-range behavioral dependencies inherent in real-world content. We present a generative framework for infinite-horizon raw gaze prediction in videos of arbitrary length. By leveraging an autoregressive diffusion model, we synthesize gaze trajectories characterized by continuous spatial coordinates and high-resolution timestamps. Our model is conditioned on a saliency-aware visual latent space. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing approaches in long-range spatio-temporal accuracy and trajectory realism.

AI.tldr: Autoregressive diffusion model for infinite-horizon gaze prediction in videos, generating continuous gaze trajectories conditioned on a saliency-aware latent space, surpassing short-term models in long-range spatio-temporal accuracy and realism.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap of limited temporal dynamics in existing gaze models (saliency maps and scanpaths) by enabling long-range, high-resolution gaze trajectories over arbitrarily long video sequences.
AI.method: Use an autoregressive diffusion process to synthesize continuous spatial gaze coordinates with high-resolution timestamps. The model is conditioned on a saliency-aware visual latent space, allowing gaze generation to follow salient content across extended video lengths.
AI.result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations indicate significant improvements over existing approaches in long-range spatio-temporal accuracy and trajectory realism for extended video sequences.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of infinite-horizon raw gaze prediction, enabling richer modeling of human gaze dynamics in videos and potentially improving downstream vision–language and multimodal tasks.

title: SoftMimicGen: A Data Generation System for Scalable Robot Learning in Deformable Object Manipulation
summary: Large-scale robot datasets have facilitated the learning of a wide range of robot manipulation skills, but these datasets remain difficult to collect and scale further, owing to the intractable amount of human time, effort, and cost required. Simulation and synthetic data generation have proven to be an effective alternative to fuel this need for data, especially with the advent of recent work showing that such synthetic datasets can dramatically reduce real-world data requirements and facilitate generalization to novel scenarios unseen in real-world demonstrations. However, this paradigm has been limited to rigid-body tasks, which are easy to simulate. Deformable object manipulation encompasses a large portion of real-world manipulation and remains a crucial gap to address towards increasing adoption of the synthetic simulation data paradigm. In this paper, we introduce SoftMimicGen, an automated data generation pipeline for deformable object manipulation tasks.
