AI.tldr: FB-MEBE is an online zero-shot RL method for quadrupedal robots that combines entropy-based unsupervised exploration with a regularization critic, producing diverse, natural policies from reward-free data and enabling direct hardware deployment without fine-tuning; outperforms other exploration strategies in simulated tasks.
AI.motivation: To enable learning a family of policies from reward-free data and recover optimal policies for any reward at test time, particularly for real-world quadrupedal control. Challenges include lack of task priors and undirected exploration yielding low-diversity data that hamper downstream performance and hardware deployment.
AI.method: Build upon the Forward-Backward (FB) algorithm to an online zero-shot RL framework (FB-MEBE) by (i) maximizing the entropy of the behavior distribution to drive diverse, exploratory data collection and (ii) adding a regularization critic to bias recovered policies toward natural, physically plausible behaviors. Evaluated on real quadrupedal hardware with simulated downstream tasks.
AI.result: FB-MEBE achieves improved performance compared to other exploration strategies across simulated downstream tasks and yields natural policies that can be deployed on hardware without additional finetuning.
AI.conclusion: FB-MEBE demonstrates that coupling entropy-driven exploration with a regularization critic in an online zero-shot RL setting enables practical, hardware-ready policies for quadrupedal control, addressing data diversity and realism without task-specific rewards. Code and videos are available on the project site.

title: C2W-Tune: Cavity-to -Wall Transfer Learning for Thin Atrial Wall Segmentation in 3D Late Gadolinium-enhanced Magnetic Resonance
comment: Submitted this to the International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Medicine (AIME 2026)
summary: Accurate segmentation of the left atrial (LA) wall in 3D late gadolinium-enhanced MRI (LGE-MRI) is essential for wall thickness mapping and fibrosis quantification, yet it remains challenging due to the wall's thinness, complex anatomy, and low contrast. We propose C2W-Tune, a two-stage cavity-to-wall transfer framework that leverages a high-accuracy LA cavity model as an anatomical prior to improve thin-wall delineation. Using a 3D U-Net with a ResNeXt encoder and instance normalization, Stage 1 pre-trains the network to segment the LA cavity, learning robust atrial representations. Stage 2 transfers these weights and adapts the network to LA wall segmentation using a progressive layer-unfreezing schedule to preserve endocardial features while enabling wall-specific refinement. Experiments on the 2018 LA Segmentation Challenge dataset demonstrate substantial gains over an architecture-matched baseline trained from scratch: wall Dice improves from 0.623 to 0.814, and Surface Dice at 1 mm improves from 0.553 to 0.731. Boundary errors were substantially reduced, with the 95th-percentile Hausdorff distance (HD95) decreasing from 2.95 mm to 2.55 mm and the average symmetric surface distance (ASSD) from 0.71 mm to 0.63 mm. Furthermore, even with reduced supervision (70 training volumes sampled from the same training pool), C2W-Tune achieved a Dice score of 0.78 and an HD95 of 3.15 mm, maintaining competitive performance and exceeding multi-class benchmarks that typically report Dice values around 0.6-0.7. These results show that anatomically grounded task transfer with controlled fine-tuning improves boundary accuracy for thin LA wall segmentation in 3D LGE-MRI.

AI.tldr: Two-stage cavity-to-wall transfer (C2W-Tune) using a pre-trained LA cavity model to guide thin-wall segmentation in 3D LGE-MRI, yielding substantial improvements in Dice, surface metrics, and robustness with reduced supervision.
AI.motivation: The LA wall is thin, has complex anatomy, and appears with low contrast in 3D LGE-MRI, making accurate segmentation challenging but essential for wall thickness mapping and fibrosis quantification.
AI.method: A 3D U-Net with a ResNeXt encoder and instance normalization. Stage 1 pre-trains on LA cavity segmentation to learn robust atrial representations. Stage 2 transfers weights with progressive layer unfreezing to adapt to LA wall segmentation, preserving endocardial features while enabling wall-specific refinement.
AI.result: On the 2018 LA Segmentation Challenge dataset, wall Dice improved from 0.623 to 0.814; Surface Dice at 1 mm from 0.553 to 0.731; HD95 from 2.95 mm to 2.55 mm; ASSD from 0.71 mm to 0.63 mm. Under reduced supervision (70 volumes), Dice 0.78 and HD95 3.15 mm; competitive with multi-class benchmarks that typically report Dice ~0.6–0.7.
AI.conclusion: Anatomically grounded task transfer with controlled fine-tuning improves boundary accuracy for thin LA wall segmentation in 3D LGE-MRI and remains robust under limited supervision.

title: Not a fragment, but the whole: Map-based evaluation of data-driven Fire Danger Index models
summary: A growing body of literature has focused on predicting wildfire occurrence using machine learning methods, capitalizing on high-resolution data and fire predictors that canonical process-based frameworks largely ignore. Standard evaluation metrics for an ML classifier, while important, provide a potentially limited measure of the model's operational performance for the Fire Danger Index (FDI) forecast. Furthermore, model evaluation is frequently conducted without adequately accounting for false positive rates, despite their critical relevance in operational contexts. In this paper, we revisit the daily FDI model evaluation paradigm and propose a novel method for evaluating a forest fire forecasting model that is aligned with real-world decision-making. Furthermore, we systematically assess performance in accurately predicting fire activity and the false positives (false alarms). We further demonstrate that an ensemble of ML models improves both fire identification and reduces false positives.

AI.tldr: Proposes a decision-aligned evaluation for daily Fire Danger Index forecasts, explicitly assessing both accurate fire activity prediction and false alarm rates, and shows that an ensemble of ML models improves detection while reducing false positives.
AI.motivation: Operational wildfire forecasting requires evaluation metrics that reflect real decision-making costs and consequences; traditional ML accuracy metrics may misrepresent practical performance and neglect false positives which drive resource allocation and response strategies.
AI.method: Develop and apply a new evaluation framework for daily FDI forecasts that aligns with decision-making; systematically evaluate models on true/false positive performance for fire activity; investigate ensembles to boost detection and curb false alarms.
AI.result: An ensemble of ML models achieves better fire identification and lower false positive rates compared to individual models, under the proposed decision-oriented evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: Decision-aligned evaluation is essential for operational wildfire forecasting, and ensemble ML approaches can provide robust improvements in both detection and false alarm reduction.

title: Relaxed Rigidity with Ray-based Grouping for Dynamic Gaussian Splatting
summary: The reconstruction of dynamic 3D scenes using 3D Gaussian Splatting has shown significant promise. A key challenge, however, remains in modeling realistic motion, as most methods fail to align the motion of Gaussians with real-world physical dynamics. This misalignment is particularly problematic for monocular video datasets, where failing to maintain coherent motion undermines local geometric structure, ultimately leading to degraded reconstruction quality. Consequently, many state-of-the-art approaches rely heavily on external priors, such as optical flow or 2D tracks, to enforce temporal coherence. In this work, we propose a novel method to explicitly preserve the local geometric structure of Gaussians across time in 4D scenes. Our core idea is to introduce a view-space ray grouping strategy that clusters Gaussians intersected by the same ray, considering only those whose $α$-blending weights exceed a threshold. We then apply constraints to these groups to maintain a consistent spatial distribution, effectively preserving their local geometry. This approach enforces a more physically plausible motion model by ensuring that local geometry remains stable over time, eliminating the reliance on external guidance. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method by integrating it into two distinct baseline models. Extensive experiments on challenging monocular datasets show that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving superior temporal consistency and reconstruction quality.

AI.tldr: A method for 4D dynamic 3D reconstruction using 3D Gaussian Splatting that preserves local Gaussian geometry over time via view-space ray grouping. It clusters Gaussians intersected by the same ray (with α above a threshold) and enforces constraints to maintain spatial distribution, reducing dependence on external priors and improving temporal consistency on monocular data. It is integrated into two baselines and validated with monocular datasets.
AI.motivation: Dynamic 3D scene reconstruction with Gaussian splatting suffers from motion misalignment with physical dynamics, especially in monocular videos, leading to degraded geometry unless external priors (optical flow/2D tracks) are used.
AI.method: Introduce a view-space ray grouping strategy: cluster Gaussians intersected by the same camera ray and with α-blending weight above a threshold; apply constraints within each group to maintain consistent spatial distribution over time, enforcing a physically plausible motion model; integrate this constraint mechanism into two baseline models without external guidance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on challenging monocular datasets show significant improvements over existing methods in both temporal consistency and reconstruction quality, without relying on external priors.
AI.conclusion: Preserving local Gaussian geometry over time via ray-based grouping yields a more physically plausible motion model for dynamic 3D reconstruction, improves temporal coherence, and reduces dependence on external guidance; demonstrated across baselines on monocular data.

title: Retraining as Approximate Bayesian Inference
summary: Model retraining is usually treated as an ongoing maintenance task. But as Harrison Katz now argues, retraining can be better understood as approximate Bayesian inference under computational constraints. The gap between a continuously updated belief state and your frozen deployed model is "learning debt," and the retraining decision is a cost minimization problem with a threshold that falls out of your loss function. In this article Katz provides a decision-theoretic framework for retraining policies. The result is evidence-based triggers that replace calendar schedules and make governance auditable. For readers less familiar with the Bayesian and decision-theoretic language, key terms are defined in a glossary at the end of the article.

AI.tldr: Retraining is framed as approximate Bayesian updating under computational constraints, with learning debt guiding cost-based, auditable retraining triggers instead of calendar schedules.
AI.motivation: To optimize retraining decisions under compute limits and provide transparent, auditable governance by formalizing the trade-offs in Bayesian/decision-theoretic terms.
AI.method: A decision-theoretic framework that treats retraining as approximate Bayesian inference, defines learning debt, derives thresholds from the loss function, and yields evidence-based retraining triggers with a glossary for readers.
AI.result: A principled retraining policy that minimizes expected loss under constraints, replacing calendar schedules with auditable, evidence-based triggers.
AI.conclusion: Links retraining decisions to Bayesian and decision-theory principles, offering practical, interpretable, and governance-friendly strategies for model maintenance.

title: Causal-INSIGHT: Probing Temporal Models to Extract Causal Structure
summary:
Understanding directed temporal interactions in multivariate time series is essential for interpreting complex dynamical systems and the predictive models trained on them. We present Causal-INSIGHT, a model-agnostic, post-hoc interpretation framework for extracting model-implied (predictor-dependent), directed, time-lagged influence structure from trained temporal predictors. Rather than inferring causal structure at the level of the data-generating process, Causal-INSIGHT analyzes how a fixed, pre-trained predictor responds to systematic, intervention-inspired input clamping applied at inference time.
  From these responses, we construct directed temporal influence signals that reflect the dependencies the predictor relies on for prediction, and introduce Qbic, a sparsity-aware graph selection criterion that balances predictive fidelity and structural complexity without requiring ground-truth graph labels. Experiments across synthetic, simulated, and realistic benchmarks show that Causal-INSIGHT generalizes across diverse backbone architectures, maintains competitive structural accuracy, and yields significant improvements in temporal delay localization when applied to existing predictors.

AI.tldr: Causal-INSIGHT provides a model-agnostic, post-hoc framework to extract predictor-dependent, directed, time-lagged influences from trained temporal predictors via inference-time input perturbations, with Qbic for sparse graph selection; validated across synthetic, simulated, and realistic benchmarks, showing robust generalization and improved delay localization.
AI.motivation: Interpreting how trained temporal predictors rely on past and cross-series inputs is crucial for understanding, diagnosing, and trusting models. Traditional causal discovery on data-generating processes may misalign with the predictor's actual decision logic; a predictor-centric, time-aware interpretation can reveal actionable dependencies without requiring ground-truth dynamics.
AI.method: Intervention-inspired clamping at inference to perturb inputs; derive directed, time-lagged influence signals that reflect predictor dependencies; introduce Qbic, a sparsity-aware graph selection criterion to balance fidelity vs. complexity without ground-truth graphs; model-agnostic and applicable to diverse backbones.
AI.result: Causal-INSIGHT generalizes across backbone architectures, maintains competitive structural accuracy, and improves temporal delay localization when applied to existing predictors; demonstrated across synthetic, simulated, and realistic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, predictor-centric interpretation framework for multivariate temporal models, combining a post-hoc intervention-based analysis with a principled sparsity criterion to reveal meaningful, time-lagged influence structures.

title: Distributed Real-Time Vehicle Control for Emergency Vehicle Transit: A Scalable Cooperative Method
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
summary: Rapid transit of emergency vehicles is critical for saving lives and reducing property loss but often relies on surrounding ordinary vehicles to cooperatively adjust their driving behaviors. It is important to ensure rapid transit of emergency vehicles while minimizing the impact on ordinary vehicles. Centralized mathematical solver and reinforcement learning are the state-of-the-art methods. The former obtains optimal solutions but is only practical for small-scale scenarios. The latter implicitly learns through extensive centralized training but the trained model exhibits limited scalability to different traffic conditions. Hence, existing methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: high computational cost and lack of scalability. To overcome above limitations, this work proposes a scalable distributed vehicle control method, where vehicles adjust their driving behaviors in a distributed manner online using only local instead of global information. We proved that the proposed distributed method using only local information is approximately equivalent to the one using global information, which enables vehicles to evaluate their candidate states and make approximately optimal decisions in real time without pre-training and with natural adaptability to varying traffic conditions. Then, a distributed conflict resolution mechanism is further proposed to guarantee vehicles' safety by avoiding their decision conflicts, which eliminates the single-point-of-failure risk of centralized methods and provides deterministic safety guarantees that learned methods cannot offer. Compared with existing methods, simulation experiments based on real-world traffic datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves faster decision-making, less impact on ordinary vehicles, and maintains much stronger scalability across different traffic densities and road configurations.

AI.tldr: A scalable distributed vehicle-control framework enables emergency-vehicle priority using only local information, with a distributed conflict-resolution mechanism. It achieves near-equivalence to global-information control, avoids pre-training, and improves real-time performance and scalability over centralized or RL approaches.
AI.motivation: To address two fundamental limitations of state-of-the-art methods: high computational cost and poor scalability of centralized optimization and RL for dynamic, large-scale traffic scenarios involving emergency-vehicle transit.
AI.method: Vehicles independently evaluate candidate states using only local information, selecting approximately optimal actions in real time without pre-training. A distributed conflict resolution mechanism ensures safety by avoiding decision conflicts, removing single points of failure inherent in centralized methods.
AI.result: Simulation experiments on real-world traffic datasets show faster decision-making, reduced impact on ordinary vehicles, and strong scalability across traffic densities and road configurations compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: A locally-informed distributed control approach with a safety-oriented conflict-resolution layer can achieve scalable, real-time, near-optimal emergency-vehicle transit with deterministic safety guarantees, outperforming centralized and reinforcement-learning methods.

title: EcoThink: A Green Adaptive Inference Framework for Sustainable and Accessible Agents
summary: As the Web transitions from static retrieval to generative interaction, the escalating environmental footprint of Large Language Models (LLMs) presents a critical sustainability challenge. Current paradigms indiscriminately apply computation-intensive strategies like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to billions of daily queries, causing LLM overthinking, a redundancy that amplifies carbon emissions and operational barriers. This inefficiency directly undermines UN Sustainable Development Goals 13 (Climate Action) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities) by hindering equitable AI access in resource-constrained regions. To address this, we introduce EcoThink, an energy-aware adaptive inference framework designed to reconcile high-performance AI intelligence with environmental responsibility. EcoThink employs a lightweight, distillation-based router to dynamically assess query complexity, skipping unnecessary reasoning for factoid retrieval while reserving deep computation for complex logic. Extensive evaluations across 9 diverse benchmarks demonstrate that EcoThink reduces inference energy by 40.4% on average (up to 81.9% for web knowledge retrieval) without statistically significant performance loss. By mitigating algorithmic waste, EcoThink offers a scalable path toward a sustainable, inclusive, and energy-efficient generative AI Agent.

AI.tldr: EcoThink is an energy-aware adaptive inference framework that reduces LLM inference energy by selectively skipping reasoning for factoid queries via a distillation-based router.
AI.motivation: The environmental and accessibility costs of running large language models at scale, due to indiscriminate use of compute-intensive reasoning like chain-of-thought, leading to carbon emissions and limited access for resource-constrained regions.
AI.method: A lightweight distillation-based router that dynamically assesses query complexity to skip unnecessary reasoning for factoid retrieval while reserving deep computation for complex logic, enabling adaptive inference paths.
AI.result: Across 9 benchmarks, EcoThink reduces inference energy by 40.4% on average (up to 81.9% for web knowledge retrieval) with no statistically significant performance loss.
AI.conclusion: Reducing algorithmic waste is feasible with energy-aware routing, offering a scalable path toward sustainable and inclusive generative AI.

title: How Class Ontology and Data Scale Affect Audio Transfer Learning
summary: Transfer learning is a crucial concept within deep learning that allows artificial neural networks to benefit from a large pre-training data basis when confronted with a task of limited data. Despite its ubiquitous use and clear benefits, there are still many open questions regarding the inner workings of transfer learning and, in particular, regarding the understanding of when and how well it works. To that extent, we perform a rigorous study focusing on audio-to-audio transfer learning, in which we pre-train various model states on (ontology-based) subsets of AudioSet and fine-tune them on three computer audition tasks, namely acoustic scene recognition, bird activity recognition, and speech command recognition. We report that increasing the number of samples and classes in the pre-training data both have a positive impact on transfer learning. This is, however, generally surpassed by similarity between pre-training and the downstream task, which can lead the model to learn comparable features.

AI.tldr: In audio-to-audio transfer learning, pre-training on AudioSet subsets and fine-tuning on three tasks shows that larger pre-training data helps, but task similarity between pre-training and downstream tasks is the dominant factor in transfer effectiveness.
AI.motivation: To understand the conditions under which transfer learning works in audio domains and identify the balance between data quantity and task similarity.
AI.method: Pre-train multiple model states on ontology-based subsets of AudioSet and fine-tune them on three downstream tasks: acoustic scene recognition, bird activity recognition, and speech command recognition. Systematically vary pre-training data size and class coverage to assess transfer performance.
AI.result: Increasing pre-training samples and classes generally improves transfer performance, but the similarity between the pre-training task and downstream task often yields comparable or stronger gains, indicating that learned features align with downstream task structure.
AI.conclusion: Data quantity in pre-training helps, yet task similarity is a strong predictor of transfer success. When designing audio-domain transfer learning, aligning pre-training data with the downstream task may be more impactful than merely expanding pre-training scale.

title: Interpretable Zero-shot Referring Expression Comprehension with Query-driven Scene Graphs
summary: Zero-shot referring expression comprehension (REC) aims to locate target objects in images given natural language queries without relying on task-specific training data, demanding strong visual understanding capabilities. Existing Vision-Language Models~(VLMs), such as CLIP, commonly address zero-shot REC by directly measuring feature similarities between textual queries and image regions. However, these methods struggle to capture fine-grained visual details and understand complex object relationships. Meanwhile, Large Language Models~(LLMs) excel at high-level semantic reasoning, their inability to directly abstract visual features into textual semantics limits their application in REC tasks. To overcome these limitations, we propose \textbf{SGREC}, an interpretable zero-shot REC method leveraging query-driven scene graphs as structured intermediaries. Specifically, we first employ a VLM to construct a query-driven scene graph that explicitly encodes spatial relationships, descriptive captions, and object interactions relevant to the given query. By leveraging this scene graph, we bridge the gap between low-level image regions and higher-level semantic understanding required by LLMs. Finally, an LLM infers the target object from the structured textual representation provided by the scene graph, responding with detailed explanations for its decisions that ensure interpretability in the inference process. Extensive experiments show that SGREC achieves top-1 accuracy on most zero-shot REC benchmarks, including RefCOCO val (66.78\%), RefCOCO+ testB (53.43\%), and RefCOCOg val (73.28\%), highlighting its strong visual scene understanding.

AI.tldr: SGREC uses a query-driven scene graph as an intermediate representation to solve zero-shot Referring Expression Comprehension, bridging VLMs and LLMs for interpretable reasoning; it achieves strong top-1 results on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of CLIP-like REC in capturing fine-grained visual details and object relations, and the limitations of LLMs in grounding visual features, by introducing an interpretable, structured intermediate representation.
AI.method: A VLM constructs a query-driven scene graph that encodes spatial relations, captions, and object interactions relevant to the query; an LLM reasons over this structured textual graph to identify the target and provides explanations for its decisions, enabling interpretability.
AI.result: Top-1 accuracy reported on several zero-shot REC benchmarks (e.g., RefCOCO val 66.78%, RefCOCO+ testB 53.43%, RefCOCOg val 73.28%), indicating strong visual scene understanding.
AI.conclusion: SGREC demonstrates that structured scene-graph intermediaries can effectively bridge vision and language for zero-shot REC, offering interpretable reasoning and competitive performance across benchmarks.

title: Voxtral TTS
summary: We introduce Voxtral TTS, an expressive multilingual text-to-speech model that generates natural speech from as little as 3 seconds of reference audio. Voxtral TTS adopts a hybrid architecture that combines auto-regressive generation of semantic speech tokens with flow-matching for acoustic tokens. These tokens are encoded and decoded with Voxtral Codec, a speech tokenizer trained from scratch with a hybrid VQ-FSQ quantization scheme. In human evaluations conducted by native speakers, Voxtral TTS is preferred for multilingual voice cloning due to its naturalness and expressivity, achieving a 68.4\% win rate over ElevenLabs Flash v2.5. We release the model weights under a CC BY-NC license.

AI.tldr: Voxtral TTS proposes an expressive multilingual TTS that can clone voices from as little as 3 seconds of reference audio, using a hybrid architecture (auto-regressive semantic tokens + flow-matching acoustic tokens) and a self-trained Voxtral Codec tokenizer with hybrid VQ-FSQ quantization. In native-speaker tests, it outperforms ElevenLabs Flash v2.5 in naturalness/expressivity (68.4% win rate); model weights are released under CC BY-NC.
AI.motivation: Enable high-quality, expressive multilingual TTS with minimal reference audio and a robust, learnable speech tokenizer to improve naturalness and voice cloning capabilities across languages.
AI.method: Hybrid architecture combining auto-regressive generation of semantic speech tokens with flow-matching for acoustic tokens. Semantic and acoustic tokens are encoded/decoded by Voxtral Codec, a speech tokenizer trained from scratch using a hybrid VQ-FSQ quantization scheme.
AI.result: In native-speaker human evaluations, Voxtral TTS is preferred for multilingual voice cloning due to naturalness and expressivity, achieving a 68.4% win rate over ElevenLabs Flash v2.5.
AI.conclusion: Model weights released under CC BY-NC; highlights include a novel tokenizer (Voxtral Codec) and a hybrid token-generation approach that enables high-quality, multilingual voice cloning from very limited reference audio.

title: Interpretable PM2.5 Forecasting for Urban Air Quality: A Comparative Study of Operational Time-Series Models
summary: Accurate short-term air-quality forecasting is essential for public health protection and urban management, yet many recent forecasting frameworks rely on complex, data-intensive, and computationally demanding models. This study investigates whether lightweight and interpretable forecasting approaches can provide competitive performance for hourly PM2.5 prediction in Beijing, China. Using multi-year pollutant and meteorological time-series data, we developed a leakage-aware forecasting workflow that combined chronological data partitioning, preprocessing, feature selection, and exogenous-driver modeling under the Perfect Prognosis setting. Three forecasting families were evaluated: SARIMAX, Facebook Prophet, and NeuralProphet. To assess practical deployment behavior, the models were tested under two adaptive regimes: weekly walk-forward refitting and frozen forecasting with online residual correction. Results showed clear differences in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. Under walk-forward refitting, Facebook Prophet achieved the strongest completed performance, with an MAE of $37.61$ and an RMSE of $50.10$, while also requiring substantially less execution time than NeuralProphet. In the frozen-model regime, online residual correction improved Facebook Prophet and SARIMAX, with corrected SARIMAX yielding the lowest overall error (MAE $32.50$; RMSE $46.85$). NeuralProphet remained less accurate and less stable across both regimes, and residual correction did not improve its forecasts. Notably, corrected Facebook Prophet reached nearly the same error as its walk-forward counterpart while reducing runtime from $15$ min $21.91$ sec to $46.60$ sec. These findings show that lightweight additive forecasting strategies can remain highly competitive for urban air-quality prediction, offering a practical balance between accuracy, interpretability, ...

AI.tldr: Lightweight additive models (SARIMAX, Prophet, NeuralProphet) under leakage-aware Perfect Prognosis can approach heavier models for hourly Beijing PM2.5 forecasting, with Prophet excelling under walk-forward and SARIMAX performing best in a frozen, residual-corrected regime; NeuralProphet underperforms.
AI.motivation: Address the need for accurate short-term air-quality forecasts using interpretable, computationally efficient methods, reducing reliance on data-intensive models.
AI.method: Use multi-year pollutant and meteorological time-series; leakage-aware workflow with chronological data partitioning, preprocessing, feature selection, exogenous drivers; evaluate three models (SARIMAX, Prophet, NeuralProphet); test under weekly walk-forward refitting and frozen forecasting with online residual correction.
AI.result: Walk-forward Prophet yields MAE 37.61, RMSE 50.10, with faster run time than NeuralProphet; in frozen regime, online residual correction improves Prophet and SARIMAX, with corrected SARIMAX attaining MAE 32.50, RMSE 46.85; NeuralProphet less accurate/stable and not improved by residuals; corrected Prophet reduces run time from 15m21.91s to 46.60s while maintaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight additive forecasting strategies can be competitive for urban air-quality prediction, offering a practical balance between accuracy and interpretability; model choice may depend on deployment regime and the benefit of residual corrections.

title: Improving Fine-Grained Rice Leaf Disease Detection via Angular-Compactness Dual Loss Learning
summary: Early detection of rice leaf diseases is critical, as rice is a staple crop supporting a substantial share of the world's population. Timely identification of these diseases enables more effective intervention and significantly reduces the risk of large-scale crop losses. However, traditional deep learning models primarily rely on cross entropy loss, which often struggles with high intra-class variance and inter-class similarity, common challenges in plant pathology datasets. To tackle this, we propose a dual-loss framework that combines Center Loss and ArcFace Loss to enhance fine-grained classification of rice leaf diseases. The method is applied into three state-of-the-art backbone architectures: InceptionNetV3, DenseNet201, and EfficientNetB0 trained on the public Rice Leaf Dataset. Our approach achieves significant performance gains, with accuracies of 99.6%, 99.2% and 99.2% respectively. The results demonstrate that angular margin-based and center-based constraints substantially boost the discriminative strength of feature embeddings. In particular, the framework does not require major architectural modifications, making it efficient and practical for real-world deployment in farming environments.

AI.tldr: A dual-loss framework combining Center Loss and ArcFace Loss improves fine-grained rice leaf disease classification across three backbones on the Rice Leaf Dataset, achieving near-perfect accuracies with minimal architectural changes.
AI.motivation: To address high intra-class variance and inter-class similarity in plant pathology datasets, where standard cross-entropy loss underperforms for fine-grained tasks.
AI.method: Integrate Center Loss (for compact class feature centers) with ArcFace Loss (angular margin-based discrimination) into training of InceptionNetV3, DenseNet201, and EfficientNetB0. No major architectural changes; applies to a public Rice Leaf Dataset.
AI.result: Significant performance gains: 99.6% (InceptionNetV3), 99.2% (DenseNet201), 99.2% (EfficientNetB0). Indicates angular and center-based constraints boost feature discriminability.
AI.conclusion: Angular-margin and center-based constraints effectively enhance discriminative embeddings for fine-grained plant disease classification and are hardware-efficient for real-world deployment.

title: Missing-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Unified Microservice Incident Management
summary: Automated incident management is critical for microservice reliability. While recent unified frameworks leverage multimodal data for joint optimization, they unrealistically assume perfect data completeness. In practice, network fluctuations and agent failures frequently cause missing modalities. Existing approaches relying on static placeholders introduce imputation noise that masks anomalies and degrades performance. To address this, we propose ARMOR, a robust self-supervised framework designed for missing modality scenarios. ARMOR features: (i) a modality-specific asymmetric encoder that isolates distribution disparities among metrics, logs, and traces; and (ii) a missing-aware gated fusion mechanism utilizing learnable placeholders and dynamic bias compensation to prevent cross-modal interference from incomplete inputs. By employing self-supervised auto-regression with mask-guided reconstruction, ARMOR jointly optimizes anomaly detection (AD), failure triage (FT), and root cause localization (RCL). AD and RCL require no fault labels, while FT relies solely on failure-type annotations for the downstream classifier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ARMOR achieves state-of-the-art performance under complete data conditions and maintains robust diagnostic accuracy even with severe modality loss.

AI.tldr: A robust self-supervised framework for missing-modality incident management (ARMOR) that uses modality-specific encoders and a missing-aware fusion mechanism; it achieves state-of-the-art results with complete data and remains robust when modalities are lost, by leveraging auto-regressive self-supervision for AD, FT, and RCL.
AI.motivation: Practical MIS (microservice incident systems) face frequent missing modalities due to network fluctuations and agent failures. Existing methods rely on placeholders or require complete data, which introduces imputation noise and degrades anomaly detection and localization. A self-supervised, missing-aware approach is needed to maintain performance without fault labels.
AI.method: 1) Modality-specific asymmetric encoders to align distributions across metrics, logs, and traces. 2) Missing-aware gated fusion with learnable placeholders and dynamic bias compensation to prevent cross-modal interference from partial inputs. 3) Self-supervised auto-regression with mask-guided reconstruction. 4) Joint optimization for anomaly detection (no fault labels), failure triage (requires failure-type annotations for downstream classifier), and root cause localization (no fault labels).
AI.result: ARMOR achieves state-of-the-art performance under complete data and maintains robust diagnostic accuracy under severe modality loss, demonstrating resilience to missing modalities in realistic settings.
AI.conclusion: A principled, self-supervised approach that mitigates imputation noise from missing modalities and preserves diagnostic capabilities (AD, FT, RCL) in microservice incident management; compatible with real-world networks and agent failures, with potential for extension to additional modalities or tasks.

title: Few TensoRF: Enhance the Few-shot on Tensorial Radiance Fields
summary: This paper presents Few TensoRF, a 3D reconstruction framework that combines TensorRF's efficient tensor based representation with FreeNeRF's frequency driven few shot regularization. Using TensorRF to significantly accelerate rendering speed and introducing frequency and occlusion masks, the method improves stability and reconstruction quality under sparse input views. Experiments on the Synthesis NeRF benchmark show that Few TensoRF method improves the average PSNR from 21.45 dB (TensorRF) to 23.70 dB, with the fine tuned version reaching 24.52 dB, while maintaining TensorRF's fast \(\approx10-15\) minute training time. Experiments on the THuman 2.0 dataset further demonstrate competitive performance in human body reconstruction, achieving 27.37 - 34.00 dB with only eight input images. These results highlight Few TensoRF as an efficient and data effective solution for real-time 3D reconstruction across diverse scenes.

AI.tldr: Few TensoRF blends TensorRF's fast tensor-based rendering with FreeNeRF's frequency-driven few-shot regularization to improve 3D reconstruction quality under sparse views, while preserving TensorRF's fast training times.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between rendering speed, data efficiency, and reconstruction quality in neural radiance fields when only few input views are available.
AI.method: Integrates TensorRF's tensor-based representation for fast rendering with FreeNeRF's frequency-driven regularization and introduces frequency and occlusion masks to enhance stability and quality under sparse views.
AI.result: On Synthesis NeRF: PSNR improves from 21.45 dB (TensorRF) to 23.70 dB, with a fine-tuned version reaching 24.52 dB, while training time remains around 10–15 minutes. On THuman 2.0: 27.37–34.00 dB with only eight images, showing competitive human body reconstruction performance.
AI.conclusion: Few TensoRF is an efficient, data-efficient solution for real-time 3D reconstruction across diverse scenes, combining speed and accuracy benefits from TensorRF and FreeNeRF.

title: Agent Factories for High Level Synthesis: How Far Can General-Purpose Coding Agents Go in Hardware Optimization?
summary:
We present an empirical study of how far general-purpose coding agents -- without hardware-specific training -- can optimize hardware designs from high-level algorithmic specifications. We introduce an agent factory, a two-stage pipeline that constructs and coordinates multiple autonomous optimization agents.
  In Stage~1, the pipeline decomposes a design into sub-kernels, independently optimizes each using pragma and code-level transformations, and formulates an Integer Linear Program (ILP) to assemble globally promising configurations under an area constraint. In Stage~2, it launches $N$ expert agents over the top ILP solutions, each exploring cross-function optimizations such as pragma recombination, loop fusion, and memory restructuring that are not captured by sub-kernel decomposition.
  We evaluate the approach on 12 kernels from HLS-Eval and Rodinia-HLS using Claude Code (Opus~4.5/4.6) with AMD Vitis HLS. Scaling from 1 to 10 agents yields a mean $8.27\times$ speedup over baseline, with larger gains on harder benchmarks: streamcluster exceeds $20\times$ and kmeans reaches approximately $10\times$. Across benchmarks, agents consistently rediscover known hardware optimization patterns without domain-specific training, and the best designs often do not originate from top-ranked ILP candidates, indicating that global optimization exposes improvements missed by sub-kernel search. These results establish agent scaling as a practical and effective axis for HLS optimization.

AI.tldr: Two-stage, agent-based optimization framework (agent factory) for HLS design. Stage 1 decomposes designs into sub-kernels, applies transformations, and uses ILP to assemble promising configurations under an area constraint; Stage 2 runs N expert agents on top ILP solutions to explore cross-function optimizations. Evaluated on 12 kernels with Claude Code and Vitis HLS; increasing agents from 1 to 10 yields ~8.27x mean speedup over baseline, with >20x on hard cases. Best designs can come from non-top ILP candidates, showing global optimization finds improvements missed by sub-kernel search. Conclusion: agent scaling is a practical, effective axis for HLS optimization.
AI.motivation: Assess whether general-purpose coding agents can optimally design hardware from high-level specifications without domain-specific training, and whether scaling the number of agents yields substantial gains beyond sub-kernel ILP-driven search.
AI.method: Stage 1: Decompose design into sub-kernels, apply pragma and code-level transformations, formulate an ILP to assemble globally promising configurations under an area constraint. Stage 2: Launch N expert agents over the top ILP solutions to explore cross-function optimizations such as pragma recombination, loop fusion, and memory restructuring. Evaluation on 12 kernels from HLS-Eval and Rodinia-HLS using Claude Code with AMD Vitis HLS. Vary agent count from 1 to 10.
AI.result: Mean speedup of 8.27× over baseline when scaling from 1 to 10 agents; larger gains on harder benchmarks (e.g., streamcluster >20×, kmeans ≈10×). Agents consistently rediscover known hardware optimization patterns without domain-specific training; best designs often do not originate from top-ranked ILP candidates, indicating global optimization reveals improvements missed by sub-kernel search.
AI.conclusion: Agent scaling is a practical and effective axis for HLS optimization, enabling substantial performance improvements by leveraging global search across sub-kernel decompositions and cross-function optimizations.

title: An Integrative Genome-Scale Metabolic Modeling and Machine Learning Framework for Predicting and Optimizing Biofuel-Relevant Biomass Production in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
summary: Saccharomyces cerevisiae is a cornerstone organism in industrial biotechnology, valued for its genetic tractability and robust fermentative capacity. Accurately predicting biomass flux across diverse environmental and genetic perturbations remains a significant challenge for rational strain design. We present a computational framework combining the Yeast9 genome-scale metabolic model with machine learning and optimization to predict, interpret, and enhance biomass flux. Flux balance analysis generated 2,000 flux profiles by varying glucose, oxygen, and ammonium uptake rates. Random Forest and XGBoost regressors achieved R2 of 0.99989 and 0.9990, respectively. A variational autoencoder revealed four distinct metabolic clusters, and SHAP analysis identified glycolysis, the TCA cycle, and lipid biosynthesis as key biomass determinants. In silico overexpression achieved a biomass flux of 0.979 gDW/hr, while Bayesian optimization of nutrient constraints produced a 12-fold increase (0.0858 to 1.041 gDW/hr). A generative adversarial network proposed stoichiometrically feasible novel flux configurations. This framework demonstrates how genome-scale simulation, interpretable ML, and generative modeling can advance yeast metabolic engineering.

AI.tldr: Integrates Yeast9 GEM with ML and optimization to predict and optimize biomass flux in S. cerevisiae; achieves high predictive accuracy and actionable design insights.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of predicting biomass flux across environmental and genetic perturbations for rational strain design by combining genome-scale modeling with machine learning and generative modeling.
AI.method: Generate 2,000 flux profiles via Flux Balance Analysis by varying glucose, oxygen, and ammonium uptake; train Random Forest and XGBoost regressors; use a variational autoencoder to identify metabolic clusters; apply SHAP for feature attribution; perform in silico overexpression; optimize nutrient constraints with Bayesian optimization; develop a generative adversarial network to propose new flux configurations.
AI.result: Random Forest R2 = 0.99989; XGBoost R2 = 0.9990; VAEs reveal four metabolic clusters; SHAP identifies glycolysis, TCA cycle, and lipid biosynthesis as key biomass determinants; in silico overexpression yields biomass flux of 0.979 gDW/hr; Bayesian optimization increases flux 12-fold from 0.0858 to 1.041 gDW/hr; GAN proposes stoichiometrically feasible novel flux configurations.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a synergistic framework that integrates genome-scale simulation, interpretable machine learning, and generative modeling to advance yeast metabolic engineering.

title: GDPO-Listener: Expressive Interactive Head Generation via Auto-Regressive Flow Matching and Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization
summary: Generating realistic 3D head motion for dyadic interactions is a significant challenge in virtual human synthesis. While recent methods achieve impressive results with speaking heads, they frequently suffer from the `Regression-to-the-Mean' problem in listener motions, collapsing into static faces, and lack the parameter space for complex nonverbal motions. In this paper, we propose GDPO-Listener, a novel framework that achieves highly expressive speaking and listening motion generation. First, we introduce an Auto-Regressive Flow Matching architecture enabling stable supervised learning. Second, to overcome kinematic stillness, we apply the Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization (GDPO). By isolating reward normalization across distinct FLAME parameter groups, GDPO explicitly incentivizes high variance expressive generations. Finally, we enable explicit semantic text control for customizable responses. Extensive evaluations across the Seamless Interaction and DualTalk datasets demonstrate superior performance compared to existing baselines on long-term kinematic variance, visual expressivity and semantic controllability.

AI.tldr: GDPO-Listener introduces an Auto-Regressive Flow Matching framework combined with Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization to generate expressive, long-horizon 3D head motions for dyadic interactions, with semantic text control and improved variance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Addresses the Regression-to-the-Mean issue in listener motions and limited expressivity in nonverbal dynamics, aiming for high-variance, long-term, natural facial motion with controllable semantics.
AI.method: Auto-Regressive Flow Matching for stable supervised learning; GDPO decouples reward normalization across FLAME parameter groups to promote expressive motion; includes explicit semantic text control for customizable responses.
AI.result: Evaluations on Seamless Interaction and DualTalk datasets show superior long-term kinematic variance, visual expressivity, and semantic controllability compared with existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: GDPO-Listener provides a robust framework for expressive speaking and listening motion in virtual humans, mitigating static-face degeneration and enabling controllable, varied nonverbal behavior.

title: R-C2: Cycle-Consistent Reinforcement Learning Improves Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Robust perception and reasoning require consistency across sensory modalities. Yet current multimodal models often violate this principle, yielding contradictory predictions for visual and textual representations of the same concept. Rather than masking these failures with standard voting mechanisms, which can amplify systematic biases, we show that cross-modal inconsistency provides a rich and natural signal for learning. We introduce RC2, a reinforcement learning framework that resolves internal conflicts by enforcing cross-modal cycle consistency. By requiring a model to perform backward inference, switch modalities, and reliably reconstruct the answer through forward inference, we obtain a dense, label-free reward. This cyclic constraint encourages the model to align its internal representations autonomously. Optimizing for this structure mitigates modality-specific errors and improves reasoning accuracy by up to 7.6 points. Our results suggest that advanced reasoning emerges not only from scaling data, but also from enforcing a structurally consistent understanding of the world.

AI.tldr: Introduces RC2, a cross-modal cycle-consistency reinforcement learning framework that enforces backward inference, modality switching, and forward reconstruction to align visual and textual representations, yielding a dense, label-free reward and up to 7.6-point gains in reasoning accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models often produce inconsistent predictions across modalities, revealing a hidden failure signal. Standard voting can mask biases and errors; a stronger internal consistency signal is desirable to improve reasoning.
AI.method: RC2 trains via cross-modal cycle consistency: perform backward inference, switch modalities, and reliably reconstruct the answer through forward inference. This creates a dense, label-free reward that guides the model to align its internal representations across modalities.
AI.result: Optimization under this cyclic constraint reduces modality-specific errors and improves reasoning accuracy by up to 7.6 points, indicating stronger cross-modal coherence leads to better reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: Structural, cycle-based consistency across modalities is a powerful inductive bias for multimodal reasoning. The key payoff comes from enforcing consistency internally rather than solely scaling data or model size.

title: Revisiting On-Policy Distillation: Empirical Failure Modes and Simple Fixes
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) is appealing for large language model (LLM) post-training because it evaluates teacher feedback on student-generated rollouts rather than fixed teacher traces. In long-horizon settings, however, the common sampled-token variant is fragile: it reduces distribution matching to a one-token signal and becomes increasingly unreliable as rollouts drift away from prefixes the teacher commonly visits. We revisit OPD from the estimator and implementation sides. Theoretically, token-level OPD is biased relative to sequence-level reverse-KL, but it has a much tighter worst-case variance bound; our toy study shows the same tradeoff empirically, with stronger future-reward coupling producing higher gradient variance and less stable learning. Empirically, we identify three failure modes of sampled-token OPD: an imbalanced one-token signal, unreliable teacher guidance on student-generated prefixes, and distortions caused by tokenizer or special-token mismatch. We address these issues with teacher top-K local support matching, implemented as truncated reverse-KL with top-p rollout sampling and special-token masking. Across single-task math reasoning and multi-task agentic-plus-math training, this objective yields more stable optimization and better downstream performance than sampled-token OPD.

AI.tldr: Token-level on-policy distillation is biased but has lower worst-case variance; a top-K local support matching remedy with truncated reverse-KL and top-p rollout sampling improves stability and downstream performance over standard sampled-token OPD in long-horizon LLM post-training.
AI.motivation: OPD evaluates teacher feedback on student-generated rollouts in long-horizon settings, avoiding reliance on fixed teacher traces; however, sampled-token OPD is fragile as rollouts drift away from teacher prefixes. A stable, effective objective is needed for post-training of LLMs.
AI.method: The paper analyzes token-level OPD vs sequence-level reverse-KL, noting a bias–variance trade-off with token-level OPD. It identifies three failure modes of sampled-token OPD (imbalanced token signal, unreliable guidance on student prefixes, and tokenizer/special-token mismatches). It then introduces teacher top-K local support matching as a truncated reverse-KL objective, implemented with top-p rollout sampling and special-token masking.
AI.result: Empirically, the proposed objective yields more stable optimization and better downstream performance than sampled-token OPD across single-task math reasoning and multi-task agentic-plus-math training.
AI.conclusion: Top-K local support matching mitigates major failure modes of token-level OPD and provides a practical, more stable alternative for long-horizon OPD in LLM post-training.

title: VideoTIR: Accurate Understanding for Long Videos with Efficient Tool-Integrated Reasoning
summary: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often suffer from hallucinations in long video understanding (LVU), primarily due to the imbalance between textual and visual tokens. Observing that MLLMs handle short visual inputs well, recent LVU works alleviate hallucinations by automatically parsing the vast visual data into manageable segments that can be effectively processed by MLLMs. SFT-based tool-calling methods can serve this purpose, but they typically require vast amounts of fine-grained, high-quality data and suffer from constrained tool-calling trajectories. We propose a novel VideoTIR that leverages Reinforcement Learning (RL) to encourage proper usage of comprehensive multi-level toolkits for efficient long video understanding. VideoTIR explores both Zero-RL and SFT cold-starting to enable MLLMs to retrieve and focus on meaningful video segments/images/regions, enhancing long video understanding both accurately and efficiently. To reduce redundant tool-calling, we propose Toolkit Action Grouped Policy Optimization (TAGPO), which enhances the efficiency of the calling process through stepwise reward assignment and reuse of failed rollouts. Additionally, we develop a sandbox-based trajectory synthesis framework to generate high-quality trajectories data. Extensive experiments on three long-video QA benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our method.

AI.tldr: VideoTIR is an RL-based framework for long video understanding that manages tool usage with a multi-level toolkit, featuring TAGPO for efficient tool-calling and sandbox-based trajectory synthesis, achieving improved accuracy and efficiency on three long-video QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long video understanding suffers from hallucinations due to textual-visual token imbalance and the excessive visual data in LVU; existing SFT tool-calling requires large high-quality datasets and can be inefficient. A learnable RL-based approach that can retrieve and focus on meaningful segments and reduce redundant tool calls is needed.
AI.method: VideoTIR employs reinforcement learning to optimize the usage of a comprehensive multi-level toolkit for long videos. It explores Zero-RL and SFT cold-starting to retrieve relevant segments/images/regions. It introduces Toolkit Action Grouped Policy Optimization (TAGPO) to assign stepwise rewards and reuse failed rollouts, reducing redundant tool-calling. A sandbox-based trajectory synthesis framework generates high-quality trajectories to train and evaluate. Experiments evaluate on three long-video QA benchmarks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that VideoTIR improves accuracy and efficiency in long video understanding, effectively guiding MLLMs to retrieve and focus on meaningful visual content while reducing unnecessary tool-calling.
AI.conclusion: VideoTIR with TAGPO and sandboxed trajectory synthesis offers a scalable, efficient solution to LVU, mitigating hallucinations and enabling accurate long-video QA by structured multi-level tool usage.

title: Back to Basics: Revisiting ASR in the Age of Voice Agents
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems have achieved near-human accuracy on curated benchmarks, yet still fail in real-world voice agents under conditions that current evaluations do not systematically cover. Without diagnostic tools that isolate specific failure factors, practitioners cannot anticipate which conditions, in which languages, will cause what degree of degradation. We introduce WildASR, a multilingual (four-language) diagnostic benchmark sourced entirely from real human speech that factorizes ASR robustness along three axes: environmental degradation, demographic shift, and linguistic diversity. Evaluating seven widely used ASR systems, we find severe and uneven performance degradation, and model robustness does not transfer across languages or conditions. Critically, models often hallucinate plausible but unspoken content under partial or degraded inputs, creating concrete safety risks for downstream agent behavior. Our results demonstrate that targeted, factor-isolated evaluation is essential for understanding and improving ASR reliability in production systems. Besides the benchmark itself, we also present three analytical tools that practitioners can use to guide deployment decisions.

AI.tldr: WildASR is a multilingual diagnostic benchmark for evaluating ASR robustness across real-world conditions, revealing severe and uneven degradation and safety risks from hallucinations; it factorizes robustness along environmental, demographic, and linguistic dimensions and provides three analytical tools for deployment decisions.
AI.motivation: Current ASR benchmarks and evaluations do not systematically cover real-world failure factors across languages, environments, and user demographics, making it hard to anticipate degradation in production.
AI.method: Construct a four-language, real-speech dataset (WildASR) and factorize ASR robustness along three axes: environmental degradation, demographic shift, and linguistic diversity. Evaluate seven widely used ASR systems, analyze degradation patterns and hallucinations under degraded inputs, and present three analytical tools to guide deployment.
AI.result: Found severe and uneven performance degradation across languages and conditions; robustness does not transfer across languages or conditions; models often hallucinate plausible content under degraded inputs, posing safety risks; targeted, factor-isolated evaluation is essential; introduced three practical analytical tools.
AI.conclusion: Factor-isolated evaluation is necessary for reliable production deployment of ASR. The benchmark and the accompanying tools help practitioners make informed deployment decisions and improve ASR reliability.

title: Spatiotemporal System Forecasting with Irregular Time Steps via Masked Autoencoder
summary: Predicting high-dimensional dynamical systems with irregular time steps presents significant challenges for current data-driven algorithms. These irregularities arise from missing data, sparse observations, or adaptive computational techniques, reducing prediction accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose a novel method: a Physics-Spatiotemporal Masked Autoencoder. This method integrates convolutional autoencoders for spatial feature extraction with masked autoencoders optimised for irregular time series, leveraging attention mechanisms to reconstruct the entire physical sequence in a single prediction pass. The model avoids the need for data imputation while preserving physical integrity of the system. Here, 'physics' refers to high-dimensional fields generated by underlying dynamical systems, rather than the enforcement of explicit physical constraints or PDE residuals. We evaluate this approach on multiple simulated datasets and real-world ocean temperature data. The results demonstrate that our method achieves significant improvements in prediction accuracy, robustness to nonlinearities, and computational efficiency over traditional convolutional and recurrent network methods. The model shows potential for capturing complex spatiotemporal patterns without requiring domain-specific knowledge, with applications in climate modelling, fluid dynamics, ocean forecasting, environmental monitoring, and scientific computing.

AI.tldr: Novel physics-informed disguise? No. It presents a Physics-Spatiotemporal Masked Autoencoder for high-dimensional, irregularly timed data, avoiding imputation and aiming for accurate, robust predictions in one pass.
AI.motivation: Irregular time steps due to missing data, sparse observations, or adaptive computation impede current data-driven models on high-dimensional dynamical systems. A method that handles irregular time steps without imputing data and preserves physical integrity is needed.
AI.method: A hybrid architecture: convolutional autoencoders extract spatial features; a masked autoencoder handles irregular time series; attention mechanisms enable reconstruction of the entire spatiotemporal sequence in a single pass; the approach eschews explicit physical constraints or PDE residuals and treats 'physics' as high-dimensional fields rather than enforcing equations.
AI.result: Evaluated on simulated datasets and real-world ocean temperature data. Reported improvements in prediction accuracy, robustness to nonlinearities, and computational efficiency relative to traditional convolutional and recurrent models.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows potential for capturing complex spatiotemporal patterns without domain-specific knowledge, with broad applicability to climate modelling, fluid dynamics, ocean forecasting, environmental monitoring, and scientific computing.

title: CARE: Training-Free Controllable Restoration for Medical Images via Dual-Latent Steering
summary: Medical image restoration is essential for improving the usability of noisy, incomplete, and artifact-corrupted clinical scans, yet existing methods often rely on task-specific retraining and offer limited control over the trade-off between faithful reconstruction and prior-driven enhancement. This lack of controllability is especially problematic in clinical settings, where overly aggressive restoration may introduce hallucinated details or alter diagnostically important structures. In this work, we propose CARE, a training-free controllable restoration framework for real-world medical images that explicitly balances structure preservation and prior-guided refinement during inference. CARE uses a dual-latent restoration strategy, in which one branch enforces data fidelity and anatomical consistency while the other leverages a generative prior to recover missing or degraded information. A risk-aware adaptive controller dynamically adjusts the contribution of each branch based on restoration uncertainty and local structural reliability, enabling conservative or enhancement-focused restoration modes without additional model training. We evaluate CARE on noisy and incomplete medical imaging scenarios and show that it achieves strong restoration quality while better preserving clinically relevant structures and reducing the risk of implausible reconstructions and show that it achieves strong restoration quality while better preserving clinically relevant structures and reducing the risk of implausible reconstructions. The proposed approach offers a practical step toward safer, more controllable, and more deployment-ready medical image restoration.

AI.tldr: Proposes CARE, a training-free, controllable medical image restoration framework that uses a dual-latent strategy and a risk-aware controller to balance data fidelity and prior-driven enhancement at inference, enabling safe, adjustable restoration without retraining.
AI.motivation: Current medical image restoration methods rely on task-specific retraining and offer limited control over the trade-off between faithful reconstruction and prior-driven enhancement, risking hallucinations in clinical settings.
AI.method: A dual-latent restoration design: (1) a data-fidelity/anatomical-consistency branch, and (2) a generative-prior branch for degraded information recovery. A risk-aware adaptive controller dynamically tunes each branch’s contribution based on restoration uncertainty and local structural reliability, enabling conservative or enhancement-focused inference without additional training.
AI.result: Evaluations on noisy/incomplete medical imaging show strong restoration quality with better preservation of clinically relevant structures and reduced risk of implausible reconstructions, contributing to safer, more deployable restoration.
AI.conclusion: CARE offers a practical, training-free path to controllable, safer medical image restoration suitable for real-world deployment, balancing fidelity and prior-guided refinement during inference.

title: Training the Knowledge Base through Evidence Distillation and Write-Back Enrichment
summary: The knowledge base in a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system is typically assembled once and never revised, even though the facts a query requires are often fragmented across documents and buried in irrelevant content. We argue that the knowledge base should be treated as a trainable component and propose WriteBack-RAG, a framework that uses labeled examples to identify where retrieval succeeds, isolate the relevant documents, and distill them into compact knowledge units that are indexed alongside the original corpus. Because the method modifies only the corpus, it can be applied once as an offline preprocessing step and combined with any RAG pipeline. Across four RAG methods, six benchmarks, and two LLM backbones, WriteBack-RAG improves every evaluated setting, with gains averaging +2.14%. Cross-method transfer experiments further show that the distilled knowledge benefits RAG pipelines other than the one used to produce it, confirming that the improvement resides in the corpus itself.

AI.tldr: Treat the retrieval-augmented knowledge base as a trainable, offline-preprocessed component by distilling relevant documents into compact knowledge units that augment the corpus, improving RAG performance across methods and backbones.
AI.motivation: RAG systems rely on static knowledge bases that may misalign with user queries, since relevant facts are dispersed across documents and buried in noise. A trainable KB that can be updated via labeled signals can improve retrieval precision and factual coverage.
AI.method: Introduce WriteBack-RAG: use labeled examples to identify where retrieval succeeds, extract the relevant documents, and distill them into compact knowledge units, which are indexed alongside the original corpus. This offline preprocessing step is compatible with any RAG pipeline and does not modify the model—only the corpus.
AI.result: Across four RAG methods, six benchmarks, and two LLM backbones, WriteBack-RAG yields improvements in every setting, averaging a +2.14% gain. Cross-method transfer experiments show the distilled knowledge benefits RAG pipelines beyond the producer method, indicating the gains arise from corpus enhancements rather than model changes.
AI.conclusion: Distilling and indexing evidence-backed, compact knowledge units into the corpus yields corpus-level improvements that generalize across RAG pipelines, validating the approach as a practical offline preprocess to enhance retrieval-based generation.

title: GeoNDC: A Queryable Neural Data Cube for Planetary-Scale Earth Observation
summary: Satellite Earth observation has accumulated massive spatiotemporal archives essential for monitoring environmental change, yet these remain organized as discrete raster files, making them costly to store, transmit, and query. We present GeoNDC, a queryable neural data cube that encodes planetary-scale Earth observation data as a continuous spatiotemporal implicit neural field, enabling on-demand queries and continuous-time reconstruction without full decompression. Experiments on a 20-year global MODIS MCD43A4 reflectance record (7 bands, 5\,km, 8-day sampling) show that the learned representation supports direct spatiotemporal queries on consumer hardware. On Sentinel-2 imagery (10\,m), continuous temporal parameterization recovers cloud-free dynamics with high fidelity ($R^2 > 0.85$) under simulated 2-km cloud occlusion. On HiGLASS biophysical products (LAI and FPAR), GeoNDC attains near-perfect accuracy ($R^2 > 0.98$). The representation compresses the 20-year MODIS archive to 0.44\,GB -- approximately 95:1 relative to an optimized Int16 baseline -- with high spectral fidelity (mean $R^2 > 0.98$, mean RMSE $= 0.021$). These results suggest GeoNDC offers a unified AI-native representation for planetary-scale Earth observation, complementing raw archives with a compact, analysis-ready data layer integrating query, reconstruction, and compression in a single framework.

AI.tldr: GeoNDC encodes large-scale Earth observation archives as a continuous spatiotemporal implicit neural field (a neural data cube) to enable on-demand queries and time-continuous reconstruction without full decompression, achieving strong fidelity and dramatic compression.
AI.motivation: Massive spatiotemporal EO archives are stored as discrete raster files, incurring high storage, transfer, and query costs. A unified AI-native representation could enable efficient querying, reconstruction, and compression in a single framework.
AI.method: Train a continuous spatiotemporal implicit neural field to represent planetary-scale EO data. Demonstrated on MODIS MCD43A4 (20-year global, 7 bands, 5 km, 8-day), Sentinel-2 imagery (10 m) with continuous temporal parameterization, and HiGLASS biophysical products (LAI and FPAR). Evaluate on on-consumer hardware, test cloud occlusion robustness (2 km simulated), and measure compression.
AI.result: The learned representation supports direct spatiotemporal queries and curve-based reconstruction. For Sentinel-2, continuous temporal parameterization recovers cloud-free dynamics with R^2 > 0.85 under simulated 2-km occlusion. For HiGLASS LAI/FPAR, R^2 > 0.98. The MODIS archive compresses from the original size to 0.44 GB, about 95:1 relative to an optimized Int16 baseline, with mean R^2 > 0.98 and RMSE 0.021. These results hold on consumer hardware.
AI.conclusion: GeoNDC provides a unified AI-native representation for planet-scale EO data, complementing raw archives with a compact, analysis-ready data layer that enables query, reconstruction, and compression within a single framework.

title: Anchored-Branched Steady-state WInd Flow Transformer (AB-SWIFT): a metamodel for 3D atmospheric flow in urban environments
summary: Air flow modeling at a local scale is essential for applications such as pollutant dispersion modeling or wind farm modeling. To circumvent costly Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) computations, deep learning surrogate models have recently emerged as promising alternatives. However, in the context of urban air flow, deep learning models struggle to adapt to the high variations of the urban geometry and to large mesh sizes. To tackle these challenges, we introduce Anchored Branched Steady-state WInd Flow Transformer (AB-SWIFT), a transformer-based model with an internal branched structure uniquely designed for atmospheric flow modeling. We train our model on a specially designed database of atmospheric simulations around randomised urban geometries and with a mixture of unstable, neutral, and stable atmospheric stratifications. Our model reaches the best accuracy on all predicted fields compared to state-of-the-art transformers and graph-based models. Our code and data is available at https://github.com/cerea-daml/abswift.

AI.tldr: AB-SWIFT is a transformer-based surrogate for local-scale urban air flow that uses an anchored branched architecture to accommodate urban geometry variation and large meshes, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy across predicted fields on a dataset of randomized urban geometries with mixed stratifications.
AI.motivation: Local-scale urban air flow modeling is computationally expensive with CFD; existing DL surrogates struggle to generalize to diverse urban geometries and large meshes, hindering pollutant dispersion and wind-farm analyses.
AI.method: Introduces Anchored Branched Steady-state Wind Flow Transformer (AB-SWIFT), a transformer with an internal branched structure tailored for atmospheric flow. Trained on a curated dataset of atmospheric simulations around randomized urban geometries with unstable, neutral, and stable stratifications.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art transformers and graph-based models on all predicted fields. The authors release code and data at GitHub for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: AB-SWIFT demonstrates that an anchored-branched transformer architecture can robustly model urban atmospheric flow across varying geometries and large meshes, enabling efficient, accurate predictions and facilitating applications in pollutant dispersion and wind farm studies.

title: MoRGS: Efficient Per-Gaussian Motion Reasoning for Streamable Dynamic 3D Scenes
summary: Online reconstruction of dynamic scenes aims to learn from streaming multi-view inputs under low-latency constraints. The fast training and real-time rendering capabilities of 3D Gaussian Splatting have made on-the-fly reconstruction practically feasible, enabling online 4D reconstruction. However, existing online approaches, despite their efficiency and visual quality, fail to learn per-Gaussian motion that reflects true scene dynamics. Without explicit motion cues, appearance and motion are optimized solely under photometric loss, causing per-Gaussian motion to chase pixel residuals rather than true 3D motion. To address this, we propose MoRGS, an efficient online per-Gaussian motion reasoning framework that explicitly models per-Gaussian motion to improve 4D reconstruction quality. Specifically, we leverage optical flow on a sparse set of key views as lightweight motion cues that regularize per-Gaussian motion beyond photometric supervision. To compensate for the sparsity of flow supervision, we learn a per-Gaussian motion offset field that reconciles discrepancies between projected 3D motion and observed flow across views and time. In addition, we introduce a per-Gaussian motion confidence that separates dynamic from static Gaussians and weights Gaussian attribute residual updates, thereby suppressing redundant motion in static regions for better temporal consistency and accelerating the modeling of large motions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MoRGS achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and motion fidelity among online methods, while maintaining streamable performance.

AI.tldr: MoRGS introduces online per-Gaussian motion reasoning for 3D Gaussian Splatting to capture dynamic scene motion in streaming inputs, using sparse optical flow cues, a per-Gaussian motion offset field, and a per-Gaussian motion confidence mechanism to improve 4D reconstruction while maintaining efficiency.
AI.motivation: Online 4D reconstruction with 3D Gaussian Splatting struggles to learn true per-Gaussian motion; optimization relies on photometric loss alone, causing motion to chase residuals. Explicit motion modeling and robust supervision under sparse flow cues are needed to accurately capture scene dynamics.
AI.method: MoRGS framework leverages optical flow on a sparse set of key views as lightweight motion cues, learns a per-Gaussian motion offset field to reconcile projected 3D motion with observed flow across views/time, and introduces a per-Gaussian motion confidence that separates dynamic from static Gaussians to weight residual updates and suppress redundant motion in static regions, improving temporal consistency and enabling handling of large motions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and motion fidelity among online methods, while preserving streamable online performance.
AI.conclusion: MoRGS demonstrates that explicit per-Gaussian motion modeling with sparse flow cues, offset fields, and motion confidence yields superior online 4D reconstruction quality and motion fidelity, with robust handling of large motions and improved temporal consistency.

title: Uncertainty-Guided Label Rebalancing for CPS Safety Monitoring
comment: 10 pages (main content), 3 pages references, 5 figures, 5 tables. Under review
summary: Safety monitoring is essential for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs). However, unsafe events are rare in real-world CPS operations, creating an extreme class imbalance that degrades safety predictors. Standard rebalancing techniques perform poorly on time-series CPS telemetry, either generating unrealistic synthetic samples or overfitting on the minority class. Meanwhile, behavioral uncertainty in CPS operations, defined as the degree of doubt or uncertainty in CPS decisions , is often correlated with safety outcomes but unexplored in safety monitoring. To that end, we propose U-Balance, a supervised approach that leverages behavioral uncertainty to rebalance imbalanced datasets prior to training a safety predictor. U-Balance first trains a GatedMLP-based uncertainty predictor that summarizes each telemetry window into distributional kinematic features and outputs an uncertainty score. It then applies an uncertainty-guided label rebalancing (uLNR) mechanism that probabilistically relabels \textit{safe}-labeled windows with unusually high uncertainty as \textit{unsafe}, thereby enriching the minority class with informative boundary samples without synthesizing new data. Finally, a safety predictor is trained on the rebalanced dataset for safety monitoring. We evaluate U-Balance on a large-scale UAV benchmark with a 46:1 safe-to-unsafe ratio. Results confirm a moderate but significant correlation between behavioral uncertainty and safety. We then identify uLNR as the most effective strategy to exploit uncertainty information, compared to direct early and late fusion. U-Balance achieves a 0.806 F1 score, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14.3 percentage points, while maintaining competitive inference efficiency. Ablation studies confirm that both the GatedMLP-based uncertainty predictor and the uLNR mechanism contribute significantly to U-Balance's effectiveness.

AI.tldr: U-Balance uses uncertainty-guided rebalancing to address extreme class imbalance in CPS safety monitoring, achieving a new SOTA F1 of 0.806 on a UAV benchmark by leveraging a GatedMLP-based uncertainty predictor and a probabilistic relabeling mechanism (uLNR) that enriches the minority 'unsafe' class without data synthesis.
