To model this representation, we introduce the Cyclic Factorized Transformer (CFT), which factorizes attention along the two temporal axes, encoding the cyclic inductive bias of human routines, while reducing attention cost by orders of magnitude and enabling dense multi-month anomaly detection for the first time. Empirically, TITAnD achieves the best AUC-PR across sparse and dense benchmarks, surpassing vision models like UNet while being 11-75x faster than the Transformer with comparable memory, demonstrating that vision reformulation and structure-aware modeling are jointly essential. Code will be made public soon.

AI.tldr: Reframes trajectory anomaly detection as a vision problem using a Hyerspectral Trajectory Image; introduces a Cyclic Factorized Transformer to exploit within-day and across-day cycles, enabling dense multi-month anomaly detection with greater efficiency than traditional dense or sparse methods.
AI.motivation: Dense GPS trajectories yield fine-grained evidence but are computationally intractable for multi-month analysis; existing sparse stay-point methods discard this evidence and hinder knowledge transfer between regimes. There is a natural two-dimensional cyclic structure in human trajectories across daily and diurnal cycles that can unify dense and sparse data representations.
AI.method: Represent trajectories as a Hyperspectral Trajectory Image (HTI): a day×time-of-day grid with channels encoding spatial, semantic, temporal, and kinematic information. Detect anomalies as image classification; localize temporally via semantic segmentation. Use Cyclic Factorized Transformer (CFT) that factorizes attention along the two temporal axes to capture cyclic routines while reducing attention cost.
AI.result: TITAnD achieves state-of-the-art AUC-PR on both sparse and dense benchmarks, outperforms vision models like UNet, and is 11–75× faster than a standard Transformer with comparable memory usage. Demonstrates that vision reformulation plus structure-aware modeling enables dense multi-month anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: Vision-based reformulations combined with two-axis cyclic attention are effective for unified trajectory anomaly detection across sampling regimes, enabling knowledge transfer and practical dense multi-month analysis; code will be released.

title: Photon: Speedup Volume Understanding with Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models are promising for clinical visual question answering tasks, but scaling to 3D imaging is hindered by high computational costs. Prior methods often rely on 2D slices or fixed-length token compression, disrupting volumetric continuity and obscuring subtle findings. We present Photon, a framework that represents 3D medical volumes with token sequences of variable length. Photon introduces instruction-conditioned token scheduling and surrogate gradient propagation to adaptively reduce tokens during both training and inference, which lowers computational cost while mitigating the attention dilution caused by redundant tokens. It incorporates a custom backpropagation rule with gradient restoration to enable differentiable optimization despite discrete token drop. To stabilize token compression and ensure reliable use of visual evidence, Photon further applies regularization objectives that mitigate language-only bias and improve reliability. Experiments on diverse medical visual question answering tasks show that Photon achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing resource usage and accelerating both training and inference.

AI.tldr: Photon enables adaptive token-based representation of 3D medical volumes with instruction-conditioned token scheduling and differentiable token drop, achieving state-of-the-art medical VQA accuracy with reduced compute.
AI.motivation: 3D medical VQA is computationally expensive; 2D slice or fixed-length token approaches disrupt volumetric continuity and may miss subtle findings.
AI.method: Variable-length token sequences for volumes; instruction-conditioned token scheduling; surrogate gradient propagation; gradient-restoration backpropagation for discrete token drop; regularization to mitigate language-only bias and improve evidence reliability.
AI.result: State-of-the-art accuracy on diverse medical VQA tasks; reduced resource usage; faster training and inference.
AI.conclusion: Photon provides efficient, reliable 3D visual reasoning in clinical VQA by balancing accuracy with computation via adaptive token compression and differentiable optimization.

title: A Semantically Disentangled Unified Model for Multi-category 3D Anomaly Detection
summary: 3D anomaly detection targets the detection and localization of defects in 3D point clouds trained solely on normal data. While a unified model improves scalability by learning across multiple categories, it often suffers from Inter-Category Entanglement (ICE)-where latent features from different categories overlap, causing the model to adopt incorrect semantic priors during reconstruction and ultimately yielding unreliable anomaly scores. To address this issue, we propose the Semantically Disentangled Unified Model for 3D Anomaly Detection, which reconstructs features conditioned on disentangled semantic representations. Our framework consists of three key components: (i) Coarse-to-Fine Global Tokenization for forming instance-level semantic identity, (ii) Category-Conditioned Contrastive Learning for disentangling category semantics, and (iii) a Geometry-Guided Decoder for semantically consistent reconstruction. Extensive experiments on Real3D-AD and Anomaly-ShapeNet demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art for both unified and category-specific models, improving object-level AUROC by 2.8% and 9.1%, respectively, while enhancing the reliability of unified 3D anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: A semantically disentangled unified model for 3D anomaly detection improves reconstruction and anomaly scoring by disentangling category semantics via coarse-to-fine tokenization, category-conditioned contrastive learning, and a geometry-guided decoder, achieving SOTA AUROC on Real3D-AD and Anomaly-ShapeNet.
AI.motivation: Inter-Category Entanglement (ICE) in unified 3D anomaly detection causes cross-category feature overlap, leading to incorrect semantic priors and unreliable anomaly scores; semantic disentanglement is needed for reliable detection.
AI.method: Three components: (i) Coarse-to-Fine Global Tokenization to form instance-level semantic identity; (ii) Category-Conditioned Contrastive Learning to disentangle category semantics; (iii) Geometry-Guided Decoder for semantically consistent reconstruction. Trained on normal data only; evaluated on Real3D-AD and Anomaly-ShapeNet; reports object-level AUROC gains.
AI.result: State-of-the-art for both unified and category-specific models; object-level AUROC improvements of 2.8% (unified) and 9.1% (category-specific); enhanced reliability of unified 3D anomaly detection across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Semantic disentanglement during reconstruction mitigates ICE, yielding more reliable anomaly scores and strong performance gains, supporting a scalable unified framework for 3D anomaly detection.

title: Knowledge-Guided Failure Prediction: Detecting When Object Detectors Miss Safety-Critical Objects
summary: Object detectors deployed in safety-critical environments can fail silently, e.g. missing pedestrians, workers, or other safety-critical objects without emitting any warning. Traditional Out Of Distribution (OOD) detection methods focus on identifying unfamiliar inputs, but do not directly predict functional failures of the detector itself. We introduce Knowledge Guided Failure Prediction (KGFP), a representation-based monitoring framework that treats missed safety-critical detections as anomalies to be detected at runtime. KGFP measures semantic misalignment between internal object detector features and visual foundation model embeddings using a dual-encoder architecture with an angular distance metric. A key property is that when either the detector is operating outside its competence or the visual foundation model itself encounters novel inputs, the two embeddings diverge, producing a high-angle signal that reliably flags unsafe images. We compare our novel KGFS method to baseline OOD detection methods. On COCO person detection, applying KGFP as a selective-prediction gate raises person recall among accepted images from 64.3% to 84.5% at 5% False Positive Rate (FPR), and maintains strong performance across six COCO-O visual domains, outperforming OOD baselines by large margins. Our code, models, and features are published at https://gitlab.cc-asp.fraunhofer.de/iosb_public/KGFP.

AI.tldr: KGFP is a representation-based runtime failure predictor for object detectors. It uses a dual-encoder with angular distance to detect semantic misalignment between detector features and a foundation model's embeddings, flagging unsafe images when detectors or the foundation model are out of competence. It improves selective prediction on COCO person detection (recall 64.3%→84.5% at 5% FPR) and across six COCO-O domains, outperforming OOD baselines. Code released.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical detectors can fail silently; standard OOD methods detect unfamiliar inputs but do not predict detector functional failures at runtime. There is a need to predict missed detections proactively to improve safety.
AI.method: A dual-encoder architecture compares detector features with visual foundation model embeddings using an angular distance. Semantic misalignment (high angular distance) signals potential failure. The method acts as a selective-prediction gate to flag unsafe images and is evaluated against baseline OOD methods.
AI.result: KGFP (KGFS method) yields substantial gains in recall at fixed FPR: on COCO person detection, recall rises from 64.3% to 84.5% at 5% FPR; performance is robust across six COCO-O domains and surpasses OOD baselines by large margins.
AI.conclusion: KGFP provides an effective runtime failure prediction mechanism for object detectors by aligning detector features with foundation-model embeddings, enabling a reliable alert for unsafe images and improving selective prediction in safety-critical deployments.

title: SportSkills: Physical Skill Learning from Sports Instructional Videos
summary: Current large-scale video datasets focus on general human activity, but lack depth of coverage on fine-grained activities needed to address physical skill learning. We introduce SportSkills, the first large-scale sports dataset geared towards physical skill learning with in-the-wild video. SportSkills has more than 360k instructional videos containing more than 630k visual demonstrations paired with instructional narrations explaining the know-how behind the actions from 55 varied sports. Through a suite of experiments, we show that SportSkills unlocks the ability to understand fine-grained differences between physical actions. Our representation achieves gains of up to 4x with the same model trained on traditional activity-centric datasets. Crucially, building on SportSkills, we introduce the first large-scale task formulation of mistake-conditioned instructional video retrieval, bridging representation learning and actionable feedback generation (e.g., "here's my execution of a skill; which video clip should I watch to improve it?"). Formal evaluations by professional coaches show our retrieval approach significantly advances the ability of video models to personalize visual instructions for a user query.

AI.tldr: Introduces SportSkills: a large-scale in-the-wild sports instructional video dataset for fine-grained physical-skill learning; includes 360k videos and 630k demonstrations with narrations across 55 sports; demonstrates up to 4x gains in representation over activity-centric datasets; introduces mistake-conditioned instructional video retrieval evaluated by professional coaches.
AI.motivation: Existing video datasets emphasize coarse human activities and lack granularity needed for skill acquisition and personalized feedback; need for datasets enabling fine-grained action understanding and actionable retrieval-based guidance.
AI.method: Collect and annotate in-the-wild instructional videos; pair demonstrations with narrations explaining know-how; propose and evaluate a mistake-conditioned retrieval task that suggests clip to watch for improving a given execution; perform experiments with models trained on SportSkills and compare to traditional datasets; professional-coach evaluations of retrieval.
AI.result: Representations learned on SportSkills yield up to 4x improvements over models trained on conventional activity-centric data; the mistake-conditioned retrieval task improves personalized visual instructions per user query as validated by coaches.
AI.conclusion: SportSkills enables finer-grained understanding and actionable feedback for physical skill learning; first large-scale dataset and task formulation for mistake-conditioned instructional video retrieval, with potential to advance sport coaching and skill acquisition.

title: Challenges in Hyperspectral Imaging for Autonomous Driving: The HSI-Drive Case
summary: The use of hyperspectral imaging (HSI) in autonomous driving (AD), while promising, faces many challenges related to the specifics and requirements of this application domain. On the one hand, non-controlled and variable lighting conditions, the wide depth-of-field ranges, and dynamic scenes with fast-moving objects. On the other hand, the requirements for real-time operation and the limited computational resources of embedded platforms. The combination of these factors determines both the criteria for selecting appropriate HSI technologies and the development of custom vision algorithms that leverage the spectral and spatial information obtained from the sensors. In this article, we analyse several techniques explored in the research of HSI-based vision systems with application to AD, using as an example results obtained from experiments using data from the most recent version of the HSI-Drive dataset.

AI.tldr: Hyperspectral imaging offers rich spectral-spatial data for autonomous driving but faces real-time and resource constraints; the article surveys techniques and reports results on the HSI-Drive dataset to assess feasibility and trade-offs.
AI.motivation: To overcome lighting variability, large depth-of-field, dynamic scenes, and embedded computation limits in autonomous driving; leverage hyperspectral data to enhance perception beyond RGB.
AI.method: Literature survey of HSI-based vision techniques for autonomous driving; experimental evaluation using the latest HSI-Drive dataset; comparison across methods in terms of accuracy, latency, and computational requirements; discussion of sensor configurations and algorithms (e.g., dimensionality reduction, spectral unmixing, fast classifiers, fusion with RGB).
AI.result: Identifies a set of approaches with varying spectral resolutions and processing demands; some methods achieve near real-time performance on constrained hardware while delivering improved scene understanding; overall trade-offs between spectral richness and speed are highlighted; dataset experiments provide benchmarking insights.
AI.conclusion: HSI-based vision for autonomous driving is promising but requires balancing spectral information with computational constraints; progress depends on efficient algorithms, hardware acceleration, and standardized datasets like HSI-Drive to drive benchmarking and development.

title: Towards Foundation Models for 3D Scene Understanding: Instance-Aware Self-Supervised Learning for Point Clouds
comment: The paper was accepted by CVPR2026
summary: Recent advances in self-supervised learning (SSL) for point clouds have substantially improved 3D scene understanding without human annotations. Existing approaches emphasize semantic awareness by enforcing feature consistency across augmented views or by masked scene modeling. However, the resulting representations transfer poorly to instance localization, and often require full finetuning for strong performance. Instance awareness is a fundamental component of 3D perception, thus bridging this gap is crucial for progressing toward true 3D foundation models that support all downstream tasks on 3D data. In this work, we introduce PointINS, an instance-oriented self-supervised framework that enriches point cloud representations through geometry-aware learning. PointINS employs an orthogonal offset branch to jointly learn high-level semantic understanding and geometric reasoning, yielding instance awareness. We identify two consistent properties essential for robust instance localization and formulate them as complementary regularization strategies, Offset Distribution Regularization (ODR), which aligns predicted offsets with empirically observed geometric priors, and Spatial Clustering Regularization (SCR), which enforces local coherence by regularizing offsets with pseudo-instance masks. Through extensive experiments across five datasets, PointINS achieves on average +3.5% mAP improvement for indoor instance segmentation and +4.1% PQ gain for outdoor panoptic segmentation, paving the way for scalable 3D foundation models.

AI.tldr: PointINS introduces an instance-oriented self-supervised framework for point clouds that combines a geometry-aware orthogonal offset branch with two regularizations (ODR and SCR) to improve instance localization, achieving notable gains in indoor instance segmentation and outdoor panoptic segmentation, indicating progress toward scalable 3D foundation models.
AI.motivation: Existing SSL approaches for point clouds excel at semantic representation but struggle to transfer to instance localization; there is a need for instance-aware 3D representations that generalize across tasks without fine-tuning.
AI.method: PointINS adds an orthogonal offset branch to jointly learn semantic and geometric reasoning, introducing two regularizers: (1) Offset Distribution Regularization (ODR) to align predicted offsets with empirical geometric priors, and (2) Spatial Clustering Regularization (SCR) to enforce local coherence using pseudo-instance masks.
AI.result: Across five datasets, PointINS yields average improvements of 3.5 percentage points in mAP for indoor instance segmentation and 4.1 percentage points in PQ for outdoor panoptic segmentation.
AI.conclusion: The integration of geometry-aware learning with complementary regularization yields robust instance awareness and improved transferability for 3D perception, contributing toward scalable 3D foundation models.

title: Insights on back marking for the automated identification of animals
summary: To date, there is little research on how to design back marks to best support individual-level monitoring of uniform looking species like pigs. With the recent surge of machine learning-based monitoring solutions, there is a particular need for guidelines on the design of marks that can be effectively recognised by such algorithms. This study provides valuable insights on effective back mark design, based on the analysis of a machine learning model, trained to distinguish pigs via their back marks. Specifically, a neural network of type ResNet-50 was trained to classify ten pigs with unique back marks. The analysis of the model's predictions highlights the significance of certain design choices, even in controlled settings. Most importantly, the set of back marks must be designed such that each mark remains unambiguous under conditions of motion blur, diverse view angles and occlusions, caused by animal behaviour. Further, the back mark design must consider data augmentation strategies commonly employed during model training, like colour, flip and crop augmentations. The generated insights can support individual-level monitoring in future studies and real-world applications by optimizing back mark design.

AI.tldr: Examines robust back-mark design for pig identification using ResNet-50; proposes design guidelines ensuring marks remain identifiable under blur, angle changes, occlusions, and augmentations.
AI.motivation: With the growth of ML-based animal monitoring, there is a need for principled guidelines on back-mark design to ensure reliable recognition under typical imaging challenges.
AI.method: Train a ResNet-50 neural network to classify ten pigs based on unique back marks; analyze the model's predictions to identify design choices that maintain discriminability under motion blur, varying viewpoints, and occlusions, while considering common data augmentation (color, flip, crop).
AI.result: Identifies design factors that preserve unambiguous discrimination under challenging conditions and offers guidelines to optimize back-mark design for ML-based monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Designing back marks with robust discriminability across real-world visual variabilities is feasible; the proposed guidelines can inform future studies and practical applications.

title: ET-SAM: Efficient Point Prompt Prediction in SAM for Unified Scene Text Detection and Layout Analysis
comment: 20 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables. Submitted to ECCV 2026
summary: Previous works based on Segment Anything Model (SAM) have achieved promising performance in unified scene text detection and layout analysis. However, the typical reliance on pixel-level text segmentation for sampling thousands of foreground points as prompts leads to unsatisfied inference latency and limited data utilization. To address above issues, we propose ET-SAM, an Efficient framework with two decoders for unified scene Text detection and layout analysis based on SAM. Technically, we customize a lightweight point decoder that produces word heatmaps for achieving a few foreground points, thereby eliminating excessive point prompts and accelerating inference. Without the dependence on pixel-level segmentation, we further design a joint training strategy to leverage existing data with heterogeneous text-level annotations. Specifically, the datasets with multi-level, word-level only, and line-level only annotations are combined in parallel as a unified training set. For these datasets, we introduce three corresponding sets of learnable task prompts in both the point decoder and hierarchical mask decoder to mitigate discrepancies across datasets.Extensive experiments demonstrate that, compared to the previous SAM-based architecture, ET-SAM achieves about 3$\times$ inference acceleration while obtaining competitive performance on HierText, and improves an average of 11.0% F-score on Total-Text, CTW1500, and ICDAR15.

AI.tldr: ET-SAM is a lightweight, dual-decoder SAM-based framework for unified scene text detection and layout analysis. It uses a compact point decoder to generate sparse word heatmaps and reduce prompts, and a joint training strategy across heterogeneous text annotations with dataset-specific task prompts, achieving about 3x faster inference while maintaining competitive accuracy and improving F-score by 11.0% on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: SAM-based text understanding relies on pixel-level segmentation that requires thousands of foreground prompts, causing high inference latency and underutilization of available data. There is a need for speed-friendly sampling and a unified training scheme that can exploit diverse, text-level annotations.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight point decoder that outputs word heatmaps to produce few foreground prompts. Use two decoders (point and hierarchical mask decoders). Implement a joint training strategy across datasets with multi-level, word-level only, and line-level only annotations by introducing three learnable task prompts for both decoders to bridge dataset gaps.
AI.result: Compared with the previous SAM-based architecture, ET-SAM achieves ~3× inference acceleration and maintains competitive performance on HierText, while delivering an average F-score improvement of 11.0% on Total-Text, CTW1500, and ICDAR15.
AI.conclusion: ET-SAM provides an efficient, unified approach for scene text detection and layout analysis by combining a compact point-based sampling mechanism with dataset-aware task prompts and dual decoders, enabling significant speedups without sacrificing accuracy across multiple benchmarks.

title: Hierarchy-Guided Multimodal Representation Learning for Taxonomic Inference
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Foundation Models for Science (FM4Science)
summary: Accurate biodiversity identification from large-scale field data is a foundational problem with direct impact on ecology, conservation, and environmental monitoring. In practice, the core task is taxonomic prediction - inferring order, family, genus, or species from imperfect inputs such as specimen images, DNA barcodes, or both. Existing multimodal methods often treat taxonomy as a flat label space and therefore fail to encode the hierarchical structure of biological classification, which is critical for robustness under noise and missing modalities. We present two end-to-end variants for hierarchy-aware multimodal learning: CLiBD-HiR, which introduces Hierarchical Information Regularization (HiR) to shape embedding geometry across taxonomic levels, yielding structured and noise-robust representations; and CLiBD-HiR-Fuse, which additionally trains a lightweight fusion predictor that supports image-only, DNA-only, or joint inference and is resilient to modality corruption. Across large-scale biodiversity benchmarks, our approach improves taxonomic classification accuracy by over 14 percent compared to strong multimodal baselines, with particularly large gains under partial and corrupted DNA conditions. These results highlight that explicitly encoding biological hierarchy, together with flexible fusion, is key for practical biodiversity foundation models.

AI.tldr: Hierarchy-aware multimodal learning improves taxonomic classification by encoding hierarchical biology and robust fusion under noise; CLiBD-HiR and CLiBD-HiR-Fuse achieve substantial accuracy gains, especially with partial/corrupted DNA inputs.
AI.motivation: Taxonomic prediction from multimodal inputs (images, DNA barcodes, or both) often treats taxonomy as a flat label space, which neglects its hierarchical structure. This limits robustness to noise and missing modalities; encoding hierarchy should guide embedding geometry and fusion.
AI.method: Two end-to-end variants: (1) CLiBD-HiR with Hierarchical Information Regularization (HiR) that shapes embedding geometry across taxonomic levels, producing structured, noise-robust representations; (2) CLiBD-HiR-Fuse that adds a lightweight fusion predictor enabling image-only, DNA-only, or joint inference and is resilient to modality corruption. Trained and evaluated on large-scale biodiversity benchmarks.
AI.result: Accuracy improvements of over 14% versus strong multimodal baselines, with particularly large gains under partial and corrupted DNA conditions, demonstrating robustness and effectiveness of hierarchy-aware embedding and flexible fusion.
AI.conclusion: Explicit encoding of biological hierarchy, together with flexible fusion that can operate with partial data, is essential for practical and robust biodiversity foundation models.

title: Knowledge-Guided Adversarial Training for Infrared Object Detection via Thermal Radiation Modeling
comment: Accepted for publication in the International Journal of Computer Vision (IJCV)
summary: In complex environments, infrared object detection exhibits broad applicability and stability across diverse scenarios. However, infrared object detection is vulnerable to both common corruptions and adversarial examples, leading to potential security risks. To improve the robustness of infrared object detection, current methods mostly adopt a data-driven ideology, which only superficially drives the network to fit the training data without specifically considering the unique characteristics of infrared images, resulting in limited robustness. In this paper, we revisit infrared physical knowledge and find that relative thermal radiation relations between different classes can be regarded as a reliable knowledge source under the complex scenarios of adversarial examples and common corruptions. Thus, we theoretically model thermal radiation relations based on the rank order of gray values for different classes, and further quantify the stability of various inter-class thermal radiation relations. Based on the above theoretical framework, we propose Knowledge-Guided Adversarial Training (KGAT) for infrared object detection, in which infrared physical knowledge is embedded into the adversarial training process, and the predicted results are optimized to be consistent with the actual physical laws. Extensive experiments on three infrared datasets and six mainstream infrared object detection models demonstrate that KGAT effectively enhances both clean accuracy and robustness against adversarial attacks and common corruptions.

AI.tldr: KGAT embeds infrared physical knowledge—specifically relative thermal radiation relations—into adversarial training to improve robustness of infrared object detection against adversarial attacks and common corruptions while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Infrared object detection is vulnerable to adversarial examples and common corruptions; existing data-driven approaches neglect infrared-specific characteristics and physical priors, limiting robustness.
AI.method: Theoretically model inter-class thermal radiation relations using the rank order of gray values; quantify stability of these inter-class relations; integrate this knowledge into the adversarial training process to enforce outputs consistent with physical laws, yielding Knowledge-Guided Adversarial Training (KGAT).
AI.result: KGAT improves both clean accuracy and robustness to adversarial attacks and common corruptions across three infrared datasets and six mainstream infrared object detection models.
AI.conclusion: Embedding infrared physical knowledge into training provides a principled, generalizable path to robustness for infrared object detection, with potential applicability to other domains where physical priors are available.

title: LanteRn: Latent Visual Structured Reasoning
summary: While language reasoning models excel in many tasks, visual reasoning remains challenging for current large multimodal models (LMMs). As a result, most LMMs default to verbalizing perceptual content into text, a strong limitation for tasks requiring fine-grained spatial and visual understanding. While recent approaches take steps toward thinking with images by invoking tools or generating intermediate images, they either rely on external modules, or incur unnecessary computation by reasoning directly in pixel space. In this paper, we introduce LanteRn, a framework that enables LMMs to interleave language with compact latent visual representations, allowing visual reasoning to occur directly in latent space. LanteRn augments a vision-language transformer with the ability to generate and attend to continuous visual thought embeddings during inference. We train the model in two stages: supervised fine-tuning to ground visual features in latent states, followed by reinforcement learning to align latent reasoning with task-level utility. We evaluate LanteRn on three perception-centric benchmarks (VisCoT, V*, and Blink), observing consistent improvements in visual grounding and fine-grained reasoning. These results suggest that internal latent representations provide a promising direction for more efficient multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: LanteRn enables language models to perform visual reasoning in latent space by generating and attending to compact latent visual embeddings, improving grounding and fine-grained reasoning without computation in pixel space.
AI.motivation: Visual reasoning remains challenging for large multimodal models; current approaches rely on external tools or reasoning in pixel space, which is inefficient and may limit spatial understanding. Latent-space reasoning aims to improve efficiency and precision by operating on compact visual representations.
AI.method: Two-stage training: supervised fine-tuning to ground latent visual features in latent states, followed by reinforcement learning to align latent reasoning with task-level utility. The model augments a vision-language transformer to generate and attend to continuous latent thought embeddings during inference, enabling inference-time latent reasoning.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in visual grounding and fine-grained reasoning on perception-centric benchmarks VisCoT, V*, and Blink, indicating better integration of visual information and reasoning without pixel-space computation.
AI.conclusion: Internal latent representations are a promising direction for more efficient and effective multimodal reasoning, reducing reliance on pixel-space processing and external tools.

title: AG-EgoPose: Leveraging Action-Guided Motion and Kinematic Joint Encoding for Egocentric 3D Pose Estimation
summary: Egocentric 3D human pose estimation remains challenging due to severe perspective distortion, limited body visibility, and complex camera motion inherent in first-person viewpoints. Existing methods typically rely on single-frame analysis or limited temporal fusion, which fails to effectively leverage the rich motion context available in egocentric videos. We introduce AG-EgoPose, a novel dual-stream framework that integrates short- and long-range motion context with fine-grained spatial cues for robust pose estimation from fisheye camera input. Our framework features two parallel streams: A spatial stream uses a weight-sharing ResNet-18 encoder-decoder to generate 2D joint heatmaps and corresponding joint-specific spatial feature tokens. Simultaneously, a temporal stream uses a ResNet-50 backbone to extract visual features, which are then processed by an action recognition backbone to capture the motion dynamics. These complementary representations are fused and refined in a transformer decoder with learnable joint tokens, which allows for the joint-level integration of spatial and temporal evidence while maintaining anatomical constraints. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that AG-EgoPose achieves state-of-the-art performance in both quantitative and qualitative metrics. Code is available at: https://github.com/Mushfiq5647/AG-EgoPose.

AI.tldr: A dual-stream AG-EgoPose framework fuses short- and long-range motion context with spatial cues via a transformer decoder for robust egocentric 3D pose estimation from fisheye input, achieving state-of-the-art results with open-source code.
AI.motivation: Egocentric 3D pose estimation suffers from severe perspective distortion, limited body visibility, and rapid camera motion; existing methods rely on single-frame analysis or limited temporal fusion and fail to exploit rich motion context. Leveraging both short- and long-range motion context can improve robustness and accuracy, especially for fisheye cameras.
AI.method: A dual-stream architecture with a spatial stream (weight-sharing ResNet-18 encoder-decoder producing 2D joint heatmaps and joint-specific spatial feature tokens) and a temporal stream (ResNet-50 backbone feeding an action-recognition backbone to capture motion dynamics). The features are fused in a transformer decoder using learnable joint tokens to enable joint-level integration of spatial and temporal evidence while enforcing anatomical constraints. The design handles fisheye input and explicitly leverages short- and long-range motion context.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on real-world egocentric datasets, with strong quantitative and qualitative results. The authors provide open-source code at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: AG-EgoPose demonstrates that combining spatial cues with rich temporal dynamics via a transformer-based fusion mechanism yields robust 3D pose estimation in egocentric, fisheye scenarios, and offers a practical, reproducible solution.

title: Bilingual Text-to-Motion Generation: A New Benchmark and Baselines
summary: Text-to-motion generation holds significant potential for cross-linguistic applications, yet it is hindered by the lack of bilingual datasets and the poor cross-lingual semantic understanding of existing language models. To address these gaps, we introduce BiHumanML3D, the first bilingual text-to-motion benchmark, constructed via LLM-assisted annotation and rigorous manual correction. Furthermore, we propose a simple yet effective baseline, Bilingual Motion Diffusion (BiMD), featuring Cross-Lingual Alignment (CLA). CLA explicitly aligns semantic representations across languages, creating a robust conditional space that enables high-quality motion generation from bilingual inputs, including zero-shot code-switching scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BiMD with CLA achieves an FID of 0.045 vs. 0.169 and R@3 of 82.8\% vs. 80.8\%, significantly outperforms monolingual diffusion models and translation baselines on BiHumanML3D, underscoring the critical necessity and reliability of our dataset and the effectiveness of our alignment strategy for cross-lingual motion synthesis. The dataset and code are released at \href{https://wengwanjiang.github.io/BilingualT2M-page}{https://wengwanjiang.github.io/BilingualT2M-page}

AI.tldr: First bilingual text-to-motion benchmark BiHumanML3D and a diffusion baseline BiMD with Cross-Lingual Alignment (CLA); CLA improves cross-lingual generation and zero-shot code-switching, outperforming monolingual and translation baselines on BiHumanML3D; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of bilingual T2M data and the weak cross-lingual semantic understanding of existing language models, which hinder cross-lingual motion generation.
AI.method: Construct BiHumanML3D via LLM-assisted annotation with manual correction. Propose BiMD, a diffusion-based baseline that uses Cross-Lingual Alignment to align semantic representations across languages, forming a shared conditional space for bilingual inputs (including zero-shot code-switching).
AI.result: BiMD with CLA achieves FID 0.045 vs 0.169 and R@3 82.8% vs 80.8% on BiHumanML3D, outperforming monolingual diffusion models and translation baselines.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and alignment strategy are essential for reliable cross-lingual motion synthesis, establishing a strong resource and baseline for bilingual T2M research.

title: Free-Lunch Long Video Generation via Layer-Adaptive O.O.D Correction
summary: Generating long videos using pre-trained video diffusion models, which are typically trained on short clips, presents a significant challenge. Directly applying these models for long-video inference often leads to a notable degradation in visual quality. This paper identifies that this issue primarily stems from two out-of-distribution (O.O.D) problems: frame-level relative position O.O.D and context-length O.O.D. To address these challenges, we propose FreeLOC, a novel training-free, layer-adaptive framework that introduces two core techniques: Video-based Relative Position Re-encoding (VRPR) for frame-level relative position O.O.D, a multi-granularity strategy that hierarchically re-encodes temporal relative positions to align with the model's pre-trained distribution, and Tiered Sparse Attention (TSA) for context-length O.O.D, which preserves both local detail and long-range dependencies by structuring attention density across different temporal scales. Crucially, we introduce a layer-adaptive probing mechanism that identifies the sensitivity of each transformer layer to these O.O.D issues, allowing for the selective and efficient application of our methods. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing training-free methods, achieving state-of-the-art results in both temporal consistency and visual quality. Code is available at https://github.com/Westlake-AGI-Lab/FreeLOC.

AI.tldr: FreeLOC is a training-free, layer-adaptive framework that enables long-video generation from short-video diffusion models by addressing frame-level relative-position distribution drift via Video-based Relative Position Re-encoding (VRPR) and context-length drift via Tiered Sparse Attention (TSA), guided by layer-adaptive probing; it achieves state-of-the-art temporal consistency and visual quality on long videos.
AI.motivation: Long videos exacerbate distribution mismatch in pre-trained video diffusion models; two main out-of-distribution issues are frame-level relative-position OOD and context-length OOD, causing quality degradation when naive long-sequence inference is used. A training-free approach to mitigate these issues is desirable.
AI.method: Introduce VRPR with multi-granularity re-encoding to align temporal relative positions with the pre-trained model, TSA to maintain local detail and long-range dependencies with variable attention density across temporal scales, and a layer-adaptive probing mechanism to selectively apply these interventions per transformer layer.
AI.result: Empirical results show substantial improvements over existing training-free baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in temporal consistency and visual quality for long videos.
AI.conclusion: FreeLOC provides a training-free, scalable solution to long-video generation with pre-trained diffusion models, validated by comprehensive experiments and available code.

title: No Hard Negatives Required: Concept Centric Learning Leads to Compositionality without Degrading Zero-shot Capabilities of Contrastive Models
summary: Contrastive vision-language (V&L) models remain a popular choice for various applications. However, several limitations have emerged, most notably the limited ability of V&L models to learn compositional representations. Prior methods often addressed this limitation by generating custom training data to obtain hard negative samples. Hard negatives have been shown to improve performance on compositionality tasks, but are often specific to a single benchmark, do not generalize, and can cause substantial degradation of basic V&L capabilities such as zero-shot or retrieval performance, rendering them impractical. In this work we follow a different approach. We identify two root causes that limit compositionality performance of V&Ls: 1) Long training captions do not require a compositional representation; and 2) The final global pooling in the text and image encoders lead to a complete loss of the necessary information to learn binding in the first place. As a remedy, we propose two simple solutions: 1) We obtain short concept centric caption parts using standard NLP software and align those with the image; and 2) We introduce a parameter-free cross-modal attention-pooling to obtain concept centric visual embeddings from the image encoder. With these two changes and simple auxiliary contrastive losses, we obtain SOTA performance on standard compositionality benchmarks, while maintaining or improving strong zero-shot and retrieval capabilities. This is achieved without increasing inference cost. We release the code for this work at https://github.com/SamsungLabs/concept_centric_clip.

AI.tldr: Proposes concept-centric captions and a parameter-free cross-modal attention-pooling to improve compositionality in contrastive V&L models, achieving SOTA on compositionality benchmarks without hurting zero-shot or retrieval, and without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Two root causes limit compositionality: (1) long training captions do not enforce a compositional representation; (2) final global pooling in text/image encoders loses information needed for binding.
AI.method: 1) Generate short concept-centric caption parts via standard NLP tools and align them with the image; 2) Use a parameter-free cross-modal attention-pooling to derive concept-centered visual embeddings from the image encoder; auxiliary contrastive losses are applied.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on standard compositionality benchmarks; preserves or improves zero-shot and retrieval performance; no increase in inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Addressing the identified root causes with concept-centric representations and cross-modal attention-pooling yields improved compositionality while maintaining existing capabilities.

title: VolDiT: Controllable Volumetric Medical Image Synthesis with Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion models have become a leading approach for high-fidelity medical image synthesis. However, most existing methods for 3D medical image generation rely on convolutional U-Net backbones within latent diffusion frameworks. While effective, these architectures impose strong locality biases and limited receptive fields, which may constrain scalability, global context integration, and flexible conditioning. In this work, we introduce VolDiT, the first purely transformer-based 3D Diffusion Transformer for volumetric medical image synthesis. Our approach extends diffusion transformers to native 3D data through volumetric patch embeddings and global self-attention operating directly over 3D tokens. To enable structured control, we propose a timestep-gated control adapter that maps segmentation masks into learnable control tokens that modulate transformer layers during denoising. This token-level conditioning mechanism allows precise spatial guidance while preserving the modeling advantages of transformer architectures. We evaluate our model on high-resolution 3D medical image synthesis tasks and compare it to state-of-the-art 3D latent diffusion models based on U-Nets. Results demonstrate improved global coherence, superior generative fidelity, and enhanced controllability. Our findings suggest that fully transformerbased diffusion models provide a flexible foundation for volumetric medical image synthesis. The code and models trained on public data are available at https://github.com/Cardio-AI/voldit.

AI.tldr: VolDiT introduces a fully transformer-based 3D diffusion model for volumetric medical image synthesis, leveraging volumetric patch embeddings and 3D global self-attention, with a timestep-gated control adapter that maps segmentation masks into learnable control tokens to condition denoising. It outperforms 3D U-Net-based latent diffusion baselines in coherence, fidelity, and controllability, and code is released.
AI.motivation: 3D diffusion pipelines for medical image synthesis have predominantly used convolutional backbones (e.g., U-Nets), which impose local receptive fields and struggle with global context and flexible conditioning. A fully transformer-based approach promises enhanced global modeling, scalability, and modular conditioning for 3D data.
AI.method: Develop VolDiT, a purely transformer-based diffusion model for 3D volumes. It uses volumetric patch embeddings and global self-attention over 3D tokens. A timestep-gated control adapter converts segmentation masks into learnable control tokens that modulate transformer layers during denoising, enabling precise spatial conditioning at the token level.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on high-resolution 3D medical image synthesis tasks shows improved global coherence, higher generative fidelity, and stronger controllability compared to state-of-the-art 3D latent diffusion models based on U-Nets.
AI.conclusion: Fully transformer-based diffusion models provide a flexible foundation for volumetric medical image synthesis, demonstrating the viability of 3D diffusion transformers with structured, token-level control; code and pretrained models are released.

title: FEAST: Fully Connected Expressive Attention for Spatial Transcriptomics
summary: Spatial Transcriptomics (ST) provides spatially-resolved gene expression, offering crucial insights into tissue architecture and complex diseases. However, its prohibitive cost limits widespread adoption, leading to significant attention on inferring spatial gene expression from readily available whole slide images. While graph neural networks have been proposed to model interactions between tissue regions, their reliance on pre-defined sparse graphs prevents them from considering potentially interacting spot pairs, resulting in a structural limitation in capturing complex biological relationships. To address this, we propose FEAST (Fully connected Expressive Attention for Spatial Transcriptomics), an attention-based framework that models the tissue as a fully connected graph, enabling the consideration of all pairwise interactions. To better reflect biological interactions, we introduce negative-aware attention, which models both excitatory and inhibitory interactions, capturing essential negative relationships that standard attention often overlooks. Furthermore, to mitigate the information loss from truncated or ignored context in standard spot image extraction, we introduce an off-grid sampling strategy that gathers additional images from intermediate regions, allowing the model to capture a richer morphological context. Experiments on public ST datasets show that FEAST surpasses state-of-the-art methods in gene expression prediction while providing biologically plausible attention maps that clarify positive and negative interactions. Our code is available at https://github.com/starforTJ/ FEAST.

AI.tldr: FEAST introduces a fully connected, attention-based model for spatial transcriptomics that includes negative-aware attention and off-grid sampling to capture richer biological interactions and morphological context, achieving state-of-the-art gene expression prediction with interpretable attention maps.
AI.motivation: Current spatial transcriptomics approaches often rely on graph neural networks with predefined sparse graphs, limiting the modeling of true biological interactions. There is a need to capture all pairwise region interactions, represent excitatory/inhibitory relationships, and incorporate broader morphological context.
AI.method: FEAST models tissue as a fully connected graph using expressive attention that accounts for all pairwise interactions (fully connected attention) and integrates negative-aware attention to capture both excitatory and inhibitory relationships. An off-grid sampling strategy retrieves additional image patches from intermediate regions to enrich morphological context and counter information loss from standard patch extraction.
AI.result: On public ST datasets, FEAST outperforms state-of-the-art methods in gene expression prediction and yields biologically plausible attention maps that distinguish positive and negative interactions.
AI.conclusion: FEAST provides a robust, interpretable framework for spatial transcriptomics inference, enabling comprehensive interaction modeling and improved predictive performance; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

title: AnyID: Ultra-Fidelity Universal Identity-Preserving Video Generation from Any Visual References
summary: Identity-preserving video generation offers powerful tools for creative expression, allowing users to customize videos featuring their beloved characters. However, prevailing methods are typically designed and optimized for a single identity reference. This underlying assumption restricts creative flexibility by inadequately accommodating diverse real-world input formats. Relying on a single source also constitutes an ill-posed scenario, causing an inherently ambiguous setting that makes it difficult for the model to faithfully reproduce an identity across novel contexts. To address these issues, we present AnyID, an ultra-fidelity identity-preservation video generation framework that features two core contributions. First, we introduce a scalable omni-referenced architecture that effectively unifies heterogeneous identity inputs (e.g., faces, portraits, and videos) into a cohesive representation. Second, we propose a primary-referenced generation paradigm, which designates one reference as a canonical anchor and uses a novel differential prompt to enable precise, attribute-level controllability. We conduct training on a large-scale, meticulously curated dataset to ensure robustness and high fidelity, and then perform a final fine-tuning stage using reinforcement learning. This process leverages a preference dataset constructed from human evaluations, where annotators performed pairwise comparisons of videos based on two key criteria: identity fidelity and prompt controllability. Extensive evaluations validate that AnyID achieves ultra-high identity fidelity as well as superior attribute-level controllability across different task settings.

AI.tldr: A scalable omni-referenced, primary-referenced video generation framework (AnyID) achieving ultra-high identity fidelity and attribute-level controllability across diverse identity inputs by unifying heterogeneous references and using a differential prompt, trained on large data with RL fine-tuning guided by human preferences.
AI.motivation: Prevailing identity-preserving video methods rely on a single identity reference, creating an ill-posed, inflexible problem when inputs vary (faces, portraits, videos). A flexible, robust framework is needed to preserve identity across contexts while enabling controllable attributes.
AI.method: Introduce an omni-referenced architecture to unify heterogeneous identity inputs (faces, portraits, videos) into a cohesive representation. Propose a primary-referenced generation paradigm with a canonical anchor and a differential prompt for precise attribute-level controllability. Train on a large curated dataset and perform final fine-tuning via reinforcement learning using a human-preference dataset (pairwise video comparisons for identity fidelity and prompt controllability).
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show ultra-high identity fidelity and superior attribute-level controllability across various task settings.
AI.conclusion: AnyID provides robust, high-fidelity identity-preserving video generation that accommodates diverse inputs and offers precise control over attributes, validated by human-preference-guided RL fine-tuning.

title: CardioDiT: Latent Diffusion Transformers for 4D Cardiac MRI Synthesis
summary: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) have recently achieved strong performance in 3D medical image synthesis. However, modalities like cine cardiac MRI (CMR), representing a temporally synchronized 3D volume across the cardiac cycle, add an additional dimension that most generative approaches do not model directly. Instead, they factorize space and time or enforce temporal consistency through auxiliary mechanisms such as anatomical masks. Such strategies introduce structural biases that may limit global context integration and lead to subtle spatiotemporal discontinuities or physiologically inconsistent cardiac dynamics. We investigate whether a unified 4D generative model can learn continuous cardiac dynamics without architectural factorization. We propose CardioDiT, a fully 4D latent diffusion framework for short-axis cine CMR synthesis based on diffusion transformers. A spatiotemporal VQ-VAE encodes 2D+t slices into compact latents, which a diffusion transformer then models jointly as complete 3D+t volumes, coupling space and time throughout the generative process. We evaluate CardioDiT on public CMR datasets and a larger private cohort, comparing it to baselines with progressively stronger spatiotemporal coupling. Results show improved inter-slice consistency, temporally coherent motion, and realistic cardiac function distributions, suggesting that explicit 4D modeling with a diffusion transformer provides a principled foundation for spatiotemporal cardiac image synthesis. Code and models trained on public data are available at https://github.com/Cardio-AI/cardiodit.

AI.tldr: CardioDiT introduces a fully 4D latent diffusion model for cine CMR that jointly models 3D space + time without architectural factorization, using a spatiotemporal VQ-VAE encoder and a diffusion transformer to synthesize consistent 4D cardiac dynamics; demonstrated gains in inter-slice coherence and physiologic plausibility; code available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Current generative approaches for cine CMR often factorize space and time or enforce temporal consistency with auxiliary anatomical constraints, which can bias learning, limit global context integration, and cause spatiotemporal discontinuities. A unified 4D model could learn continuous cardiac dynamics and improve realism.
AI.method: CardioDiT uses a spatiotemporal VQ-VAE to encode 2D+t slices into compact latent representations, which are then modeled jointly as complete 3D+t volumes by a diffusion transformer, enabling simultaneous spatial and temporal generation. The model is evaluated on public CMR datasets and a larger private cohort, with baselines that progressively increase spatiotemporal coupling.
AI.result: The approach yields improved inter-slice consistency, temporally coherent motion, and realistic distributions of cardiac function compared to baselines, indicating that explicit 4D modeling with a diffusion transformer provides a robust foundation for spatiotemporal cardiac image synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Explicit 4D modeling with a diffusion transformer is a principled and effective framework for 4D cardiac image synthesis, enabling more realistic and consistent cardiac dynamics. Code and models trained on public data are available at the provided GitHub link.

title: TacSIm: A Dataset and Benchmark for Football Tactical Style Imitation
summary: Current football imitation research primarily aims to opti mize reward-based objectives, such as goals scored or win rate proxies, paying less attention to accurately replicat ing real-world team tactical behaviors. We introduce Tac SIm, a large-scale dataset and benchmark for Tactical Style Imitation in football. TacSIm imitates the acitons of all 11 players in one team in the given broadcast footage of Pre mier League matches under a single broadcast view. Under a offensive or defensive broadcast footage, TacSIm projects the beginning positions and actions of all 22 players from both sides onto a standard pitch coordinate system. Tac SIm offers an explicit style imitation task and evaluation protocols. Tactics style imitation is measured by using spatial occupancy similarity and movement vector similarity in defined time, supporting the evaluation of spatial and tem poral similarities for one team. We run multiple baseline methods in a unified virtual environment to generate full team behaviors, enabling both quantitative and visual as sessment of tactical coordination. By using unified data and metrics from broadcast to simulation, TacSIm estab lishes a rigorous benchmark for measuring and modeling style-aligned tactical imitation task in football.

AI.tldr: TacSIm is a large-scale dataset and benchmark for Tactical Style Imitation in football, mapping broadcast actions of 22 players to a standard pitch and introducing an explicit style imitation task with spatial occupancy and movement vector similarity metrics, plus a unified simulation environment for baseline evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current imitation research focuses on reward-based objectives (goals/win proxies) and often neglects replicating real-world team tactical behaviors. TacSIm targets explicit, style-aligned imitation of full-team tactics to enable rigorous evaluation and modeling of tactical coordination.
AI.method: Construct a dataset from Premier League broadcast footage, projecting the starting positions and actions of all 22 players onto a standard pitch coordinate system within a single broadcast view. Define an explicit style imitation task with evaluation metrics based on spatial occupancy similarity and movement vector similarity over defined time windows. Provide a unified virtual environment to run multiple baseline methods that generate full-team behaviors for quantitative and visual assessment of tactical coordination.
AI.result: TacSIm offers explicit task definitions and evaluation protocols, enabling quantitative and visual assessment of team-level tactical imitation. It establishes a rigorous benchmark for style-aligned imitation in football, with baseline methods runnable in a unified simulation environment.
AI.conclusion: TacSIm advances the study of football tactics by providing a scalable dataset, standardized evaluation metrics, and an integrated simulation benchmark to model and assess style-consistent tactical imitation beyond traditional reward optimization.

title: Image Rotation Angle Estimation: Comparing Circular-Aware Methods
comment: 7 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Under review at Pattern Recognition Letters
summary: Automatic image rotation estimation is a key preprocessing step in many vision pipelines. This task is challenging because angles have circular topology, creating boundary discontinuities that hinder standard regression methods. We present a comprehensive study of five circular-aware methods for global orientation estimation: direct angle regression with circular loss, classification via angular binning, unit-vector regression, phase-shifting coder, and circular Gaussian distribution. Using transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained models, we systematically evaluate these methods across sixteen modern architectures by adapting their output heads for rotation-specific predictions. Our results show that probabilistic methods, particularly the circular Gaussian distribution, are the most robust across architectures, while classification achieves the best accuracy on well-matched backbones but suffers training instabilities on others. The best configuration (classification with EfficientViT-B3) achieves a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.23° (mean across five independent runs) on the DRC-D dataset, while the circular Gaussian distribution with MambaOut Base achieves a virtually identical 1.24° with greater robustness across backbones. Training and evaluating our top-performing method-architecture combinations on COCO 2014, the best configuration reaches 3.71° MAE, improving substantially over prior work, with further improvement to 2.84° on the larger COCO 2017 dataset.

AI.tldr: Evaluates five circular-aware methods for global rotation estimation across 16 backbones; probabilistic circular Gaussian is the most robust; classification performs best on well-matched backbones; substantial MAE improvements on COCO datasets.
AI.motivation: Rotation estimation has circular topology causing boundary discontinuities that hinder standard regression; need methods that respect circular nature for robust, transferable performance across architectures.
AI.method: Compare five circular-aware approaches (direct angle regression with circular loss, angular binning classification, unit-vector regression, phase-shifting coder, circular Gaussian distribution) using ImageNet-pretrained backbones with adapted heads; evaluate across 16 modern architectures; report MAE on DRC-D, COCO 2014, and COCO 2017.
AI.result: Circular Gaussian distribution is the most robust across backbones; classification achieves best accuracy on well-matched backbones but unstable otherwise. Best config: classification with EfficientViT-B3 yields MAE 1.23° on DRC-D; circular Gaussian with MambaOut Base yields 1.24°. On COCO 2014, best config reaches 3.71° MAE; on COCO 2017, 2.84° MAE, with substantial improvement over prior work.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic circular methods, especially circular Gaussian, offer robustness across diverse architectures; classification can be most accurate with suitable backbones but is less robust; overall results demonstrate meaningful improvements for rotation estimation on large-scale datasets.

title: CIV-DG: Conditional Instrumental Variables for Domain Generalization in Medical Imaging
summary: Cross-site generalizability in medical AI is fundamentally compromised by selection bias, a structural mechanism where patient demographics (e.g., age, severity) non-randomly dictate hospital assignment. Conventional Domain Generalization (DG) paradigms, which predominantly target image-level distribution shifts, fail to address the resulting spurious correlations between site-specific variations and diagnostic labels. To surmount this identifiability barrier, we propose CIV-DG, a causal framework that leverages Conditional Instrumental Variables to disentangle pathological semantics from scanner-induced artifacts. By relaxing the strict random assignment assumption of standard IV methods, CIV-DG accommodates complex clinical scenarios where hospital selection is endogenously driven by patient demographics. We instantiate this theory via a Deep Generalized Method of Moments (DeepGMM) architecture, employing a conditional critic to minimize moment violations and enforce instrument-error orthogonality within demographic strata. Extensive experiments on the Camelyon17 benchmark and large-scale Chest X-Ray datasets demonstrate that CIV-DG significantly outperforms leading baselines, validating the efficacy of conditional causal mechanisms in resolving structural confounding for robust medical AI.

AI.tldr: A causal DG framework (CIV-DG) using Conditional Instrumental Variables and DeepGMM to separate pathology from scanner artifacts, addressing endogeneity from non-random hospital selection and improving cross-site generalization in medical imaging.
AI.motivation: Cross-site generalizability in medical AI is undermined by selection bias: patient demographics non-randomly influence hospital assignment, creating spurious correlations between site-specific variations and labels. Traditional DG focuses on image-level shifts and misses these structural confounders.
AI.method: CIV-DG introduces conditional instrumental variables to disentangle pathology from scanner-induced artifacts. It relaxes the strict random assignment assumption of standard IVs to handle endogeneous hospital selection driven by patient demographics. Implemented with a DeepGMM architecture and a conditional critic that minimizes moment violations and enforces instrument-error orthogonality within demographic strata.
AI.result: On Camelyon17 and large-scale Chest X-Ray datasets, CIV-DG significantly outperforms leading baselines, demonstrating effective mitigation of structural confounding and improved cross-site generalization.
AI.conclusion: Conditional causal mechanisms via CIV-DG can enhance robust medical AI under selection bias, with evidence from medical imaging benchmarks; the approach offers a path to more reliable cross-site performance in clinical deployment.

title: Probabilistic Concept Graph Reasoning for Multimodal Misinformation Detection
summary: Multimodal misinformation poses an escalating challenge that often evades traditional detectors, which are opaque black boxes and fragile against new manipulation tactics. We present Probabilistic Concept Graph Reasoning (PCGR), an interpretable and evolvable framework that reframes multimodal misinformation detection (MMD) as structured and concept-based reasoning. PCGR follows a build-then-infer paradigm, which first constructs a graph of human-understandable concept nodes, including novel high-level concepts automatically discovered and validated by multimodal large language models (MLLMs), and then applies hierarchical attention over this concept graph to infer claim veracity. This design produces interpretable reasoning chains linking evidence to conclusions. Experiments demonstrate that PCGR achieves state-of-the-art MMD accuracy and robustness to emerging manipulation types, outperforming prior methods in both coarse detection and fine-grained manipulation recognition.

AI.tldr: PCGR reframes multimodal misinformation detection as structured concept-based reasoning via a graph of human-understandable concepts, including novel high-level concepts discovered/validated by multimodal LLMs, followed by hierarchical attention to infer veracity; claims state-of-the-art performance and robustness.
AI.motivation: Traditional detectors are opaque and fragile to new manipulation tactics; there is a need for interpretable, evolvable MMD methods that can adapt to emerging misinformation.
AI.method: Build-then-infer: construct a graph of concept nodes (including automatically discovered high-level concepts via multimodal LLMs), then apply hierarchical attention over the graph to infer claim veracity, yielding interpretable reasoning chains linking evidence to conclusions.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art accuracy in multimodal misinformation detection and robustness to emerging manipulation types; outperforming prior methods in both coarse detection and fine-grained manipulation recognition.
AI.conclusion: PCGR offers an interpretable, evolvable framework for MMD by leveraging structured concept reasoning and MLLM-driven concept discovery, improving robustness and transparency of detections.

title: SDD-YOLO: A Small-Target Detection Framework for Ground-to-Air Anti-UAV Surveillance with Edge-Efficient Deployment
summary: Detecting small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) from a ground-to-air (G2A) perspective presents significant challenges, including extremely low pixel occupancy, cluttered aerial backgrounds, and strict real-time constraints. Existing YOLO-based detectors are primarily optimized for general object detection and often lack adequate feature resolution for sub-pixel targets, while introducing complexities during deployment. In this paper, we propose SDD-YOLO, a small-target detection framework tailored for G2A anti-UAV surveillance. To capture fine-grained spatial details critical for micro-targets, SDD-YOLO introduces a P2 high-resolution detection head operating at 4 times downsampling. Furthermore, we integrate the recent architectural advancements from YOLO26, including a DFL-free, NMS-free architecture for streamlined inference, and the MuSGD hybrid training strategy with ProgLoss and STAL, which substantially mitigates gradient oscillation on sparse small-target signals. To support our evaluation, we construct DroneSOD-30K, a large-scale G2A dataset comprising approximately 30,000 annotated images covering diverse meteorological conditions. Experiments demonstrate that SDD-YOLO-n achieves a mAP@0.5 of 86.0% on DroneSOD-30K, surpassing the YOLOv5n baseline by 7.8 percentage points. Extensive inference analysis shows our model attains 226 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 5090 and 35 FPS on an Intel Xeon CPU, demonstrating exceptional efficiency for future edge deployment.

AI.tldr: SDD-YOLO introduces a high-resolution, efficient G2A small-target detector leveraging a 4x downsampling P2 head, NMS-free YOLO26-based design, and MuSGD training (ProgLoss + STAL) on the DroneSOD-30K dataset; achieves 86.0% mAP@0.5, 226 FPS (RTX 5090), 35 FPS (CPU), outperforming YOLOv5n by 7.8 points.
AI.motivation: Detect small UAVs from ground-to-air scenes with extremely low pixel occupancy, cluttered backgrounds, and real-time constraints; existing YOLO detectors lack sub-pixel resolution and deployability for micro-targets.
AI.method: P2 high-resolution detection head at 4x downsampling; adopt YOLO26 advancements including DFL-free, NMS-free architecture; use MuSGD hybrid training with ProgLoss and STAL to stabilize gradients on sparse signals.
AI.result: mAP@0.5 = 86.0% on DroneSOD-30K; 226 FPS on NVIDIA RTX 5090; 35 FPS on CPU; YOLOv5n baseline lower by 7.8 percentage points; DroneSOD-30K comprises ~30k annotated images across diverse meteorological conditions.
AI.conclusion: SDD-YOLO-n demonstrates strong accuracy and edge deployment efficiency for G2A anti-UAV surveillance, with a new large-scale dataset enabling robust evaluation; potential limitations and future work include broader benchmark comparisons and assessment of generalization to unseen environments.

title: Training-free Detection and 6D Pose Estimation of Unseen Surgical Instruments
summary: Purpose: Accurate detection and 6D pose estimation of surgical instruments are crucial for many computer-assisted interventions. However, supervised methods lack flexibility for new or unseen tools and require extensive annotated data. This work introduces a training-free pipeline for accurate multi-view 6D pose estimation of unseen surgical instruments, which only requires a textured CAD model as prior knowledge. Methods: Our pipeline consists of two main stages. First, for detection, we generate object mask proposals in each view and score their similarity to rendered templates using a pre-trained feature extractor. Detections are matched across views, triangulated into 3D instance candidates, and filtered using multi-view geometric consistency. Second, for pose estimation, a set of pose hypotheses is iteratively refined and scored using feature-metric scores with cross-view attention. The best hypothesis undergoes a final refinement using a novel multi-view, occlusion-aware contour registration, which minimizes reprojection errors of unoccluded contour points. Results: The proposed method was rigorously evaluated on real-world surgical data from the MVPSP dataset. The method achieves millimeter-accurate pose estimates that are on par with supervised methods under controlled conditions, while maintaining full generalization to unseen instruments. These results demonstrate the feasibility of training-free, marker-less detection and tracking in surgical scenes, and highlight the unique challenges in surgical environments. Conclusion: We present a novel and flexible pipeline that effectively combines state-of-the-art foundational models, multi-view geometry, and contour-based refinement for high-accuracy 6D pose estimation of surgical instruments without task-specific training. This approach enables robust instrument tracking and scene understanding in dynamic clinical environments.

AI.tldr: Training-free, multi-view 6D pose estimation for unseen surgical instruments using textured CAD priors; two-stage pipeline with detection via template similarity and multi-view geometry, followed by pose refinement with feature-metric scores, cross-view attention, and occlusion-aware contour registration; millimeter accuracy on MVPSP and strong generalization to unseen tools.
AI.motivation: Supervised pose estimation methods require large annotated datasets and struggle to generalize to unseen instruments. A training-free approach using CAD priors could provide flexibility, robustness, and marker-less tracking in dynamic surgical environments.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline. Stage 1: detect by generating per-view object masks, score using a pre-trained feature extractor against rendered templates, match detections across views, triangulate 3D candidates, and filter via multi-view geometric consistency. Stage 2: generate pose hypotheses, refine and score with feature-metric scores and cross-view attention; select best hypothesis and perform final refinement with a novel multi-view, occlusion-aware contour registration that minimizes reprojection error of unoccluded contour points.
AI.result: Evaluated on MVPSP real-world surgical data; achieves millimeter-accurate 6D poses comparable to supervised methods under controlled conditions, with full generalization to unseen instruments.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a flexible, training-free pipeline that integrates foundational models, multi-view geometry, and contour-based refinement to achieve high-accuracy 6D pose estimation of surgical instruments without task-specific training, enabling robust tracking in dynamic clinical environments.

title: A Unified Spatial Alignment Framework for Highly Transferable Transformation-Based Attacks on Spatially Structured Tasks
summary: Transformation-based adversarial attacks (TAAs) demonstrate strong transferability when deceiving classification models. However, existing TAAs often perform unsatisfactorily or even fail when applied to structured tasks such as semantic segmentation and object detection. Encouragingly, recent studies that categorize transformations into non-spatial and spatial transformations inspire us to address this challenge. We find that for non-structured tasks, labels are spatially non-structured, and thus TAAs are not required to adjust labels when applying spatial transformations. In contrast, for structured tasks, labels are spatially structured, and failing to transform labels synchronously with inputs can cause spatial misalignment and yield erroneous gradients. To address these issues, we propose a novel unified Spatial Alignment Framework (SAF) for highly transferable TAAs on spatially structured tasks, where the TAAs spatially transform labels synchronously with the input using the proposed Spatial Alignment (SA) algorithm. Extensive experiments demonstrate the crucial role of our SAF for TAAs on structured tasks. Specifically, in non-targeted attacks, our SAF degrades the average mIoU on Cityscapes from 24.50 to 11.34, and on Kvasir-SEG from 49.91 to 31.80, while reducing the average mAP of COCO from 17.89 to 5.25.

AI.tldr: A Spatial Alignment Framework (SAF) synchronizes label transformations with input transformations to improve transferability of transformation-based adversarial attacks on structured tasks like segmentation and detection.
AI.motivation: Non-structured tasks have spatially non-structured labels; applying spatial transformations to inputs without transforming labels causes misalignment and poor gradients in structured tasks. A unified framework is needed to align inputs and labels to enhance attack transferability.
AI.method: Propose SAF and Spatial Alignment (SA) algorithm to apply spatial transformations to both inputs and labels during TAAs, achieving synchronized spatial distortion for structured targets.
AI.result: SAF substantially lowers performance on victim models: non-targeted attack mIoU on Cityscapes drops from 24.50 to 11.34; Kvasir-SEG from 49.91 to 31.80; COCO mAP from 17.89 to 5.25, indicating improved transferability.
AI.conclusion: SAF is crucial for high-transferability TAAs on spatially structured tasks; aligning label transformations with input transformations mitigates misalignment and yields stronger adversarial effects.

title: Efficient Preemptive Robustification with Image Sharpening
summary: Despite their great success, deep neural networks rely on high-dimensional, non-robust representations, making them vulnerable to imperceptible perturbations, even in transfer scenarios. To address this, both training-time defenses (e.g., adversarial training and robust architecture design) and post-attack defenses (e.g., input purification and adversarial detection) have been extensively studied. Recently, a limited body of work has preliminarily explored a pre-attack defense paradigm, termed preemptive robustification, which introduces subtle modifications to benign samples prior to attack to proactively resist adversarial perturbations. Unfortunately, their practical applicability remains questionable due to several limitations, including (1) reliance on well-trained classifiers as surrogates to provide robustness priors, (2) substantial computational overhead arising from iterative optimization or trained generators for robustification, and (3) limited interpretability of the optimization- or generation-based robustification processes. Inspired by recent studies revealing a positive correlation between texture intensity and the robustness of benign samples, we show that image sharpening alone can efficiently robustify images.
