To the best of our knowledge, this is the first surrogate-free, optimization-free, generator-free, and human-interpretable robustification approach. Extensive experiments demonstrate that sharpening yields remarkable robustness gains with low computational cost, especially in transfer scenarios.

AI.tldr: Sharpening images before attack acts as a surrogate-free, optimization-free preemptive robustification method, yielding strong robustness gains with low computation, especially in transfer scenarios. First approach of its kind.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of existing pre-attack defenses (reliance on robust classifiers, heavy computation, poor interpretability) by leveraging a simple, interpretable transformation tied to the observed texture-robustness link.
AI.method: Apply image sharpening as a pre-attack modification (no optimization or generators), and evaluate robustness against adversarial perturbations, including transfer scenarios, highlighting interpretability and low cost.
AI.result: Sharpening delivers significant robustness gains with low computational overhead; competitive or superior in transfer settings; constitutes a surrogate-/generator-/optimization-free, human-interpretable robustification.
AI.conclusion: Textural emphasis via sharpening is a practical and interpretable preemptive defense with broad applicability and potential to guide future preemptive robustification strategies.

title: CHIRP dataset: towards long-term, individual-level, behavioral monitoring of bird populations in the wild
summary: Long-term behavioral monitoring of individual animals is crucial for studying behavioral changes that occur over different time scales, especially for conservation and evolutionary biology. Computer vision methods have proven to benefit biodiversity monitoring, but automated behavior monitoring in wild populations remains challenging. This stems from the lack of datasets that cover a range of computer vision tasks necessary to extract biologically meaningful measurements of individual animals. Here, we introduce such a dataset (CHIRP) with a new method (CORVID) for individual re-identification of wild birds. The CHIRP (Combining beHaviour, Individual Re-identification and Postures) dataset is curated from a long-term population of wild Siberian jays studied in Swedish Lapland, supporting re-identification (re-id), action recognition, 2D keypoint estimation, object detection, and instance segmentation. In addition to traditional task-specific benchmarking, we introduce application-specific benchmarking with biologically relevant metrics (feeding rates, co-occurrence rates) to evaluate the performance of models in real-world use cases. Finally, we present CORVID (COlouR-based Video re-ID), a novel pipeline for individual identification of birds based on the segmentation and classification of colored leg rings, a widespread approach for visual identification of individual birds. CORVID offers a probability-based id tracking method by matching the detected combination of color rings with a database. We use application-specific benchmarking to show that CORVID outperforms state-of-the-art re-id methods. We hope this work offers the community a blueprint for curating real-world datasets from ethically approved biological studies to bridge the gap between computer vision research and biological applications.

AI.tldr: Introduces CHIRP, a long-term bird dataset, and CORVID, a color-ring–based re-identification pipeline. Supports re-id, action recognition, 2D keypoints, detection, and segmentation with biology-aware metrics. CORVID outperforms state-of-the-art re-id methods; aims to bridge computer vision and biology through ethically approved, real-world data.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap between CV research and biological applications by providing a real-world, multi-task dataset for individual animal monitoring, along with application-specific metrics and a practical ID-tracking method using leg color rings.
AI.method: CHIRP compiles long-term, multi-task annotations (re-id, actions, keypoints, detection, segmentation) from a wild Siberian jay population. CORVID detects color rings, segments birds, classifies rings, and matches color-ring configurations to a database to probabilistically track individual IDs over time.
AI.result: Application-specific benchmarks (feeding rates, co-occurrence) and standard CV benchmarks show CORVID outperforms existing re-id methods. The dataset and pipeline demonstrate practical utility for real-world ecological monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Provides a blueprint for ethically approved, real-world biological datasets that enable multi-task CV benchmarking and actionable metrics, thereby bridging computer vision with ecological monitoring.

title: DeepFAN, a transformer-based deep learning model for human-artificial intelligence collaborative assessment of incidental pulmonary nodules in CT scans: a multi-reader, multi-case trial
comment: 28 pages for main text and 37 pages for supplementary information, 7 figures in main text and 9 figures in supplementary information
summary: The widespread adoption of CT has notably increased the number of detected lung nodules. However, current deep learning methods for classifying benign and malignant nodules often fail to comprehensively integrate global and local features, and most of them have not been validated through clinical trials. To address this, we developed DeepFAN, a transformer-based model trained on over 10K pathology-confirmed nodules and further conducted a multi-reader, multi-case clinical trial to evaluate its efficacy in assisting junior radiologists. DeepFAN achieved diagnostic area under the curve (AUC) of 0.939 (95% CI 0.930-0.948) on an internal test set and 0.954 (95% CI 0.934-0.973) on the clinical trial dataset involving 400 cases across three independent medical institutions. Explainability analysis indicated higher contributions from global than local features. Twelve readers' average performance significantly improved by 10.9% (95% CI 8.3%-13.5%) in AUC, 10.0% (95% CI 8.9%-11.1%) in accuracy, 7.6% (95% CI 6.1%-9.2%) in sensitivity, and 12.6% (95% CI 10.9%-14.3%) in specificity (P<0.001 for all). Nodule-level inter-reader diagnostic consistency improved from fair to moderate (overall k: 0.313 vs. 0.421; P=0.019). In conclusion, DeepFAN effectively assisted junior radiologists and may help homogenize diagnostic quality and reduce unnecessary follow-up of indeterminate pulmonary nodules. Chinese Clinical Trial Registry: ChiCTR2400084624.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based DeepFAN achieves high AUC in classifying benign vs malignant lung nodules, validated in a multicenter clinical trial; improves junior radiologists' performance and inter-reader agreement; explainability favors global features; potential to standardize diagnostic quality and reduce unnecessary follow-ups.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of prior deep learning methods that inadequately integrate global and local features and lack clinical validation, aiming to assist radiologists and standardize nodule assessment.
AI.method: Train transformer-based DeepFAN on >10K pathology-confirmed nodules. Evaluate on internal test set (AUC 0.939) and on a multicenter clinical trial with 400 cases across three institutions. Conduct a multi-reader, multi-case study with 12 readers. Perform explainability analysis to compare global vs local feature contributions. Report AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity with 95% CIs and statistical significance, plus inter-reader agreement (kappa).
AI.result: Internal AUC 0.939 (95% CI 0.930–0.948); clinical trial AUC 0.954 (95% CI 0.934–0.973). Readers’ average performance improved by 10.9% in AUC, 10.0% in accuracy, 7.6% in sensitivity, 12.6% in specificity (P<0.001 for all). Nodule-level inter-reader kappa improved from 0.313 to 0.421 (P=0.019). Explainability shows greater contribution from global features than local features.
AI.conclusion: DeepFAN effectively assists junior radiologists, potentially homogenizing diagnostic quality and reducing unnecessary follow-up for indeterminate nodules. Registry: ChiCTR2400084624.

title: Demographic Fairness in Multimodal LLMs: A Benchmark of Gender and Ethnicity Bias in Face Verification
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently been explored as face verification systems that determine whether two face images are of the same person. Unlike dedicated face recognition systems, MLLMs approach this task through visual prompting and rely on general visual and reasoning abilities. However, the demographic fairness of these models remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we present a benchmarking study that evaluates nine open-source MLLMs from six model families, ranging from 2B to 8B parameters, on the IJB-C and RFW face verification protocols across four ethnicity groups and two gender groups. We measure verification accuracy with the Equal Error Rate and True Match Rate at multiple operating points per demographic group, and we quantify demographic disparity with four FMR-based fairness metrics. Our results show that FaceLLM-8B, the only face-specialised model in our study, substantially outperforms general-purpose MLLMs on both benchmarks. The bias patterns we observe differ from those commonly reported for traditional face recognition, with different groups being most affected depending on the benchmark and the model. We also note that the most accurate models are not necessarily the fairest and that models with poor overall accuracy can appear fair simply because they produce uniformly high error rates across all demographic groups.

AI.tldr: Benchmarking nine open-source Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) on face verification fairness across IJB-C and RFW, spanning 2B–8B parameters. FaceLLM-8B (face-specialized) dominates across benchmarks; bias patterns vary by model and benchmark, contradicting traditional FRS tendencies; accuracy and fairness trade-offs observed as high accuracy does not imply fairness, and uniformly high errors can mask bias.
AI.motivation: Investigate the under-explored demographic fairness of MLLMs in face verification, addressing a gap where most studies focus on accuracy without accounting for disparate impact across demographic groups.
AI.method: Evaluate nine open-source MLLMs from six model families (2B–8B) on IJB-C and RFW face verification benchmarks. Compute verification metrics—Equal Error Rate (EER) and True Match Rate (TMR) at multiple operating points—for each demographic subgroup (four ethnicity groups and two genders). Use four FMR-based fairness metrics to quantify demographic disparities.
AI.result: FaceLLM-8B, the only face-specialized model in the study, substantially outperforms general-purpose MLLMs on both benchmarks. Demographic bias patterns differ from traditional face recognition, varying by benchmark and model. Notably, the most accurate models are not necessarily the fairest; models with poor overall accuracy can appear fair if they produce uniformly high error rates across all groups.
AI.conclusion: Demographic fairness in MLLMs for face verification is nuanced and highly model- and benchmark-dependent. The study highlights an accuracy–fairness trade-off and cautions against equating low disparity with high accuracy; specialized, high-accuracy models tend to be less biased, but no universal fairness conclusion holds across datasets. Future work should integrate fairness considerations into model design and evaluation beyond raw accuracy.

title: Towards Practical Lossless Neural Compression for LiDAR Point Clouds
summary: LiDAR point clouds are fundamental to various applications, yet the extreme sparsity of high-precision geometric details hinders efficient context modeling, thereby limiting the compression speed and performance of existing methods. To address this challenge, we propose a compact representation for efficient predictive lossless coding. Our framework comprises two lightweight modules. First, the Geometry Re-Densification Module iteratively densifies encoded sparse geometry, extracts features at a dense scale, and then sparsifies the features for predictive coding. This module avoids costly computation on highly sparse details while maintaining a lightweight prediction head. Second, the Cross-scale Feature Propagation Module leverages occupancy cues from multiple resolution levels to guide hierarchical feature propagation, enabling information sharing across scales and reducing redundant feature extraction. Additionally, we introduce an integer-only inference pipeline to enable bit-exact cross-platform consistency, which avoids the entropy-coding collapse observed in existing neural compression methods and further accelerates coding. Experiments demonstrate competitive compression performance at real-time speed. Code will be released upon acceptance. Code is available at https://github.com/pengpeng-yu/FastPCC.

AI.tldr: A compact two-module framework for predictive lossless coding of sparse LiDAR geometry, featuring a Geometry Re-Densification Module and Cross-scale Feature Propagation, plus an integer-only inference pipeline for bit-exact cross-platform consistency and real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Extreme sparsity of high-precision LiDAR details hinders context modeling and slows compression; a fast, lossless, and accurate representation is needed.
AI.method: 1) Geometry Re-Densification Module densifies encoded sparse geometry to a dense representation, extracts features at dense scale, then sparsifies features for predictive coding, maintaining a lightweight prediction head; 2) Cross-scale Feature Propagation Module uses occupancy cues from multiple resolutions to guide hierarchical feature propagation, enabling cross-scale information sharing and reducing redundant feature extraction; 3) integer-only inference to ensure bit-exact results and avoid entropy-coding collapse, accelerating coding.
AI.result: Experiments show competitive compression performance at real-time speed; code will be released (GitHub link provided).
AI.conclusion: The approach provides an efficient, scalable solution for lossless LiDAR compression by combining density-aware feature processing, multi-scale propagation, and bit-exact integer inference, achieving real-time performance.

title: Just Zoom In: Cross-View Geo-Localization via Autoregressive Zooming
summary: Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) estimates a camera's location by matching a street-view image to geo-referenced overhead imagery, enabling GPS-denied localization and navigation. Existing methods almost universally formulate CVGL as an image-retrieval problem in a contrastively trained embedding space. This ties performance to large batches and hard negative mining, and it ignores both the geometric structure of maps and the coverage mismatch between street-view and overhead imagery. In particular, salient landmarks visible from the street view can fall outside a fixed satellite crop, making retrieval targets ambiguous and limiting explicit spatial inference over the map. We propose Just Zoom In, an alternative formulation that performs CVGL via autoregressive zooming over a city-scale overhead map. Starting from a coarse satellite view, the model takes a short sequence of zoom-in decisions to select a terminal satellite cell at a target resolution, without contrastive losses or hard negative mining. We further introduce a realistic benchmark with crowd-sourced street views and high-resolution satellite imagery that reflects real capture conditions. On this benchmark, Just Zoom In achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Recall@1 within 50 m by 5.5% and Recall@1 within 100 m by 9.6% over the strongest contrastive-retrieval baseline. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of sequential coarse-to-fine spatial reasoning for cross-view geo-localization.

AI.tldr: Just Zoom In uses autoregressive zooming over city-scale overhead maps for cross-view geo-localization, avoiding contrastive learning and achieving state-of-the-art results on a realistic benchmark.
AI.motivation: Contrastive-image retrieval for CVGL relies on large batches and hard negative mining, ignores geometric map structure, and struggles with coverage mismatch between street-view and overhead imagery; there's a need for explicit spatial reasoning over maps.
AI.method: Autoregressive zooming: start from a coarse satellite view and iteratively zoom into the city-scale map to select a terminal satellite cell at the target resolution; no contrastive losses or hard negative mining; leverages coarse-to-fine spatial reasoning.
AI.result: On a new realistic benchmark with crowd-sourced street views and high-resolution satellite imagery, Just Zoom In achieves state-of-the-art performance, with Recall@1 within 50 m improving by 5.5% and Recall@1 within 100 m improving by 9.6% over the strongest contrastive-retrieval baseline.
AI.conclusion: Sequential coarse-to-fine spatial reasoning over overhead maps is effective for CVGL, enabling improved localization without reliance on contrastive training or large-batch mining.

title: ViewSplat: View-Adaptive Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Feed-Forward Synthesis
summary: We present ViewSplat, a view-adaptive 3D Gaussian splatting network for novel view synthesis from unposed images. While recent feed-forward 3D Gaussian splatting has significantly accelerated 3D scene reconstruction by bypassing per-scene optimization, a fundamental fidelity gap remains. We attribute this bottleneck to the limited capacity of single-step feed-forward networks to regress static Gaussian primitives that satisfy all viewpoints. To address this limitation, we shift the paradigm from static primitive regression to view-adaptive dynamic splatting. Instead of a rigid Gaussian representation, our pipeline learns a view-adaptable latent representation. Specifically, ViewSplat initially predicts base Gaussian primitives alongside the weights of dynamic MLPs. During rendering, these MLPs take target view coordinates as input and predict view-dependent residual updates for each Gaussian attribute (i.e., 3D position, scale, rotation, opacity, and color). This mechanism, which we term view-adaptive dynamic splatting, allows each primitive to rectify initial estimation errors, effectively capturing high-fidelity appearances. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ViewSplat achieves state-of-the-art fidelity while maintaining fast inference (17 FPS) and real-time rendering (154 FPS).

AI.tldr: ViewSplat introduces view-adaptive dynamic splatting, replacing static Gaussian primitives with a base set plus view-conditioned MLPs that predict residual updates per view. This enables higher fidelity rendering for unposed images while maintaining fast inference and real-time rendering.
AI.motivation: Bridge the fidelity gap in feed-forward 3D Gaussian splatting caused by static primitives that cannot satisfy all viewpoints. Leverage view-conditioned residual refinements to achieve high-fidelity 3D reconstructions from unposed images.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) learn base Gaussian primitives and weights of dynamic MLPs; (2) during rendering, feed target view coordinates into the MLPs to produce view-dependent residual updates for Gaussian attributes (position, scale, rotation, opacity, color), enabling per-view refinement of the primitives.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art fidelity among feed-forward Gaussian splatting methods, with high rendering speed: 17 FPS for inference and 154 FPS for rendering.
AI.conclusion: ViewSplat demonstrates that view-adaptive dynamic splatting can surpass static-primitives approaches in fidelity without sacrificing speed, by incorporating per-view residual updates via conditionally dynamic MLPs that adjust Gaussian attributes to match target views.

title: EagleNet: Energy-Aware Fine-Grained Relationship Learning Network for Text-Video Retrieval
summary: Text-video retrieval tasks have seen significant improvements due to the recent development of large-scale vision-language pre-trained models. Traditional methods primarily focus on video representations or cross-modal alignment, while recent works shift toward enriching text expressiveness to better match the rich semantics in videos. However, these methods use only interactions between text and frames/video, and ignore rich interactions among the internal frames within a video, so the final expanded text cannot capture frame contextual information, leading to disparities between text and video. In response, we introduce Energy-Aware Fine-Grained Relationship Learning Network (EagleNet) to generate accurate and context-aware enriched text embeddings. Specifically, the proposed Fine-Grained Relationship Learning mechanism (FRL) first constructs a text-frame graph by the generated text candidates and frames, then learns relationships among texts and frames, which are finally used to aggregate text candidates into an enriched text embedding that incorporates frame contextual information. To further improve fine-grained relationship learning in FRL, we design Energy-Aware Matching (EAM) to model the energy of text-frame interactions and thus accurately capture the distribution of real text-video pairs. Moreover, for more effective cross-modal alignment and stable training, we replace the conventional softmax-based contrastive loss with the sigmoid loss. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the superiority of EagleNet across MSRVTT, DiDeMo, MSVD, and VATEX. Codes are available at https://github.com/draym28/EagleNet.

AI.tldr: EagleNet enriches text embeddings for text-video retrieval by modeling fine-grained text-frame relationships and frame context, using energy-aware matching and sigmoid loss for stable cross-modal alignment, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current methods largely focus on either video representations or text-frame interactions, neglecting internal frame context and the distribution of real text-video pairs. There is a need for context-aware text representations that incorporate frame-level interactions within videos to better align with video semantics.
AI.method: FRL constructs a text-frame graph using generated text candidates and video frames, learns fine-grained relationships among texts and frames, and aggregates text candidates into an enriched text embedding that captures frame context. EAM models the energy of text-frame interactions to reflect the distribution of real text-video pairs. A sigmoid loss is used instead of the conventional softmax-based contrastive loss for improved stability and cross-modal alignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate EagleNet's superiority across MSRVTT, DiDeMo, MSVD, and VATEX.
AI.conclusion: EagleNet effectively enriches text embeddings with frame-contextual information through FRL and improves cross-modal alignment with energy-aware matching and sigmoid loss, achieving strong performance on standard benchmarks; code is available online.

title: V2U4Real: A Real-world Large-scale Dataset for Vehicle-to-UAV Cooperative Perception
summary: Modern autonomous vehicle perception systems are often constrained by occlusions, blind spots, and limited sensing range. While existing cooperative perception paradigms, such as Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) and Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I), have demonstrated their effectiveness in mitigating these challenges, they remain limited to ground-level collaboration and cannot fully address large-scale occlusions or long-range perception in complex environments. To advance research in cross-view cooperative perception, we present V2U4Real, the first large-scale real-world multi-modal dataset for Vehicle-to-UAV (V2U) cooperative object perception. V2U4Real is collected by a ground vehicle and a UAV equipped with multi-view LiDARs and RGB cameras. The dataset covers urban streets, university campuses, and rural roads under diverse traffic scenarios, comprising over 56K LiDAR frames, 56K multi-view camera images, and 700K annotated 3D bounding boxes across four classes. To support a wide range of research tasks, we establish benchmarks for single-agent 3D object detection, cooperative 3D object detection, and object tracking. Comprehensive evaluations of several state-of-the-art models demonstrate the effectiveness of V2U cooperation in enhancing perception robustness and long-range awareness. The V2U4Real dataset and codebase is available at https://github.com/VjiaLi/V2U4Real.

AI.tldr: A new large-scale real-world multi-modal dataset (V2U4Real) for Vehicle-to-UAV cooperative perception, enabling single-agent, cooperative 3D detection, and tracking benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome occlusions, blind spots, and limited sensing range inherent in ground-only perception by leveraging UAV perspectives and multi-view sensors, addressing the shortage of real-world cross-view V2U data.
AI.method: Collect a dataset from a ground vehicle and a UAV equipped with multi-view LiDARs and RGB cameras across urban, campus, and rural settings. The dataset includes 56K LiDAR frames, 56K multi-view camera images, and 700K annotated 3D bounding boxes across four classes. Establish benchmarks for single-agent 3D object detection, cooperative 3D object detection, and object tracking; provide codebase at GitHub.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations of state-of-the-art models show the effectiveness of V2U cooperation in improving perception robustness and long-range awareness for challenging environments.
AI.conclusion: V2U4Real is a first-of-its-kind large-scale real-world multi-modal V2U dataset; it supports a broad set of tasks and is publicly available with code, fostering cross-view cooperative perception research.

title: Out of Sight but Not Out of Mind: Hybrid Memory for Dynamic Video World Models
summary: Video world models have shown immense potential in simulating the physical world, yet existing memory mechanisms primarily treat environments as static canvases. When dynamic subjects hide out of sight and later re-emerge, current methods often struggle, leading to frozen, distorted, or vanishing subjects. To address this, we introduce Hybrid Memory, a novel paradigm requiring models to simultaneously act as precise archivists for static backgrounds and vigilant trackers for dynamic subjects, ensuring motion continuity during out-of-view intervals. To facilitate research in this direction, we construct HM-World, the first large-scale video dataset dedicated to hybrid memory. It features 59K high-fidelity clips with decoupled camera and subject trajectories, encompassing 17 diverse scenes, 49 distinct subjects, and meticulously designed exit-entry events to rigorously evaluate hybrid coherence. Furthermore, we propose HyDRA, a specialized memory architecture that compresses memory into tokens and utilizes a spatiotemporal relevance-driven retrieval mechanism. By selectively attending to relevant motion cues, HyDRA effectively preserves the identity and motion of hidden subjects. Extensive experiments on HM-World demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both dynamic subject consistency and overall generation quality.

AI.tldr: Proposes Hybrid Memory to jointly preserve static backgrounds and track dynamic subjects with occlusions, backed by a new HM-World dataset and the HyDRA memory architecture; achieves superior dynamic coherence and generation quality.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of current video world models that treat environments as static canvases, failing to preserve identity and motion of dynamic subjects during occlusions or out-of-view intervals.
AI.method: Introduce Hybrid Memory paradigm; construct HM-World, a large-scale video dataset with decoupled camera and subject trajectories (59K clips, 17 scenes, 49 subjects) and exit-entry events; propose HyDRA, a memory architecture that compresses memory into tokens and uses spatiotemporal relevance-driven retrieval to attend to motion cues.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on HM-World show HyDRA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in dynamic subject consistency and overall generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Presents a new hybrid memory paradigm, supported by a dedicated dataset and a token-based, relevance-driven memory retrieval architecture, advancing the handling of occlusions and motion continuity in video world models.

title: PixelSmile: Toward Fine-Grained Facial Expression Editing
comment: 21 Pages; Project Page: https://ammmob.github.io/PixelSmile/ Code: https://github.com/Ammmob/PixelSmile
summary: Fine-grained facial expression editing has long been limited by intrinsic semantic overlap. To address this, we construct the Flex Facial Expression (FFE) dataset with continuous affective annotations and establish FFE-Bench to evaluate structural confusion, editing accuracy, linear controllability, and the trade-off between expression editing and identity preservation. We propose PixelSmile, a diffusion framework that disentangles expression semantics via fully symmetric joint training. PixelSmile combines intensity supervision with contrastive learning to produce stronger and more distinguishable expressions, achieving precise and stable linear expression control through textual latent interpolation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PixelSmile achieves superior disentanglement and robust identity preservation, confirming its effectiveness for continuous, controllable, and fine-grained expression editing, while naturally supporting smooth expression blending.

AI.tldr: Introduces PixelSmile, a diffusion-based framework for fine-grained facial expression editing that achieves continuous, controllable edits with strong disentanglement and identity preservation, aided by a new FFE dataset and FFE-Bench metrics.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained editing is hindered by intrinsic semantic overlap among expressions and the lack of continuous affective annotations; there is a need for robust disentanglement and reliable control without sacrificing identity.
AI.method: Proposes PixelSmile with fully symmetric joint training that mixes intensity supervision and contrastive learning; uses diffusion modeling with textual latent interpolation for linear, controllable expression editing; leverages the new Flex Facial Expression (FFE) dataset and FFE-Bench evaluation suite to analyze structural confusion, editing accuracy, linear controllability, and identity trade-offs.
AI.result: PixelSmile achieves stronger, more distinguishable expressions, better disentanglement, and robust identity preservation, enabling precise linear control and smooth blending for continuous, controllable, fine-grained expression editing.
AI.conclusion: PixelSmile is effective for continuous, controllable, fine-grained facial expression editing and naturally supports smooth expression blending, with demonstrated superiority in disentanglement and identity preservation.

title: Adaptive Learned Image Compression with Graph Neural Networks
summary: Efficient image compression relies on modeling both local and global redundancy. Most state-of-the-art (SOTA) learned image compression (LIC) methods are based on CNNs or Transformers, which are inherently rigid. Standard CNN kernels and window-based attention mechanisms impose fixed receptive fields and static connectivity patterns, which potentially couple non-redundant pixels simply due to their proximity in Euclidean space. This rigidity limits the model's ability to adaptively capture spatially varying redundancy across the image, particularly at the global level. To overcome these limitations, we propose a content-adaptive image compression framework based on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Specifically, our approach constructs dual-scale graphs that enable flexible, data-driven receptive fields. Furthermore, we introduce adaptive connectivity by dynamically adjusting the number of neighbors for each node based on local content complexity. These innovations empower our Graph-based Learned Image Compression (GLIC) model to effectively model diverse redundancy patterns across images, leading to more efficient and adaptive compression. Experiments demonstrate that GLIC achieves state-of-the-art performance, achieving BD-rate reductions of 19.29%, 21.69%, and 18.71% relative to VTM-9.1 on Kodak, Tecnick, and CLIC, respectively. Code will be released at https://github.com/UnoC-727/GLIC.

AI.tldr: Graph-based LIC (GLIC) uses dual-scale graphs with adaptive neighbor counts to capture local/global redundancy, achieving notable BD-rate gains over VTM-9.1 on Kodak/Tecnick/CLIC.
AI.motivation: CNN/Transformer-based LIC have fixed receptive fields and rigid connectivity, limiting global redundancy modeling; need adaptive, content-aware receptive fields.
AI.method: Construct dual-scale graphs; dynamic neighbor selection based on local content complexity; train a GNN-based LIC model (GLIC); release code.
AI.result: BD-rate reductions of 19.29% on Kodak, 21.69% on Tecnick, 18.71% on CLIC relative to VTM-9.1; state-of-the-art performance claimed.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based adaptive receptive fields in LIC enable more efficient compression by modeling diverse redundancy patterns; promising approach with potential for broader applicability.

title: PackForcing: Short Video Training Suffices for Long Video Sampling and Long Context Inference
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable progress, yet they remain bottlenecked by intractable linear KV-cache growth, temporal repetition, and compounding errors during long-video generation. To address these challenges, we present PackForcing, a unified framework that efficiently manages the generation history through a novel three-partition KV-cache strategy. Specifically, we categorize the historical context into three distinct types: (1) Sink tokens, which preserve early anchor frames at full resolution to maintain global semantics; (2) Mid tokens, which achieve a massive spatiotemporal compression (32x token reduction) via a dual-branch network fusing progressive 3D convolutions with low-resolution VAE re-encoding; and (3) Recent tokens, kept at full resolution to ensure local temporal coherence. To strictly bound the memory footprint without sacrificing quality, we introduce a dynamic top-$k$ context selection mechanism for the mid tokens, coupled with a continuous Temporal RoPE Adjustment that seamlessly re-aligns position gaps caused by dropped tokens with negligible overhead. Empowered by this principled hierarchical context compression, PackForcing can generate coherent 2-minute, 832x480 videos at 16 FPS on a single H200 GPU. It achieves a bounded KV cache of just 4 GB and enables a remarkable 24x temporal extrapolation (5s to 120s), operating effectively either zero-shot or trained on merely 5-second clips. Extensive results on VBench demonstrate state-of-the-art temporal consistency (26.07) and dynamic degree (56.25), proving that short-video supervision is sufficient for high-quality, long-video synthesis. https://github.com/ShandaAI/PackForcing

AI.tldr: Three-partition KV-cache (Sink, Mid, Recent) with dynamic top-k mid-token selection and Temporal RoPE Alignment enables long-video autoregressive diffusion with bounded memory, achieving 2-minute 832x480 videos at 16 FPS on a single H200 GPU and 24x temporal extrapolation, with strong temporal consistency on VBench.
AI.motivation: Overcoming intractable linear KV-cache growth, temporal repetition, and compounding errors in long-video generation.
AI.method: Introduce a three-partition context: Sink tokens (full-res early frames for global semantics), Mid tokens (32x compressed via dual-branch network combining 3D convs with low-res VAE re-encoding, with dynamic top-k to bound memory), and Recent tokens (full-res for local coherence). Add Temporal RoPE Adjustment to realign position gaps caused by dropped mid tokens and maintain alignment with negligible overhead.
AI.result: Bounded KV-cache of 4 GB enabling 2-minute videos at 16 FPS (832x480) on a single H200 GPU; 24x temporal extrapolation (5s to 120s); zero-shot or trained on 5s clips. On VBench, reported state-of-the-art temporal consistency (26.07) and dynamic degree (56.25).
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical context compression with dynamic mid-token selection and Temporal RoPE allows high-quality long-video synthesis with controlled memory growth, suggesting short-video supervision suffices for long-video performance.

title: MACRO: Advancing Multi-Reference Image Generation with Structured Long-Context Data
summary: Generating images conditioned on multiple visual references is critical for real-world applications such as multi-subject composition, narrative illustration, and novel view synthesis, yet current models suffer from severe performance degradation as the number of input references grows. We identify the root cause as a fundamental data bottleneck: existing datasets are dominated by single- or few-reference pairs and lack the structured, long-context supervision needed to learn dense inter-reference dependencies. To address this, we introduce MacroData, a large-scale dataset of 400K samples, each containing up to 10 reference images, systematically organized across four complementary dimensions -- Customization, Illustration, Spatial reasoning, and Temporal dynamics -- to provide comprehensive coverage of the multi-reference generation space. Recognizing the concurrent absence of standardized evaluation protocols, we further propose MacroBench, a benchmark of 4,000 samples that assesses generative coherence across graded task dimensions and input scales. Extensive experiments show that fine-tuning on MacroData yields substantial improvements in multi-reference generation, and ablation studies further reveal synergistic benefits of cross-task co-training and effective strategies for handling long-context complexity. The dataset and benchmark will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: A large-scale, multi-reference image-generation dataset (MacroData) with up to 10 references per sample, plus MacroBench for evaluation; fine-tuning on MacroData improves multi-reference generation; cross-task co-training and long-context strategies are beneficial; dataset and benchmark will be released publicly.
AI.motivation: Current multi-reference generation datasets are dominated by single- or few-reference pairs, causing a data bottleneck for learning dense inter-reference dependencies. There is also a lack of standardized evaluation protocols for long-context, multi-reference generation.
AI.method: Construct MacroData with 400K samples containing up to 10 reference images, organized across four axes—Customization, Illustration, Spatial reasoning, Temporal dynamics—to cover the multi-reference generation space. Create MacroBench with 4,000 samples to evaluate generative coherence across task dimensions and input scales. Train and evaluate models via fine-tuning on MacroData and perform ablation studies, including cross-task co-training and long-context handling strategies.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on MacroData yields substantial improvements in multi-reference generation. Ablations show synergistic gains from cross-task co-training and effective long-context strategies. The dataset and benchmark will be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: MacroData and MacroBench address the data bottleneck and evaluation gap in multi-reference image generation, enabling more coherent and scalable generation when many references are provided, with potential broad impact on real-world applications.

title: HeSS: Head Sensitivity Score for Sparsity Redistribution in VGGT
summary: Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) has advanced 3D vision, yet its global attention layers suffer from quadratic computational costs that hinder scalability. Several sparsification-based acceleration techniques have been proposed to alleviate this issue, but they often suffer from substantial accuracy degradation. We hypothesize that the accuracy degradation stems from the heterogeneity in head-wise sparsification sensitivity, as the existing methods apply a uniform sparsity pattern across all heads. Motivated by this hypothesis, we present a two-stage sparsification pipeline that effectively quantifies and exploits headwise sparsification sensitivity. In the first stage, we measure head-wise sparsification sensitivity using a novel metric, the Head Sensitivity Score (HeSS), which approximates the Hessian with respect to two distinct error terms on a small calibration set. In the inference stage, we perform HeSS-Guided Sparsification, leveraging the pre-computed HeSS to reallocate the total attention budget-assigning denser attention to sensitive heads and sparser attention to more robust ones. We demonstrate that HeSS effectively captures head-wise sparsification sensitivity and empirically confirm that attention heads in the global attention layers exhibit heterogeneous sensitivity characteristics. Extensive experiments further show that our method effectively mitigates performance degradation under high sparsity, demonstrating strong robustness across varying sparsification levels. Code is available at https://github.com/libary753/HeSS.

AI.tldr: HeSS is a headwise sparsification sensitivity-aware approach for VGGT that reallocates attention budget to mitigate accuracy loss under high sparsity.
AI.motivation: Global attention in VGGT incurs quadratic cost; sparsification methods reduce cost but often degrade accuracy. The degradation is hypothesized to stem from heterogeneous sensitivity across attention heads, which uniform sparsity fails to address.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline. Stage 1 computes Head Sensitivity Score (HeSS) by approximating the Hessian with respect to two error terms on a small calibration set. Stage 2 performs HeSS-Guided Sparsification at inference, reweighting or reallocating the total attention budget so that sensitive heads are kept denser while robust heads are pruned.
AI.result: HeSS effectively captures per-head sensitivity, revealing heterogeneity across global-attention heads. Empirical results show improved robustness to high sparsity and less accuracy degradation compared with uniform sparsification; performance remains strong across varying sparsification levels; code is released.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly accounting for headwise sparsification sensitivity yields more efficient sparsification for VGGTs, enabling scalable 3D vision transformers with controlled accuracy loss; the two-stage HeSS pipeline is practical and effective.

title: InstanceAnimator: Multi-Instance Sketch Video Colorization
summary: We propose InstanceAnimator, a novel Diffusion Transformer framework for multi-instance sketch video colorization. Existing methods suffer from three core limitations: inflexible user control due to heavy reliance on single reference frames, poor instance controllability leading to misalignment in multi-character scenarios, and degraded detail fidelity in fine-grained regions. To address these challenges, we introduce three corresponding innovations. First, a Canvas Guidance Condition eliminates workflow fragmentation by allowing free placement of reference elements and background, enabling unprecedented user flexibility. Second, an Instance Matching Mechanism resolves misalignment by integrating instance features with the sketches, ensuring precise control over multiple characters. Third, an Adaptive Decoupled Control Module enhances detail fidelity by injecting semantic features from characters, backgrounds, and text conditions into the diffusion process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that InstanceAnimator achieves superior multi-instance colorization with enhanced user control, high visual quality, and strong instance consistency.

AI.tldr: InstanceAnimator: a Diffusion Transformer framework for multi-instance sketch video colorization that uses Canvas Guidance, Instance Matching, and Adaptive Decoupled Control to improve user control, alignment, and detail fidelity; experiments show superior quality and consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing methods suffer from inflexible single-reference control, misalignment in multi-character scenarios, and degraded detail fidelity in fine-grained regions.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) Canvas Guidance Condition enables free placement of reference elements and background; (2) Instance Matching Mechanism integrates instance features with sketches to ensure precise multi-character control; (3) Adaptive Decoupled Control Module injects semantic features from characters, backgrounds, and text conditions into the diffusion process.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate superior multi-instance colorization with enhanced user control, high visual quality, and strong instance consistency.
AI.conclusion: InstanceAnimator provides a flexible and high-fidelity solution for multi-instance sketch video colorization, outperforming baselines on control, quality, and consistency.

title: CLIP-RD: Relational Distillation for Efficient CLIP Knowledge Distillation
summary: CLIP aligns image and text embeddings via contrastive learning and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization. Its large-scale architecture requires substantial computational and memory resources, motivating the distillation of its capabilities into lightweight student models. However, existing CLIP distillation methods do not explicitly model multi-directional relational dependencies between teacher and student embeddings, limiting the student's ability to preserve the structural relationships encoded by the teacher. To address this, we propose a relational knowledge distillation framework that introduces two novel methods, Vertical Relational Distillation (VRD) and Cross Relational Distillation (XRD). VRD enforces consistency of teacher-student distillation strength across modalities at the distribution level, while XRD imposes bidirectional symmetry on cross-modal teacher-student similarity distributions. By jointly modeling multi-directional relational structures, CLIP-RD promotes faithful alignment of the student embedding geometry with that of the teacher, outperforming existing methods by 0.8%p.

AI.tldr: Introduces CLIP-RD, a relational knowledge distillation framework with Vertical Relational Distillation (VRD) and Cross Relational Distillation (XRD) to better preserve teacher-student embedding geometry in CLIP, achieving a 0.8 percentage-point improvement over baselines.
AI.motivation: Reduce the performance gap between a large CLIP teacher and lightweight student without losing the teacher's structural relationships in the embedding space.
AI.method: Proposes VRD and XRD under a relational KD framework. VRD enforces consistent distillation strength across modalities at the distribution level; XRD enforces bidirectional symmetry on cross-modal teacher-student similarity distributions, jointly modeling multi-directional relational structures to align student geometry with the teacher.
AI.result: CLIP-RD yields improved embedding alignment over existing CLIP distillation methods, with a performance gain of 0.8 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Modeling multi-directional relational structures via VRD and XRD enables faithful transfer of the teacher's embedding geometry to lightweight students, improving zero-shot and retrieval-style evaluation metrics.

title: Multimodal Dataset Distillation via Phased Teacher Models
summary: Multimodal dataset distillation aims to construct compact synthetic datasets that enable efficient compression and knowledge transfer from large-scale image-text data. However, existing approaches often fail to capture the complex, dynamically evolving knowledge embedded in the later training stages of teacher models. This limitation leads to degraded student performance and compromises the quality of the distilled data. To address critical challenges such as pronounced cross-stage performance gaps and unstable teacher trajectories, we propose Phased Teacher Model with Shortcut Trajectory (PTM-ST) -- a novel phased distillation framework. PTM-ST leverages stage-aware teacher modeling and a shortcut-based trajectory construction strategy to accurately fit the teacher's learning dynamics across distinct training phases. This enhances both the stability and expressiveness of the distillation process. Through theoretical analysis and comprehensive experiments, we show that PTM-ST significantly mitigates optimization oscillations and inter-phase knowledge gaps, while also reducing storage overhead. Our method consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines on Flickr30k and COCO, achieving up to 13.5% absolute improvement and an average gain of 9.53% on Flickr30k. Code: https://github.com/Previsior/PTM-ST.

AI.tldr: A phased distillation framework with stage-aware teachers and shortcut trajectories (PTM-ST) to capture evolving learning dynamics in multimodal data distillation, improving stability, reducing storage, and achieving state-of-the-art results on Flickr30k and COCO.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal dataset distillation methods fail to model dynamic knowledge across training stages, causing cross-stage performance gaps and unstable teacher trajectories.
AI.method: Introduce PTM-ST: stage-aware teacher modeling and shortcut trajectory construction; theoretical analysis and empirical evaluation; demonstrate reduced optimization oscillations and inter-phase gaps; lower storage overhead.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA baselines on Flickr30k and COCO, with up to 13.5% absolute improvement and 9.53% average gain on Flickr30k; code released.
AI.conclusion: PTM-ST effectively captures teacher learning dynamics through phased distillation, yielding more stable and expressive data distillation with practical efficiency gains.

title: FSGNet: A Frequency-Aware and Semantic Guidance Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
summary: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) aims to identify and distinguish small targets from complex backgrounds. Leveraging the powerful multi-scale feature fusion capability of the U-Net architecture, IRSTD has achieved significant progress. However, U-Net suffers from semantic degradation when transferring high-level features from deep to shallow layers, limiting the precise localization of small targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes FSGNet, a lightweight and effective detection framework incorporating frequency-aware and semantic guidance mechanisms. Specifically, a multi-directional interactive attention module is proposed throughout the encoder to capture fine-grained and directional features, enhancing the network's sensitivity to small, low-contrast targets. To suppress background interference propagated through skip connections, a multi-scale frequency-aware module leverages Fast Fourier transform to filter out target-similar clutter while preserving salient target structures. At the deepest layer, a global pooling module captures high-level semantic information, which is subsequently upsampled and propagated to each decoder stage through the global semantic guidance flows, ensuring semantic consistency and precise localization across scales. Extensive experiments on four public IRSTD datasets demonstrate that FSGNet achieves superior detection performance and maintains high efficiency, highlighting its practical applicability and robustness. The codes will be released on https://github.com/Wangtao-Bao/FSGNet.

AI.tldr: FSGNet is a lightweight infrared small target detector that uses frequency-aware filtering and semantic guidance to overcome semantic degradation in U-Net and improve small-target localization and robustness.
AI.motivation: IR small target detection faces severe background clutter and semantic degradation when transferring high-level features to shallow layers; there is a need for a lightweight, accurate, and robust architecture.
AI.method: FSGNet introduces (1) a multi-directional interactive attention module in the encoder for fine-grained directional features; (2) a multi-scale frequency-aware module using FFT to suppress target-similar clutter while preserving target structures; (3) a global pooling module at the deepest layer to capture high-level semantics, propagated to decoders via global semantic guidance flows for cross-scale semantic consistency.
AI.result: Experiments on four public IRSTD datasets show superior detection performance and high efficiency; code will be released on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: FSGNet effectively mitigates semantic degradation in U-Net-based IRSTD, achieving accurate localization of small targets with low false positives while maintaining efficiency and robustness.

title: PMT: Plain Mask Transformer for Image and Video Segmentation with Frozen Vision Encoders
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) pre-trained at scale enable a single frozen encoder to serve multiple downstream tasks simultaneously. Recent VFM-based encoder-only models for image and video segmentation, such as EoMT and VidEoMT, achieve competitive accuracy with remarkably low latency, yet they require finetuning the encoder, sacrificing the multi-task encoder sharing that makes VFMs practically attractive for large-scale deployment. To reconcile encoder-only simplicity and speed with frozen VFM features, we propose the Plain Mask Decoder (PMD), a fast Transformer-based segmentation decoder that operates on top of frozen VFM features. The resulting model, the Plain Mask Transformer (PMT), preserves the architectural simplicity and low latency of encoder-only designs while keeping the encoder representation unchanged and shareable. The design seamlessly applies to both image and video segmentation, inheriting the generality of the encoder-only framework. On standard image segmentation benchmarks, PMT matches the frozen-encoder state of the art while running up to ~3x faster. For video segmentation, it even performs on par with fully finetuned methods, while being up to 8x faster than state-of-the-art frozen-encoder models. Code: https://github.com/tue-mps/pmt.

AI.tldr: PMT is a fast, transformer-based decoder that operates on frozen Vision Foundation Model features to deliver encoder-free yet high-performance image and video segmentation, matching or surpassing state-of-the-art while significantly reducing latency.
AI.motivation: To reconcile the advantages of encoder-only speed and the benefits of frozen VFM representations with the practical desire for encoder sharing across tasks; finetuning encoders breaks multi-task sharing and large-scale deployment.
AI.method: Introduce Plain Mask Decoder (PMD), a fast Transformer-based segmentation decoder designed to work on top of frozen VFM features, forming the Plain Mask Transformer (PMT). Applicable to both image and video segmentation, preserving encoder-free simplicity and low latency while keeping encoder representations frozen.
AI.result: On standard image segmentation benchmarks, PMT matches the frozen-encoder state-of-the-art while being up to ~3x faster. For video segmentation, PMT matches fully finetuned methods and is up to 8x faster than state-of-the-art frozen-encoder models.
AI.conclusion: PMT provides encoder-sharing-friendly, low-latency segmentation by operating on frozen VFM features with a lightweight Transformer-based decoder, generalizing to both image and video tasks and enabling scalable deployment.

title: HiSpatial: Taming Hierarchical 3D Spatial Understanding in Vision-Language Models
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project page: https://microsoft.github.io/HiSpatial
summary: Achieving human-like spatial intelligence for vision-language models (VLMs) requires inferring 3D structures from 2D observations, recognizing object properties and relations in 3D space, and performing high-level spatial reasoning. In this paper, we propose a principled hierarchical framework that decomposes the learning of 3D spatial understanding in VLMs into four progressively complex levels, from geometric perception to abstract spatial reasoning. Guided by this framework, we construct an automated pipeline that processes approximately 5M images with over 45M objects to generate 3D spatial VQA pairs across diverse tasks and scenes for VLM supervised fine-tuning. We also develop an RGB-D VLM incorporating metric-scale point maps as auxiliary inputs to further enhance spatial understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial understanding and reasoning benchmarks, surpassing specialized spatial models and large proprietary systems such as Gemini-2.5-pro and GPT-5. Moreover, our analysis reveals clear dependencies among hierarchical task levels, offering new insights into how multi-level task design facilitates the emergence of 3D spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical four-level framework for 3D spatial understanding in vision-language models (VLMs), supported by an automated data-generation pipeline (~5M images, 45M objects) that creates 3D spatial VQA pairs for supervised fine-tuning, plus an RGB-D VLM with metric-scale point maps. Achieves state-of-the-art results on spatial reasoning benchmarks, surpassing specialized models and large proprietary systems; reveals dependencies across hierarchical task levels and implications for multi-level task design.
AI.motivation: To enable human-like spatial intelligence in VLMs by decomposing 3D spatial understanding into progressively complex, structured tasks and validating the approach with large-scale data-driven fine-tuning and an enhanced RGB-D model.
AI.method: Proposes a four-level hierarchical framework from geometric perception to abstract spatial reasoning. Builds an automated pipeline to generate ~3D spatial VQA pairs across diverse tasks/scenes from ~5M images (45M objects). Trains an RGB-D VLM that uses metric-scale point maps as auxiliary inputs. Evaluates on multiple spatial understanding/reasoning benchmarks and compares with specialized models and large proprietary systems.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on several spatial benchmarks, surpassing specialized spatial models and large systems such as Gemini-2.5-pro and GPT-5. Demonstrates clear dependencies among hierarchical task levels and how multi-level task design facilitates 3D spatial intelligence emergence.
AI.conclusion: A multi-level, data-driven training regime, augmented with metric-scale RGB-D cues, can yield robust 3D spatial understanding in VLMs and provide insights into how hierarchical task design drives emergent spatial intelligence.

title: VideoWeaver: Multimodal Multi-View Video-to-Video Transfer for Embodied Agents
summary:
Recent progress in video-to-video (V2V) translation has enabled realistic resimulation of embodied AI demonstrations, a capability that allows pretrained robot policies to be transferable to new environments without additional data collection. However, prior works can only operate on a single view at a time, while embodied AI tasks are commonly captured from multiple synchronized cameras to support policy learning. Naively applying single-view models independently to each camera leads to inconsistent appearance across views, and standard transformer architectures do not scale to multi-view settings due to the quadratic cost of cross-view attention.
  We present VideoWeaver, the first multimodal multi-view V2V translation framework. VideoWeaver is initially trained as a single-view flow-based V2V model. To achieve an extension to the multi-view regime, we propose to ground all views in a shared 4D latent space derived from a feed-forward spatial foundation model, namely, Pi3. This encourages view-consistent appearance even under wide baselines and dynamic camera motion. To scale beyond a fixed number of cameras, we train views at distinct diffusion timesteps, enabling the model to learn both joint and conditional view distributions. This in turn allows autoregressive synthesis of new viewpoints conditioned on existing ones.
  Experiments show superior or similar performance to the state-of-the-art on the single-view translation benchmarks and, for the first time, physically and stylistically consistent multi-view translations, including challenging egocentric and heterogeneous-camera setups central to world randomization for robot learning.

AI.tldr: VideoWeaver introduces a multimodal multi-view video-to-video translation framework that grounds multiple synchronized camera views in a shared 4D latent space via a Pi3 foundation, enabling scalable, view-consistent synthesis with diffusion-based autoregressive generation across views, achieving competitive single-view performance and first consistent multi-view translations.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where single-view V2V models yield inconsistent appearance when applied to multi-view embodied AI data, and scale cross-view attention beyond a fixed camera count for stable multi-view translation.
AI.method: Start from a single-view flow-based V2V model, ground all views in a shared 4D latent space derived from the Pi3 spatial foundation model, train views at distinct diffusion timesteps to learn joint and conditional view distributions, enabling autoregressive synthesis of new viewpoints conditioned on existing ones.
AI.result: Competitive or superior performance on single-view benchmarks; first demonstration of physically and stylistically consistent multi-view translations, including challenging egocentric and heterogeneous-camera setups relevant to world randomization for robot learning.
AI.conclusion: VideoWeaver enables view-consistent, scalable multi-view V2V translation by grounding views in a shared 4D latent space and using diffusion-based autoregressive generation, advancing multi-camera data utilization for embodied AI policy transfer.

title: DC-Reg: Globally Optimal Point Cloud Registration via Tight Bounding with Difference of Convex Programming
summary: Achieving globally optimal point cloud registration under partial overlaps and large misalignments remains a fundamental challenge. While simultaneous transformation ($\boldsymbolθ$) and correspondence ($\mathbf{P}$) estimation has the advantage of being robust to nonrigid deformation, its non-convex coupled objective often leads to local minima for heuristic methods and prohibitive convergence times for existing global solvers due to loose lower bounds. To address this, we propose DC-Reg, a robust globally optimal framework that significantly tightens the Branch-and-Bound (BnB) search. Our core innovation is the derivation of a holistic concave underestimator for the coupled transformation-assignment objective, grounded in the Difference of Convex (DC) programming paradigm. Unlike prior works that rely on term-wise relaxations (e.g., McCormick envelopes) which neglect variable interplay, our holistic DC decomposition captures the joint structural interaction between $\boldsymbolθ$ and $\mathbf{P}$. This formulation enables the computation of remarkably tight lower bounds via efficient Linear Assignment Problems (LAP) evaluated at the vertices of the search boxes. We validate our framework on 2D similarity and 3D rigid registration, utilizing rotation-invariant features for the latter to achieve high efficiency without sacrificing optimality. Experimental results on synthetic data and the 3DMatch benchmark demonstrate that DC-Reg achieves significantly faster convergence and superior robustness to extreme noise and outliers compared to state-of-the-art global techniques.

AI.tldr: DC-Reg presents a globally optimal point-cloud registration framework by a holistic DC-based concave underestimator of the joint transformation-assignment objective, yielding tight BnB bounds via linear assignment at box vertices and faster convergence with robustness to noise/outliers.
AI.motivation: Partial overlaps and large misalignments render joint transformation and correspondence estimation highly non-convex. Loose lower bounds in existing global solvers lead to slow convergence; a tight, interaction-aware bound is needed.
AI.method: Derive a holistic difference-of-convex (DC) decomposition of the coupled transformation-assignment objective to obtain a concave underestimator. Compute tight lower bounds by solving Linear Assignment Problems (LAP) at the vertices of BnB search boxes. Apply to 2D similarity and 3D rigid registration using rotation-invariant features for efficiency, integrated within a Branch-and-Bound framework.
AI.result: Significantly faster convergence and improved robustness to extreme noise and outliers compared to state-of-the-art global methods, demonstrated on synthetic data and the 3DMatch benchmark.
AI.conclusion: DC-Reg delivers an effective globally optimal registration framework by capturing joint variable interactions in a holistic DC decomposition, enabling tight bounds and efficient computation; shows strong empirical performance and potential for broader applicability.

title: CIAR: Interval-based Collaborative Decoding for Image Generation Acceleration
summary: Auto-regressive (AR) models have recently made notable progress in image generation, achieving performance comparable to diffusion-based approaches. However, their computational intensity and sequential nature impede on-device deployment, causing disruptive latency. We address this via a cloud-device collaboration framework \textbf{CIAR}, which utilizes on-device self-verification to handle two key properties of visual synthesis: \textit{the vast token vocabulary} required for high-fidelity images and \textit{inherent spatial redundancy} which leads to extreme predictability in homogeneous regions, while object boundaries exhibit high uncertainty. Uniform verification wastes resources on such redundant tokens. Our solution centers on an on-device token uncertainty quantifier, which adopts continuous probability intervals to accelerate processing and make it feasible for large visual vocabularies instead of conventional discrete solution sets. Additionally, we incorporate a Interval-enhanced decoding module to further speed up decoding while maintaining visual fidelity and semantic consistency via a distribution alignment training strategy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CIAR achieves a 2.18x speed-up and reduces cloud requests by 70\%, while preserving image quality compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: CIAR introduces on-device token uncertainty quantification and interval-based decoding for autoregressive image generation, enabling cloud-device collaboration with substantial speedups and reduced cloud traffic while preserving fidelity.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive image models achieve diffusion-like quality but are compute-intensive and sequential; on-device deployment is bottlenecked by large vocabularies and redundancy; aim to offload cloud requests intelligently without quality loss.
AI.method: On-device token uncertainty quantifier using continuous probability intervals to prune unlikely tokens; interval-enhanced decoding to accelerate decoding; distribution-alignment training to keep fidelity; CIAR framework for cloud-device collaboration.
AI.result: 2.18x speed-up and 70% reduction in cloud requests with preserved image quality relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: CIAR demonstrates effective acceleration of on-device AR image synthesis via uncertainty-guided token processing and interval decoding, enabling practical deployment with reduced cloud dependence.

title: GridVAD: Open-Set Video Anomaly Detection via Spatial Reasoning over Stratified Frame Grids
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are powerful open-set reasoners, yet their direct use as anomaly detectors in video surveillance is fragile: without calibrated anomaly priors, they alternate between missed detections and hallucinated false alarms. We argue the problem is not the VLM itself but how it is used. VLMs should function as anomaly proposers, generating open-set candidate descriptions that are then grounded and tracked by purpose-built spatial and temporal modules. We instantiate this propose-ground-propagate principle in GridVAD, a training-free pipeline that produces pixel-level anomaly masks without any domain-specific training. A VLM reasons over stratified grid representations of video clips to generate natural-language anomaly proposals. Self-Consistency Consolidation (SCC) filters hallucinations by retaining only proposals that recur across multiple independent samplings. Grounding DINO anchors each surviving proposal to a bounding box, and SAM2 propagates it as a dense mask through the anomaly interval. The per-clip VLM budget is fixed at M+1 calls regardless of video length, where M can be set according to the proposals needed. On UCSD Ped2, GridVAD achieves the highest Pixel-AUROC (77.59) among all compared methods, surpassing even the partially fine-tuned TAO (75.11) and outperforms other zero-shot approaches on object-level RBDC by over 5x. Ablations reveal that SCC provides a controllable precision-recall tradeoff: filtering improves all pixel level metrics at a modest cost in object-level recall. Efficiency experiments show GridVAD is 2.7x more call-efficient than uniform per-frame VLM querying while additionally producing dense segmentation masks.Code and qualitative video results are available at https://gridvad.github.io.

AI.tldr: A training-free, VLM-based anomaly detection pipeline (GridVAD) that uses a propose-ground-propagate paradigm with Self-Consistency Consolidation to generate pixel-level anomaly masks; achieves state-of-the-art pixel-level AUROC on UCSD Ped2 (77.59), surpasses partially fine-tuned methods, with an efficient M+1 per-clip query budget and dense segmentation masks.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models are powerful but fragile as anomaly detectors in video surveillance due to missing calibrated priors. To leverage VLMs effectively, they should act as anomaly proposers, generating open-set descriptions that are then grounded and temporally propagated by dedicated modules.
AI.method: GridVAD implements a propose-ground-propagate pipeline: the VLM reasons over stratified grid representations of video clips to produce natural-language anomaly proposals. Self-Consistency Consolidation (SCC) filters hallucinations by retaining proposals that recur across multiple independent samplings. Grounding DINO anchors surviving proposals to bounding boxes, and SAM2 propagates them as dense masks over the anomaly interval. The per-clip budget is fixed at M+1 VLM calls, enabling a predictable compute budget. The approach is training-free and app-based on pixel-level segmentation.
AI.result: On UCSD Ped2, GridVAD achieves Pixel-AUROC of 77.59, the highest among reported methods, surpassing TAO (75.11) even though TAO is partially fine-tuned. It also outperforms other zero-shot approaches on object-level RBDC by over 5x. Ablations show SCC improves the precision-recall trade-off with manageable cost to recall. Efficiency: GridVAD is 2.7x more call-efficient than uniform per-frame VLM querying and provides dense segmentation masks.
AI.conclusion: The propose-ground-propagate paradigm enables robust, training-free anomaly detection in video using VLMs, by decoupling proposal generation from grounding and temporal propagation. SCC offers a tunable precision-recall trade-off, and the method delivers pixel-level masks with efficient Q-budget. Code and results are available at gridvad.github.io.

title: AdaSFormer: Adaptive Serialized Transformers for Monocular Semantic Scene Completion from Indoor Environments
summary: Indoor monocular semantic scene completion (MSSC) is notably more challenging than its outdoor counterpart due to complex spatial layouts and severe occlusions. While transformers are well suited for modeling global dependencies, their high memory cost and difficulty in reconstructing fine-grained details have limited their use in indoor MSSC. To address these limitations, we introduce AdaSFormer, a serialized transformer framework tailored for indoor MSSC. Our model features three key designs: (1) an Adaptive Serialized Transformer with learnable shifts that dynamically adjust receptive fields; (2) a Center-Relative Positional Encoding that captures spatial information richness; and (3) a Convolution-Modulated Layer Normalization that bridges heterogeneous representations between convolutional and transformer features. Extensive experiments on NYUv2 and Occ-ScanNet demonstrate that AdaSFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/alanWXZ/AdaSFormer.

AI.tldr: AdaSFormer is a serialized transformer framework for indoor monocular semantic scene completion (MSSC) that uses adaptive shifts for receptive fields, center-relative positional encoding, and convolution-modulated LayerNorm, achieving state-of-the-art results on NYUv2 and Occ-ScanNet with publicly available code.
AI.motivation: Indoor MSSC presents greater difficulty than outdoor MSSC due to occlusions and complex spatial layouts. Standard transformers struggle with memory costs and reconstructions of fine-grained details, limiting their effectiveness for indoor scenes.
AI.method: AdaSFormer comprises three components: (1) Adaptive Serialized Transformer with learnable shifts to dynamically adjust receptive fields; (2) Center-Relative Positional Encoding to enrich spatial information; (3) Convolution-Modulated Layer Normalization to bridge convolutional and transformer representations. The framework is tailored for indoor MSSC and evaluated on NYUv2 and Occ-ScanNet.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on NYUv2 and Occ-ScanNet datasets. Code is publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: AdaSFormer demonstrates that a carefully designed serialized transformer with adaptive receptive fields, spatially informative positional encoding, and hybrid normalization can effectively model indoor MSSC, balancing global context with fine-grained detail while maintaining efficiency.

title: RealRestorer: Towards Generalizable Real-World Image Restoration with Large-Scale Image Editing Models
summary: Image restoration under real-world degradations is critical for downstream tasks such as autonomous driving and object detection. However, existing restoration models are often limited by the scale and distribution of their training data, resulting in poor generalization to real-world scenarios. Recently, large-scale image editing models have shown strong generalization ability in restoration tasks, especially for closed-source models like Nano Banana Pro, which can restore images while preserving consistency. Nevertheless, achieving such performance with those large universal models requires substantial data and computational costs. To address this issue, we construct a large-scale dataset covering nine common real-world degradation types and train a state-of-the-art open-source model to narrow the gap with closed-source alternatives. Furthermore, we introduce RealIR-Bench, which contains 464 real-world degraded images and tailored evaluation metrics focusing on degradation removal and consistency preservation. Extensive experiments demonstrate our model ranks first among open-source methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: An open-source image restoration framework narrows the gap to closed-source models by (1) assembling a large-scale, real-world degradation dataset, (2) training a state-of-the-art open-source model, and (3) introducing RealIR-Bench with 464 real degraded images and specialized metrics, achieving state-of-the-art results among open-source methods.
AI.motivation: Generalization gap in real-world restoration due to limited training data and distribution shift; need accessible, cost-effective restoration solutions that perform well on real degradations.
AI.method: Construct a large-scale dataset covering nine degradation types; train a leading open-source restoration model; propose RealIR-Bench with 464 real-world degraded images and metrics that measure both degradation removal and consistency preservation.
AI.result: The open-source model ranks first among open-source methods and achieves state-of-the-art performance on the benchmark, demonstrating strong generalization and the effectiveness of the dataset and evaluation design.
AI.conclusion: Open-source restoration with carefully designed datasets and benchmarks can approach or match closed-source performance while reducing data and compute requirements; RealIR-Bench provides a standardized platform to evaluate future work.

title: Beyond the Golden Data: Resolving the Motion-Vision Quality Dilemma via Timestep Selective Training
summary: Recent advances in video generation models have achieved impressive results. However, these models heavily rely on the use of high-quality data that combines both high visual quality and high motion quality. In this paper, we identify a key challenge in video data curation: the Motion-Vision Quality Dilemma. We discovered that visual quality and motion intensity inherently exhibit a negative correlation, making it hard to obtain golden data that excels in both aspects. To address this challenge, we first examine the hierarchical learning dynamics of video diffusion models and conduct gradient-based analysis on quality-degraded samples. We discover that quality-imbalanced data can produce gradients similar to golden data at appropriate timesteps. Based on this, we introduce the novel concept of Timestep selection in Training Process. We propose Timestep-aware Quality Decoupling (TQD), which modifies the data sampling distribution to better match the model's learning process. For certain types of data, the sampling distribution is skewed toward higher timesteps for motion-rich data, while high visual quality data is more likely to be sampled during lower timesteps. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that TQD enables training exclusively on separated imbalanced data to achieve performance surpassing conventional training with better data, challenging the necessity of perfect data in video generation. Moreover, our method also boosts model performance when trained on high-quality data, showcasing its effectiveness across different data scenarios.

AI.tldr: Introduces Timestep-aware Quality Decoupling (TQD) to address the Motion–Vision Quality Dilemma in video diffusion model training by reweighting data sampling across learning timesteps, enabling training on quality-imbalanced data to outperform conventional training that relies on high-quality data alone.
AI.motivation: Video generation models require both high visual quality and high motion quality; these properties are negatively correlated in real data, complicating data curation and limiting model performance. The study aims to decouple quality aspects during training to relax data prerequisites.
AI.method: Gradient-based analysis of quality-degraded samples to study learning dynamics in video diffusion models; define Timestep Selection in Training Process; propose TQD that adjusts data sampling distribution by timesteps—favoring higher timesteps for motion-rich data and lower timesteps for high-visual-quality data—so the model learns to balance quality aspects.
AI.result: Empirical results show that training with the proposed imbalanced data under TQD achieves performance surpassing conventional training on better data; TQD also improves performance when trained on high-quality data, demonstrating effectiveness across data regimes.
