AI.conclusion: TQD enables robust video generation training using quality-imbalanced data by aligning the sampling schedule with the model’s learning dynamics, reducing reliance on golden data and enhancing performance across scenarios.

title: BFMD: A Full-Match Badminton Dense Dataset for Dense Shot Captioning
summary: Understanding tactical dynamics in badminton requires analyzing entire matches rather than isolated clips. However, existing badminton datasets mainly focus on short clips or task-specific annotations and rarely provide full-match data with dense multimodal annotations. This limitation makes it difficult to generate accurate shot captions and perform match-level analysis. To address this limitation, we introduce the first Badminton Full Match Dense (BFMD) dataset, with 19 broadcast matches (including both singles and doubles) covering over 20 hours of play, comprising 1,687 rallies and 16,751 hit events, each annotated with a shot caption. The dataset provides hierarchical annotations including match segments, rally events, and dense rally-level multimodal annotations such as shot types, shuttle trajectories, player pose keypoints, and shot captions. We develop a VideoMAE-based multimodal captioning framework with a Semantic Feedback mechanism that leverages shot semantics to guide caption generation and improve semantic consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that multimodal modeling and semantic feedback improve shot caption quality over RGB-only baselines. We further showcase the potential of BFMD by analyzing the temporal evolution of tactical patterns across full matches.

AI.tldr: First Badminton Full Match Dense dataset (BFMD) with 19 matches, 1,687 rallies, 16,751 hit events and dense multimodal annotations; paired with a VideoMAE-based captioning framework using Semantic Feedback to improve shot captions and enable full-match tactical analysis.
AI.motivation: Current badminton datasets are clip-focused and lack full-match dense multimodal annotations, hindering accurate shot captions and match-level tactical analysis.
AI.method: Construct BFMD with hierarchical annotations (match segments, rally events, dense rally-level data: shot types, shuttle trajectories, player pose keypoints, shot captions). Propose a VideoMAE-based multimodal captioning framework with Semantic Feedback that uses shot semantics to steer caption generation.
AI.result: Demonstrate improved caption quality with multimodal modeling and semantic feedback over RGB-only baselines; show potential to analyze temporal evolution of tactical patterns across full matches.
AI.conclusion: BFMD enables comprehensive full-match tactical analysis and robust match-level analytics; the semantic-guided multimodal captioning framework validates the value of integrating shot semantics into caption generation.

title: PAWS: Perception of Articulation in the Wild at Scale from Egocentric Videos
summary: Articulation perception aims to recover the motion and structure of articulated objects (e.g., drawers and cupboards), and is fundamental to 3D scene understanding in robotics, simulation, and animation. Existing learning-based methods rely heavily on supervised training with high-quality 3D data and manual annotations, limiting scalability and diversity. To address this limitation, we propose PAWS, a method that directly extracts object articulations from hand-object interactions in large-scale in-the-wild egocentric videos. We evaluate our method on the public data sets, including HD-EPIC and Arti4D data sets, achieving significant improvements over baselines. We further demonstrate that the extracted articulations benefit downstream tasks, including fine-tuning 3D articulation prediction models and enabling robot manipulation. See the project website at https://aaltoml.github.io/PAWS/.

AI.tldr: PAWS extracts object articulations from hand-object interactions in in-the-wild egocentric videos to reduce reliance on 3D data and manual annotations, enabling scalable articulation understanding.
AI.motivation: Current articulation perception methods require costly supervised 3D data and annotations, limiting scalability and diversity. There is a need to leverage abundant in-the-wild data to learn articulations.
AI.method: PAWS directly extracts articulations from hand-object interactions in large-scale egocentric video data. Evaluation on public datasets (HD-EPIC, Arti4D) shows significant improvement over baselines. Demonstrates downstream benefits for fine-tuning 3D articulation models and robot manipulation.
AI.result: Significant improvements over baselines on HD-EPIC and Arti4D datasets; articulations extracted from egocentric video prove useful for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: PAWS demonstrates scalable articulation discovery from wild video data, improves 3D articulation modeling, and aids robot manipulation, suggesting strong potential for broad 3D scene understanding applications.

title: Towards Comprehensive Real-Time Scene Understanding in Ophthalmic Surgery through Multimodal Image Fusion
summary:
Purpose: The integration of multimodal imaging into operating rooms paves the way for comprehensive surgical scene understanding. In ophthalmic surgery, by now, two complementary imaging modalities are available: operating microscope (OPMI) imaging and real-time intraoperative optical coherence tomography (iOCT). This first work toward temporal OPMI and iOCT feature fusion demonstrates the potential of multimodal image processing for multi-head prediction through the example of precise instrument tracking in vitreoretinal surgery.
  Methods: We propose a multimodal, temporal, real-time capable network architecture to perform joint instrument detection, keypoint localization, and tool-tissue distance estimation. Our network design integrates a cross-attention fusion module to merge OPMI and iOCT image features, which are efficiently extracted via a YoloNAS and a CNN encoder, respectively. Furthermore, a region-based recurrent module leverages temporal coherence.
  Results: Our experiments demonstrate reliable instrument localization and keypoint detection (95.79% mAP50) and show that the incorporation of iOCT significantly improves tool-tissue distance estimation, while achieving real-time processing rates of 22.5 ms per frame. Especially for close distances to the retina (below 1 mm), the distance estimation accuracy improved from 284 $μm$ (OPMI only) to 33 $μm$ (multimodal).
  Conclusion: Feature fusion of multimodal imaging can enhance multi-task prediction accuracy compared to single-modality processing and real-time processing performance can be achieved through tailored network design. While our results demonstrate the potential of multi-modal processing for image-guided vitreoretinal surgery, they also underline key challenges that motivate future research toward more reliable, consistent, and comprehensive surgical scene understanding.

AI.tldr: Multimodal OPMI+iOCT with temporal fusion yields real-time instrument detection, keypoint localization, and tool-tissue distance estimation in vitreoretinal surgery, outperforming OPMI alone, esp. near retina.
AI.motivation: In ophthalmic surgery, combining microscope imaging with real-time iOCT can provide comprehensive scene understanding and improve metric like tool-tissue distance estimation; single-modality limits require fusion.
AI.method: A real-time multimodal network using cross-attention fusion to merge OPMI features from a YoloNAS encoder and iOCT features from a CNN encoder, plus a region-based recurrent module to exploit temporal coherence for joint detection, keypoint localization, and distance estimation.
AI.result: mAP50 = 95.79% for instrument localization and keypoint detection; distance estimation improves from 284 μm (OPMI only) to 33 μm (multimodal) for distances below 1 mm; processing at 22.5 ms per frame (real-time).
AI.conclusion: Multimodal feature fusion enhances multi-task predictive accuracy over single-modality processing; tailored architecture achieves real-time performance; however, challenges remain for reliable and comprehensive surgical scene understanding.

title: GeoHeight-Bench: Towards Height-Aware Multimodal Reasoning in Remote Sensing
summary: Current Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) in Earth Observation typically neglect the critical "vertical" dimension, limiting their reasoning capabilities in complex remote sensing geometries and disaster scenarios where physical spatial structures often outweigh planar visual textures. To bridge this gap, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework dedicated to height-aware remote sensing understanding. First, to overcome the severe scarcity of annotated data, we develop a scalable, VLM-driven data generation pipeline utilizing systematic prompt engineering and metadata extraction. This pipeline constructs two complementary benchmarks: GeoHeight-Bench for relative height analysis, and a more challenging GeoHeight-Bench+ for holistic, terrain-aware reasoning. Furthermore, to validate the necessity of height perception, we propose GeoHeightChat, the first height-aware remote sensing LMM baseline. Serving as a strong proof of concept, our baseline demonstrates that synergizing visual semantics with implicitly injected height geometric features effectively mitigates the "vertical blind spot", successfully unlocking a new paradigm of interactive height reasoning in existing optical models.

AI.tldr: Introduces height-aware evaluation for Earth Observation LMMs, with two synthetic benchmarks (GeoHeight-Bench and GeoHeight-Bench+) and a baseline GeoHeightChat that fuses height geometry with visual features to address the vertical blind spot.
AI.motivation: Current Large Multimodal Models in Earth Observation largely ignore the vertical dimension, limiting reasoning in 3D remote sensing geographies and disaster scenarios; scarcity of annotated height data; need for dedicated benchmarks and baselines to evaluate height perception.
AI.method: Develops a scalable VLM-driven data generation pipeline using systematic prompt engineering and metadata extraction to construct two benchmarks (GeoHeight-Bench for relative height analysis; GeoHeight-Bench+ for terrain-aware reasoning) and proposes GeoHeightChat as the first height-aware remote sensing LMM baseline to validate height perception.
AI.result: Preliminary results indicate that injecting height geometry features alongside visual semantics mitigates the vertical blind spot and enables interactive height reasoning in existing optical models (proof-of-concept).
AI.conclusion: Height perception is essential for EO LMMs; the proposed framework and benchmarks enable systematic evaluation and development of height-aware reasoning capabilities.

title: UNIC: Neural Garment Deformation Field for Real-time Clothed Character Animation
summary: Simulating physically realistic garment deformations is an essential task for virtual immersive experience, which is often achieved by physics simulation methods. However, these methods are typically time-consuming, computationally demanding, and require costly hardware, which is not suitable for real-time applications. Recent learning-based methods tried to resolve this problem by training graph neural networks to learn the garment deformation on vertices, which, however, fail to capture the intricate deformation of complex garment meshes with complex topologies. In this paper, we introduce a novel neural deformation field-based method, named UNIC, to animate the garments of an avatar in real time, given the motion sequences. Our key idea is to learn the instance-specific neural deformation field to animate the garment meshes. Such an instance-specific learning scheme does not require UNIC to generalize to new garments but only to new motion sequences, which greatly reduces the difficulty in training and improves the deformation quality. Moreover, neural deformation fields map the 3D points to their deformation offsets, which not only avoids handling topologies of the complex garments but also injects a natural smoothness constraint in the deformation learning. Extensive experiments have been conducted on various kinds of garment meshes to demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of UNIC over baseline methods, making it potentially practical and useful in real-world interactive applications like video games.

AI.tldr: Introduces UNIC, an instance-specific neural deformation field method for real-time garment animation, addressing limitations of physics-based and graph-based methods by learning deformation offsets per garment instance to achieve high-quality, topology-agnostic results with improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: Real-time garment animation is computationally intensive with physics simulations; graph neural networks struggle with complex garment topology; a scalable, efficient alternative that preserves deformation quality is desired.
AI.method: Train an instance-specific neural deformation field that maps 3D points to deformation offsets to animate garment meshes for a given motion sequence, avoiding explicit topology handling and enforcing smoothness inherent to neural fields; operates in real time and targets various garment meshes.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness and efficiency over baseline methods; capable of real-time animation for different motion sequences on diverse garment meshes; shows practical potential for video games and interactive applications.
AI.conclusion: Instance-specific neural deformation fields offer a practical, high-quality alternative to physics-based and generalizable graph-based garment models, balancing realism and efficiency for real-time use.

title: Designing Any Imaging System from Natural Language: Agent-Constrained Composition over a Finite Primitive Basis
comment: 28 pages, 7 figures, 8 tables, includes Supplementary Information (sections S1-S6)
summary:
Designing a computational imaging system -- selecting operators, setting parameters, validating consistency -- requires weeks of specialist effort per modality, creating an expertise bottleneck that excludes the
  broader scientific community from prototyping imaging instruments. We introduce spec.md, a structured specification format, and three autonomous agents -- Plan, Judge, and Execute -- that translate a one-sentence
  natural-language description into a validated forward model with bounded reconstruction error. A design-to-real error theorem decomposes total reconstruction error into five independently bounded terms, each linked
  to a corrective action. On 6 real-data modalities spanning all 5 carrier families, the automated pipeline matches expert-library quality (98.1 +/- 4.2%). Ten novel designs -- composing primitives into chains from 3D
  to 5D -- demonstrate compositional reach beyond any single-modality tool.

AI.tldr: Automates design of computational imaging systems via spec.md and autonomous agents (Plan, Judge, Execute), with a design-to-real error theorem; achieves expert-like reconstruction across modalities and demonstrates compositional design beyond single-modality tools.
AI.motivation: Lower the expertise bottleneck in designing computational imaging systems; enable broader community to prototype imaging instruments quickly by converting natural-language descriptions into validated forward models with bounded errors.
AI.method: Introduce a structured specification format (spec.md) and three autonomous agents (Plan, Judge, Execute) to translate natural-language descriptions into validated forward models; derive a design-to-real error theorem that decomposes total reconstruction error into five independently bounded terms, each linked to a corrective action; validate on 6 real-data modalities across 5 carrier families; demonstrate compositional design by constructing 10 novel designs by chaining primitives from 3D to 5D.
AI.result: Automated pipeline matches expert-library quality (98.1 ± 4.2%) on real data; 10 novel designs demonstrate compositional reach beyond any single-modality tool.
AI.conclusion: The spec.md format and autonomous agents enable rapid, validated design of imaging systems, reducing expert effort and enabling cross-modality prototyping; the error decomposition provides actionable guidance for corrective steps and ensures bounded reconstruction error.

title: LEMMA: Laplacian pyramids for Efficient Marine SeMAntic Segmentation
comment: Accepted at the MaCVi Workshop, CVPR 2026
summary:
Semantic segmentation in marine environments is crucial for the autonomous navigation of unmanned surface vessels (USVs) and coastal Earth Observation events such as oil spills. However, existing methods, often relying on deep CNNs and transformer-based architectures, face challenges in deployment due to their high computational costs and resource-intensive nature. These limitations hinder the practicality of real-time, low-cost applications in real-world marine settings.
  To address this, we propose LEMMA, a lightweight semantic segmentation model designed specifically for accurate remote sensing segmentation under resource constraints. The proposed architecture leverages Laplacian Pyramids to enhance edge recognition, a critical component for effective feature extraction in complex marine environments for disaster response, environmental surveillance, and coastal monitoring. By integrating edge information early in the feature extraction process, LEMMA eliminates the need for computationally expensive feature map computations in deeper network layers, drastically reducing model size, complexity and inference time. LEMMA demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across datasets captured from diverse platforms while reducing trainable parameters and computational requirements by up to 71x, GFLOPs by up to 88.5\%, and inference time by up to 84.65\%, as compared to existing models. Experimental results highlight its effectiveness and real-world applicability, including 93.42\% IoU on the Oil Spill dataset and 98.97\% mIoU on Mastr1325.

AI.tldr: LEMMA is a lightweight marine semantic segmentation model that uses Laplacian pyramids to inject edge information early, achieving substantial efficiency gains (up to 71x fewer parameters, 88.5% fewer GFLOPs, 84.65% faster inference) while delivering strong performance (Oil Spill IoU 93.42%, Mastr1325 mIoU 98.97%).
AI.motivation: Marine semantic segmentation for USVs and coastal monitoring is hampered by the high computational cost of CNNs/transformers, limiting real-time deployment on resource-constrained platforms. Edge details are critical for accurate segmentation in cluttered, dynamic marine scenes and disaster scenarios.
AI.method: LEMMA integrates Laplacian pyramids to enhance edge recognition and propagates edge information early in feature extraction, reducing dependence on deep, compute-heavy feature maps and resulting in smaller, faster models with lower inference time.
AI.result: State-of-the-art or strong performance across diverse marine datasets, with dramatic reductions in trainable parameters (up to 71x), GFLOPs (up to 88.5%), and inference time (up to 84.65%), plus high IoU scores (Oil Spill 93.42% IoU; Mastr1325 98.97% mIoU).
AI.conclusion: LEMMA offers a practical, real-time capable solution for marine semantic segmentation under resource constraints, enabling effective deployment on USVs and coastal monitoring systems; future work may explore broader datasets and sensor fusion.

title: Wan-Weaver: Interleaved Multi-modal Generation via Decoupled Training
summary: Recent unified models have made unprecedented progress in both understanding and generation. However, while most of them accept multi-modal inputs, they typically produce only single-modality outputs. This challenge of producing interleaved content is mainly due to training data scarcity and the difficulty of modeling long-range cross-modal context. To address this issue, we decompose interleaved generation into textual planning and visual consistency modeling, and introduce a framework consisting of a planner and a visualizer. The planner produces dense textual descriptions for visual content, while the visualizer synthesizes images accordingly. Under this guidance, we construct large-scale textual-proxy interleaved data (where visual content is represented in text) to train the planner, and curate reference-guided image data to train the visualizer. These designs give rise to Wan-Weaver, which exhibits emergent interleaved generation ability with long-range textual coherence and visual consistency. Meanwhile, the integration of diverse understanding and generation data into planner training enables Wan-Weaver to achieve robust task reasoning and generation proficiency. To assess the model's capability in interleaved generation, we further construct a benchmark that spans a wide range of use cases across multiple dimensions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, even without access to any real interleaved data, Wan-Weaver achieves superior performance over existing methods.

AI.tldr: Wan-Weaver introduces a planner-visualizer framework to enable interleaved textual and visual generation by decomposing the task into textual planning and visual consistency modeling. It creates textual-proxy interleaved data to train the planner and reference-guided image data to train the visualizer, achieving emergent interleaved capabilities without real interleaved data and strong cross-modal reasoning.
AI.motivation: The main motivation is to overcome training-data scarcity and long-range cross-modal context modeling when producing interleaved multi-modal outputs, by decoupling planning from visual generation and leveraging proxy data to train each component.
AI.method: A two-module architecture: a planner that generates dense textual descriptions of visual content, and a visualizer that synthesizes images conditioned on that content. Training uses textual-proxy interleaved data (textual representations of visuals) for the planner and reference-guided image data for the visualizer, enabling cross-modal coherence and long-range planning.
AI.result: Wan-Weaver shows emergent interleaved generation with long-range textual coherence and visual consistency. The model benefits from joint understanding/generation data in planner training and achieves robust task reasoning and generation. On a newly constructed benchmark spanning multiple use cases, it outperforms existing methods even without access to real interleaved data.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing interleaved multi-modal generation into planning and visual synthesis, with proxy data and cross-modal training, yields a data-efficient framework that achieves strong interleaved generation and robust reasoning capabilities.

title: TRACE: Object Motion Editing in Videos with First-Frame Trajectory Guidance
summary: We study object motion path editing in videos, where the goal is to alter a target object's trajectory while preserving the original scene content. Unlike prior video editing methods that primarily manipulate appearance or rely on point-track-based trajectory control, which is often challenging for users to provide during inference, especially in videos with camera motion, we offer a practical, easy-to-use approach to controllable object-centric motion editing. We present Trace, a framework that enables users to design the desired trajectory in a single anchor frame and then synthesizes a temporally consistent edited video. Our approach addresses this task with a two-stage pipeline: a cross-view motion transformation module that maps first-frame path design to frame-aligned box trajectories under camera motion, and a motion-conditioned video re-synthesis module that follows these trajectories to regenerate the object while preserving the remaining content of the input video. Experiments on diverse real-world videos show that our method produces more coherent, realistic, and controllable motion edits than recent image-to-video and video-to-video methods.

AI.tldr: Introduce Trace: a two-stage framework for user-friendly, anchor-frame-based object motion editing in videos that maps a desired first-frame trajectory to frame-aligned trajectories under camera motion and re-synthesizes the video accordingly.
AI.motivation: Current video editing techniques either manipulate appearance or rely on point-based trajectories that are difficult for users to specify, especially with camera motion. There is a need for a practical, trajectory-centric editing method that is easy to use.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) cross-view motion transformation module maps the user-designed first-frame path to frame-aligned box trajectories under camera motion, and (2) a motion-conditioned video re-synthesis module follows these trajectories to regenerate the object while preserving the rest of the video content. Editing is anchored to a single frame.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse real-world videos show Trace yields more coherent, realistic, and controllable motion edits than recent image-to-video and video-to-video methods.
AI.conclusion: Trace provides a practical, easy-to-use solution for object-centric video motion editing with improved coherence and realism, validating the effectiveness of the proposed two-stage approach.

title: Seeing to Ground: Visual Attention for Hallucination-Resilient MDLLMs
summary: Multimodal Diffusion Large Language Models (MDLLMs) achieve high-concurrency generation through parallel masked decoding, yet the architectures remain prone to multimodal hallucinations. This structural vulnerability stems from an algorithmic flaw: the decoder ranks candidate tokens based on textual likelihood without verifying localized visual support. We establish that this language-only ranking induces an objective mismatch, where language probability mass acts as a misspecified proxy for the intended multimodal task. Consequently, we reinterpret hallucination as a localized optimization error, a phenomenon where the decoder exploits language shortcuts to maximize a proxy score at the expense of visual grounding. To address this objective mismatch, we introduce VISAGE, a training-free decoding framework that calibrates the objective at inference time. VISAGE estimates the proxy discrepancy by quantifying the spatial entropy of cross-attention distributions. By enforcing a localization consensus across attention heads, the method penalizes spatially uniform distributions and re-ranks token commitments to favor visually grounded outcomes. We provide an analytical stability guarantee establishing that VISAGE maintains a bounded objective loss under estimation error. Evaluations across hallucination-sensitive and general-purpose benchmarks demonstrate the robustness of the framework, yielding relative gains of 8.59% on MMMU-val and 7.75% on HallusionBench.

AI.tldr: Introduces VISAGE, a training-free decoding framework for Multimodal Diffusion LLMs that addresses objective mismatch between language likelihood and multimodal grounding by penalizing spatially uniform cross-attention and enforcing localization across heads, yielding robustness gains on hallucination-sensitive benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MDLLMs suffer from multimodal hallucinations due to using language-only token ranking without verifying visual support; objective mismatch between text likelihood and multimodal tasks motivates new decoding-time calibration.
AI.method: VISAGE estimates proxy discrepancy at inference by measuring the spatial entropy of cross-attention distributions and enforces localization consensus across attention heads to penalize uniform attention, re-ranking tokens to favor visually grounded outputs; no additional training required.
AI.result: Analytical stability guarantee showing bounded objective loss under estimation error. Empirical results show relative gains of 8.59% on MMMU-val and 7.75% on HallusionBench, indicating improved robustness to hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: VISAGE provides a training-free, inference-time calibration that aligns decoding with multimodal grounding, offering provable stability and practical gains across benchmarks.

title: AnyHand: A Large-Scale Synthetic Dataset for RGB(-D) Hand Pose Estimation
summary: We present AnyHand, a large-scale synthetic dataset designed to advance the state of the art in 3D hand pose estimation from both RGB-only and RGB-D inputs. While recent works with foundation approaches have shown that an increase in the quantity and diversity of training data can markedly improve performance and robustness in hand pose estimation, existing real-world-collected datasets on this task are limited in coverage, and prior synthetic datasets rarely provide occlusions, arm details, and aligned depth together at scale. To address this bottleneck, our AnyHand contains 2.5M single-hand and 4.1M hand-object interaction RGB-D images, with rich geometric annotations. In the RGB-only setting, we show that extending the original training sets of existing baselines with AnyHand yields significant gains on multiple benchmarks (FreiHAND and HO-3D), even when keeping the architecture and training scheme fixed. More impressively, the model trained with AnyHand shows stronger generalization to the out-of-domain HO-Cap dataset, without any fine-tuning. We also contribute a lightweight depth fusion module that can be easily integrated into existing RGB-based models. Trained with AnyHand, the resulting RGB-D model achieves superior performance on the HO-3D benchmark, showing the benefits of depth integration and the effectiveness of our synthetic data.

AI.tldr: AnyHand is a large-scale synthetic RGB and RGB-D hand pose dataset (2.5M single-hand + 4.1M hand-object images) with rich annotations, designed to improve 3D hand pose estimation; it augments existing baselines and enables improved generalization and depth fusion integration.
AI.motivation: Existing real-world datasets fail to cover sufficient hand poses, occlusions, and arm details; synthetic datasets often lack depth-aligned coverage at scale. There is a need for large-scale, diverse, RGB and depth data with rich annotations to boost 3D hand pose estimation and generalization across domains.
AI.method: Introduce AnyHand: a large-scale synthetic dataset containing 2.5M single-hand and 4.1M hand-object interaction RGB-D images with rich geometric annotations; include occlusions and depth alignment; propose a lightweight depth fusion module that can be integrated into RGB-based models; evaluate by augmenting RGB baselines on FreiHAND and HO-3D, demonstrate cross-domain generalization to HO-Cap without fine-tuning, and report that the RGB-D model trained with AnyHand achieves superior HO-3D performance.
AI.result: Augmenting RGB baselines with AnyHand yields significant gains on FreiHAND and HO-3D while keeping architecture/training unchanged; the AnyHand-trained model generalizes better to HO-Cap without fine-tuning; the proposed depth fusion module enables RGB models to achieve superior performance on HO-3D when combined with AnyHand.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale synthetic data with depth information and a lightweight depth fusion module substantially improves 3D hand pose estimation performance and generalization, indicating the efficacy of AnyHand for advancing RGB and RGB-D hand pose estimation research.

title: BizGenEval: A Systematic Benchmark for Commercial Visual Content Generation
summary: Recent advances in image generation models have expanded their applications beyond aesthetic imagery toward practical visual content creation. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on natural image synthesis and fail to systematically evaluate models under the structured and multi-constraint requirements of real-world commercial design tasks. In this work, we introduce BizGenEval, a systematic benchmark for commercial visual content generation. The benchmark spans five representative document types: slides, charts, webpages, posters, and scientific figures, and evaluates four key capability dimensions: text rendering, layout control, attribute binding, and knowledge-based reasoning, forming 20 diverse evaluation tasks. BizGenEval contains 400 carefully curated prompts and 8000 human-verified checklist questions to rigorously assess whether generated images satisfy complex visual and semantic constraints. We conduct large-scale benchmarking on 26 popular image generation systems, including state-of-the-art commercial APIs and leading open-source models. The results reveal substantial capability gaps between current generative models and the requirements of professional visual content creation. We hope BizGenEval serves as a standardized benchmark for real-world commercial visual content generation.

AI.tldr: BizGenEval is a new benchmark for commercial visual content generation evaluating five document types and four capability dimensions, with 20 tasks, 400 prompts, and 8000 checklist questions, benchmarking 26 models to reveal gaps to professional workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize natural image synthesis and neglect structured, multi-constraint tasks of real-world commercial design; a standardized benchmark is needed for evaluating AI tools in professional visual content creation.
AI.method: Introduce BizGenEval benchmark framework covering slides, charts, webpages, posters, and scientific figures; four capability axes (text rendering, layout control, attribute binding, knowledge-based reasoning); 20 evaluation tasks; 400 prompts and 8000 checklist questions; large-scale benchmarking across 26 commercial APIs and open-source models using human-verified criteria.
AI.result: The benchmark reveals substantial capability gaps between current generative models and professional visual content creation requirements across the tested systems.
AI.conclusion: BizGenEval can serve as a standardized benchmark to drive and assess progress in real-world commercial visual content generation.

title: SlotVTG: Object-Centric Adapter for Generalizable Video Temporal Grounding
comment: Accepted to GRAIL-V workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong performance on Video Temporal Grounding (VTG). However, their coarse recognition capabilities are insufficient for fine-grained temporal understanding, making task-specific fine-tuning indispensable. This fine-tuning causes models to memorize dataset-specific shortcuts rather than faithfully grounding in the actual visual content, leading to poor Out-of-Domain (OOD) generalization. Object-centric learning offers a promising remedy by decomposing scenes into entity-level representations, but existing approaches require re-running the entire multi-stage training pipeline from scratch. We propose SlotVTG, a framework that steers MLLMs toward object-centric, input-grounded visual reasoning at minimal cost. SlotVTG introduces a lightweight slot adapter that decomposes visual tokens into abstract slots via slot attention and reconstructs the original sequence, where objectness priors from a self-supervised vision model encourage semantically coherent slot formation. Cross-domain evaluation on standard VTG benchmarks demonstrates that our approach significantly improves OOD robustness while maintaining competitive In-Domain (ID) performance with minimal overhead.

AI.tldr: SlotVTG introduces a lightweight slot adapter that turns visual tokens into object-centric slots using slot attention, guided by self-supervised objectness priors, enabling input-grounded reasoning for VTG with improved OOD generalization at low overhead, while maintaining competitive ID performance.
AI.motivation: MLLMs excel at Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) but rely on coarse recognition; fine-tuning for VTG leads to dataset-specific shortcuts and poor OOD generalization. Object-centric representations promise better grounding, but existing approaches require expensive re-training.
AI.method: A lightweight slot adapter decomposes visual tokens into abstract slots via slot attention and reconstructs the original sequence. Objectness priors from a self-supervised vision model guide semantically coherent slot formation. Evaluations on cross-domain VTG benchmarks show improved OOD robustness with minimal overhead and competitive ID performance.
AI.result: Significant improvement in OOD robustness with minimal overhead, while maintaining competitive In-Domain performance.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that object-centric, input-grounded reasoning can be achieved with limited retraining cost, offering better generalization for VTG and potential applicability to related video understanding tasks.

title: Unleashing Guidance Without Classifiers for Human-Object Interaction Animation
summary: Generating realistic human-object interaction (HOI) animations remains challenging because it requires jointly modeling dynamic human actions and diverse object geometries. Prior diffusion-based approaches often rely on hand-crafted contact priors or human-imposed kinematic constraints to improve contact quality. We propose LIGHT, a data-driven alternative in which guidance emerges from the denoising pace itself, reducing dependence on manually designed priors. Building on diffusion forcing, we factor the representation into modality-specific components and assign individualized noise levels with asynchronous denoising schedules. In this paradigm, cleaner components guide noisier ones through cross-attention, yielding guidance without auxiliary classifiers. We find that this data-driven guidance is inherently contact-aware, and can be enhanced when training is augmented with a broad spectrum of synthetic object geometries, encouraging invariance of contact semantics to geometric diversity. Extensive experiments show that pace-induced guidance more effectively mirrors the benefits of contact priors than conventional classifier-free guidance, while achieving higher contact fidelity, more realistic HOI generation, and stronger generalization to unseen objects and tasks.

AI.tldr: LIGHT introduces data-driven pace-induced guidance for HOI animation via diffusion forcing, leveraging cross-attention across modality-specific components and asynchronous denoising; improves contact fidelity and generalization without classifiers.
AI.motivation: To remove reliance on hand-crafted contact priors and human-imposed kinematic constraints by deriving guidance directly from the denoising process in diffusion models for human–object interaction (HOI) animation.
AI.method: Factor the representation into modality-specific components with individualized noise levels and asynchronous denoising schedules. Use cross-attention to let cleaner components guide noisier ones, enabling guidance without auxiliary classifiers. Train with diverse synthetic object geometries to encourage contact-semantics invariance to geometry.
AI.result: PACE-induced guidance better mirrors the benefits of contact priors than classifier-free guidance, achieving higher contact fidelity, more realistic HOI generation, and stronger generalization to unseen objects/tasks.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven pace-induced guidance provides effective priors-free HOI synthesis; diversity in training geometries enhances robustness of contact semantics across objects and tasks.

title: How good was my shot? Quantifying Player Skill Level in Table Tennis
summary: Gauging an individual's skill level is crucial, as it inherently shapes their behavior. Quantifying skill, however, is challenging because it is latent to the observed actions. To explore skill understanding in human behavior, we focus on dyadic sports -- specifically table tennis -- where skill manifests not just in complex movements, but in the subtle nuances of execution conditioned on game context. Our key idea is to learn a generative model of each player's tactical racket strokes and jointly embed them in a common latent space that encodes individual characteristics, including those pertaining to skill levels. By training these player models on a large-scale dataset of 3D-reconstructed professional matches and conditioning them on comprehensive game context -- including player positioning and opponent behaviors -- the models capture individual tactical identities within their latent space. We probe this learned player space and find that it reflects distinct play styles and attributes that collectively represent skill. By training a simple relative ranking network on these embeddings, we demonstrate that both relative and absolute skill predictions can be achieved. These results demonstrate that the learned player space effectively quantifies skill levels, providing a foundation for automated skill assessment in complex, interactive behaviors.

AI.tldr: Generative, context-aware player models in table tennis embed players in a shared latent space to quantify skill, enabling both absolute and relative skill assessment from 3D-reconstructed match data.
AI.motivation: Skill is latent and hard to observe; a representation is needed that captures individual tactical identities and skill in an interactive sport.
AI.method: Train per-player generative models of tactical strokes; learn a shared latent space conditioned on game context (positioning, opponent behavior) using 3D-reconstructed professional matches; train a simple ranking network on embeddings to predict skill.
AI.result: The latent space encodes distinct play styles and skill-related attributes; the ranking model achieves both relative and absolute skill predictions; demonstrates quantification of skill and potential for automated assessment.
AI.conclusion: A learned player space provides a foundation for automated skill assessment in complex interactive behaviors; could enable scalable evaluation and coaching insights.

title: PSDesigner: Automated Graphic Design with a Human-Like Creative Workflow
summary: Graphic design is a creative and innovative process that plays a crucial role in applications such as e-commerce and advertising. However, developing an automated design system that can faithfully translate user intentions into editable design files remains an open challenge. Although recent studies have leveraged powerful text-to-image models and MLLMs to assist graphic design, they typically simplify professional workflows, resulting in limited flexibility and intuitiveness. To address these limitations, we propose PSDesigner, an automated graphic design system that emulates the creative workflow of human designers. Building upon multiple specialized components, PSDesigner collects theme-related assets based on user instructions, and autonomously infers and executes tool calls to manipulate design files, such as integrating new assets or refining inferior elements. To endow the system with strong tool-use capabilities, we construct a design dataset, CreativePSD, which contains a large amount of high-quality PSD design files annotated with operation traces across a wide range of design scenarios and artistic styles, enabling models to learn expert design procedures. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PSDesigner outperforms existing methods across diverse graphic design tasks, empowering non-specialists to conveniently create production-quality designs.

AI.tldr: PSDesigner is an automated graphic-design system that mimics the human design workflow by collecting assets, autonomously applying tool calls to PSD files, and leveraging a PSD-annotated dataset (CreativePSD) to learn expert procedures; experiments show superior performance over existing methods across multiple design tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between end-to-end text-to-image/LLM-based design and professional workflows that require editable files and precise, iterative tool manipulation. There is a need for flexible, intuitive automation that can translate user intent into editable design assets while supporting production-quality results.
AI.method: PSDesigner comprises specialized components that (1) retrieve theme-related assets based on user instructions, (2) infer and execute tool calls to modify design files (e.g., integrate assets, refine elements), and (3) learn design procedures from the CreativePSD dataset, which contains PSDs annotated with operation traces across diverse scenarios and styles.
AI.result: PSDesigner outperforms existing methods on diverse graphic design tasks, enabling non-specialists to create production-quality designs with greater flexibility and fidelity to user intent.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that emulating professional design workflows and enabling robust tool-use via a PSD-annotated dataset can significantly advance automated graphic design, with CreativePSD supporting learning of expert procedures and potential for broader tool integration and more complex workflows.

title: MegaFlow: Zero-Shot Large Displacement Optical Flow
summary: Accurate estimation of large displacement optical flow remains a critical challenge. Existing methods typically rely on iterative local search or/and domain-specific fine-tuning, which severely limits their performance in large displacement and zero-shot generalization scenarios. To overcome this, we introduce MegaFlow, a simple yet powerful model for zero-shot large displacement optical flow. Rather than relying on highly complex, task-specific architectural designs, MegaFlow adapts powerful pre-trained vision priors to produce temporally consistent motion fields. In particular, we formulate flow estimation as a global matching problem by leveraging pre-trained global Vision Transformer features, which naturally capture large displacements. This is followed by a few lightweight iterative refinements to further improve the sub-pixel accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MegaFlow achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across multiple optical flow benchmarks. Moreover, our model also delivers highly competitive zero-shot performance on long-range point tracking benchmarks, demonstrating its robust transferability and suggesting a unified paradigm for generalizable motion estimation. Our project page is at: https://kristen-z.github.io/projects/megaflow.

AI.tldr: MegaFlow is a zero-shot large-displacement optical flow model that uses pre-trained Vision Transformer features to formulate flow estimation as global matching, followed by lightweight refinements, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot results and strong transferability.
AI.motivation: Large-displacement optical flow estimation remains challenging, with existing methods relying on iterative local search or task-specific fine-tuning, hindering zero-shot generalization and general applicability across benchmarks.
AI.method: Formulate flow estimation as a global matching problem by leveraging pre-trained global Vision Transformer features to capture large displacements, then apply a few lightweight iterative refinements for sub-pixel accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across multiple optical flow benchmarks and shows competitive zero-shot performance on long-range point tracking benchmarks, indicating robust transferability.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that simple, priors-based design can yield generalizable motion estimation; supports a unified paradigm for zero-shot, generalizable motion estimation using pre-trained priors.

title: RefAlign: Representation Alignment for Reference-to-Video Generation
summary: Reference-to-video (R2V) generation is a controllable video synthesis paradigm that constrains the generation process using both text prompts and reference images, enabling applications such as personalized advertising and virtual try-on. In practice, existing R2V methods typically introduce additional high-level semantic or cross-modal features alongside the VAE latent representation of the reference image and jointly feed them into the diffusion Transformer (DiT). These auxiliary representations provide semantic guidance and act as implicit alignment signals, which can partially alleviate pixel-level information leakage in the VAE latent space. However, they may still struggle to address copy--paste artifacts and multi-subject confusion caused by modality mismatch across heterogeneous encoder features. In this paper, we propose RefAlign, a representation alignment framework that explicitly aligns DiT reference-branch features to the semantic space of a visual foundation model (VFM). The core of RefAlign is a reference alignment loss that pulls the reference features and VFM features of the same subject closer to improve identity consistency, while pushing apart the corresponding features of different subjects to enhance semantic discriminability. This simple yet effective strategy is applied only during training, incurring no inference-time overhead, and achieves a better balance between text controllability and reference fidelity. Extensive experiments on the OpenS2V-Eval benchmark demonstrate that RefAlign outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in TotalScore, validating the effectiveness of explicit reference alignment for R2V tasks.

AI.tldr: RefAlign introduces a training-time reference alignment loss that explicitly aligns DiT's reference-branch features with a visual foundation model's semantic space to improve identity consistency and reduce cross-subject confusion in reference-to-video generation, without adding inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing R2V methods risk pixel-level leakage in VAE latent spaces and suffer from copy-paste artifacts and multi-subject confusion due to modality mismatch across heterogeneous encoder features. A semantic-space alignment could strengthen identity preservation and discriminability.
AI.method: Propose RefAlign, a representation alignment framework that adds a reference alignment loss during training to pull DiT reference-branch features toward the semantic space of a visual foundation model (VFM) for the same subject, and push apart features of different subjects; no inference-time overhead.
AI.result: On the OpenS2V-Eval benchmark, RefAlign achieves higher TotalScore than state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating improved reference fidelity and text controllability balance.
AI.conclusion: Explicit reference alignment to a visual foundation model semantic space is effective for R2V tasks, yielding better identity consistency and navigation of cross-modal features without incurring inference costs.

title: MuRF: Unlocking the Multi-Scale Potential of Vision Foundation Models
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have become the cornerstone of modern computer vision, offering robust representations across a wide array of tasks. While recent advances allow these models to handle varying input sizes during training, inference typically remains restricted to a single, fixed scale. This prevalent single-scale paradigm overlooks a fundamental property of visual perception: varying resolutions offer complementary inductive biases, where low-resolution views excel at global semantic recognition and high-resolution views are essential for fine-grained refinement. In this work, we propose Multi-Resolution Fusion (MuRF), a simple yet universally effective strategy to harness this synergy at inference time. Instead of relying on a single view, MuRF constructs a unified representation by processing an image at multiple resolutions through a frozen VFM and fusing the resulting features. The universality of MuRF is its most compelling attribute. It is not tied to a specific architecture, serving instead as a fundamental, training-free enhancement to visual representation. We empirically validate this by applying MuRF to a broad spectrum of critical computer vision tasks across multiple distinct VFM families - primarily DINOv2, but also demonstrating successful generalization to contrastive models like SigLIP2.

AI.tldr: MuRF is a training-free, universal inference-time strategy that fuses multi-resolution features from a frozen Vision Foundation Model to improve representations across tasks; validated primarily on DINOv2 and also on SigLIP2.
AI.motivation: Single-scale inference underutilizes the complementary inductive biases available across resolutions. Low-resolution views favor global semantics, while high-resolution views enable fine-grained refinement. A universal, training-free method to exploit this multi-resolution information at inference is desirable.
AI.method: Process an image at multiple resolutions through a frozen Vision Foundation Model and fuse the resulting features into a unified representation. The approach is architecture-agnostic and training-free, designed as a universal enhancement to visual representations across VFM families.
AI.result: Empirical validation across critical CV tasks and multiple VFM families, with primary demonstration on DINOv2 and successful generalization to SigLIP2, indicating the universality of MuRF as a training-free enhancement.
AI.conclusion: MuRF provides a universal, training-free enhancement for visual representations by enabling multi-resolution fusion at inference, applicable across diverse VFM architectures.

title: Less Gaussians, Texture More: 4K Feed-Forward Textured Splatting
summary: Existing feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting methods predict pixel-aligned primitives, leading to a quadratic growth in primitive count as resolution increases. This fundamentally limits their scalability, making high-resolution synthesis such as 4K intractable. We introduce LGTM (Less Gaussians, Texture More), a feed-forward framework that overcomes this resolution scaling barrier. By predicting compact Gaussian primitives coupled with per-primitive textures, LGTM decouples geometric complexity from rendering resolution. This approach enables high-fidelity 4K novel view synthesis without per-scene optimization, a capability previously out of reach for feed-forward methods, all while using significantly fewer Gaussian primitives. Project page: https://yxlao.github.io/lgtm/

AI.tldr: LGTM introduces a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting method that uses fewer, compact Gaussians with per-primitive textures, decoupling geometry from rendering resolution and enabling 4K novel view synthesis without per-scene optimization.
AI.motivation: Current feed-forward Gaussian Splatting methods incur quadratic growth in primitive count with display resolution, hindering scalability to high-res rendering like 4K.
AI.method: LGTM predicts compact Gaussian primitives accompanied by per-primitive textures, decoupling geometric complexity from rendering resolution and avoiding per-scene optimization; reduces primitive count while maintaining high fidelity.
AI.result: Demonstrates high-fidelity 4K novel view synthesis with significantly fewer Gaussians; achieves comparable or superior quality to existing methods without per-scene optimization.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling geometry from rendering resolution via texture-enriched, compact primitives overcomes the resolution scaling bottleneck in feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling scalable high-resolution synthesis.

title: ShotStream: Streaming Multi-Shot Video Generation for Interactive Storytelling
summary: Multi-shot video generation is crucial for long narrative storytelling, yet current bidirectional architectures suffer from limited interactivity and high latency. We propose ShotStream, a novel causal multi-shot architecture that enables interactive storytelling and efficient on-the-fly frame generation. By reformulating the task as next-shot generation conditioned on historical context, ShotStream allows users to dynamically instruct ongoing narratives via streaming prompts. We achieve this by first fine-tuning a text-to-video model into a bidirectional next-shot generator, which is then distilled into a causal student via Distribution Matching Distillation. To overcome the challenges of inter-shot consistency and error accumulation inherent in autoregressive generation, we introduce two key innovations. First, a dual-cache memory mechanism preserves visual coherence: a global context cache retains conditional frames for inter-shot consistency, while a local context cache holds generated frames within the current shot for intra-shot consistency. And a RoPE discontinuity indicator is employed to explicitly distinguish the two caches to eliminate ambiguity. Second, to mitigate error accumulation, we propose a two-stage distillation strategy. This begins with intra-shot self-forcing conditioned on ground-truth historical shots and progressively extends to inter-shot self-forcing using self-generated histories, effectively bridging the train-test gap. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ShotStream generates coherent multi-shot videos with sub-second latency, achieving 16 FPS on a single GPU. It matches or exceeds the quality of slower bidirectional models, paving the way for real-time interactive storytelling. Training and inference code, as well as the models, are available on our

AI.tldr: A causal, streaming next-shot video generator (ShotStream) enables interactive multi-shot storytelling with low latency via dual caches and RoPE-based cache separation, plus a two-stage distillation strategy; achieves ~16 FPS on a single GPU and matches or surpasses slower bidirectional models.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time, interactive long-form video storytelling with coherent inter-shot and intra-shot consistency, addressing high latency and limited interactivity in existing bidirectional video generation models.
AI.method: Fine-tune a text-to-video model into a bidirectional next-shot generator, then distill into a causal student via Distribution Matching Distillation. Introduce a dual-cache memory: a global context cache for inter-shot consistency and a local context cache for intra-shot consistency, with a RoPE discontinuity indicator to distinguish caches. Employ a two-stage distillation: intra-shot self-forcing on ground-truth history, followed by inter-shot self-forcing with self-generated histories to bridge train-test gaps.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show coherent multi-shot videos with sub-second latency, achieving 16 FPS on a single GPU, matching or exceeding the quality of slower bidirectional models.
AI.conclusion: ShotStream demonstrates real-time, interactive multi-shot video generation with maintained coherence across shots. Its architectural innovations (dual caches, RoPE indicator, two-stage distillation) enable streaming prompts without significant quality loss.
